China.act: Announcement No. 37 of 2022 by the Ministry of Commerce 《商务部公告2022年第37号 关于原产于日本和韩国的进口非色散位移单模光纤所适用反倾销措施期终复审裁定的公告》
China.act_norm: announcement no 37 of 2022 by the ministry of commerce 2022 37
acts_norm: announcement no 37 of 2022 by the ministry of commerce 2022 37
China.coverage:
Product: Dispersion unshifted single-mode optical fibres (HS 9001.1000)

Countries: Japan, Republic of Korea
China.impact: In January 2005, the Ministry of Commerce of the People's Republic of China announced anti-dumping duties on non-displacement single-mode optical fibers (used, for example, for long-distance telephony and multichannel television broadcasting systems) (HS code: 9001.1000) imported from Japan and South Korea. This measure was reviewed and extended in January 2011 and, subsequently, in January 2017 and January 2023 for five years. The rate of duty imposed on imports originating in Japan is 46%, while for imports originating in the Republic of Korea ranges from 7.9% to 46% depending on the company.
China.timeframe: Since January 2005, extended in January 2011, 2017 and 2023, until January 2028

China.coverage:
Product: Dispersion unshifted single-mode optical fibres (HS 9001.1000)

Countries: European Union, United States
China.impact: In April 2011, the Ministry of Commerce of the People's Republic of China announced anti-dumping duties on non-displacement single-mode optical fibers (used, for example, for long-distance telephony and multichannel television broadcasting systems) (HS code: 9001.1000) imported from the European Union and the United States This measure was reviewed and extended in April 2017 and, subsequently in April 2023 for five years. The rate of duty on imports originating from the European Union ranges from 12.9% to 29.1%, depending on the company. The rate of duty on imports originating from the United States ranges from 33.3% to 78.2%, depending on the company.
China.timeframe: Since April 2011, extended in April 2017 and 2022, until April 2028

China.act: Administrative Measures for the Multi-level Protection of Information Security 《信息安全等级管理办法》
China.act_norm: administrative measures for the multi level protection of information security
acts_norm: administrative measures for the multi level protection of information security
China.coverage: Information Systems including network infrastructure, cloud computing systems, mobile application platforms, connected devices and industrial control systems
China.impact:
The MLPS requires all IT systems in China to be classified on different levels of security, from one to five (with the most sensitive systems designated as level 5).

According to Article 21 of the Administrate Measures: Information systems classified as Level 3 or above shall select and use information security products that meet the following conditions: (1) The product development and manufacturing entity is invested in by Chinese citizens, legal persons, or the State, or is State-controlled, and has independent legal entity status within the territory of the People's Republic of China; (2) The product's core technology and key components possess independent intellectual property rights of China; and (3) For products listed in the information security product certification catalog, certification must be obtained from the national information security product certification authority.

China.act_norm: government procurement law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: government procurement law of the people s republic of china
China.impact: As per Article 10 of the Govenment Procurement Law, the government must procure domestic goods, construction and services except when: (i) the goods, construction and services needed are not available in China and cannot be required on reasonable commercial terms; (ii) the items to be procured are for use abroad; (iii) otherwise provided for by other laws and administrative regulations.
China.timeframe: Since June 2002, last amended in August 2014

China.act:
Administrative Measures for the Multi-level Protection of Information Security 《信息安全等级管理办法》;

GB/T 22239-2019 Information Security Technology – Baseline for Multi-level Protection of Cyber Security 《信息安全技术 网络安全等级保护基本要求》
China.act_norm: administrative measures for the multi level protection of information security gb t 22239 2019 information security technology baseline for multi level protection of cyber security
acts_norm: administrative measures for the multi level protection of information security
gb t 22239 2019 information security technology baseline for multi level protection of cyber security
China.coverage: Information Systems including network infrastructure, cloud computing systems, mobile application platforms, connected devices and industrial control systems
China.impact:
The MLPS requires all IT systems in China to be classified on different levels of security, from one to five (with the most sensitive systems designated as level 5).

Article 8.1.9.3 of the GB/T 22239-2019 states that in terms of product procurement and usage, companies classified as level 3 and above need to ensure that (1) the procurement and usage of cybersecurity products comply with relevant national regulations; (2) the procurement and usage of encryption products and services comply with the requirements of the national encryption management authority; and (3) preliminary product selection testing shall be carried out to define the scope of candidate products. The list of selected products shall be reviewed and updated on a regular basis.

So far, there are no requirements for source code disclosure or for using specific encryption in order to win a tender.

China.act: Special Management Measures for Foreign Investment Access (Negative list), 2024《外国投资准入特别管理措施 负面清单 2024》
China.act_norm: special management measures for foreign investment access 2024 2024
acts_norm: special management measures for foreign investment access 2024 2024
China.coverage:
Information Transmission, Software, and Information Technology Services Industry;

Culture, Sports, and Entertainment Industry
China.impact:
As per the 2024 Negative List, it is prohibited to invest in internet news information services, online publishing services, online audiovisual program services, internet cultural operations (except for music), and internet public information dissemination services (except for content that China has committed to opening up in its WTO commitments).

The negative further prohibits foreign investment in news organisations (including but not limited to news agencies), electronic publication, film production companies, ditribution companies, and film import businesses.

Investment in various levels of radio stations (stations), television stations (stations), radio and television channels (frequencies), radio and television transmission coverage networks (including transmission stations, relay stations, microwave stations, monitoring stations, and online radio and television transmission coverage networks) is prohibited. Investment in radio and television video-on-demand services and satellite ground receiving facilities installation services is also prohibited.

China.act_norm: provisions on the administration of foreign invested telecommunications enterprises notice of the state council on temporarily adjusting the implementation of relevant administrative regulations in pilot free trade zones approval on temporarily adjusting the implementation of relevant administrative regulations and departmental rules approved by the state council in beijing municipality
acts_norm: provisions on the administration of foreign invested telecommunications enterprises
notice of the state council on temporarily adjusting the implementation of relevant administrative regulations in pilot free trade zones
approval on temporarily adjusting the implementation of relevant administrative regulations and departmental rules approved by the state council in beijing municipality
China.impact:
In January 2014, the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) and the Shanghai Municipal Government jointly issued the "Opinions of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology and the Shanghai Municipal People’s Government on Further Opening Up Value-Added Telecommunications Services in the China (Shanghai) Pilot Free Trade Zone." The document proposed a pilot program for domestic internet virtual private network (VPN) services, with foreign ownership capped at 50%. The enterprise's registered location and service facilities were required to be within the pilot free trade zone.

In January 2020, the "Notice of the State Council on Temporarily Adjusting the Implementation of Relevant Administrative Regulations in Pilot Free Trade Zones" proposed extending the pilot policies originally implemented in the Shanghai Pilot Free Trade Zone (28.8 square kilometers) to all pilot free trade zones.

In September 2020, the State Council approved a plan allowing foreign firms to own up to 50 percent in joint ventures providing virtual private network (VPN) services for foreign companies in Beijing.

China.act_norm: special management measures for foreign investment access 2024 2024
acts_norm: special management measures for foreign investment access 2024 2024

In particular, a director is not required to be resident in China and there is no nationality requirement.

China.act: Provisions on the Administration of Foreign Invested Telecommunications Enterprises《外商投资电信企业管理规定》
China.act_norm: provisions on the administration of foreign invested telecommunications enterprises
acts_norm: provisions on the administration of foreign invested telecommunications enterprises
China.impact:
Article 10 of the Provisions states that foreign-invested telecommunications enterprises, after legally completing market entity registration, shall apply to the competent department of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology of the State Council for a telecommunications business operating license. The competent department of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology of the State Council shall, upon receiving the application, review the relevant documents within the prescribed timeframe:

- For applications related to basic telecommunications services, the review shall be completed within 180 days from the date of receipt of the application, and a decision shall be made to approve or disapprove the application.
- For applications related to value-added telecommunications services, the review shall be completed within 60 days from the date of receipt of the application, and a decision shall be made to approve or disapprove the application.

For approved applications, a Telecommunications Business Operating License shall be issued. For disapproved applications, the applicant shall be notified in writing with an explanation of the reasons.

Additionally, 
Article 11: The main content of the statement on the investor's situation for foreign-invested telecommunications enterprises includes: the name and basic information of the investor, the shareholding ratios of all parties, and the control situation of foreign investors over the foreign-invested telecommunications enterprise, among other details.

Article 12: Foreign-invested telecommunications enterprises operating cross-border telecommunications services must obtain approval from the competent department of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology of the State Council. These services shall be conducted through international telecommunications entry and exit bureaus established with the approval of the competent department of the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology of the State Council.
China.timeframe: Since December 2001, last amended in March 2022

China.act: Circular of the General Office of the State Council on the Establishment of Security Review System Regarding Merger and Acquisition of Domestic Enterprises by Foreign Investors《国务院办公厅关于建立外国投资者并购境内企业安全审查制度的通知》
China.act_norm: circular of the general office of the state council on the establishment of security review system regarding merger and acquisition of domestic enterprises by foreign investors
acts_norm: circular of the general office of the state council on the establishment of security review system regarding merger and acquisition of domestic enterprises by foreign investors
China.coverage: Sectors related to key industries or national economic security
China.impact:
Article 1 of the Circular sets the scope of merger and acquisition security reviews, including: (1) Foreign investors acquiring domestic military enterprises and enterprises supporting military industries, enterprises located near key and sensitive military facilities, as well as other entities related to national defense security; and (2) Foreign investors acquiring domestic enterprises involved in key areas related to national security, such as important agricultural products, critical energy and resources, vital infrastructure, essential transportation services, core technologies, and major equipment manufacturing, where the actual control of the enterprise may be obtained by foreign investors.

Article 2 states the content of merger and acquisition security reviews:
1. The impact of the merger or acquisition transaction on national defense security, including its effect on the domestic production capacity of products needed for national defense, the ability to provide domestic services, and related equipment and facilities.
2. The impact of the merger or acquisition transaction on the stable operation of the national economy.
3. The impact of the merger or acquisition transaction on the basic social living order.
4. The impact of the merger or acquisition transaction on the research and development capabilities of critical technologies related to national security.

China.act: Measures for Security Review of Foreign Investments 《外商投资安全审查办法》
China.act_norm: measures for security review of foreign investments
acts_norm: measures for security review of foreign investments
China.impact:
According to Article 2 of the Measure for Security Review of Foreign Investments, the types of transactions carried out by foreign investors directly or indirectly in China that may be subject to security review include the following: (i) investments by foreign investors by themselves or jointly with other investors, that are investing in any new project or establishing a business in China; (ii) foreign investors that are acquiring equity or assets of domestic business through mergers and acquisitions; and (iii) foreign investors making investments into China through other methods.

Article 4 states that Foreign investments in the important information technology and Internet products and services, major equipment, key technologies sectors must be declared to the office of the Operational Mechanism (the office is set up under the Measure for the review of foreign investments), to the extent that the investment has a bearing on national security and the that actual control of the investee enterprise is required. Actual control has been been said to exist when the total amount of foreign investment is larger than 50% or the foreign investor is able to affect the board, shareholder meetings or the essential personnel who decide the operation, human resources, finance or key technology of the company.

Pursuant to review, the Operational Mechanism can decide that the foreign investment will not affect national security, in which case the security review has been passed. If it is decided that the foreign investment will affect national security, the parties to the investment may be required to accept the attached conditions which will govern the investment. If the Operational Mechanism decides to prohibit the investment, the same shall not be carried out.

China.act: Ministry of Commerce Order No. 4 of 2020 on the List of Unreliable Entities 《商务部2020年第4号令 不可靠实体名单规定》
China.act_norm: ministry of commerce order no 4 of 2020 on the list of unreliable entities 2020 4
acts_norm: ministry of commerce order no 4 of 2020 on the list of unreliable entities 2020 4
China.impact:
The Ministry of Commerce Order No. 4 of 2020 on the List of Unreliable Entities sets up a list of unreliable enterprises. As per the Order, foreign entities classified as unreliable entities could face sanctions in the form of restrictions or prohibition on the foreign entity from engaging in China-related import or export activities, restrictions on prohibition on the foreign entity from investing in China etc. Foreign entities that could be included in the unreliable entity list include those involved in international commerce or related activities that engage in endangering national sovereignty, security or development interests in China, suspending normal transactions against any organization or individual in China, entities which violate market principles and cause damage to the rights and interests of the Chinese entity. Under this Order, a special unit in MOFCOM will be responsible for identifying foreign entities (which include foreign enterprises, organizations and individuals) that will be subject to restrictions. 

In February 2023, the Unreliable Entities List Working Mechanism headed by China’s Ministry of Commerce has for the first time designated two US aerospace and defense companies as “Unreliable Entities”. So far, no firms in digital trade related sectors have been designated as an unreliable entity.

China.act: National Security Law of the People's Republic of China 《中华人民共和国国家安全法》
China.act_norm: national security law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: national security law of the people s republic of china
China.impact: Article 59 of the National Security Law states that the state shall establish a system and mechanism for national security review and supervision. It conducts national security reviews on foreign investments, specific items and critical technologies, network information technology products and services, construction projects involving national security matters, as well as other major issues and activities that affect or may affect national security. This aims to effectively prevent and mitigate national security risks.

China.act_norm: regulations of the people s republic of china on telecommunications regulation on internet information services of the people s republic of china measures for the administration of telecommunication business operation license
acts_norm: regulations of the people s republic of china on telecommunications
regulation on internet information services of the people s republic of china
measures for the administration of telecommunication business operation license
China.impact:
Article 7 of the Regulation on Internet Information Services of the People's Republic of China: "For the operation of internet information services, the applicant should submit a request to the provincial, autonomous region, or directly governed municipality telecommunications management authority or the national industrial and information technology management authority to apply for an internet information service value-added telecommunications business operating license (hereinafter referred to as the "operating license").

The provincial, autonomous region, or directly governed municipality telecommunications management authority or the national industrial and information technology management authority should, upon receiving the application, complete the review within 60 days. If approved, the operating license should be issued; if not approved, the applicant should be notified in writing with an explanation of the reasons. After obtaining the operating license, the applicant should proceed with registration and continue with the relevant processing for business registration."

Such commercial operations include: E-commerce, online advertising, social media, WeChat H5 pages and mini programmes, forums and discussion platforms, and user-generated content websites.

Furthermore, according to Article 6 of the Measures for the Administration of Telecommunication Business Operation License, in order to obtain a lisence, The operator must meet the following conditions: 1) The operator is a legally established company; 2) The operator has sufficient funds and professional personnel suitable for carrying out business activities; 3) The operator has the reputation or ability to provide long-term services to users; 4) For operations within a province, autonomous region, or directly governed municipality, the minimum registered capital is 1 million RMB; for operations nationwide or across provinces, autonomous regions, or directly governed municipalities, the minimum registered capital is 10 million RMB; 5) The operator has the necessary premises, facilities, and technical plans; 6) The company and its major investors and key management personnel are not listed on the telecommunications business dishonesty list; 7) Other conditions as stipulated by the state.

China.act: Patent Law of the People's Republic of China《中华人民共和国专利法》
China.act_norm: patent law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: patent law of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Article 18 of the amended Chinese Patent Law establishes that non-resident foreigners or organizations without business establishments in China shall entrust the patent agency established by law to handle patent applications or other patent-related matters in China. 

Article 19 provides that any entity or individual intending to file a patent application in a foreign country for an invention or utility model completed in China shall submit a request for confidential examination to the patent administration department under the State Council in advance. The patent administration department of the State Council shall handle international patent applications in accordance with the relevant international treaties to which the People's Republic of China is a party, this Law and the relevant provisions of the State Council.
China.timeframe: Since April 1984, last amended in June 2021

China.act: Patent Law of the People's Republic of China《中华人民共和国专利法》
China.act_norm: patent law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: patent law of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Civil and administrative procedures:
Articles 46 and 63 allow parties to appeal administrative decisions related to invalidity and compulsory licensing by filing lawsuits with the courts.
Article 46: Any organization or individual who believes that the grant of a patent does not comply with the provisions of this Law may request the administrative department for patent affairs under the State Council to declare the patent right invalid. The administrative department for patent affairs under the State Council shall promptly examine and decide on the request for invalidation of the patent right and notify the parties concerned. The decision on the invalidation of a patent right shall be registered and announced by the administrative department for patent affairs under the State Council. The decision shall be final and binding.
Article 63: In the event of a dispute over patent infringement, the parties concerned may resolve the dispute through mediation. If mediation fails, or if one of the parties refuses mediation, they may bring a lawsuit to the people's court or request the relevant administrative department for patents to handle the matter.
When the relevant administrative department for patents determines the matter, if a party is dissatisfied with the handling decision, they may bring a lawsuit to the people's court within 15 days from the date of receiving the notification. If no lawsuit is brought within the prescribed time limit, the decision shall come into effect and be enforced by the people's court.

Remedies:
Article 71: When the amount of damages for patent infringement cannot be determined based on the losses suffered by the patent holder, the benefits obtained by the infringer, or a reasonable multiple of the patent license fee, the People's Court may award damages ranging from RMB 30,000 to RMB 5,000,000, depending on factors such as the type of patent, nature, and circumstances of the infringement. Where intentional infringement occurs under severe circumstances, the People's Court may determine compensation up to five times the amount calculated based on the above methods. The amount of damages shall also include the reasonable expenses incurred by the patent holder in stopping the infringement.

Provisional Measures:
Article 69 provides patent enforcement agencies the authority to inspect, seize, and secure evidence during suspected patent infringement or counterfeiting investigations.
China.timeframe: Since April 1984, last amended in June 2021

China.act: Copyright Law of the People's Republic of China《中华人民共和国著作权法》
China.act_norm: copyright law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: copyright law of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Civil and administrative measures:
Article 49. To protect copyright and rights related to copyright, rights holders may adopt technical measures.
Without the permission of the rights holder, no organization or individual may intentionally circumvent or disable such technical measures, manufacture, import, or provide to the public devices or components intended for the purpose of circumventing or disabling technical measures, or intentionally provide technical services to others for the same purpose. Exceptions apply in circumstances where circumvention is permitted by law or administrative regulations.
The "technical measures" referred to in this law are effective technologies, devices, or components used to prevent or restrict the unauthorized browsing, viewing, or enjoyment of works, performances, or audio-visual recordings, or their provision to the public through information networks.

Remedies:
Article 52 states that those commit infringement shall, depending on the circumstances, bear civil liabilities such as ceasing the infringement, eliminating the impact, making a formal apology, and compensating for losses.
Furthermore, Article 53 states that If the infringement also harms the public interest, the copyright administration department shall order the cessation of the infringing act, issue a warning, confiscate any illegal gains, and seize or harmlessly destroy the infringing copies, as well as the materials, tools, and equipment primarily used to produce such copies. If the illegal business revenue exceeds 50,000 yuan, a fine of one to five times the revenue may also be imposed. If there is no illegal business revenue, or the revenue is difficult to calculate or less than 50,000 yuan, a fine of up to 250,000 yuan may be imposed. If the act constitutes a crime, criminal liability shall be pursued in accordance with the law.

Provisional measures:
Article 56
If a copyright owner or a holder of rights related to copyright has evidence proving that another party is infringing or is about to infringe upon their rights, thereby obstructing the realization of their rights, and if failure to stop such actions in time may cause irreparable harm to their legitimate interests, they may apply to the People's Court for measures such as property preservation, orders to perform specific actions, or orders to prohibit specific actions before filing a lawsuit, in accordance with the law.
Article 57
To prevent infringement, in cases where evidence may be destroyed or difficult to obtain later, the copyright owner or a holder of rights related to copyright may apply to the People's Court for evidence preservation before filing a lawsuit, in accordance with the law.
China.timeframe: Since September 1990, last amended in June 2021

China.act:
National Security Law of the People's Republic of China 《中华人民共和国国家安全法》;

Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China 《中华人民共和国网络安全法》
China.act_norm: national security law of the people s republic of china cybersecurity law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: national security law of the people s republic of china
cybersecurity law of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Article 25 of the Chinese government’s 2015 National Security Law states that "the State shall establishes a national network and information security safeguard system, raising the capacity to protect network and information security; increasing innovative research, development and use of network and information technologies; to bring about security core techniques and key infrastructure for networks and information, information systems in important fields, as well as data"

Furthermore, Article 28 of the Cybersecurity Law states that "network operators shall provide technical support and assistance to public security organs and national security organs that are safeguarding national security and investigating criminal activities in accordance with the law.”

China.act_norm: anti unfair competition law of the people s republic of china criminal law of the people s republic of china civil code of the people s republic of china labour law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: anti unfair competition law of the people s republic of china
criminal law of the people s republic of china
civil code of the people s republic of china
labour law of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
China has not legislated a unified law specifically directed to trade secret protection. So far in China, the laws related to trade secret protection scatter across the Anti-Unfair Competition Law (revised in 2019), the Civil Code (effective in 2021), the Labour Law (revised in 2018), and the Criminal Law (revised in 2020).

According to the Anti-Unfair Competition Law: trade secrets refer to any technical information, operational information or commercial information, which is not known to the public and has commercial value, and for which its infringer adopted measures to ensure its confidentiality (Article 9). 

Article 21 of the Anti-Unfair Competition Law further states that where a business operator or any other natural person, legal person or unincorporated organisation infringes any trade secret in violation of Article 9 herein, the supervision and inspection authority shall order it to cease the illegal act, confiscated the illegal gains and impose on it a fine of between CNY100, 000 and CNY1 million; where the circumstance is serious, the fine shall be between CNY500, 000 and CNY5 million.

Furthermore, Article 219 of the Criminal Law writes:
Anyone who commits any of the following acts of infringing on trade secrets, and if the circumstances are serious, shall be sentenced to imprisonment of not more than three years, along with a fine or a standalone fine; if the circumstances are particularly serious, they shall be sentenced to imprisonment of more than three years but not more than ten years, and fined:

- Obtaining a trade secret of the rights holder through theft, bribery, fraud, coercion, electronic intrusion, or other improper means;

- Disclosing, using, or allowing others to use the trade secret of the rights holder obtained through the means described in the previous item;

- Disclosing, using, or allowing others to use a trade secret in their possession in violation of confidentiality obligations or the requirements of the rights holder regarding the confidentiality of the trade secret.

Any person who, knowing the acts listed in the preceding paragraph, obtains, discloses, uses, or allows others to use the trade secret shall be deemed to have infringed on trade secrets.

China.act: Telecommunications Regulations of the People's Republic of China 《中华人民共和国电信条例》
China.act_norm: telecommunications regulations of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: telecommunications regulations of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Passive sharing is mandated and is effective in both the mobile and fixed sectors. 

Section 2 of the Telecommunication Regulations stipulates measures on interconnection of telecommunication networks, specifically: 

Article 17
Telecommunication networks shall achieve interconnection and intercommunication in accordance with the principles of technical feasibility, economic rationality, fairness, impartiality, and mutual cooperation.

A dominant telecommunication service provider shall not refuse interconnection and intercommunication requests made by other telecommunication service providers or dedicated network operators.

The "dominant telecommunication service provider" mentioned in the preceding paragraph refers to an operator that controls essential telecommunication infrastructure, holds a significant market share in the telecommunication services market, and can substantially affect the ability of other operators to enter the market.

The dominant telecommunication service provider shall be determined by the competent department of the State Council responsible for information industry management.

Article 18
A dominant telecommunication service provider shall formulate interconnection procedures, time limits, and a directory of unbundled network elements, among other contents, in accordance with the principles of non-discrimination and transparency. These interconnection regulations shall be submitted to the competent department of the State Council responsible for information industry management for review and approval.

The interconnection regulations shall be binding on the interconnection and intercommunication activities of the dominant telecommunication service provider.
China.timeframe: Since September 2000, last amended in February 2016

For foreign-invested telecommunications enterprises engaged in basic telecommunications services (excluding wireless paging services), the share of capital contributed by foreign investors shall not exceed 49%, unless otherwise stipulated by the state.

For foreign-invested telecommunications enterprises engaged in value-added telecommunications services (including wireless paging services within basic telecommunications services), the share of capital contributed by foreign investors shall not exceed 50%, unless otherwise stipulated by the state.

China.act: Administrative Measures for the Licensing of Telecommunication Business 《电信业务经营许可管理办法》
China.act_norm: administrative measures for the licensing of telecommunication business
acts_norm: administrative measures for the licensing of telecommunication business
China.impact:
In China, telecommunication services can be categorized into two groups: basic telecom services and value-added telecom services.

- Basic telecom services refer to services that provide public network infrastructure, public data transmission, and basic voice communication services.
- Value-added telecom services refer to services that utilize public network infrastructure to provide telecom and information services.

According to Article 5 of the Administrative Measures, to operate basic telecom services, the following conditions must be met: (1) the operator must be a company legally established specifically for engaging in basic telecom services, with no less than 51% of state-owned equity or shares; (2) there must be a business development research report and a network construction technical plan; (3) there must be sufficient funds and professional personnel suitable for the business activities; (4) there must be premises, facilities, and corresponding resources for conducting business activities; (5) there must be a reputation or capability to provide long-term services to users; (6) for operations within a province, autonomous region, or municipality, the minimum registered capital must be 100 million RMB, while for nationwide or cross-provincial operations, the minimum registered capital must be 1 billion RMB; (7) the company and its major investors and senior management personnel must not be listed on the telecom business dishonesty list; and (8) other conditions stipulated by the state.

Furthermore, Article 6 states that to operate value-added telecom services, the following conditions must be met: (1) the operator must be a company legally established; (2) possess sufficient funds and professional personnel suitable for conducting business activities; (3) have the credibility or capability to provide long-term services to users; (4) for operations within a province, autonomous region, or municipality, the minimum registered capital requirement is 1 million RMB, while for nationwide or cross-provincial operations, the minimum registered capital requirement is 10 million RMB; (5) have necessary premises, facilities, and technical solutions; (6) the company and its major investors and senior management personnel must not be listed on the telecom business dishonesty list; and (7) meet other conditions stipulated by the state.

China.act: Data Protection Measure for Industrial and Telecom Companies 《工业和信息化领域数据安全管理办法（试行）》
China.act_norm: data protection measure for industrial and telecom companies
acts_norm: data protection measure for industrial and telecom companies
China.impact:
According to Article 3 of the Measures, Industrial data refers to data generated and collected during research and development, design, production, manufacturing, business management, operation and maintenance, and platform operation across various industries and fields within the industrial sector. Telecommunications data refers to data generated and collected during telecommunications business operations. Radio data refers to data generated and collected during radio business activities, including radio frequency data, station parameters, and other radio wave parameters.

Furthermore, Chapter 2 classifies data into "general", "important" and "core", and require firms to take different degrees of protection measures when collecting, processing, transferring, and disposing of data. 

In particular, Article 21 states that data processors in the industrial and information technology sector that collect and generate important data and core data within the territory of the People's Republic of China shall store such data domestically if required by laws and administrative regulations. If it is necessary to provide the data abroad, a data export security assessment shall be conducted in accordance with laws and regulations.

China.act_norm: personal information protection law of the people s republic of china cybersecurity law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: personal information protection law of the people s republic of china
cybersecurity law of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Under the Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL):

Article 36: Personal information processed by state agencies shall be stored within the territory of the People's Republic of China. If it is necessary to provide such information abroad, a security assessment shall be conducted. The security assessment may require support and assistance from relevant departments.

Article 40: Operators of critical information infrastructure and personal information processors that handle personal information in quantities specified by the national cybersecurity authority shall store the personal information collected and generated within the territory of the People's Republic of China. If it is necessary to provide such information abroad, a security assessment organized by the national cybersecurity authority must be conducted. If laws, administrative regulations, or provisions of the national cybersecurity authority exempt such security assessments, those provisions shall prevail.

Similarly, Article 37 of the Cybersecurity Law states exactly the same with Article 40 of the PIPL.

China.act: Provisions on Promoting and Regulating Cross-border Data Flow《促进和规范数据跨境流动规定》
China.act_norm: provisions on promoting and regulating cross border data flow
acts_norm: provisions on promoting and regulating cross border data flow
China.impact:
The Provisions clarify the applicable standards for data processors in cross-border data transfers, including security assessment, standard contract for outbound transfer of personal data, and personal information protection certification. Critical information infrastructure operators (CIIOs) shall undergo a security assessment when transferring personal information or important data outside China.  Other entities also need to conduct a security assessment when transferring important data or a large volume of personal information, with specific thresholds set at personal information (without sensitive personal information) of more than 1 million individuals or sensitive personal information of more than 10,000 individuals accumulated from January 1 of the current year.

Furthermore, the Provisions includes specific scenario exemptions: 

Article 2: Data processors shall identify and report important data in accordance with relevant regulations. If the relevant authorities or regions have not notified or publicly designated the data as important data, data processors are not required to declare it as important data for a data export security assessment.

Article 3: Data generated from international activities (e.g., trade, transportation, academic cooperation, manufacturing, marketing) that does not include personal or important data is exempt from cross-border data security assessments and compliance requirements.

Article 4: Personal information collected abroad and processed in China before being transferred overseas, as long as no domestic personal or important data is involved, is exempt from cross-border data security assessments and compliance requirements.

Article 5: Certain cross-border personal information transfers are exempt from security assessments and compliance requirements, including (1) fulfilling contracts (e.g., cross-border shopping, visa processing), (2) managing international human resources, (3) handling emergencies, and (4) transferring personal data abroad under a 100,000-person threshold (excluding sensitive data). Personal information provided abroad must not include important data.

acts_norm: personal information protection law of the people s republic of china
China.impact: The Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) is applicable to any organization and individual who processes personal information in China. For companies outside China, the PIPL is also applicable if they provide services or products to people in China or analyze and evaluate the activities of people in China. The PIPL sets out the conditions for transfer of personal information out of China, protection of sensitive personal information, classification and retention of personal information and other compliance requirements.

China.act: Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China《中华人民共和国数据安全法》
China.act_norm: data security law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: data security law of the people s republic of china
China.impact: On June 10, 2021, the Data Security Law of the People’s Republic of China was officially passed during the 29th session of the Standing Committee of the 13th National People’s Congress. Aiming to further strengthen the current protection regime for the country’s fast-growing digital economy, the law stipulates how data is used, collected, developed, and protected in China. It emphasizes on top-down coordination of data security implementation among local governments and differentiated fines based on severity of violations.

China.act_norm: management standards for the entry and removal of digital educational resources on the national smart education platform notice on further advancing the electronic information management of physicians interim measures for data security management of accounting firms implementation plan for establishing a carbon footprint management system
acts_norm: management standards for the entry and removal of digital educational resources on the national smart education platform
notice on further advancing the electronic information management of physicians
interim measures for data security management of accounting firms
implementation plan for establishing a carbon footprint management system
China.impact:
In addition to comprehensive legislation that applies horizontally across all sectors, China has also developed and implemented a wide range of sector-specific regulations related to data protection, offering detailed guidance tailored to different industries. For example, in just the first half of 2024 alone, the Chinese government issued over 20 national laws, industry regulations, and local policies concerning data security. A selection of these is listed below.

Education sector: 
The General Office of the Ministry of Education Issued the “Management Standards for the Entry and Removal of Digital Educational Resources on the National Smart Education Platform”. According to the Standards, platform service providers must comply with relevant laws, regulations, and national standards, and adopt technical and other necessary measures to ensure the security of the platform and its resources, as well as retain network logs in accordance with the law.

Healthcare sector: 
The National Health Commission, the National Administration of Traditional Chinese Medicine, and the National Disease Control and Prevention Administration jointly issued the "Notice on Further Advancing the Electronic Information Management of Physicians". The Notice calls on local authorities to implement a data security accountability system, strengthen data security monitoring and early warning mechanisms, regularly conduct security assessments and risk evaluations, and establish disaster recovery and data backup systems to ensure the security of information within electronic registration systems.

Finance sector: 
The Ministry of Finance and the Cyberspace Administration of China jointly issued the "Interim Measures for Data Security Management of Accounting Firms". The Measures require accounting firms to classify and categorize data in accordance with relevant laws, regulations, and the data classification standards applicable to the industry of the audited entity. Firms must identify core data, important data, and general data, and comply with specific requirements regarding the storage, transmission, and logging of core and important data.

Energy sector: 
The Ministry of Ecology and Environment, together with 14 other departments, jointly issued the "Implementation Plan for Establishing a Carbon Footprint Management System". The Plan calls for strengthening the protection of data security and intellectual property related to product carbon footprints. It emphasizes the implementation of data security laws and regulations, enhanced oversight of data circulation, and the creation of a secure and reliable environment for data exchange. The Plan also encourages digital technology enterprises to provide cloud-based security services to improve overall data security standards.

China.act: Counter-Espionage Law of the People's Republic of China《中华人民共和国反间谍法》
China.act_norm: counter espionage law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: counter espionage law of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Article 25 and 26 of the Counterespionage Law provides for state security organ personnel to gain entry to restricted regions, venues or units and to inspect, read or collect relevant archives, materials or items. Such access is permitted on the basis of relevant national regulations and upon approval and presentation of appropriate documents. Further, state security organ personnel can also check electronic communication tools, equipment and facilities in accordance with the regulations.

However, court order is not necessary when accessing archive, data, and information. Instead, Article 26 of the Law states that approval is required from the person in charge of the national security agency at the municipal level or above. In China, national security agency (国家安全机关) does not include the court.
China.timeframe: Since November 2014, last amended in July 2023

Unless the public security organisation or procuratorial organisation makes an inspection on the contents of correspondence pursuant to the procedures prescribed in law when required by national security or investigation of crimes, no organisation or individual shall infringe upon any citizen’s privacy of correspondence on any pretext.”

A requirement for court order is not present in the measures.

China.act: Interim Administrative Measures for Generative Artificial Intelligence Services《生成式人工智能服务管理暂行办法》
China.act_norm: interim administrative measures for generative artificial intelligence services
acts_norm: interim administrative measures for generative artificial intelligence services
China.impact:
Article 19: Relevant regulatory authorities shall conduct supervision and inspections of generative artificial intelligence (AI) services in accordance with their responsibilities. Service providers must cooperate as required by law and provide explanations regarding the sources, scale, types, and labeling rules of training data, as well as algorithm mechanisms and principles. They must also offer necessary technical, data, and other forms of support and assistance.

Institutions and personnel involved in the security assessment and supervision of generative AI services must keep confidential any state secrets, trade secrets, personal privacy, or personal information they become aware of in the course of their duties. They are prohibited from disclosing or unlawfully providing such information to others.

China.act: Regulation on Protection of the Right to Network Dissemination of Information《信息网络传播权保护条例》
China.act_norm: regulation on protection of the right to network dissemination of information
acts_norm: regulation on protection of the right to network dissemination of information
China.impact:
Articles 20 - 23 of the Regulations for the Protection of Information Network Dissemination Rights state that service providers who qualify for safe harbour protection will not be held liable for certain illegal activities, including copyright violations, committed by their customers. 

To qualify for safe harbor immunity, service providers must meet several conditions outlined in the Regulations. These conditions vary depending on the type of safe harbor protection being claimed. Regardless of the specific immunity sought, service providers must ensure that they have not actively engaged in accessing, transmitting, copying, storing, linking to, or searching for unauthorized information.

Additionally, to receive safe harbour for storage and linking services, service providers must follow a notice and removal procedure. When servise providers are notified of infringing activities but fail to remove the unauthorized information from their sites, their claim for safe harbour protection will be denied.

China.impact:
Article 8 of the Administrative Measures on Internet Forum Community Service states that internet forum and community service providers shall follow the principle of "real-name authentication in the background, voluntary display in the foreground." They must require users to register accounts through real identity verification and implement real identity information filing and periodic verification for section initiators and administrators. If users do not provide real identity information, internet forum and community service providers shall not provide them with information publishing services.

Moreover, Article 4 of the Provisions on the Management of Internet Post Comments Services stipulates that providers of comment and reply services shall strictly fulfill their primary responsibility for managing such services and perform the following duty in accordance with the law:

Adhering to the principle of "real-name registration in the backend, user anonymity in the frontend," they must verify the real identity information of registered users using methods such as mobile phone numbers, ID card numbers, or unified social credit codes. They shall not provide comment and reply services to users who fail to authenticate their real identity information or who impersonate organizations or other individuals.

China.impact:
Article 8: Application providers shall be responsible for the presentation of information content results. They must not produce or disseminate illegal information and shall actively prevent and resist harmful information.

Article 20: Application distribution platforms shall review applications submitted for listing or updates. If any application is found to have illegal or harmful information in its name, icon, or description, discrepancies between the application and the registered entity’s real identity, or unlawful or non-compliant business types, the platform shall not provide services to such applications.

Application distribution platforms shall also strengthen the daily management of listed applications. They must not provide services to applications that contain illegal or harmful information, engage in fraudulent activities such as falsifying download numbers or evaluation metrics, pose data security risks, unlawfully collect or use personal information, or infringe upon the legitimate rights and interests of others.

China.impact:
Article 5: Providers of comment and reply services shall strictly fulfill their primary responsibilities and perform the following obligations in accordance with the law:

- For providing comment and reply services on news information, a pre-review system must be established before comments are published.

- Establish and improve information security management systems for reviewing and managing comments, real-time monitoring, and emergency response. Illegal information must be promptly identified, addressed, and reported to the relevant authorities.

- Develop technologies for the protection and management of comment and reply information security, innovate management methods, and use anti-spam management systems to improve the handling of spam. Security flaws, vulnerabilities, and risks in comment services must be promptly identified, remedied, and reported to the relevant authorities.

China.impact: Providers are held accountable as “content producers” according to the Generative AI Measures (Article 9). When “illegal content” is identified among users of these services, providers must promptly take actions, including suspending content generation and transmission, taking down the content, and addressing the issue through model optimization. Additionally, providers are required to report such incidents to regulators (Article 14).

China.act: Regulation on Internet Information Service of the People's Republic of China 《互联网信息服务管理办法》
China.act_norm: regulation on internet information service of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: regulation on internet information service of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Article 15 stipulates that internet information service providers shall not produce, copy, publish, or disseminate information containing the following content:
1. Opposing the basic principles as they are confirmed in the Constitution.
2. Jeopardizing the security of the nation, divulging state secrets, subverting state power, or jeopardizing the integrity of the nation's unity
3. Harming the honor or the interests of the nation
4. Inciting hatred against peoples, racism against peoples, or disrupting the solidarity of peoples
5. Disrupting national policies on religion, propagating evil cults and feudal superstitions
6. Spreading rumors, disturbing social order or disrupting social stability
7. Spreading obscenity, pornography, gambling, violence, murder, terror, or abetting the commission of a crime
8. Insulting or defaming third parties, infringing on the legal rights and interests of third parties
9. Containing any other content prohibited by law or administrative rules

Furthermore, Article 20: Producing, copying, publishing, or disseminating any of the content listed in Article 15 of these Measures that constitutes a crime shall be subject to criminal liability in accordance with the law. For commercial internet information service providers, the licensing authority shall order them to suspend operations for rectification or revoke their business license and notify the enterprise registration authority. For non-commercial internet information service providers, the filing authority shall order the temporary closure of the website, up to and including permanent shutdown.

Thus, this Regulation provides legal basis for blocking and filtering commercial web content.
China.timeframe: Since September 2000, last amended in January 2011

China.act: Commercial Web Content Blocking and Filtering in Practice
China.act_norm: commercial web content blocking and filtering in practice
acts_norm: commercial web content blocking and filtering in practice
China.impact:
According to Freedom on the Net 2024 Country Report, the government maintains control over China’s gateways to the global internet, giving authorities the ability to restrict connectivity or access to content hosted on servers outside the country. This arrangement is the foundation for the “Great Firewall,” the informal name for the government’s comprehensive internet censorship system. All service providers must subscribe via the gateway operators, which are overseen by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT).

According to GreatFire.org—an anticensorship group that tracks filtering in China—as of February 2024, over 100,000 websites were blocked in China. Many international news outlets and their Chinese-language websites are blocked, such as those of the New York Times, Reuters, the Wall Street Journal, the Australian Broadcasting Corporation (ABC), and the British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC).

Most international social media and messaging platforms are blocked, including Facebook, WhatsApp, Twitter, Instagram, Signal, Clubhouse, YouTube, Telegram, Snapchat, Line, and Pinterest. The popular discussion forum Reddit was blocked in August 2018, while all languages of Wikipedia were blocked in April 2019. A number of Google services—including Maps, Translate, Calendar, Docs, Drive, Scholar, and Analytics—remained blocked as of the end of the coverage period.

Blocks on global search engines severely limit the content available on the Chinese internet. Google’s search engine has been blocked since 2012, while the Yahoo search function was blocked in 2018.

A minority of Chinese internet users, though they number in the tens of millions, access blocked websites with circumvention tools like VPNs. However, the government has intensified its restrictions on these tools since 2017, when the MIIT banned the use of unlicensed VPNs.

China.act: Regulation on Internet Information Services of the People's Republic of China 《互联网信息服务管理办法》
China.act_norm: regulation on internet information services of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: regulation on internet information services of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
Under the regulation, Internet information services are classified into two categories: commercial and non-commercial.

- Commercial internet information services refer to activities that provide information, webpage creation, or other services to internet users for a fee.
- Non-commercial internet information services refer to activities that provide open and shared information to internet users free of charge.

According to Article 7, entities engaging in commercial internet information services must apply for an Internet Information Service Value-Added Telecommunications Business License (hereinafter referred to as the Operating License) from the telecommunications regulatory authority of the province, autonomous region, municipality, or the competent department of the State Council responsible for the information industry.

The relevant telecommunications regulatory authority shall review the application and make a decision within 60 days of receiving it. If approved, an Operating License shall be issued. If denied, the applicant shall be notified in writing with an explanation of the reasons.

After obtaining the Operating License, the applicant must use it to complete the business registration process with the relevant enterprise registration authority.
China.timeframe: Since September 2000, last amended in January 2011

circular on clearing up and regulating the internet access service market
China.impact:
According to Article 8 of the Interim Provisions, entities intending to engage in international internet business activities must apply for an International Internet Business License from the competent authority responsible for handling such applications. Entities without this license are prohibited from conducting international internet business activities. Moreover, entities intending to engage in non-commercial international internet access must seek approval from the relevant competent authority responsible for handling non-commercial internet access applications. Without such approval, they are not permitted to connect to the global internet.

In 2017, the Circular on Clearing up and Regulating the Internet Access Service Market urged basic telecommunications enterprises and internet network access service providers to conduct a comprehensive self-inspection of their network infrastructure, IP address usage, bandwidth allocation, and other network access resources to rectify any unauthorized cross-border business activities. Specifically, without approval from the telecommunications regulatory authority, they shall not independently establish or lease dedicated lines (including Virtual Private Networks, VPNs) or other channels for cross-border business operations. For international dedicated lines leased to users by basic telecommunications enterprises, user records must be centrally maintained. Users must be explicitly informed that such dedicated lines are strictly for internal office use and must not be used to connect domestic and overseas data centers or business platforms for telecommunications business operations.

Administrative Measures for the Prohibition and Restriction of Technology Imports《禁止进口限制进口技术管理办法》
China.act_norm: mofcom notice 106 2018 106 2018 administrative measures for the prohibition and restriction of technology imports

administrative measures for the prohibition and restriction of technology imports
China.impact:
On 26 December, 2018, the Ministry of Commerce (MOFCOM) and the General Administration of Customs issued the adjusted catalogue of used mechanical and electrical products prohibited from import to be effective from 1 January 2019. The adjusted catalogue includes part of the mechanical and electrical products. Some of the products included in this list are video recorders, sound recorder or playback equipment using semiconductor media, disc type broadcast video recorder, color LCD monitors that can be directly connected and designed for automatic data processing equipment. 

However, this measure is implemented under the National Sword Policy which is a ban on import e-waste. Thus, this is not a restrictive measure to digital trade but a measure concerning environmental damage. 

Furthermore, according to Article 2 of the Administrative Measures, any technology listed as prohibited for import in the Catalogue of Prohibited and Restricted Technology Imports of China (to be published separately) shall not be imported. So far, no ICT technology has been listed.

China.act: Catalogue for the Administration of Import and Export Licenses for Dual-Use Items and Technologies《两用物项和技术进出口许可证管理目录》
China.act_norm: catalogue for the administration of import and export licenses for dual use items and technologies
acts_norm: catalogue for the administration of import and export licenses for dual use items and technologies
China.impact:
According to the 2025 Catalogue for the Administration of Import and Export Licenses for Dual-Use Items and Technologies, the following ICT goods require an export license: 

high-performance digital computers, integrated circuit chips (security chips), encryption devices, encryption cards, encrypted VPN devices, cryptographic analysis equipment, electronic devices and components, scanning cameras, framing cameras with a recording rate exceeding 225,000 frames per second, framing cameras with a frame exposure time of ≤50 ns, and radiation-resistant television cameras and their lenses.

Notably, the names of the goods listed are based on the reference product names provided in the Catalogue for the Administration of Import and Export Licenses for Dual-Use Items and Technologies. However, the original catalogue provides more detailed descriptions of the specific types of items that require an export license.

For example, "high-performance digital computers" is quoted based on the reference product name, while the original catalogue provides a more precise description of this category. The full description in the catalogue is high-performance digital computers, electronic components, and related equipment, which includes specific types such as:

1. Digital computers with an adjusted peak performance (APP) greater than 8.0 Weighted TeraFLOPS
2. Electronic components specifically designed or modified to enhance performance, utilizing processor aggregation to achieve an APP greater than 8.0 Weighted TeraFLOPS

This distinction highlights that while the reference product names provide a general categorization, the original descriptions in the catalogue define the technical specifications that determine whether an item requires an export license.

Furthermore, on April 4, 2025, the Ministry of Commerce, in coordination with the General Administration of Customs, issued an announcement on implementing export control measures on seven types of medium and heavy rare earth-related items — specifically samarium (Sm), gadolinium (Gd), terbium (Tb), dysprosium (Dy), lutetium (Lu), scandium (Sc), and yttrium (Y). The measures took effect from the date of publication. Although rare-earth materials are not ICT goods, they are crucial in the production of various high-tech products, such as batteries, and display screends in electronic devices.
references: https://www.mofcom.gov.cn/cms_files/filemanager/600926013/attach/202412/8db3aacb2b7c47f0a4402203cff7f635.pdf?fileName=2025年度《两用物项和技术进出口许可证管理目录》.pdf

China.impact:
The catalogue was last revised in 2023, and after the revision, the number of items was reduced from 164 to 134. Among these, prohibited export items related to digital trade include:
1. Information security and confidentiality technologies involving state secrets used by government, financial, industrial, and scientific research sectors in our country, including information concealment technology and security threat detection technology.
2. Integrated circuit manufacturing technology.
3. Beidou satellite navigation system information transmission encryption technology, including the corresponding software.

Furthermore, ICT technologies subject to export restrictions (requiring a license) include, but are not limited to, electronic device manufacturing technology, optical fiber manufacturing and optical fiber communication technology, computer core hardware manufacturing technology, wireless communication technology, computer network technology, satellite application technology, cryptographic security technology, information processing technology, general-purpose computer software development technology, and security enhancement technology for basic software. (Note: The names listed here are general descriptions of the technologies. For more specific details, please refer directly to the catalogue.)

On January 2, 2025, the Ministry of Commerce issued a notice seeking public comments on the adjustment of the Catalogue of Technologies Prohibited and Restricted from Export in China. The deadline for feedback is February 1, 2025.

China.act:
Measures for the Administration of Mandatory National Standards《国家标准管理办法》;

Guiding Opinions on Foreign-Funded Enterprises Participating in the Standardization Work of China《外商投资企业参与我国标准化工作的指导意见》
China.act_norm: measures for the administration of mandatory national standards guiding opinions on foreign funded enterprises participating in the standardization work of china
acts_norm: measures for the administration of mandatory national standards
guiding opinions on foreign funded enterprises participating in the standardization work of china
China.impact:
Under the Measures for the Administration of Mandatory National Standards:

Article 6: Actively promote the adoption of international standards in accordance with national conditions. National standards drafted based on international standards shall comply with the copyright policies of relevant international organizations.

Article 28: The draft version of national standards and explanatory documents shall be publicly solicited for feedback through relevant portals, the National Public Service Platform for Standardization Information, and other channels. Simultaneously, feedback shall be sought from relevant stakeholders, including other competent administrative departments of the State Council, enterprises, public institutions, social organizations, consumer organizations, and research institutions.

The public consultation period for national standards shall generally be no less than 60 days. When soliciting opinions on mandatory national standards, notification to foreign parties shall be made in accordance with the requirements of the World Trade Organization (WTO).

Furthermore, in 2017, the National Standardization Administration, the National Development and Reform Commission, and the Ministry of Commerce jointly issued the Guiding Opinions on Foreign-Funded Enterprises Participating in the Standardization Work of China, aiming to create a fair standardization environment for foreign-invested enterprises.

Specifically, Article 1 of the Guiding Opinions stipulates that foreign-invested enterprises participating in China’s standardization work shall enjoy equal treatment with domestic enterprises. Article 4 then states that foreign-invested enterprises may participate in the drafting of national standards and the translation of national standards into foreign languages. They may also provide opinions and suggestions during processes such as project initiation, solicitation of opinions, and implementation of standards.

China.impact:
The Self-Declaration of Compulsory Product Certification has two product types known as “Type A” and “Type B.” Type A products can be allowed to be tested in own or self-determined laboratories, whereas Type B products must be tested in laboratories accredited by the Chinese authority CNAS. Products for which self-certification (Type A) is allowed are mostly electrical tools, without ICT products. Type B products, include but not limited to, small power electric motor, low-voltage components, electric motor-compressor, and vehicle safety accessories. 

Another 13 categories of ICT products are subject to China Compulsory Certification (CCC). According to Article 10 of the Provisions on the Administration of Compulsory Product Certification, producers, sellers, or importers of products listed in the catalogue (hereinafter collectively referred to as certification applicants) shall entrust certification bodies designated by the State. In addition, after accepting the certification application, the certification body shall arrange for product type testing and factory inspection in accordance with the provisions of the specific product certification rules.

Article 3 of the Measures stipulates a network access licensing system for telecommunications terminal equipment connected to public telecommunications networks, radio communication equipment, and telecommunications equipment involving inter-network connectivity.

Moreover, in order to receive the permission, a quality management system certification certificate or audit report and a test report or product certification certificate need to be submitted. 

According to Article 11, radio communication equipment, inter-network connectivity equipment, or new products applying for a network access license must undergo testing on telecommunications networks within China or on simulated test networks designated by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) for a minimum of three months. A test report must be issued by the testing organization. The Telecommunications Administration Bureau of the MIIT will organize experts to evaluate the overall technical scheme, test report, detection report, and other relevant documents of the telecommunications equipment mentioned in the preceding paragraph. Based on the expert evaluation, if the equipment meets the required conditions upon review, a network access license will be issued.

China.act:
Regulation on the Administration of Commercial Cryptography《商用密码管理条例》;

Cryptography Law of the People's Republic of China《中华人民共和国密码法》
China.act_norm: regulation on the administration of commercial cryptography cryptography law of the people s republic of china
acts_norm: regulation on the administration of commercial cryptography
cryptography law of the people s republic of china
China.impact:
According to Article 9 of the Regulation on the Administration of Commercial Cryptography, the national cryptography administration organizes the review and evaluation of commercial cryptographic technologies, including encryption algorithms, cryptographic protocols, and key management mechanisms, used in networks and information systems that are required by laws, administrative regulations, and relevant national provisions to be protected with commercial cryptography.

Moreover, under the Cryptography Law of the People's Republic of China:

Article 26: Commercial cryptographic products related to national security, the national economy and people's livelihood, and public interest shall be legally included in the catalogue of critical network equipment and specialized cybersecurity products. They may only be sold or provided after being tested and certified as qualified by accredited institutions.

Article 27: Operators of critical information infrastructure (CII) that are required by laws, administrative regulations, and relevant national provisions to use commercial cryptography for protection must use commercial cryptography for security and conduct commercial cryptography application security assessments either independently or through an accredited commercial cryptography testing institution. Such assessments shall be integrated with the security testing and evaluation of critical information infrastructure and the cybersecurity grading evaluation system to avoid redundant assessments and evaluations.

If operators of critical information infrastructure purchase network products and services involving commercial cryptography that may impact national security, they shall undergo a national security review organized by the national cyberspace administration.

China.act: Regulation on the Administration of Commercial Cryptography《商用密码管理条例》
China.act_norm: regulation on the administration of commercial cryptography
acts_norm: regulation on the administration of commercial cryptography
China.impact:
No deviations from international encryption standards have been identified.

China has developed several international encryption standards in recent years. According to the State Cryptography Administration, on April 24, 2020, at the 60th Working Group Meeting of the ISO/IEC Joint Technical Committee 1 Subcommittee 27 on Information Security (ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 27), ISO/IEC 18033-4/AMD1 Encryption Algorithms – Part 4: Stream Ciphers – Amendment 1, which includes China’s ZUC stream cipher algorithm, was unanimously approved as an ISO/IEC international standard and entered the publication stage.

The ZUC stream cipher marks another Chinese commercial cryptographic algorithm that has successfully become an ISO/IEC international standard, following SM2, SM3, and SM9.
China.timeframe: Since October 1999, last amended in April 2023

China.act: Notice on Lifting Foreign Equity Ratio Restrictions for Online Data Processing and Transaction Processing Services (Operational E-commerce)《关于放开在线数据处理与交易处理业务（经营类电子商务）外资股比限制的通告》

China.impact:
The notice, published by the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, states that 'To implement the spirit of the Third Plenary Session of the 18th CPC Central Committee, support the development of e-commerce in China, encourage and guide foreign investment participation, and further stimulate market competition, the Ministry has decided to lift foreign equity ratio restrictions for online data processing and transaction processing services (operational e-commerce) nationwide. This decision builds on the pilot program conducted in the China (Shanghai) Pilot Free Trade Zone, allowing foreign investors to hold up to 100% equity.'

Thus, in China, full ownership (100%) is allowed in e-commerce sector.
