India.impact: The Information Technology Intermediaries Guidelines Rules require intermediaries to deploy technology based automated tools or appropriate mechanisms with appropriate controls, for proactively identifying and removing or disabling public access to unlawful information or content (Clause 9).

India.act: Information Technology Act No.21 2000
India.act_norm: information technology act no 21 2000
acts_norm: information technology act no 21 2000
India.impact:
Section 69 (Power to issue directions for interception or monitoring or decryption of any information through
any computer resource) of the Indian Information Technology Act (IITA) requires intermediaries to extend all facilities and technical assistance to intercept, monitor or decrypt information as well as to provide information stored in a computer or provide access to a computer resource, when called upon to do so by certain agencies. This extends to online intermediaries which are required to designate an officer to facilitate the execution of such orders. Intermediaries that fail to meet these obligations may be punished with imprisonment of up to seven years.

(Note: Section 69A provides power to issue directions for blocking for public access of any information through any
computer resource, and Section 69B provides power to authorise to monitor and collect traffic data or information through any computer
resource for cyber security).

India.impact: Under Part II Due Diligence by Intermediaries and Grievance Redressal Mechanism, Rule 3 Due diligence by an intermediary, a significant social media intermediary (defined as a social media intermediary having number of registered users in India above 5,000,000) providing messaging services must enable identification of the first originator of the information on its computer resource as may be required by a judicial order or an order passed by a competent authority. In complying with an order for identification of the first originator, a significant social media intermediary will not be required to disclose the contents of the electronic message related to the first originator or other users. No order must be passed in cases where there are less intrusive means of identifying the originator of the information.

On 4 April 2022, the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting, utilizing the emergency powers under the IT Rules, 2021, has issued orders on 04.04.2022 for blocking of twenty-two (22) YouTube based news channels, three (3) Twitter accounts, one (1) Facebook account, and one (1) news website. 

9.1.2 Section 69A of the Information Technology Act, 2000 enables blocking of Information in the interest of sovereignty and integrity of India, defence of India, security of the State, friendly relations with foreign States or public order, or for preventing incitement to the commission of any cognizable offence relating to above. 

Part III of the IT Rules, 2021 provide two procedures for blocking of news and current affairs content by Ministry of I&B:
(i) Blocking on recommendation of the IDC [Rule 14(5)(f) and rule 15]
(ii) Blocking of information in case of emergency [Rule 16]

Blocking on recommendation of the IDC [under rule 14(5)(f) and rule 15]
(i) The requests received by the Ministry are referred to the Inter-Departmental Committee(IDC) and the Committee makes recommendations to the Ministry.
(ii) Thereafter, the recommendation of the Committee is placed before the Secretary, Ministry of Information and Broadcasting for taking appropriate decision under Rule 15 with clause (f) of sub rule (5) of rule 14 of the IT Rules, 2021.
(iii) The Authorized Officer, on approval of the decision of the Secretary, Ministry of Information and Broadcasting, directs the publisher, any agency of the Government.

India.act:
The Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code)
Rules, 2021
India.act_norm: the information technology rules 2021
acts_norm: the information technology
rules 2021
India.impact:
India amended its IT law on to prohibit Facebook, Twitter and other social media firms from publishing, hosting or sharing false or misleading information about “any business” of the government and said the firms will be required to rely on New Delhi’s own fact-check unit to determine the authenticity of any claim.

Under part II "Due Diligence by Intermediaries and Grievance Redressal Mechanism" section 3(1) (b) point v states that deceives or misleads the addressee about the origin of the message or knowingly and intentionally communicates any misinformation or information which is patently false and untrue or misleading in nature [or is identified as fake or false by the fact check unit at the Press Information Bureau of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting or other agency authorised by the Central Government for fact checking or, in respect of any business of the Central Government, by its department in which such business is transacted under the rules of business made under clause (3) of article 77 of the Constitution]

Failure to comply with the rule, which also impacts internet service providers such as Jio and Airtel, risks the firms losing their safe harbor protections. The rule assigns a unit of the government arbitrary and over-broad powers to determine the authenticity of online content and bypasses the principles of natural justice. The notification of these amended rules cement the chilling effect on the fundamental right to speech and expression, particularly on news publishers, journalists, activists, etc. The fact check unit could effectively issue a takedown order to social media platforms and even other intermediaries across the internet stack, potentially bypassing the process statutorily prescribed under the Section 69A (Power to issue directions for blocking for public access of any information through any computer resource) of the IT Act, 2000.

India.impact:
The directive states that all advertisers/ Advertising Agencies must submit a ‘Self-Declaration Certificate’ before publishing or broadcasting any advertisement.

The self-declaration certificate is to certify that the advertisement (i) does not contain misleading claims, and (ii) complies with all relevant regulatory guidelines, including those stipulated in Rule 7 of the Cable Television Networks Rules, 1994 and the Norms of Journalistic Conduct of Press Council of India. Advertiser must provide proof of uploading the Self-Declaration Certificate to the relevant broadcaster, printer, publisher, or electronic media platform for their records. As per the Hon’ble Supreme Court’s directive, no advertisement will be permitted to run on television, print media, or the internet without a valid Self-Declaration Certificate.

India.impact: Over the course of 2020, the Ministry of Information and Technology, invoking Section 69A of the Information Technology Act read with the relevant provisions of the Information Technology (Procedure and Safeguards for Blocking of Access of Information by Public) Rules 2009, banned 267 apps in view of the emergent nature of security threats posed by these apps and the prejudicial nature of these apps to the sovereignty, defence and public order in India. India.act: Development of standards
India.act_norm: development of standards
acts_norm: development of standards
India.impact:
The Telecommunication Engineering Centre (TEC) actively encourages its stakeholders to participate in developmental process of standards in national and international forums and participate in National Working Group meetings to agree upon national stance on a particular standard. There is an increased effort in setting up public-private partnership organizations for formulating standards focused on information and communications (ICT) standardization.

India has recently begun efforts to promote standard-setting coordination among various stakeholders. An example of these efforts is a new standards development organization created in September 2012, Telecommunications Standards Development Society, India (TSDSI), which seeks to develop India-specific standards in telecommunications and to facilitate Indian participation in international standards development. There are also private associations with domain experts, which have participation from the industry, academia, individuals, and the government.

The government agency dealing with National Standards is the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS). India imposes its own standards on global products but the standards required by the Indian government are based on international standards, such as IEC, ISO, ITU.

India.impact:
The procedure for mandatory testing and certification procedures, and the Indian Telegraph (Amendment) Rules, 2017, provide that every telecom equipment must undergo mandatory testing and certification prior to sale, import of use in India. The final detailed procedure for Mandatory Testing and Certification of Telecom Equipments (MTCTE) under these rules has been notified separately. The testing is to be carried out for conformance to Essential Requirements for the equipment, by Indian Accredited Labs designated by TEC and based upon their test reports, certificate shall be issued by Telecom engineering center (TEC).

The 74 labs designated by TEC are Indian companies.

India.act: Government of India, Ministry of Commerce and Industry, Foreign Direct Investment Policy Circular 2016, 2017, 2020
India.act_norm: government of india ministry of commerce and industry foreign direct investment policy circular 2016 2017 2020
acts_norm: government of india ministry of commerce and industry foreign direct investment policy circular 2016 2017 2020
India.impact:
Under section 5.2.15.2 .3 (i) in all amendements India permits up to 100 percent Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) in business-to-business (“marketplace-based”) electronic commerce, i.e. "providing of an information technology platform by an e-commerce entity on a digital & electronic network to act as a facilitator between buyer and seller." However, under (ii) India prohibits foreign investment in business-to-consumer (or “inventory-based”) electronic commerce, also defined as "e-commerce activity where inventory of goods and services is owned by e-commerce entity and is sold to the consumers directly". 

As per section 5.2.15.2.2 (iii) in all amendements , when a marketplace e-commerce entity exercises ownership or control over the inventory, the business is categorized into the inventory-based model. Additionally, India implemented regulations that expressly prohibit subsidiaries of foreign-owned marketplace-based electronic commerce sites from selling products on their parent companies’ sites. The rules also prohibit exclusivity arrangements by which electronic commerce retailers can offer a product on an exclusive basis. 

As per 2017 amendment, section 5.2.5.2, the only exceptions for FDI in inventory-based electronic commerce are for food-product retailing and single-brand retailers that meet certain conditions, including the operation of physical stores in India. Since 2020, under section 5.2.15.3 (g) of the 2020 amendment the FDI policy stipulates that retail trading through e-commerce can also be undertaken prior to opening physical stores, subject to the condition that the entity opens physical stores within two years from the date of start of online retail. Overall, it is reported that these narrow exceptions limit the ability of many electronic commerce service suppliers to serve the Indian market.

India.act:
Guidelines on Regulation of Payment Aggregators and Payment Gateways; 

Payment and Settlement Systems Act 2007
India.act_norm: guidelines on regulation of payment aggregators and payment gateways payment and settlement systems act 2007
acts_norm: guidelines on regulation of payment aggregators and payment gateways
payment and settlement systems act 2007
India.impact:
To start or operate a payment system, one must apply for authorization under the Payment and Settlement Systems (PSS) Act, 2007 (Q 5). Although foreign entities can apply, there are some potentially restrictive conditions:

Authorization Fees and Security Deposit: The RBI can charge authorization fees and request a security deposit for the smooth operation of the payment system (Q 7).

Setting Standards: The RBI can establish standards for various aspects of the payment system, including formats, timings, membership criteria, and participation conditions (Q 10).

These conditions provide the RBI with regulatory flexibility, potentially affecting the licensing process.

Moreover, Under Capital Requirements (section 4):  Existing Payment Aggregators (PAs) must have a net worth of ₹15 crore by March 31, 2021, and reach ₹25 crore by March 31, 2023, which must be maintained thereafter. New PAs must start with ₹15 crore at the time of applying for authorization and achieve ₹25 crore within three years of getting the license, maintaining this level afterward.

Similarly, in the guidelines section 3.1 it states that Non-bank PAs shall require authorisation from RBI under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007 (PSSA). Which may inturn be subject to possible restrictive fees and standards.

India.impact:
According to the Article 2 of the Processing and Settlement of Export related receipts facilitated by Online Payment Gateways from 2011, the Reserve Bank of India restricts export-related payments for goods and services through online payment gateways. In terms of RBI instructions on Payment Aggregator – Cross Border (PA – CB) issued vide its circular CO.DPSS.POLC.No.S-786/02-14-008/2023-24 dated October 31, 2023 (Which replaces the erstwhile instructions on Processing and Settlement of Export related receipts facilitated by Online Payment Gateways, PA – CB providing payment facilities can facilitate payment for export and import of permitted goods and services. Further, these regulations provide that the maximum value per unit of goods / services sold / purchased shall be ₹25,00,000. Payments for imports can be carried out using any payment instrument provided by authorised payment systems in India, except small PPIs. 

Regarding payments for exports through online payment gateways – As per the directions of the Bank the facility shall only be available for export of goods and services (as permitted in the prevalent Foreign Trade Policy) of value not exceeding USD 10,000 (US Dollar ten thousand) per transaction. The reference to a certain payment gateway may not be necessary.

India.act: Courier Imports and Exports (Electronic Declaration and Processing) Regulation 2010
India.act_norm: courier imports and exports regulation 2010
acts_norm: courier imports and exports regulation 2010
India.impact:
The Courier Imports and Exports Regulation distinguishes the values of "documents", "gifts", "low value dutiable consignment" and "samples" in Regulation 3. For "documents", they are not liable to any customs duty; for imported "gifts", they are bona fide articles for personal use not exceeding 10,000 rupees (INR 10,000) per consignment; for "low value dutiable consignment", invoice value does not exceed 1 lakh rupees (INR 100,000) (approximately USD 1,200 as of December 2023) and does not include documents, gifts and samples (which are below INR 10,000); for imported "samples", they are bona fide commercial samples and prototypes of goods supplied for free of charge with a value not exceeding INR 10,000 (approximately to USD 120 as of December 2023) per consignment. For the other goods the de minimis regime in place is 100 INR (Duty and GST exempt) and 1000 INR (GST exempt).                                                                                                   

The Central Government issued a notification exempting any acquisition, merger or amalgamation, if the enterprise being acquired, taken control of, merged or amalgamated has (i) assets less than INR 350 crore, or (ii) turnover less than INR 1000 crore. This exemption is valid for a period of 5 years from the date of publication of its notification in the official gazette. The de-minimis has been extended by Central Government for a further 5 year period vide notification dated 16th March 2022.

India.impact:
The Act under section 24. Compulsory registeration in certain cases (xi) every person supplying online information and database access or retrieval services from a place outside India to a person in India, other than a registered person; in required to apply for GST. This is like a customs duty to online services as described in the GST flyers.

At the Working Session on Work Programme on E-Commerce at the World Trade Organization’s 13th Ministerial Conference on 29 February 2024, India reiterated that with the digital revolution still unfolding and with increasing diffusion of technologies such as additive manufacturing and 3D printing, data analytics, Artificial Intelligence, Internet-of-Things etc., There was a need for re-examination of the implications of the moratorium on customs duties on electronic transmissions, particularly for the developing countries and the LDCs.

As of now India still adheres to the moratorium on customs duties on electronic transmissions at the World Trade Organization (WTO).

India.act: Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules 2020
India.act_norm: consumer protection rules 2020
acts_norm: consumer protection rules 2020
India.impact: In July, 2020, the Consumer Protection (E-Commerce) Rules, 2020 were introduced which apply to all goods and services bought or sold over digital or electronic network including digital products, all models of e-commerce and all e-commerce retail. The Rules apply to any Indian entity engaging in e-commerce abroad or any foreign entity offering goods and services to Indian consumers over e-commerce platforms. The Rules introduce several requirements such as displaying requirements for name and details of sellers, details of return, refund, exchange, warranty and guarantee etc. The Rules require sellers on the platform to provide an undertaking affirming the accuracy of descriptions, images and other content of the goods or services on the platform, including that they correspond directly with the appearance, nature, quality and purpose. No seller can falsely represent itself as a consumer and post reviews about goods or services or misrepresent the quality of the features of goods.

India.act: The Consumer Protection Act No. 35 2019
India.act_norm: the consumer protection act no 35 2019
acts_norm: the consumer protection act no 35 2019
India.impact: This Act includes provisions specifically designed to protect consumers engaging in online transactions. It defines e-commerce entities, establishes liability for misleading advertisements, and sets out mechanisms for consumer grievance redressal. One important feature introduced by this act is the Central Consumer Protection Authority (CCPA), which has the power to take action against unfair trade practices and misleading advertisements online.

Indonesia.act:
Regulation of the Minister of Trade No.34/M-DAG/PER/6/2014 on Antidumping Measure, Compulsory Measure, and Trade Safeguard Measure 2011;

Komite Anti Dumping Indonesia (KADI) website
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 34 m dag per 6 2014 on antidumping measure compulsory measure and trade safeguard measure 2011 komite anti dumping indonesia website
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 34 m dag per 6 2014 on antidumping measure compulsory measure and trade safeguard measure 2011
komite anti dumping indonesia website
Indonesia.impact:
Regulation of the Minister of Trade of the Republic of Indonesia Number 34/M-DAG/PER/6/2014, primarily outlines the organization and work procedures of the Indonesian Trade Security Committee (KPPI). It does not explicitly mention ICT-related goods (Information and Communication Technology) or any specific articles related to ICT. Instead, it provides a general framework for handling trade security measures, including investigations into serious losses or threats to domestic industries caused by surges in imports.

- Article 2: KPPI is tasked with addressing issues related to serious losses or threats to domestic industries caused by a surge in imports. If ICT-related goods are identified as causing such issues, KPPI would handle the investigation.
- Article 3: KPPI's functions include: Conducting investigations into serious losses or threats to domestic industries caused by imports. Collecting and processing evidence related to such investigations. Recommending trade security actions to the Minister of Trade.
- Article 12: The Sub Committee of Investigation in the Sector of Industry and Mining is responsible for investigating serious losses or threats to domestic industries in the industrial and mining sectors. ICT-related goods, being part of the industrial sector, could fall under this category.
- Article 14: Financing for KPPI's activities, including investigations into specific goods (potentially ICT-related), is covered by the National Budget of the Ministry of Trade.

From the Penyelidikan Berjalan (Ongoing Investigations) section of the KADI website, there are currently no explicit cases related to ICT (Information and Communication Technology) goods. The ongoing investigations listed are:
- PP Copolymer
- Benang Filamen Sintetik (Synthetic Filament Yarn)
- Kertas Duplex (Duplex Paper)
- PP Homopolymer

Since July 2011;

Last reviewed in January 2025

Indonesia.act: Presidential Regulation No.16 on Government Procurement of Goods and Services 2018
Indonesia.act_norm: presidential regulation no 16 on government procurement of goods and services 2018
acts_norm: presidential regulation no 16 on government procurement of goods and services 2018
Indonesia.impact:
The Presidential Regulation No.54 on Government Procurement of Goods and Services 2010 has been revoked and replaced by Presidential Regulation 16/2018 where there is currently a second revision of Presidential Regulation 16/2016 (amendment) 

Presidential Regulation No.16 Year 2018 on Government Procurement and its amendments:
-	The Presidential Regulation underlines the importance of key procurement principles, such as transparent, open, effective, competitive and accountable as well  as value for money.
-	According to article 63, foreign companies may participate in international procurement under the following thresholds:
○	Rp 1 trillion (approx. 60.8 million USD*) for construction works;
○	Rp50 billion (approx. 3.01 million USD*) for goods and other services; and
○	Rp25 billion (approx. 1.52 million USD*) for consulting services.
-	Furthermore, foreign companies are also allowed to participate under such thresholds in a case there are no qualified domestic companies to fulfill requirements/technical specification. Therefore, the participation of foreign companies is not restricted to extremely large bids.
-	In the same article, it is stipulated that international procurement is announced on the website of the Ministries/Institutions/Local Governments and the international community website. 

Article 66 mentions the use of domestic products in government procurement, however, there is also the possibility to procure imported goods in the event that the goods cannot be produced domestically or domestic production volume is unable to meet the demand.

Foreign business actors can also participate in procurement in Indonesia below a predetermined threshold value as long as there are no capable and qualified national business actors. This means that Indonesian government procurement does not exclude foreign business actors in general or a group of foreign business actors or only allow foreign business actors for procurement packages above the threshold.

Indonesia.act: President Instruction No. 22/2022 on Accelerating the Increase in the Use of Domestic Products and Products from Micro, Small Enterprises, and Cooperatives to Support the Success of the National Movement "Proudly Made in Indonesia" in the Implementation of Government Procurement of Goods and Services 2022
Indonesia.act_norm: president instruction no 22 2022 on accelerating the increase in the use of domestic products and products from micro small enterprises and cooperatives to support the success of the national movement proudly made in indonesia in the implementation of government procurement of goods and services 2022
acts_norm: president instruction no 22 2022 on accelerating the increase in the use of domestic products and products from micro small enterprises and cooperatives to support the success of the national movement proudly made in indonesia in the implementation of government procurement of goods and services 2022
Indonesia.impact: On 30 March 2022, the government of Indonesia issued President Instruction No. 22/2022, mandating all government agencies to spend at least 40% of their budget on local products produced by SMEs. According to the regulation, the objective is to increase the use of local products to support the "Proud of Using Products Made in Indonesia" campaign. The regulation entered into force on 30 March 2022.

Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 29 on industrial empowerment 2018 regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 28 on the implementation of the industrial sector 2021 regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 46 on amendments to regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 28 on the implementation of the industrial sector 2023
acts_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 29 on industrial empowerment 2018
regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 28 on the implementation of the industrial sector 2021
regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 46 on amendments to regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 28 on the implementation of the industrial sector 2023
Indonesia.impact:
Under the Regulation of the Government of the Republic of Indonesia No.29 of 2018 on Industrial Empowerment 2018 as amended by Government Regulation (PP) No.28 of 2021
Maintenance of Industrial Fields, Articles 57 and 58 specify the obligation of utilizing domestic products for government procurement if funded by the state or regional budgets, including details on compliance.

According to Article 57, the following entities are required to utilize domestic products in the procurement of goods and services: State institutions, ministries, non-ministerial government institutions, other government institutions, and regional working units when the procurement is financed by:
- The state budget (APBN) or regional budget (APBD).
- Domestic or foreign loans or grants.
State-owned enterprises (SOEs), other state-owned legal entities, regionally-owned enterprises, and private enterprises in the procurement of goods and services when:
- Financing is derived from the state budget or regional budget.
- Work is conducted under a cooperation scheme between the Central Government and/or Local Governments with enterprises.
- The procurement involves the exploitation of state-controlled resources (e.g., natural resources, telecommunications frequencies, etc.).

Article 58 specifies that the obligation to utilize domestic products applies at both the planning and implementation stages of the procurement process.
Entities must prepare an annual plan for the goods and services they need, including technical specifications, amounts, prices, and procurement schedules. 

Article 61 requires that domestic products must be utilized if the combined value of their National Contribution Value (TKDN) and Corporate Contribution Value is at least 40%. The TKDN value of the domestic product must be at least 25%.

Article 64 mandates price preferences for domestic products:
- A maximum 25% price preference for goods with a TKDN value of at least 25%.
- A maximum 7.5% price preference for construction services provided by domestic companies over foreign competitors.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Communication and Informatics of the Republic of Indonesia No.41/PER/M.KOMINFO/10/2009 on Procedures for Assessing the Achievement of Domestic Component Levels in the Implementation of Telecommunications 2009
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 41 per m kominfo 10 2009 on procedures for assessing the achievement of domestic component levels in the implementation of telecommunications 2009
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 41 per m kominfo 10 2009 on procedures for assessing the achievement of domestic component levels in the implementation of telecommunications 2009
Indonesia.impact:
Decree No. 41/PER/M.KOMINFO/10/2009: Article 1(4) and subsequent sections define and outline the procedures for assessing domestic content (TKDN) in telecommunications.
Article 1(4): Definition of Level of Domestic Component (TKDN). TKDN (LDC) is defined as the portion of domestic components in goods, services, or a combination of both, used in telecommunications.
It includes:
- Goods: Telecommunication materials and equipment.
- Services: Design, engineering, and other supporting services.
- Combination: A mix of goods and services.
The domestic component must involve local manufacturing, fabrication, assembling, work settlement, and services performed domestically, using local experts and software.

Article 3(1) and 3(2) of Calculation of TKDN and Foreign Component provides the formula for calculating the Level of Domestic Component (TKDN) and explicitly identifies the Foreign Component (FC) as part of the calculation. The formulas are:

Formula 1:
% TKDN (Capex) = ((Total Capex − Foreign Component Capex) /Total Capex) × 100 %

Formula 2:
% TKDN (Capex) = (Domestic Component Capex / Total Capex) ×100%

This explicitly separates foreign components (e.g., imported goods, foreign services, foreign software) from domestic components, ensuring that foreign elements are excluded from the TKDN percentage.

Article 4(1) and 4(2) of Proof of Domestic Expenditure requires telecommunication providers to show proof of domestic expenditure for telecommunication materials/equipment sourced from domestic vendors. It also mandates that the percentage of TKDN for these materials/equipment must be authenticated by:
- An authorized agency or
- An independent survey institute assigned by the government.
This ensures that foreign components are excluded unless they are explicitly verified as domestic.

Article 5 of Verification of Domestic Components  mandates that any assessment of TKDN using telecommunication materials/equipment from domestic providers must be verified by:
- An authorized agency, or
- An independent survey institute.
This verification process ensures that foreign components are not misrepresented as domestic.

Article 6 of Local Content Threshold states that telecommunication materials/equipment with ≥ 50% local content are considered to have 100% local content for TKDN purposes. This provision effectively excludes foreign components from being counted as domestic unless they meet the 50% threshold.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Industry No. 15/M-IND/PER/3/2016 on Specification Standards and Price Standards for Domestic Transmission Towers and Conductors to Accelerate the Development of Electricity Infrastructure 2016
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of industry no 15 m ind per 3 2016 on specification standards and price standards for domestic transmission towers and conductors to accelerate the development of electricity infrastructure 2016
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of industry no 15 m ind per 3 2016 on specification standards and price standards for domestic transmission towers and conductors to accelerate the development of electricity infrastructure 2016
Indonesia.impact:
Regulation No. 15/M-IND/PER/3/2016 primarily focuses on Tower Transmission and Conductor products, the principles outlined in Articles 2, 4, 6, 7, and 8 regarding the prioritization of domestic content (TKDN) and compliance monitoring can be applied to ICT goods and digital services in public procurement.

Article 2 states that Tower Transmission and Conductor products must comply with Standar Spesifikasi (Standard Specifications) and Standar Harga (Standard Prices). The specifications and pricing standards are likely designed to favor domestic products, indirectly excluding foreign goods that do not align with these standards.

Article 4 establishes a benchmark pricing system for the procurement of domestic Transmission Towers and Conductors. This ensures that public procurement adheres to fair and consistent pricing, avoiding overpricing or underpricing. By mandating adherence to Standard Prices for domestic products, Article 4 indirectly excludes foreign products that may not align with these price benchmarks. This is because foreign products are not explicitly included in the pricing standards, and the regulation prioritizes domestic goods. The Standard Prices are tied to the domestic content requirements (TKDN) outlined in Article 6. 

Article 6 article mandates that Tower Transmission and Conductor products used in the acceleration of electricity infrastructure must meet a minimum Tingkat Komponen Dalam Negeri (TKDN) (Domestic Component Level) of 40%. This provision explicitly prioritizes domestic products and components, effectively excluding foreign goods and services that do not meet the required TKDN threshold from public procurement, because only products meeting the minimum TKDN threshold (40%) are eligible for procurement, and their prices must align with the standards in Lampiran I and II.

Article 7: The TKDN value must be verified and certified through a formal process, as outlined in this article. Ensuring that only products meeting the domestic content requirements are eligible for procurement, further excluding foreign products that fail to meet these standards.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Government of the Republic of Indonesia No.71 on the Provision of Electronic System and Transaction 2019
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 71 on the provision of electronic system and transaction 2019
acts_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 71 on the provision of electronic system and transaction 2019
Indonesia.coverage: Electronic systems operators for public scope
Indonesia.impact:
Government Regulation No. 82 of 2012 on the Implementation of System and Electronic Transactions. Specifically, Article 8: Requires that providers who develop software specifically for a government agency must submit the source code and related documentation to the agency. If direct submission is not feasible, the source code and documentation should be entrusted to a reliable third party. We are of the view that the notes contained in the column Findings/ Inquiries regarding the sub-pillars may provide some insight into the content of Article 8. Article 9: Emphasizes the confidentiality of the source code, allowing for inspection only if necessary for investigation purposes. 

Government Regulation No. 71 of 2019 on the Implementation of System and Electronic Transactions regarding the Implementation of Electronic Systems and Transactions, article 9 specifies that software developers providing custom software for public electronic systems must submit source code and documentation to the relevant public institution. If internal storage is not feasible, a third party trusted for source code safekeeping can be used. This ensures government institutions maintain control over critical software. This regulation do not explicitly link trade secrets to public procurement but imply the need for protecting sensitive information. For instance, Article 15 emphasizes maintaining the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of personal data and related documentation. The first and second sentences in bullet point 2.2.1(a) are true because can be referred to Article 9 paragraph (1) and (3). However, the next sentence is an author's interpretation that is not included in the body or explanatory section of PP 71/2019.

Based on Article 103 paragraph (2) of Government Regulation No. 71/2019, Government Regulation No. 82/2012 has been revoked. Therefore, following the legal principle lex posterior derogat legi priori (a newer regulation overrides an older one), the provisions under Government Regulation No. 71 of 2019 now serve as the main legal reference for the implementation of electronic systems and transactions.

Indonesia.impact:
Article 63 requires that domestic service companies must be involved in the procurement of services or a combination of goods and services. These companies must:

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Industry No.02/M-IND/PER/1/2014 on Guidelines of Increasing Use of Domestic Products 2014
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of industry no 02 m ind per 1 2014 on guidelines of increasing use of domestic products 2014
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of industry no 02 m ind per 1 2014 on guidelines of increasing use of domestic products 2014
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Industry Regulation No. 02/M-IND/PER/1/2014 Article 16.1 restricts the participation of foreign suppliers in public procurement. According to the law, Articles 17 and 20 discuss the Domestic Component Level (TKDN) and the Benefit to Company Points (BMP) which is based on investments of the suppliers in Indonesia. According to Article 10, domestic service suppliers need to be prioritized. To be considered as a domestic service supplier, the majority of shares have to belong to an Indonesian citizen and two-thirds of the board members have to be locals. If no domestic service suppliers are participating in the procurement, service suppliers where more than 50% up to 90% is owned by a foreign company or foreign citizen will be taken into consideration. 

Ministry of Industry Regulation No. 02/M-IND/PER/1/2014: Article 3 and Article 4 cover the guidelines for the promotion and mandatory use of domestic products in government procurement, detailing conditions for verifying TKDN values. Article 10 mandates that service procurement must prioritize home services companies, which must be established under Indonesian law and operate domestically, with more than 50% shareholding by Indonesian citizens or state entities, and two-thirds of the board comprising Indonesian citizens. Articles 15 and 16 allow for participation by foreign service companies when domestic or national service companies are unavailable, specifying that such foreign companies can participate under specific conditions.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Government of the Republic of Indonesia No.71 on the Provision of Electronic System and Transaction 2019
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 71 on the provision of electronic system and transaction 2019
acts_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 71 on the provision of electronic system and transaction 2019
Indonesia.coverage: Electronic systems operators for public scope
Indonesia.impact:
Government Regulation No. 71/2019 states that the electronic systems operators for public scope should store the electronic transaction data in Indonesia, unless the technology needed is unavailable in Indonesia. Public ESOs are defined to include 
(a) public bodies (such as central and regional executive, legislative, judicative bodies and any other bodies established pursuant to a statutory mandate); and 
(b) entities appointed by public bodies to operate electronic systems on their behalf. Previously, Government Regulation 82 (Article 17) required “electronic systems operators for public service” to connect their data to a data centre and a disaster recovery centre established in Indonesian territory for law enforcement and data protection. 

Although “public services” was not defined under the Government Regulation 82, this term had been interpreted in practice by MOCI broadly. Therefore, the local storage obligations were widely understood to cover all services offered to the public via the internet, effectively affecting many private sector companies.

Note that Government Regulation No. 82 of 2012, replaced by Government Regulation No. 71/2019 regarding the Provision of Electronic System and Transaction.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Finance Regulation No.115/PMK.06/2020 on the Utilization of State-Owned Assets 2020
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of finance regulation no 115 pmk 06 2020 on the utilization of state owned assets 2020
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of finance regulation no 115 pmk 06 2020 on the utilization of state owned assets 2020
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Finance Regulation No.115/PMK.06/2020, which replaces the Ministry of Finance Regulation No.65/PMK.06/2016 mandates foreign entities to establish an Indonesian limited liability company before utilizing the state-owned property for undertaking the public procurement of the infrastructure projects (including telecommunication infrastructure) under the public-private partnership scheme. 

This requirement was already in place since 2016. Nevertheless, this represents a liberalisation for foreign companies in Indonesian public procurements, since previous required the prioritization of domestic service suppliers.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Industry No.02/M-IND/PER/1/2014 on Guidelines of Increasing Use of Domestic Products 2014
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of industry no 02 m ind per 1 2014 on guidelines of increasing use of domestic products 2014
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of industry no 02 m ind per 1 2014 on guidelines of increasing use of domestic products 2014
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Industry Regulation No. 02/M-IND/PER/1/2014 regulates the procurement of public goods and services. 
The regulation includes price preferences for bidders with more than 25% of TKDN or striving to achieve at least 30%. The price preferences can be up to 15% for the procurement of goods and maximally 7.5% for services.

Ministry of Industry Regulation No. 02/M-IND/PER/1/2014, Article 10 requires that procurement involving services must prioritize domestic service companies. These companies must have more than 50% ownership by Indonesian citizens or state entities and two-thirds of the board must be Indonesian. Article 16 states that if domestic or national services companies do not participate, only then can the procurement process include foreign service providers.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Communication and Informatics of the Republic of Indonesia No.41/PER/M.KOMINFO/10/2009 on Procedures for Assessing the Achievement of Domestic Component Levels in the Implementation of Telecommunications 2009

acts_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 41 per m kominfo 10 2009 on procedures for assessing the achievement of domestic component levels in the implementation of telecommunications 2009
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Industry Regulation No. 49/2009, as amended by the Ministry of Industry Regulation No.102/M-IND/PER/10/2009, requires the use of domestic products and services in 558 sub-sectors for public procurement. Domestic products and services are defined as goods or services produced or prepared by a company investing and producing in Indonesia, with the possibility to use imported raw material or component in the production or working process. 

The Ministry of Industry Regulation No. 102/M-IND/PER/10/2009 mandates the use of domestic products and services in government procurement if such products meet specific local content thresholds. Domestic products are defined as those produced or processed by a company operating in Indonesia, even if they incorporate imported raw materials or components - as long as the majority of production occurs domestically.
- Article 10 states that priority must be given to domestic service providers in public procurement. A service provider qualifies as domestic if over 50% of its shares are owned by Indonesian nationals or entities, and at least two-thirds of its board members are Indonesian.
- Articles 8 and 11 mandate the use of local content levels in public procurement. Goods and services are eligible only if they meet minimum local content requirements. This stipulation indirectly limits the participation of foreign companies that cannot satisfy these requirements

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Industry No. 15/M-IND/PER/3/2016 on Specification Standards and Price Standards for Domestic Transmission Towers and Conductors to Accelerate the Development of Electricity Infrastructure 2016
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of industry no 15 m ind per 3 2016 on specification standards and price standards for domestic transmission towers and conductors to accelerate the development of electricity infrastructure 2016
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of industry no 15 m ind per 3 2016 on specification standards and price standards for domestic transmission towers and conductors to accelerate the development of electricity infrastructure 2016
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Industry Regulation 15/M-IND/PER/3/2016 Article 6 mandates that transmission towers and steel-reinforced conductors used in public procurement must meet a minimum local content level of 40%. This aligns with the regulation’s goal of boosting domestic production by setting these minimum thresholds.

Article 57 and 58 mandate that public procurement funded by state or regional budgets must utilize domestic products. This includes a requirement for products to have a minimum local content (TKDN) value.
Article 61 specifies that to be eligible for government procurement, goods and services must meet certain TKDN and Corporate Contribution Value thresholds.
Article 64 outlines price preferences for domestic products, stating that goods with TKDN values of at least 25% are given price preferences of up to 25% over foreign products

Indonesia.act: President Instruction No. 22/2022 on Accelerating the Increase in the Use of Domestic Products and Products from Micro, Small Enterprises, and Cooperatives to Support the Success of the National Movement "Proudly Made in Indonesia" in the Implementation of Government Procurement of Goods and Services 2022
Indonesia.act_norm: president instruction no 22 2022 on accelerating the increase in the use of domestic products and products from micro small enterprises and cooperatives to support the success of the national movement proudly made in indonesia in the implementation of government procurement of goods and services 2022
acts_norm: president instruction no 22 2022 on accelerating the increase in the use of domestic products and products from micro small enterprises and cooperatives to support the success of the national movement proudly made in indonesia in the implementation of government procurement of goods and services 2022
Indonesia.impact: The Presidential Instruction No.2 of 2022 regarding preference margins and local content in government procurement outlines in Article 8 states that government agencies must prioritize the use of domestic products in public procurement, particularly those with a minimum local content (TKDN) of 25%. This aligns with the mandate to promote domestic industries. Article 15 provides for price preferences for procurement of local products, specifying that goods with sufficient local content should be given a favorable margin in pricing to support the “Proud of Using Products Made in Indonesia” campaign.

Indonesia.act: Presidential Regulation No.16 on Government Procurement of Goods and Services 2018
Indonesia.act_norm: presidential regulation no 16 on government procurement of goods and services 2018
acts_norm: presidential regulation no 16 on government procurement of goods and services 2018
Indonesia.impact:
The Presidential Regulation No.16 Year 2018 on Government Procurement and its amendment:
●	Article 63 (3) specifies that foreign companies participating in international procurement must collaborate with national businesses in the form of consortia, subcontracts, or other partnerships.
●	Article 65 emphasizes participation of micro and small  enterprises with exception for procurement packages requiring technical capabilities that those businesses cannot fulfill.

One of the objectives of the requirement to cooperate with national business actors is to support the effective implementation of procurement processes - for example, in cases where after-sales service or other forms of technical support are required.
The provision regarding small and micro enterprises in public procurement aligns with international practice. However, it is important to note that while special measures are in place to support micro and small enterprises, they may be excluded from certain procurement packages if the required technical capabilities exceed what such enterprises can provide.

Indonesia.act: Law No. 25 on Investment 2007
Indonesia.act_norm: law no 25 on investment 2007
acts_norm: law no 25 on investment 2007
Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia's Investment Law No. 25 of 2007 provides a broad legal framework for investment in Indonesia, emphasizing openness and equal treatment to foreign investment (Article 6) while allowing for specific restrictions in certain sectors. For precise foreign equity limits in sectors relevant to digital trade, one would need to refer to the Positive Investment List and the Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021. The “Positive Investment List,” is part of Indonesia’s broader strategy to attract foreign investment while protecting specific sectors for local businesses. The Positive Investment List allows 100% foreign ownership in several key areas to encourage foreign investment in digital infrastructure and technology (listed in the annexes). 

This includes large-scale e-commerce platforms, classified under high-capital investment thresholds, which enable global players to operate independently, and data centers (Business Field No. 63111), which are critical for supporting Indonesia’s digital economy and data storage needs. Additionally, cloud computing services for general use are open to full foreign ownership, allowing flexibility for international technology companies to provide cloud services in Indonesia. Over-the-top (OTT) digital content platforms, such as video and music streaming services, are also unrestricted, promoting the growth of diverse content services for Indonesian consumers. These policies align with Indonesia’s digital transformation goals by enhancing technology transfer, infrastructure development, and consumer access to global digital services, while maintaining regulatory oversight for sectors with more strategic or sensitive data requirements, such as critical infrastructure and payment systems.

Indonesia.act:
Presidential Regulation No.10 on Investment Business Sectors 2021;

Presidential Regulation No.49 on Amendments to Presidential Regulation No.10 on Investment Business Sectors 2021
Indonesia.act_norm: presidential regulation no 10 on investment business sectors 2021 presidential regulation no 49 on amendments to presidential regulation no 10 on investment business sectors 2021
acts_norm: presidential regulation no 10 on investment business sectors 2021
presidential regulation no 49 on amendments to presidential regulation no 10 on investment business sectors 2021
Indonesia.impact:
Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021 amended by Presidential Regulation No. 49 of 2021: 
Article 2:
(1) All business fields are open to investment, except for those declared as closed or open with certain conditions.
(2) Business fields closed to foreign investment include:
a. Production of weapons, ammunition, explosive equipment, and warfare equipment.
b. Any business sectors explicitly declared closed based on the law.
Article 6:
(1) Business fields with specific conditions may have restrictions on foreign ownership.
(2) The government will specify business fields that are open with certain conditions based on national interest criteria.
Article 7:
This article specifies that foreign investors may only invest in certain sectors with restrictions, which are detailed in the annexes of the regulation.

Annexes:
The annexes provide a detailed list of business sectors along with their respective foreign ownership limits. For example, sectors relevant to digital trade may have specific caps, such as 67% for non-ASEAN investors and 70% for ASEAN investors, depending on the classification of the business field (Annex III of Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021).

Under Appendix I, digital news and media content platforms subject to foreign ownership limited to 49% to safeguard national media and information integrity (Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021, Appendix I, Business Field No. 60201).

Indonesia.act: Bank Indonesia Regulation No.20/6/PBI/2018 on Electronic Money 2018
Indonesia.act_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 20 6 pbi 2018 on electronic money 2018
acts_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 20 6 pbi 2018 on electronic money 2018
Indonesia.impact:
In the financial technology (fintech) sector, digital payments and e-wallets are restricted to 85% foreign ownership to ensure a balance between foreign investment and national financial security (Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 20/6/PBI/2018, Article 7).

Note that Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 11/12/PBI/2009, last amended by Bank Indonesia Regulation No.20/6/PBI/2018 regarding E-money.

Indonesia.act:
Regulation of Financial Services Authority No.40/POJK 40/2024
on Information Technology-Based Joint Financing Services 2024
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of financial services authority no 40 pojk 40 2024 on information technology based joint financing services 2024
acts_norm: regulation of financial services authority no 40 pojk 40 2024
on information technology based joint financing services 2024
Indonesia.impact:
This aspect of regulation has been amended by the Regulation of Financial Services Authority (POJK) No.40 of 2024 on Information Technology-Based Joint Financing Services (POJK 40/2024), which regulates ownership cap as follows:
-	Art. 3 (5): The provision on ownership by foreign legal entities and foreign nationals shall be implemented in accordance with government regulations concerning foreign ownership in Providers (information technology-based joint financing services providers).
-	Art. 3 (6): In the event where government regulations concerning foreign ownership in Providers has not yet been enforced:
a.	Direct or indirect foreign ownership in Providers should not exceed 85% of Providers’ paid-up capital.
b.	Grandfather clause applies for Providers with direct and indirect foreign ownership exceeding the 85% cap who have obtained a business license from OJK prior to the enforcement of this OJK Regulation.

The aim of this clause is to encourage the participation of local investors as the shareholder in a P2P Lending provider, through joint ventures with the foreign investor as well as to protect the interests of Indonesian financial consumers. This limitation aims to promote the participation of local investors as shareholders in P2P lending providers through joint ventures with foreign investors, while also safeguarding the interests of Indonesian financial consumers. Furthermore, POJK Number 77 of 2016 has been revoked by the implementation of POJK Number 40 of 2024.

Indonesia.act: Law No.32 on Broadcasting 2002
Indonesia.act_norm: law no 32 on broadcasting 2002
acts_norm: law no 32 on broadcasting 2002
Indonesia.impact:
Law No. 32 of 2002 on Broadcasting prohibits foreign equity ownership in terrestrial (free-to-air) broadcasting companies “Broadcasting agencies may be established by Indonesian legal entities, and foreign parties are prohibited from participating in the ownership of broadcasting agencies.” Broadcasting agencies are to operate in accordance with national interests, implying that foreign ownership is restricted to ensure cultural sovereignty. Covers terrestrial television and radio broadcasting companies but does not apply to subscription-based services like cable or satellite broadcasting.

- Article 16 states (1) the Private Broadcasting Institution as referred to in Article 13 paragraph (2) letter shall be a commercial broadcasting institution in the form of a juristic person established under Indonesian law, whose field of business shall only operate radio or television broadcasting services. (2) Foreign nationals shall be prohibited from being in the management of Private Broadcasting Institution, except for finance and technical fields.
- Article 17 states (1) the Private Broadcasting Institution as referred to in Article 16 paragraph (1) shall be established, the initial capital of which is wholly owned by Indonesian citizens and/or juristic persons established under Indonesian law. (2) Private Broadcasting Institution may make additions and developments in fulfilling the capital derived from foreign capital, which amounts to no more than 20% (twenty percent) of the total capital and is owned by a minimum of 2 (two) shareholders.
- Article 23 states (1) Community Broadcasting Institution shall not receive initial establishment funds and operational funds from foreign parties.
- Article 30 states (1) Foreign broadcasting institutions shall not be established in Indonesia.

Indonesia.act_norm: law no 19 on state owned entities 2003 regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 15 2013 regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 41 2021
acts_norm: law no 19 on state owned entities 2003
regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 15 2013
regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 41 2021
Indonesia.impact:
There is a limit of 49% on the shares that can be acquired by foreign investors in government-controlled firms. This includes foreign participation to SOEs in telecommunication and delivery service sectors (as regulated in the Government Regulation No. 15/2013 amended by the Government Regulation No.41/2021).

The Government Regulation No. 15 of 2013, as amended by Government Regulation No. 41 of 2021, regulates the foreign ownership limit for State-Owned Enterprises (SOEs) and their subsidiaries. This regulation emphasizes maintaining government majority control in strategic sectors. The 49% cap is designed to attract foreign investment and expertise while keeping majority ownership within the Indonesian government.

The Telecommunications and Delivery Service  sectors are classified as “strategic sectors” that are subject to heightened restrictions due to their importance to national security and public access. In these sectors, SOEs may have foreign partners, but the government generally maintains a 51% ownership or controlling interest in these enterprises.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Communication and Informatics of the Republic of Indonesia No.6 on the Provision of Internet Protocol Television (IPTV) Services 2017
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 6 on the provision of internet protocol television services 2017
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 6 on the provision of internet protocol television services 2017
Indonesia.impact: The Ministry of Communication and Information Technology Regulation No. 6/2017 introduces the requirement that any consortium providing Internet Protocol Television (IPTV) must consist of at least two Indonesian entities (Article 4). Furthermore, Article 6 of the regulation explicitly points to the fact that any foreign investor must comply with Article 4. Every consortium providing IPTV must seek approval from the Ministry of Communications and Informatics. The letter seeking approval shall also provide information on all company shares belonging to foreign investors (Article 25(7)h).

Indonesia.act: Regulation of Financial Services Authority No.12/POJK.03/2021 of 2021 on Commercial Banks 2021
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of financial services authority no 12 pojk 03 2021 of 2021 on commercial banks 2021
acts_norm: regulation of financial services authority no 12 pojk 03 2021 of 2021 on commercial banks 2021
Indonesia.impact:
Foreign ownership in banks is governed under Financial Services Authority Regulation (POJK) 12/2021 on Commercial Banks, which also covers banks that offer digital banking services). Locally incorporated banks (Badan Hukum Indonesia/BHI) may be established and/or owned by: 
(a)	Indonesian nationals and/or Indonesian legal entities; or 
(b)	a partnership between Indonesian nationals and/or Indonesian legal entities with foreign nationals and/or foreign legal entities. 

This provision was first introduced in the regulation on Commercial Banks in 1999 and has remained consistent through subsequent amendments. The concept of a Digital Bank, required to be established as a BHI, was introduced in the 2021 amendment of the commercial bank regulation. 
Since a Digital Bank must be a BHI, the same foreign ownership requirements apply as those for establishing and/or owning a BHI. 

Bank BHI (Bank Berbadan Hukum Indonesia) can be established by Indonesian citizens or legal entities, and foreign entities can hold up to 99% of the shares in partnership with Indonesian entities (Article 13).

Article 13 outlines the ownership structure, allowing foreign investors to hold up to 99% of shares in partnership with Indonesian entities, which can be interpreted as a joint venture-like requirement.
Paragraph (1): Bank BHI can be established and/or owned by:
- Indonesian citizens and/or legal entities; or
- Indonesian citizens and/or legal entities in partnership with foreign citizens and/or foreign legal entities.
Paragraph (2): Ownership by foreign citizens and/or foreign legal entities is limited to a maximum of 99% of the paid-up capital of the Bank BHI.

Article 23 defines a Digital Bank as a commercial bank that primarily operates without physical offices, aligning with the statement.
Paragraph (1): Bank BHI can operate as a Digital Bank.
Paragraph (2): A Digital Bank must have one physical office as its headquarters.
Paragraph (3): A Digital Bank conducts its business primarily through electronic channels without physical offices, except for the headquarters, or with limited physical offices.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Government of the Republic of Indonesia No.34 on the Employment of Foreign Workers in Indonesia 2021
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 34 on the employment of foreign workers in indonesia 2021
acts_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 34 on the employment of foreign workers in indonesia 2021
Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia's Government Regulation No. 34 of 2021 on the Employment of Foreign Workers in Indonesia mandates Employers to appoint an Indonesian worker as a counterpart to the foreign worker to facilitate knowledge and skill transfer. And, Employers must provide training and education to Indonesian workers to ensure the transfer of skills and technology from foreign workers.

Article 7 mandates that employers appoint an Indonesian employee as a counterpart (known as "Tenaga Kerja Pendamping") to each foreign worker. The purpose of this requirement is to ensure the transfer of knowledge and skills from the foreign worker to the Indonesian counterpart, aligning with the job qualifications required for the position occupied by the foreign worker.
Article 8 requires employers to conduct education and training for the Indonesian counterparts. The training should be in accordance with the position held by the foreign workers, ensuring that the local employees gain the necessary skills and knowledge.
