Indonesia.impact:
Law No. 11 of 2008 on Information and Electronic Transactions (ITE Law). The ITE Law is another important piece of legislation that governs electronic transactions and online content in Indonesia. It does not explicitly offer a safe harbor for online platforms in the context of copyright infringement, but it touches on issues related to online content and its regulation.
- Article 15 discusses the liability of electronic system providers. It states that they are responsible for the operation of their systems but are not liable for user-generated content if they can prove that the infringement was due to force majeure, user error, or negligence on the part of the user.
- Article 27 (1) Prohibits the distribution of content that infringes on another’s rights, including intellectual property rights. This could apply to online platforms, but it does not specify the liability of platforms for user-uploaded infringing content.
- Article 28 elaborates on the prohibition of content that violates the law, including defamation, obscenity, or the infringement of intellectual property rights. Again, this article does not address safe harbor provisions or limitations on platform liability.
- Article 29 Imposes obligations on service providers to ensure their services do not violate laws, which might include copyright infringement, but it does not include protections or exemptions for service providers.

Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics no 5 on trial of telecommunication informatics and broadcasting technology 2016 regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 80 on trading through the electronic system 2019 regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 5 on private electronic system operators 2020 minister of communication and informatics regulation no 10 on the amendment to minister of communication and informatics regulation no 5 of 2020 on private electronic system operators 2021

Indonesia.impact:
The Circular of the Minister of Communication and Information Technology No. 5/ 2016 provides the exemption to e-commerce providers from the liability for failures to comply with the relevant laws in the event of force majeure, errors, or negligence. E-commerce providers will only be responsible for prohibited content posted on their platform if they are unable to prove that the uploading of such content was caused by the users.

Government Regulation No.80/2019 provides a broad immunity to e-commerce service providers and intermediary service providers from the legal consequences arising from illegal third-party content. For e-commerce service providers, the regulation discharges them from any liability for illegal content found on their platforms, provided they have acted expeditiously to remove or disable access to such content after knowing of its existence (either by way of a report from a third party or finding it out themselves). To ensure that an e-commerce service provider is alerted of illegal content on its platform, the regulation requires such provider to provide terms of use/terms and conditions of the platform to its users and employing certain technology and/or feature in the platform for users to submit a report. For intermediary service providers, the regulation discharges them from any liability for illegal content provided that such providers are acting as a mere conduit. This will be evidenced by, among others, the provider's lack of control over the transmission. If an intermediary service provider provides an 'interactive computer service', such as a social media platform, they will be discharged from any liability for restricting or removing access to content if such action was carried out in good faith and based on a report that such content is illegal.

MOCI Regulation 5 now provides that private ESOs hosting user-generated content may be exempted from legal liability for prohibited content transmitted or distributed on their electronic systems as long as they have fulfilled their governance obligations, shared information on subscribers who uploaded the prohibited content for monitoring and law enforcement purposes, and taken down the prohibited content as regulated under MOCI Regulation 5. Private ESOs must ensure that their electronic systems do not (i) contain prohibited electronic information or documents and (ii) facilitate dissemination of prohibited electronic information or documents. They also must take down prohibited content within 24 hours or four hours (the latter is for urgent prohibited content, such as child pornography content, terrorism content and content that causes public unrest, which is very broad) after receiving the takedown notice. MOCI Regulation 5 classifies prohibited content into content that:
- is in violation of laws and regulations
- causes anxiety for society and disturbs public order based on the government’s assessment
- posts or provides access to prohibited content.

Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia’s Law No. 11 of 2008 on Electronic Information and Transactions (ITE Law) is the main regulation that governs online conduct, but it does not provide a safe harbor for online service providers when it comes to illegal activities carried out by users. The law addresses various illegal online activities, such as:
- Defamation (Article 27): Prohibits the dissemination of false information or defamation online. Criminalizes the distribution of illegal content (e.g., defamation, pornography, hate speech).
- Illegal Content Distribution (Article 27): Prohibits the distribution of pornography, gambling, or content that violates intellectual property rights.
- Article 28: Criminalizes the dissemination of false or misleading information.
- Cybercrime (Article 30 and 31): Deals with unauthorized access to computers or networks, fraud, and other forms of cybercrime.
- Article 40(2b): Requires electronic system providers (ESPs) to take down illegal content upon government request. Failure to comply may result in liability for the intermediary.

Electronic Information and Transactions Law (ITE Law) – Law No. 11 of 2008 (as amended by Law No. 19 of 2016): The ITE Law governs electronic transactions, information systems, and online content. It does not explicitly provide safe harbor protections for intermediaries.
- Article 27: Criminalizes the distribution of illegal content (e.g., defamation, pornography, hate speech).
- Article 28: Criminalizes the dissemination of false or misleading information.
- Article 40(2b): Requires electronic system providers (ESPs) to take down illegal content upon government request. Failure to comply may result in liability for the intermediary.
Implication: Intermediaries may be held liable if they fail to act on

Indonesia.impact:
Minister of Communication and Information Technology Regulation No. 14 of 2017 states that to get a prepaid phone SIM card in Indonesia, a customer must register their phone prepaid SIM card with their valid national ID and family register card, or a passport for foreigners. For the Registration process using passport, the information to be registered includes at least name, passport number, citizenship, and place and date of birth.

Minister of Communication and Information Technology Regulation No. 21 of 2021 strengthens Indonesia’s SIM card registration process by requiring both prepaid and postpaid SIM cards to be registered with valid identity information (Article 3). Indonesian citizens must use their KTP, while foreign nationals must use their passport (Article 3). Mobile operators are mandated to verify the registration information by cross-checking it with the Dukcapil (for locals) or Immigration (for foreigners) databases (Article 4). The regulation introduces the use of digital IDs and biometric verification (such as facial recognition or electronic signatures) to streamline and secure the registration process (Article 5). It also reinforces data protection measures, ensuring personal data is securely handled in line with Indonesia’s Personal Data Protection Law (Article 6). To combat fraudulent registrations, operators must implement anti-fraud measures (Article 7), and non-registered SIM cards will be blocked (Article 8). These provisions work together to enhance telecommunications security, reduce fraud, and ensure that SIM cards are linked to verified identities.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of Minister of Communications and Informatics No.5 on Telecommunications Operation 2021
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of minister of communications and informatics no 5 on telecommunications operation 2021
acts_norm: regulation of minister of communications and informatics no 5 on telecommunications operation 2021
Indonesia.impact:
The Minister of Communication and Information Technology Regulation No. 5 of 2021 includes user identity requirements, though these are primarily in the context of electronic systems and digital transactions.
- Article 3 - Obligations of Electronic system operators (ESOs) (which include online platforms, e-commerce, digital service providers, etc.) are required to verify the identity of users when conducting digital transactions. This ensures that the individuals using the platforms are properly identified to prevent fraudulent activities, protect user privacy, and ensure security during transactions.
- Article 5 ESOs are obligated to ensure that they collect and store personal data securely, and they must implement measures to protect user identities during digital transactions. The regulation ensures that personal information is only used for the intended purposes, such as verifying identities during transactions and ensuring privacy.
- Article 6 emphasizes the importance of electronic signatures, which must be linked to a verified user identity. When users sign digital contracts or engage in electronic transactions, their identity is verified using electronic signatures that provide a secure and legal way of confirming the identity of the person performing the transaction.
- Article 9 ESOs are required to ensure that the content uploaded by users, including the identity of those users, complies with Indonesian laws. This includes ensuring that illegal or fraudulent content is not published, which also relates to managing and verifying the identity of those posting or transacting on their platforms.

Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia imposes monitoring requirements under Minister of Communication and Information Technology Regulation No. 5 of 2021 concerning Private Electronic System Operators (PSE). 
- Article 9 Platforms must ensure their systems can prevent access to prohibited content.
- Article 14 Requires PSEs to actively block or remove content deemed unlawful or that disrupts public order within 24 hours of receiving a government notification. In urgent cases (e.g., terrorism or pornography), the timeframe is reduced to 4 hours.
Prohibited Content: Includes material violating laws, promoting violence, defamation, or threatening public safety. This is monitored via complaints from the public, automated systems, or direct orders from the Ministry.

Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 20 on protection of personal data in electronic systems 2016 regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 40 of 2019 on the implementation of law no 23 of 2006 on population administration as amended by law no 24 of 2013 on amendments to law no 23 of 2006 on population administration 2019

regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 40 of 2019 on the implementation of law no 23 of 2006 on population administration as amended by law no 24 of 2013 on amendments to law no 23 of 2006 on population administration 2019
Indonesia.impact:
In 2014, Indonesia blocked video-sharing websites: Reddit and Imgur. In 2015, Indonesia blocked another video-sharing website Vimeo. In March 2020, the online subtitling service Subscene.com was reportedly blocked.

Since 2016, the government can prevent the dissemination of prohibited content through blockings. Minisry of Informatics and Communications can block content and users to prevent the dissemination of prohibited content, following requests by enforcement agencies or the public.

In January 2018, the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology (MCIT) launched “Cyber Drone 9,” a crawler system driven by AI tools that are designed to proactively detect content violations. It replaced the Trust+ system, which relied on a passive database. A specialized task force monitors the new system and reviews the material it flags for filtering and blocking; the blocking itself is still carried out by ISPs. Each ISP may employ its software for blocking and thus may blacklist additional sites at its discretion. This has increased the likelihood of arbitrary, inconsistent blocking, creating uncertainty for users seeking redress when content is wrongfully blocked. In July 2020, the MCIT stated that it planned to purchase more sophisticated technology to block more categories of negative content and websites.

In Indonesia, the primary laws enabling the blocking of commercial web content are ITE Law, GR 71/2019, and PM Kominfo 5/2020, with specific focus on illegal content, system registration, and public order. These laws reflect Indonesia’s aim to regulate its digital ecosystem while balancing governance with user freedoms.

Indonesia.impact:
Ministerial Regulation No. 5 of 2020 on Private Electronic System Providers (PM Kominfo 5/2020)
- Article 7 requires ESPs, including commercial platforms, to register with Kominfo. PSEs that fail to comply with blocking requirements my face: Written warnings, Temporary suspension, Access blocking of their entire system and Revocation of their registration.
- Article 9 Defines obligations for ESPs to manage and moderate content (Contain prohibited content, Facilitate the dissemination of prohibited content). And Article 9(4) sets the grounds for blocking (Prohibited content includes: Violations of laws and regulations, Content that disturbs public order or causes public unrest, Content that provides access to other prohibited content).
- Article 13: PSEs are required to take down prohibited content upon: Receiving a request from the Ministry, law enforcement, or other authorized institutions and/or Identifying prohibited content themselves.
- Article 14 (2020 Regulation): Requests for blocking can be made by: The public, Ministries or government agencies and Law enforcement.
Courts.
- Article 15 (2020 Regulation): Content blocking is defined as Pemutusan Akses, which includes: Blocking access to specific content, Closing accounts, Deleting prohibited content.
 Article 15(6): PSEs must block content within 1x24 hours after receiving a blocking order.
 Article 15(8): For urgent cases (e.g., terrorism, child pornography), content must be blocked within 4 hours.
 Article 15(10): PSEs that fail to block prohibited content may also face administrative fines.
- Article 18: ISPs are required to block access to systems or content as instructed by the Ministry.
- Article 19: ISPs must display a landing page when blocking content, explaining the reason for the block.
- Article 21 Outlines the criteria for content takedown or blocking, including:
	•Prohibited content (e.g., pornography, hate speech).
	•Content that disrupts public order or national security.
- Article 36 Provides the legal basis for Kominfo to block unregistered ESPs.

The 2021 Amendment (Article 47) adjusts the timeline for PSE registration to align with the implementation of the Online Single Submission (OSS) system. However, it does not introduce significant changes to the content blocking provisions.

In 2022, platforms like PayPal and Steam were temporarily blocked for non-registration under this regulation.

Indonesia.act: Law No.44 on Pornography 2008
Indonesia.act_norm: law no 44 on pornography 2008
acts_norm: law no 44 on pornography 2008
Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia imposes restrictions on online advertising as stipulated in Article 4 of Law No. 44/2008 on Pornography. This article explicitly prohibits the creation, distribution, and use of pornographic content, including in advertisements across print, electronic, and online media. Consequently, online ads containing or promoting pornographic elements are classified as illegal content. Enforcement of this regulation may lead to measures such as blocking and filtering by Internet Service Providers (ISPs):
• Blocking may be applied to websites that entirely host pornographic advertisements.
• Filtering can restrict access only to specific ad content that violates the law. While internet shutdowns are not specifically mentioned in Article 4 of Law No. 44/2008, they could in principle be used as a last resort to halt access to illegal content, including pornographic ads.

Indonesia.act: Law No.8 on Consumer Protection 1999
Indonesia.act_norm: law no 8 on consumer protection 1999
acts_norm: law no 8 on consumer protection 1999
Indonesia.impact:
Law No. 8/1999 concerning Consumer Protection does regulate the prohibition of misleading or incorrect advertising practices. However, online advertising regulations in Indonesia are not limited to the misleading advertising aspect but also cover broader and contextual issues regarding the development of the digital ecosystem.

The main challenges of online advertising regulation in Indonesia include:
1. Advertisements that violate personal data.
2. Harmful content on social media.
3. Sale of illegal goods/services via digital ads.
Inter-ministerial need to monitor online advertising content such as in TikTok, Instagram and Google ads.
To reflect the Indonesian position, it should be emphasized that online advertising regulations are also regulated through additional regulations relevant to electronic commerce, electronic information, and digital security, as follows:
1.	Government Regulation No. 80/2019 concerning Electronic Commerce
●	Article 30: Regulates that electronic advertisements must be delivered correctly, clearly, and not misleading.
●	Article 59: Requires business actors to maintain and protect consumer personal data in electronic transactions.
2.	Regulation of the Minister of Trade No. 50/2020: Regulates licensing, governance, and supervision of advertisements and promotions delivered through e-commerce platforms. 
3.	Law No. 1/2024 concerning the Second Amendment to Law No. 11/2008 concerning Electronic Information and Transactions (ITE)
●	Article 28 paragraph (1): Prohibits the dissemination of electronic information that is misleading and can harm consumers.
●	Article 16A: Establishes an obligation for Electronic System Providers (PSE) to provide special protection for children in the use of electronic systems.

Indonesia.impact:
Government Regulation No. 109 of 2012 on the Control of Tobacco Products:
- Article 36 Prohibits advertisements targeting minors, showing smoking as a positive activity, or being aired in specific time slots on television.
- Article 47 Requires warnings on tobacco advertisements and bans showing active smoking.

Regulation of the Government of the Republic of Indonesia No. 28 of 2024 regulates the control of tobacco products in Indonesia, Implementing provisions aimed at minimizing the health risks associated restrictions on Selling Tobacco Products (Article 3), Standardization of Packaging (Article 6), Control of Electronic Cigarettes and Other Products (Article 12), and Enforcement and Penalties (Article 18). Regarding advertising and promotion, Article 9 stipulates that this section prohibits all forms of advertising and promotion of tobacco products, including sponsorship of events and the use of media for promotional purposes. this aims to reduce the appeal of tobacco products, especially among young people. Additionally, Article 20: Emphasizes the importance of public awareness campaigns aimed at educating the public on the hazards of tobacco use and the benefits of quitting smoking.

Indonesia.impact:
The Minister of Communication and Informatics Regulation No. 5 of 2020 on Private Electronic System Operators mandates both local and offshore Private Electronic System Operators (ESOs) to register their businesses to the relevant ministry through the online single submission system. Offshore ESOs should also appoint liaison officers, who have to be domiciled in Indonesia. The duty of the liaison officer is to facilitate any access request by government authorities and takedown request. According to the regulation, ESOs are persons, business entities, or communities that operate an electronic system. ESOs include electronic system operators that are supervised by ministers or institutions in accordance with laws and regulations, and electronic system operators that have an online portal, site, or application through the internet. The requirement was first enacted with the Government Regulation No. 71/2019 regarding the Provision of Electronic System and Transaction.

In Indonesia, the mandatory requirements for online content providers and applications is governed by PM Kominfo 5/2020 and GR 71/2019, requiring registration, content moderation, and compliance with data protection rules - not explicit licensing is required.

In Indonesia, the mandatory requirements for online content providers and applications is governed by PM Kominfo 5/2020 and GR 71/2019, requiring registration, content moderation, and compliance with data protection rules - not explicit licensing is required.

- Article 5: Applies to all electronic system providers (PSEs) operating in Indonesia.
- Article 21: PSEs providing services to public institutions must localize data within Indonesia.
- Article 99: Mandatory registration with Kominfo. Obtain additional licenses if their operations involve critical sectors such as telecommunications or government services.

Indonesia.act: Law No.36 on Telecommunications 1999
Indonesia.act_norm: law no 36 on telecommunications 1999

Indonesia.impact:
Telecommunications Law No. 36 of 1999:
The Telecommunications Law governs entities providing telecommunications services, which may include VPNs if they qualify under its definition.
- Article 1(4) Defines telecommunications services as the provision of telecommunications networks, transmission, or related facilities to transmit information. VPNs that enable secure data transmission may fall under this definition.
- Article 7 outlines the requirement for telecommunications service providers to obtain licenses, which could apply to VPNs operating as telecommunications services.
- Article 11(1) Requires operators of telecommunications services to obtain a license from the government before commencing operations.
- Article 14 Specifies that licensed entities must ensure the reliability and security of telecommunications networks, aligning with the purpose of VPNs.
- Article 17 Mandates compliance with technical standards set by the government to safeguard public interests and national security.
- Article 22 Licenses are issued selectively, requiring applicants to meet specific requirements, including operational capacity and adherence to Indonesian laws.
- Article 45 states that operating without a license may result in administrative sanctions, fines, or imprisonment.

Indonesia.coverage: iPhone 16, Google Pixel phone, digital services
Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia has recently imposed bans on certain ICT goods and digital services under its efforts to boost local manufacturing and protect domestic businesses. Notable bans include smartphones. 

The sale of the iPhone 16 and Google Pixel phones has been prohibited because these companies did not meet Indonesia’s “local content requirement,” which mandates that 40% of a device’s components or production processes occur domestically. This includes hardware assembly, software development, or local investments like research facilities. The Regulation of the Ministry of Industry No. 65, 2016 stipulates that smartphones must contain at least 40% locally made components to be sold in Indonesia.

- Digital Services: Following Regulation of the Minister of Trade No. 31 of 2023, Indonesia introduced new requirements and restrictions for both onshore and offshore e-commerce businesses offering goods and services to customers in Indonesia. Indonesia has also taken actions against certain platforms: TikTok’s e-commerce feature was banned in 2023, with the government citing its negative impact on local businesses. A similar move targeted Temu, a Chinese e-commerce platform, which was banned for potentially harming small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs).

Indonesia.act:
Regulation of the Minister of Trade
No.38/M-DAG/PER/8/2013 2013
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 38 m dag per 8 2013 2013
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of trade
no 38 m dag per 8 2013 2013
Indonesia.coverage: Mobile phones, handheld computers and tablets
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Trade Regulation No. 38/M-DAG/PER/8/2013  updated the framework for importing mobile phones, handheld computers, and tablets (Importer Requirements and Registration Article 7). This regulation builds on previous rules, including MOT Regulation No. 82/2012, and introduces specific requirements for importers, such as demonstrating prior import activities (Similar to Permendag Nomor 25 Tahun 2022. 

Article 19 requirements for imports history and establishing after-sales service networks across Indonesia. Importers must register as either Registered Importers (Importir Terdaftar, IT) or obtain Import Approval (Amendment to Nomor 83/M-DAG/PER/12/2012: Article 3 and 8) through the Directorate General of Foreign Trade.

One significant amendment in Regulation 38/2013 allows additional flexibility for importers working with overseas distributors. However, importers are mandated to either have three years of prior experience as an importer or distributor or commit to establishing local manufacturing within three years (Article 7). The requirement for a minimum network of 25 after-sales service centers remains in effect. Failure to meet these obligations can lead to the revocation of Registered Importer status.

Foreign companies face limitations since certain certifications (Article 7, Paragraph 1(e)), like the Distributor Certificate of Registration (STP), are only available to wholly Indonesian-owned entities. Despite claims that the regulation does not aim to restrict foreign investors, this provision has created practical barriers to foreign participation in the sector. Article 7(1)(e) of MOT Regulation 38/2013 requires certifications (e.g., Distributor Certificate of Registration) that are only available to Indonesian-owned entities.

Article 7(2)(b)): Importers must maintain a minimum of 25 after-sales service centers across Indonesia.
Article 8A): Importers must commit to establishing local manufacturing within three years or risk losing their Registered Importer status.

Additionally, MOT Regulation 38 Article 22 permits exemptions under specific conditions, but the process for obtaining these exemptions remains unclear.

Note that Regulation of the Minister of Trade Number 82/MDAG/PER/12/2012 concerning Provisions for Import of Cell Phones, Handheld Computers and Tablet Computers as has been amended with the Regulation of the Minister of Trade Number 38/M-DAG/PER/8/2013.

Indonesia.coverage: Mobile phones, handheld computers and tablets
Indonesia.impact:
Minister of Industry No.68/M-IND/PER/9/2016 has revoked by Ministers of Industry regulation No. 38 of 2024 
Ministers of Industry regulation No. 38 of 2024 
●	Article 2 - Registration Requirement for Importers
All cellular phones and tablet computers produced domestically and/or imported and marketed within the territory of Indonesia must be registered with the Ministry of  Industry.
The registration is evidenced by product registration marks that include:
a.	TPP Production (Production Registration Mark)
b.	TPP Import (Import Registration Mark)
c.	TPP product sample (Sample Registration Mark)

Ministers of Industry regulation No. 38 of 2024 
●	Article 5 - Application for Import Registration: The application for the issuance of TPP production, TPP Import, and/or TPP product sample must be submitted by companies that are brand owners or brand license holders in Indonesia. For TPP Import, and/or TPP product sample, applications may also be submitted by a designated representative authorized by the brand owner.

Ministers of Industry regulation No. 38 of 2024 
●	Article 6 - Submission of Application: Applications for the issuance of TPP production, TPP Import, and/or TPP product sample must be submitted electronically to the Director-General through the National Industrial Information System (SIINas).
The application must include:
a.	The brand
b.	Product Type
c.	Technical specifications
d.	TAC (Type Allocation Code) for registered devices with IMEI
e.	Number of units to be produced or imported
●	Article 19 - Monitoring: The Director-General conducts supervision of companies producing and/or importing cellular phones and tablet computers that have received TPP production, TPP Import, and/or TPP product sample. This supervision is conducted at least once a year and whenever necessary.
●	Article 20 - Sanctions for Violations: Companies selling or transferring registered cellular phones and tablet computers using TPP product sample may face administrative sanctions, including:
a.	Revocation of TPP product sample
b.	Removal of IMEI from the gray list in the central equipment identity register
c.	Deletion from the Ministry's database
d.	Prohibition from applying for TPP production, TPP Import, and/or TPP product sample for one year.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Trade No.  70/M-DAG/PER/9/2015 of 2015 on Importer Identification Number 2018
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 70 m dag per 9 2015 of 2015 on importer identification number 2018
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 70 m dag per 9 2015 of 2015 on importer identification number 2018
Indonesia.coverage: Wide range of products, including electronics
Indonesia.impact:
Ministry of Trade Regulation No.70/M-DAG/PER/9/2015, which replaces by the Ministry of Trade Regulation No.75/2018 requires all importers to obtain an import license as either importer of goods for further distribution (API-U) or as importers for their manufacturing (API-P), but importers are not permitted to obtain both types of licenses. The requirement was already in place since 2015. 

Changes Introduced by Regulation No. 75/2018. Importers now obtain a Business Identification Number (NIB), which doubles as an API. The dual-API restriction (companies must choose either API-U or API-P) remains in effect, preventing companies from holding both types. Importers must report import realization (completed or pending imports) via the designated Ministry of Trade portal. Failure to comply can result in administrative sanctions, including the suspension or revocation of the API/NIB.

Article 2 of the Ministry of Trade Regulation No. 75/2018 states that imports can only be conducted by importers who have an Angka Pengenal Importir (API), which serves as an import identification number. Article 3 specifies that the API is divided into two types: API Umum (API-U) for general importers and API Produsen (API-P) for manufacturers. This confirms the requirement for importers to obtain a specific type of import license. Article 7 explicitly states that each importer can only hold one type of API, either API-U or API-P, but not both. This maintains the restriction that companies must choose one type of license, preventing them from holding both. Article 9 aligns the issuance of the API with the Online Single Submission (OSS) system, as per Government Regulation No. 24/2018. It states that the Business Identification Number (NIB) issued by the OSS also serves as an API. Article 14 requires importers to report their import realization, whether completed or pending, every three months to the Director General, and this must also be submitted via the designated Ministry of Trade portal. Article 18 outlines that the API can be suspended if importers fail to report import realization or if they provide false information, among other violations. Article 20 states that the NIB/API can be revoked if the importer conducts business activities that do not align with the NIB/API or if declared invalid by a court decision.

Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia impose requirements primarily targeting consumer goods, including electronics under MOT Regulation 68/2020 and its amendmen. The stated goal is to encourage local production and reduce the influx of imports. The requirements involve obtaining Import Approval (PI) and submitting a Surveyor Report (LS) for importing regulated goods. Additionally, the monitoring of imports shifted from a post-border mechanism (outside customs) to a border mechanism (within customs), streamlining oversight.

Regulation 68/2020 came into effect in August 2020 and specified import conditions for various consumer goods. However, exemptions exist for certain cases, such as products for market testing or after-sales service. Furthermore, reporting obligations require importers to submit detailed monthly data electronically via the official trade website by the 15th of each month.

Note that Regulation of the Minister of Trade No.68/2020 amended by the Regulation 78/2020.

Indonesia.coverage: Software and Digital products transmitted electronically
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Finance Regulation No. 190/PMK.04/2022 (PMK-190), which specifically addresses the customs and VAT obligations for imported intangible goods such as software and digital products transmitted electronically. This regulation mandates that companies must submit an import notification form within 30 days of payment and pay relevant import duties and taxes, including VAT and income tax. It applies to software purchased electronically, including via downloads.

Article 34 on the Import of Intangible Goods. Intangible goods, such as software and other digital products, can be imported through electronic transmission. These goods are exempt from certain requirements, including transportation and manifest submission, unloading and storage in customs areas, and physical inspection. Customs obligations for these intangible goods are monitored through a customs audit mechanism. 
Article 35: Customs obligations for intangible goods are fulfilled using the Import Notification Form (PIB). The PIB must be submitted within 30 days from the date of payment for the intangible goods. Import duties for intangible goods are calculated according to the specified method in Article 22, which involves calculating duties based on the customs value. 
Article 36 imposes a collection of VAT and other taxes on intangible goods, conducted according to tax regulations concerning the use of intangible taxable goods from outside the customs area within the customs area.

Regulation of the Ministry of Communications and Information Technology (now the Ministry of Digital Communication) No.3 of 2024
Establishes mandatory certification for ICT equipment to ensure compliance with national standards, promoting the use of local manufacturers and service providers. It includes provisions aimed at increasing the local content required in ICT products, encouraging investment in local production facilities and capabilities.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Communication and Informatics of the Republic of Indonesia No. 41/PER/M.KOMINFO/10/2009 2009;
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 41 per m kominfo 10 2009 2009
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 41 per m kominfo 10 2009 2009
Indonesia.coverage: Components or services for telecommunication network
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Communication and Information Technology (MOCI) Regulation No. 12/2019, which amends MOCI Regulation No. 41/PER/M.KOMINFO/10/2009, telecommunications operators in Indonesia are required to allocate at least 50% of their capital expenditures (CapEx) for network development to locally sourced components or services. This regulation aims to promote the use of domestic products and services in the telecommunications sector. Furthermore, operators must submit an annual report detailing the percentage of local content they have procured, which must be verified by either a government agency or an accredited survey institution. These provisions are designed to encourage local industry participation in network infrastructure development.

MOCI Regulation No. 12/2019 Article 6 Specifies that components of telecommunications materials/devices with at least 50% local content are considered to have 100% local content. This effectively mandates that telecommunications operators allocate a significant portion of their capital expenditures (CapEx) for network development to locally sourced components or services. Article 10 states that Telecommunications operators are required to conduct a self-assessment of their local content achievement (TKDN) for both capital and operational expenditures annually. They must report these findings to the Director General by April 30 of the following year. Article 11 states reported local content achievements must be verified by either a government agency or an accredited survey institution. This ensures compliance and encourages the use of domestic products and services in the telecommunications sector.
Indonesia.timeframe: Since October 2009, last amended in October 2019

acts_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 13 on technical standards for telecommunication equipment and or mobile telecommunication devices based on long term evolution technology standards and international mobile telecommunication 2020 technology standards 2021

regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 27 on implementation of telecommunications network operations license and telecommunications service operations license for public telecommunications networks 2015
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Communication and Information Technology (MOCI) Regulation No. 27/2015, repealed under the MOCI Regulation No.13/2021, continues to mandate local content requirements (LCR) for telecommunications equipment such as 4G smartphones. As of 2017, the minimum local content level was set at 40% for base stations and 30% for subscriber stations (smartphones, modems, etc.). These regulations aim to foster domestic manufacturing and reduce reliance on imports in the telecommunications sector.

As of October 2024, Apple’s iPhone 16 are banned in Indonesia, it stems from the company’s failure to meet the local content threshold required for smartphones under these regulations. The LCR mandates that at least 30% of the components in devices like smartphones be sourced locally. Despite Apple’s efforts to invest locally, such as supporting developer academies, its production in Indonesia fell short of fulfilling these requirements, prompting the ban. This situation highlights Indonesia’s ongoing push to strengthen its domestic manufacturing capabilities, a central component of its regulatory framework, especially in high-tech sectors like telecommunications.

MOCI Regulation No. 27/2015 Article 4 mandates that telecommunications equipment, including base stations and subscriber stations (such as smartphones), must meet specific local content thresholds.
- Base Stations: The minimum local content level is set at 40%.
- Subscriber Stations (Smartphones, Modems, etc.): The minimum local content level is set at 30%.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Communication and Informatics of the Republic of Indonesia No.6 on the Provision of Internet Protocol Television (IPTV) Services 2017
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 6 on the provision of internet protocol television services 2017
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 6 on the provision of internet protocol television services 2017
Indonesia.impact:
The Ministry of Communication and Information Technology (MOCI) Regulation No. 6/2017 outlines specific local content requirements for IPTV providers in Indonesia, requiring Internet Protocol Set-Top Boxes to contain a minimum of 20% local content, with the requirement increasing to 50% within five years of operation. Additionally, IPTV operators must ensure that at least 10% of their broadcasting content and 30% of their multimedia services are of domestic origin. Furthermore, the regulation mandates that the number of local independent content providers in an IPTV operator’s content library must be at least 10% of the total, increasing to 50% within five years. These measures are part of Indonesia’s broader strategy to strengthen domestic production and reduce reliance on imports. Recent regulatory trends continue to emphasize local content across various industries, but no explicit amendments to these provisions have been reported. Consulting updated regulatory documents or official communications is advisable for precise compliance requirements.

Moreover, Pasal 13(2) mandates that Internet Protocol Set-Top Boxes (IP-STB) must prioritize domestic production with a minimum local content of 20%, which should be increased to at least 50% within five years. Pasal 17(1) outlines IPTV operators are required to allocate at least 10% of their broadcasting capacity to domestic content. Pasal 17(2) states For multimedia services, operators must ensure that at least 30% of their content library consists of domestic content. Pasal 17 (3) stipulates that the number of local independent content providers in an IPTV operator’s content library must be at least 10% of the total, with a gradual increase to 50% within five years.

According to Pasal 6 of this regulation, telecommunications equipment used for wireless broadband services must meet a minimum local content requirement of 30% for subscriber stations and 40% for base stations. It also states that these requirements should increase to 50% within five years.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Communication and Informatics of the Republic of Indonesia No.9 on Technical Requirements for Telecommunications Equipment and/or Devices Using Wavelength Division Multiplexing 2019

Indonesia.coverage:
TV and set-top boxes based on digital video broadcasting-terrestrial second 
generation and internet protocol set-top boxes
Indonesia.impact:
The MCIT Regulation No. 4/2019 governs technical requirements for digital video broadcasting-terrestrial second generation (DVB-T2) set-top boxes (STBs) and digital broadcasting devices. The focus of the regulation is more aligned with ensuring the equipment meets broadcasting standards and compatibility with the DVB-T2 framework rather than prescribing strict local content requirements. Reports and analyses suggest that local content requirements (LCR) in various forms are part of broader regulations under Indonesia’s industrial policy, particularly to support domestic manufacturing. 

MCIT Regulation No. 4/2019:
- Pasal 9 specifies the local content requirements (Tingkat Kandungan Dalam Negeri - TKDN) for digital broadcasting devices:
(1): Digital television receivers based on DVB-T2 must have a minimum local content of 20%.
(2): Internet Protocol Set Top Boxes must also meet a minimum local content requirement of 20%.
- Pasal 11 states that the local content requirement of at least 20% for digital television receivers and set-top boxes will be enforced starting one year after the regulation comes into effect.
- Pasal 4 outlines the types of broadcasting devices covered under the regulation, including:
(1): Digital television broadcasting devices based on DVB-T2.
(4): Internet Protocol Set Top Boxes, which are specifically mentioned as part of the digital broadcasting framework.
- Pasal 7 indicates that the technical requirements for telecommunications devices used for broadcasting are detailed in Lampiran I, which is an integral part of the regulation.

Indonesia.coverage:
Wavelength division multiplexing and internet protocol network 
devices
Indonesia.impact:
Ministry of Communications and Information Technology (MCIT) Regulation No. 9/2019 and No. 10/2019 mandate local content requirements for wavelength division multiplexing (WDM) and internet protocol (IP) network devices as part of Indonesia’s broader strategy to promote domestic manufacturing and reduce reliance on imports. These rules require a specified percentage of local components in such equipment to bolster local industry development. While the precise text of these regulations is not readily available in public records, reports confirm their continued enforcement.

MCIT Regulation No. 9/2019 Pasal 4 mandates that telecommunications equipment using wavelength division multiplexing (WDM) must meet local content requirements (Tingkat Komponen Dalam Negeri - TKDN) as per applicable regulations. This aligns with the strategy to promote domestic manufacturing and reduce reliance on imports.

MCIT Regulation No. 10/2019 Pasal 4: Similar to Regulation No. 9/2019, this article requires that internet protocol (IP) network devices comply with local content requirements. This is part of the broader strategy to support local industry development.

Indonesia.impact:
There was no record of export restrictions on digital goods and products. 

Regulation of the Minister of Energy and Mineral Resources (MEMR) No. 1/2014 affects the ICT supply chain as nickel is a critical component in electronics manufacturing. Article 3 prohibits the export of nickel ore with a nickel content of less than 1.7%.
- Article 3(4) states that certain types of metal mineral commodities, including nickel, must undergo processing and refining within Indonesia to meet the minimum processing and refining thresholds specified in Attachment I of the regulation. Attachment I (Lampiran I) explicitly sets the minimum threshold for nickel ore to be processed domestically, which includes the prohibition of exporting nickel ore with a nickel content of less than 1.7%.

Government Regulation No. 85/2015 on Bonded Zones; Ministry of Finance Regulation No. 131/2018 on SEZs provide incentives but includes limitations on exports and domestic sales. These cover 50% domestic sales limit and requirements for exemptions from duties and taxes.

Ministry of Finance Regulation No. 6/2014 imposes export duties on certain minerals, with rates varying depending on the type of mineral and its processing level, as specified in the annex. A 60% export duty may apply to some raw minerals unless specific conditions, such as smelter development, are met. While the regulation focuses on raw materials, these taxes can indirectly impact ICT production costs due to the reliance on critical minerals like nickel in electronics manufacturing.

Indonesia.act_norm: law no 20 on standardization and conformity assessment 2014 regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 8 on national standardization and conformity assessment system 2022
acts_norm: law no 20 on standardization and conformity assessment 2014
regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 8 on national standardization and conformity assessment system 2022
Indonesia.impact:
Law No.20 of 2014 states that Indonesia National Standards (SNI) can be developed in accordance with the international standards by taking into account the considerations of national interest and global competition. 

The development of Indonesian National Standard (SNI) is regulated under the Regulation of the National Standardization Body Number 8 of 2022. This regulation does not stipulate that stakeholders involved in the development of SNI originate solely from within the country. However, they are required to meet the stipulated criteria.

The formulation of the Indonesian National Standard (SNI) is carried out in a transparent manner and is accessible to the public via https://sispk.bsn.go.id. The public may provide input and feedback on the Draft SNI under development during the public enquiry stage.

Law No. 20/2014 on Standardization and Conformity Assessment establishes the framework for standardization and conformity assessment. It requires that regulated products meet mandatory SNI and undergo conformity assessment. Article 5 stipulates that technical standards should prioritize “national interests,” which can implicitly limit foreign participation in the process. And Article 8 outlines the formation of technical committees (komite teknis), which are predominantly comprised of local experts, without explicit provisions for foreign stakeholders. Article 41 states that technical standards should be based on consultation, but there is no explicit mechanism for notifying foreign stakeholders or publishing drafts for review.

Government Regulation No. 34/2018 on Trade Goods Certification and Standardization which serves as an implementing regulation for the provisions outlined in Law No. 20/2014, provides detailed rules and operational guidelines for enforcing the law, particularly concerning trade goods that must meet mandatory SNI or other technical standards.

Indonesia.act: ASEAN Sectoral Mutual Recognition Arrangement for Electrical and Electronic Equipment 2002
Indonesia.act_norm: asean sectoral mutual recognition arrangement for electrical and electronic equipment 2002
acts_norm: asean sectoral mutual recognition arrangement for electrical and electronic equipment 2002
Indonesia.impact:
Based on the ASEAN Sectoral Mutual Recognition Arrangement for Electrical and Electronic Equipment, Article 3, a member state shall accept or recognize the test reports and certifications issued by the testing laboratories and certification bodies of other parties.

Electronic products that are subject to mandatory implementation of the Indonesian National Standard (SNI) are not eligible for self-certification of product safety. Compliance with SNI requirements must be demonstrated through third-party certification, and therefore, certifications cannot be conducted solely by the suppliers.

Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 52 on telecommunications operations 2000 regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 46 on post telecommunications and broadcasting 2021 decree of the director general of resources and postal and informatics devices no 3 on the acceptance of international test reports for paperwork only type approval applications for non hkt equipment 2020

Indonesia.impact:
Government Regulation No. 46/2021, which replaces the Government Regulation No.52/2000 stipulates that a certificate of approval is required for IT and telecommunication equipment to be sold or imported into the Indonesian market. The certificate of approval is issued by the Directorate General of Resources and Equipment for Post and Information Technology (SDPPI). The SDPPI Kepdiejen Postel No.3/2020 allows third party-certification from Conformity Assessment Bodies (CABs) from a number of countries.

Self-certification limitations for product safety, particularly concerning radio transmissions, electromagnetic compatibility (EMC), and electromagnetic interference (EMI), are governed by several regulatory frameworks under the Directorate General of Resources and Equipment Standardization for Post and Informatics (SDPPI).

- SDPPI Regulation No. 7 of 2021 establishes the Type Approval process for telecommunication and radio frequency devices. Products must undergo testing in SDPPI-accredited laboratories to ensure compliance with EMC/EMI standards such as CISPR 32 and EN 301 489. Self-certification is not permitted; all results must be verified through accredited entities.
- SDPPI Regulation No. 109/2024 strengthens mandatory EMC testing parameters, such as conducted emissions and radiated emissions, for telecommunication equipment. This regulation requires conformity assessment through independent third-party testing labs accredited by SDPPI.

Foreign manufacturers are required to appoint a local Indonesian company to manage the Type Approval application and certification process. This includes the submission of technical documents and test reports.

The Minister of Communication and Information Technology (Kominfo) Regulation No. 16/2018 on Certification of Telecommunication Tools and Equipment does not allow manufacturers to self-certify their products’ compliance with Indonesian standards. Instead, a third-party testing body must assess compliance.
- Article 2: Requires all telecommunications equipment, including devices with radio transmission and EMC/EMI implications, to be certified before they can be marketed or used in Indonesia. Certification must be obtained from designated certification bodies, accredited by Kominfo. 
- Article 4, compliance with the Technical Requirements for telecommunication tools and/or equipment as referred to in Article 2 paragraph (1) must be proven by a Certificate through the certification process. 
- Article 6: Every telecommunication tool and/or equipment manufactured, assembled, or imported for trade and/or use within the territory of the Republic of Indonesia must be certified. Telecommunication tools and/or equipment that continue to be manufactured, assembled, or imported for trade in the territory of the Republic of Indonesia after three years from the effective date of the Certificate must be re-certified by the Certificate holder. 
- Article 23: Testing centers, as referred to in Article 22, conduct testing, the results of which are in the form of a Test Report. 
- Article 22 The testing centers include: a. Domestic Testing Centers; and b. Foreign Testing Centers.

Indonesia.impact:
●	Regulation No. 73/2015
-	Article 2: 
(1)	Goods traded in the domestic market must include labels in the Indonesian language.
(2)	the obligation to include labels in the Indonesian language applies to:
a.	Producers for domestically produced goods
b.	importers for imported goods. 
-	Article 3: Labels in the Indonesian language must be clear, easy to read, and easy to understand
-	Article 4: labels in the Indonesian Language can be applied in the following ways:
a.	embossed or printed directly on the product
b.	attached or affixed to the product or its packaging
c.	included as part of the product or packaging
-	Article 5: Specifies that labels must include safety and usage information, which is crucial for consumer protection. 
Labels must include the following information: 
a.	Name and address of the producer (for domestic goods)
b.	Name and address of the importer (for imported goods)
c.	Name and address of the collector trader if they sell goods from micro and small enterprises. 
-	Article 8: Exemptions to the labeling requirements:
a.	Bulk goods sold directly to consumers. 
b.	goods produced by micro and small enterprises. 

●	The Regulation of Minister of Trade Number 73 of 2015, as revoked by The regulation of Minister of Trade Number 25 of 2021 concerning the Stipulation of Goods Required to Use or be Accompanied by Labels in the Indonesian Language, does not regulate labeling provisions related to standards.
●	The Directorate of Standardization and Quality Control, The Ministry of Trade (Dit. Standardisasi dan Pengendalian Mutu, Kementerian Perdagangan) regulates the affixation of labels related to the Product Registration Number (Nomor Pendaftaran Barang/NPB) and the Registration of Goods concerning Safety, Security, Health, and Environmental Aspects (K3L) through The Regulation of Minister of Trade Number 21 of 2023.
●	The Product Registration Number (NPB) must be affixed to the goods and their packaging prior to the product being marketed or traded.
●	The amendment of Regulation No. 73/ 2015 focused on the appendix.
●	Regulation No. 25/2021
-	The list of goods subject to mandatory Indonesian-language labeling is provided in the appendix of the regulation. 
-	Labels must include the following information: (stated particularly in the Appendix Column Description/ explanation based on product type)
a.	Product name
b.	Brand name
c.	Name and address of the producer (for domestic goods) or importer (for imported goods)
d.	electrical information (voltage and frequency)
e.	Country of origin.

Indonesia.act_norm: presidential regulation no 47 on the national cybersecurity strategy and cyber crisis management 2023 law no 11 on information and electronic transactions 2008 law no 19 on amendments to law no 11 of 2008 on electronic information and transactions 2016 law no 1 on the second amendment to law no 11 of 2008 on electronic information and transactions 2024
acts_norm: presidential regulation no 47 on the national cybersecurity strategy and cyber crisis management 2023
law no 11 on information and electronic transactions 2008
law no 19 on amendments to law no 11 of 2008 on electronic information and transactions 2016
law no 1 on the second amendment to law no 11 of 2008 on electronic information and transactions 2024
Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia has the SNI ISO/IEC 19790:2015 standard, which regulates information technology, security techniques, and security requirements for cryptographic modules (ISO/IEC 19790:2012, IDT). 

The National Cyber and Crypto Agency (BSSN) is currently preparing a draft Agency Regulation concerning the Implementation of Indonesian Cryptographic Algorithms and the Cryptographic Module Security Assessment Scheme. The regulatory substance includes: general criteria and specific criteria for Indonesian cryptographic algorithms, the Indonesian cryptographic committee, and the cryptographic module security certification scheme. In this regulation, SNI ISO/IEC 19790:2015 is required to be implemented by Electronic System Operators (PSE) on Vital Information Infrastructure (IIV) protection technology.

Based on Presidential Regulation No. 47/2023 on the National Cybersecurity Strategy and Cyber Crisis Management, Indonesia emphasizes the importance of maintaining cybersecurity, including through regulations governing the use of encryption. This regulation is explicitly outlined in Article 6 of Presidential Regulation 47/2023, which addresses national cryptographic independence. Currently, Indonesia has formally adopted a national encryption policy framework that “deviates” in the sense that it emphasizes the use of domestically developed cryptographic algorithms, as stipulated in the Regulation of the National Cyber and Crypto Agency (BSSN) No. 11 of 2024 on the Implementation of Indonesian Cryptographic Algorithms and Cryptographic Module Security Conformity Assessment. This policy can be implemented by Electronic System Operators (PSE) or Critical Information Infrastructure Operators (PIIV). 

As outlined in BSSN Regulation No. 11/2024, the reference standard used is SNI ISO/IEC 19790:2015 Information Technology — Security Techniques — Security Requirements for Cryptographic Modules, and the adoption of international cryptographic systems remains possible. According to BSSN Regulation No. 11/2024, there is no application of encryption standards that deviate from internationally recognized standards. Furthermore, under the Electronic Information and Transactions Law (ITE Law) and BSSN Regulation No. 11/2024, foreign encryption products are required to undergo a certification process before being used in Indonesia. 

Although there is no explicit obligation to disclose trade secrets, the certification process may require the submission of proprietary technical documentation, which can raise concerns about the protection of sensitive business information. Essentially, these regulations aim to ensure that products and services comply with adopted security standards. It can be concluded that these policies are not intended to serve as a direct barrier to digital trade, as the regulations remain aligned with international standards and do not mandate the disclosure of trade secrets during the certification process.

Indonesia.act:
Presidential Regulation No.10 on Investment Business Sectors 2021;

Presidential Regulation No.49 on Amendments to Presidential Regulation No.10 on Investment Business Sectors 2021
Indonesia.act_norm: presidential regulation no 10 on investment business sectors 2021 presidential regulation no 49 on amendments to presidential regulation no 10 on investment business sectors 2021
acts_norm: presidential regulation no 10 on investment business sectors 2021
presidential regulation no 49 on amendments to presidential regulation no 10 on investment business sectors 2021
Indonesia.impact:
The Negative Investment List (Daftar Negatif Investasi or DNI) was historically governed by Presidential Regulation No. 44 of 2016, which outlined various business sectors where foreign ownership was restricted or prohibited. This regulation has since been replaced by the Positive Investment List under Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021, which liberalized many sectors. 

Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021 on Business Fields Open to Investment, part of the Omnibus Law (Law No. 11 of 2020 on Job Creation), sets the framework for business investment in Indonesia, including e-commerce. Article 3 and Attachment distinguishes between business activities reserved for domestic investors and those open to foreign investment. E-commerce businesses with total investment value below IDR 10 billion (excluding land and buildings) are reserved for domestic investors only. For e-commerce platforms with an investment value above IDR 10 billion, there is no restriction on foreign ownership (100% ownership is permitted).

Indonesia has made significant changes to its regulations regarding foreign equity in the e-commerce sector. The e-commerce sector in Indonesia is now open to 100% foreign ownership. Article 7 of Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021 did set a condition for foreign investment in Indonesia, stating that foreign investment is only permissible in large enterprises with an investment value exceeding IDR 10 billion, excluding land and buildings. This requirement is a general condition for foreign investors across various sectors, including e-commerce. 

Additionally, Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021 is no longer applicable due to the amendments made by Presidential Regulation No. 49 of 2021. The amendments clarify and update the regulations regarding foreign investment, containing a similar Article 7 of Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021 stating that Foreign investment can only be conducted in large enterprises with an investment value exceeding IDR 10 billion (excluding land and buildings). Again, Article 7 sets a financial threshold for foreign investments, it does not limit the percentage of foreign ownership in the e-commerce sector. 

Presidential Regulation No. 49 of 2021 
- Article 2 - All business sectors are open for investment, except for sectors that are:
(a) Closed to investment.
(b) Only to be conducted by the central government.
(1a) Open sectors include those that are commercial in nature.
- Article 6 - Business sectors with specific requirements can be undertaken by all investors, including cooperatives and SMEs, under certain conditions:
(b) Investment with restrictions on foreign ownership.
(3) Specifies that sectors with restrictions on foreign ownership include:
(a) Trade in alcoholic beverages (importers, distributors, and sub-distributors).
(b) Retail trade of alcoholic beverages.

Regulation of the Investment Coordinating Board (BKPM) No. 4 of 2021 Provides technical guidelines on implementing the provisions of Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021.
- Article 5 and Article 6 clarifies the operational requirements and investment criteria, including thresholds for small and medium enterprises (SMEs).
- Article 6(2) confirms that business fields designated for MSMEs (such as e-commerce businesses with a total investment below IDR 10 billion) are closed to foreign investors.
- Article 12(1) stipulates that foreign investment in Indonesia must be in the form of a Limited Liability Company (PT PMA) and comply with equity restrictions outlined in Presidential Regulation No. 10 of 2021.
- Article 23 elaborates on priority sectors eligible for full foreign ownership, such as large-scale e-commerce platforms (investment > IDR 10 billion).

Article 12 of BKPM Regulation No. 4 of 2021 Reiterates the 67% foreign ownership cap for telecommunication tower companies.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Director General of Taxes No.PER-12/PJ/2020 on Specific Criteria Limitations for Tax Collectors, as well as the Appointment of Tax Collectors, Collection, Remittance, and Filing of Value-Added Tax on the Utilization of Intangible Taxable Goods and/or Taxable Services from Outside the Customs Territory into the Customs Territory through Electronic Commerce 2020
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the director general of taxes no per 12 pj 2020 on specific criteria limitations for tax collectors as well as the appointment of tax collectors collection remittance and filing of value added tax on the utilization of intangible taxable goods and or taxable services from outside the customs territory into the customs territory through electronic commerce 2020
acts_norm: regulation of the director general of taxes no per 12 pj 2020 on specific criteria limitations for tax collectors as well as the appointment of tax collectors collection remittance and filing of value added tax on the utilization of intangible taxable goods and or taxable services from outside the customs territory into the customs territory through electronic commerce 2020
Indonesia.impact:
This measure does not impose restrictions on online sales and delivery. Director General of Tax Regulation No. PER-12/PJ/2020, the provision on the imposition of Value-Added Tax (VAT) on the sale of goods and services by foreign providers to Indonesian consumers should use the phrase "and/or" instead of "or" as previously stated. This means that VAT obligations apply if foreign service providers meet either or both of the following criteria: (1) minimum annual revenue of IDR 600 million from transactions in Indonesia, and/or (2) serving at least 12,000 Indonesian consumers annually.

In accordance with Article 4(b) of PER-12/PJ/2020, the criteria are also deemed satisfied if the volume of traffic or users accessing the service from Indonesia exceeds 12,000 (twelve thousand) within one year OR 1,000 (one thousand) within one month. The term "Indonesian consumers" in the prior statement must be aligned with this provision, where "traffic or users" (not merely completed transactions) serve as the benchmark. This includes all active visits/users from Indonesia, regardless of whether a transaction occurs.

Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 5 on private electronic system operators 2020 minister of communication and informatics regulation no 10 on the amendment to minister of communication and informatics regulation no 5 of 2020 on private electronic system operators 2021 ministerial regulation no 14 on electronic systems and transactions 2018

Indonesia.impact:
According Minister of Communication and Informatics Regulation No.5 of 2020, a Private ESO is defined as any person, business entity, or community that operates an electronic system that includes e-commerce platforms. The regulation requires a private ESO to register to government before operating their services.

Ministerial Regulation No. 14/2018 on Electronic Systems and Transactions: This regulation governs the electronic transactions and systems in Indonesia. All e-commerce providers must register their business and electronic systems with the Ministry of Communication and Information Technology (Kominfo).
- Article 2: Establishes that providers of electronic systems must ensure compliance with Indonesian regulations regarding their activities.
- Article 10: Requires e-commerce providers to register with Kominfo, which issues an Electronic System Operator (ESO) certification.

Indonesia, e-commerce providers are subject to various laws and regulations that require specific licenses and compliance. E-commerce businesses must obtain a Indonesian Company Registration (SIUP), a trading business license, issued by the local government. This applies to both domestic and foreign businesses operating in Indonesia. Also required a Tax Identification Number (NPWP). E-commerce businesses must also register for a Nomor Pokok Wajib Pajak (NPWP), which is required for tax purposes.

Indonesia.act: Law No.7 on Trade 2014
Indonesia.act_norm: law no 7 on trade 2014
acts_norm: law no 7 on trade 2014
Indonesia.impact:
The term ""business actor"" as referred to in Law No. 7 of 2014 pertains to those conducting business activities in the trade sector (excluding manufacturers). Business actors engaged in trade activities are required to obtain a business license in the trade sector. Article 24 requires businesses actors engaged in trading activities to obtain a business license from the relevant authority. 

Currently, the licensing process in Indonesia is integrated through the Online Single Submission (OSS) system. There is no explicit limitation on the maximum duration of up to 30 years for the validity or application of provisions.

Business licensing is also included as part of supervisory activities, as stipulated in Article 100.

Indonesia.impact:
Government Regulation No. 80 of 2019 on E-Commerce
- Article 13 E-commerce businesses are required to provide accurate information about their legal status, supported by verifiable documentation such as the Business Identification Number (NIB). The Article 13 does not only concern legal identity, but also includes information on goods and/or services as well as provisions on advertising ethics.
- Article 14 PPMSE (E-Commerce Platform Providers) platforms that host e-commerce activities must ensure their electronic systems have a Certificate of Feasibility (Sertifikat Kelaikan Sistem Elektronik).
- Article 15, Paragraph (1) mandates that any business engaging in electronic trading must obtain a business license, such as SIUP or an equivalent license under the Online Single Submission (OSS) system. The business license referred to in Article 15 paragraph (1) is categorized based on the level of business risk in accordance with Government Regulation No. 5 of 2021, where micro and small enterprises are only required to obtain a Business Identification Number (NIB), while medium and large-scale enterprises must obtain both an NIB and an E-Commerce Trade Business License (SIUPMSE). All licensing processes are conducted through the OSS system.
- Article 15, Paragraph (3): The application for a business license must be conducted via the OSS system, which integrates the registration process with risk-based licensing requirements

Minister of Trade Regulation No.31. Note that Minister of Trade Regulation No. 50 of 2020 has been repealed and replaced by Minister of Trade Regulation No. 31 of 2023.
- Business Actors are required to have a Business License in conducting PMSE business activities.

Similarly, Article 2 of Law No. 6 of 1983 on General Provisions and Taxation Procedures (as amended by Law No. 7 of 2021) requires all entities engaged in economic activities, including e-commerce businesses, to register for a Tax Identification Number (NPWP).

Law No. 7 of 2011 on Currency requires that all transactions within Indonesia must use Rupiah (IDR). This applies to both cash and non-cash transactions, including payments and settlements of financial obligations.
- Article 21: This article mandates that Rupiah (IDR) must be used for all transactions within Indonesia, including cash and non-cash transactions, except for certain cross-border transactions.
- Article 23: Prohibits rejecting Rupiah for settlement of obligations within Indonesia. This establishes the primacy of Rupiah in the domestic economy.

Indonesia.impact:
Regulation 80/2019 on E-Commerce (PP 80/2019)
- Article 9(2) states that international e-commerce transactions must comply with regulations governing exports and imports, and regulations in the field of information and electronic transactions. 
- Article 15: E-commerce operators must have a clear legal identity and comply with local financial regulations, including collaborating with licensed payment system providers (typically requiring a local bank account for the processing of payments and tax compliance).
- Article 16: Foreign e-commerce providers must comply with Indonesian tax regulations, which involve using Indonesian payment gateways and banking systems for local transactions
- Article 60: This article specifies that operators of Trade Through Electronic Systems (PPMSE) are required to make transactions in Rupiah currency as a means of payment and shall be in accordance with the provisions of laws and regulations.

Regulation 50/2020 on E-Commerce implementing regulation that complements and builds upon Regulation 80/2019 specifies that the currency used for payments must comply with Indonesia’s financial regulations, implying that transactions are expected to use Rupiah (IDR) within the country unless exceptions apply to international trade.

Indonesia.act: Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 18/40/PBI/2016 on the Implementation of Payment Systems
Indonesia.act_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 18 40 pbi 2016 on the implementation of payment systems
acts_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 18 40 pbi 2016 on the implementation of payment systems
Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia’s payment security standards, especially those outlined by Bank Indonesia, are aligned with international standards, such as ISO 27001 for information security management and PCI-DSS for card payment data security.

Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 18/40/PBI/2016 on the Implementation of Payment Systems sets out the requirements for the operation and security of payment systems in Indonesia:
- Article 7 requires payment system operators to implement security measures to ensure that payment services are secure and reliable, focusing on the integrity and confidentiality of payment data. The regulation also includes provisions for the authentication of users and data encryption, which align with global security standards like PCI-DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard) but are customized for local implementation.

Indonesia.act: Bank Indonesia Regulation No.20/6/PBI/2018 on Electronic Money 2018
Indonesia.act_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 20 6 pbi 2018 on electronic money 2018
acts_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 20 6 pbi 2018 on electronic money 2018
Indonesia.impact:
Regulation No. 11/12/PBI/2009 last amended by the Bank Indonesia Regulation No.20/6/PBI/2018 requires that any e-money issuer must be licensed by Indonesia's Central Bank. In addition, the Bank Indonesia Regulation No.20/6/PBI/2018 added the maximum amount of stored e-money which is IDR 2 million for unregistered e-money and IDR 10 million for registered e-money.

Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 20/6/PBI/2018 on Electronic Money regulation governs electronic payments and e-wallet services in Indonesia. E-commerce providers offering digital payment services must comply with Bank Indonesia’s requirements for electronic money issuance and usage. 
- Article 4: Stipulates that electronic money issuers must obtain a license from Bank Indonesia.
- Article 9: Specifies minimum capital requirements for non-bank e-money issuers, which are linked to the value of managed floating funds. E-money issuers managing a floating fund of IDR 1 billion or more are required to obtain a license from Bank Indonesia. This applies to both closed-loop and open-loop e-money systems. Non-bank issuers managing significant floating amounts must have a minimum paid-up capital of IDR 3 billion. If the floating funds exceed IDR 9 billion, the issuer must increase their capital to IDR 10 billion plus 3% of the total floating fund. For unregistered users, the maximum floating fund per user is capped at IDR 2 million, For registered users, the cap is IDR 10 million.

Article 82: Entities conducting e-money operations prior to the regulation’s enactment (May 4, 2018) were required to apply for a license within six months of the regulation taking effect. Foreign e-commerce businesses operating in Indonesia are generally subject to the same requirements if they act as e-money issuers or integrate payment systems and must obtain a license if they issue e-money or provide similar services within Indonesia. Articles 8 and 10 specify the capital and Ownership Restrictions. At least 51% of the shares in a non-bank e-money issuer must be owned by Indonesian individuals or entities, with a maximum of 49% foreign ownership.

Indonesia.act: Bank Indonesia Regulation No.20/6/PBI/2018 on Electronic Money 2018
Indonesia.act_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 20 6 pbi 2018 on electronic money 2018
acts_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 20 6 pbi 2018 on electronic money 2018
Indonesia.impact:
Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 20/6/PBI/2018 on Electronic Money regulation Article 45 specifies limits on the value of electronic money that can be stored and transacted. For instance:
- Unregistered electronic money can hold a maximum value of IDR 2,000,000 (approximately 130 USD). 
- Registered electronic money can hold a maximum value of IDR 10,000,000 (approximately 650 USD). 

Note that Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 11/12/PBI/2009, last amended by Bank Indonesia Regulation No.20/6/PBI/2018 regarding E-money.

acts_norm: bank indonesia regulation no 20 6 pbi 2018 on electronic money 2018
regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 80 on trading through the electronic system 2019
Indonesia.impact:
There are no explicit mandate for the use of specific intermediaries for online payments in Indonesia. However, the requirement to work with authorized payment system providers is emphasized in several regulations, particularly for compliance with security, consumer protection, and licensing standards. 

Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 20/6/PBI/2018 on Electronic Money:
- Article 5 states that payment service providers, including electronic money issuers, must obtain authorization from Bank Indonesia. This implies that businesses using electronic money or payment systems must work with authorized providers (e.g., acquirers, switchers, and clearing operators).
- Article 7 requires licensed issuers to ensure their systems are secure and integrated with Indonesia’s domestic payment processing networks.

Government Regulation No. 80/2019 on E-Commerce:
- Article 60 requires all e-commerce payment systems to comply with Indonesian regulations, indirectly mandating collaboration with authorized local payment intermediaries.
