Indonesia.impact:
Bank Indonesia Regulation No. 23/6/PBI/2021
Article 19 and Article 20 of the regulation stipulate that for Non-Bank Institutions applying for a Payment Service Provider (PJP) license, at least 15% of the shares must be owned by Indonesian citizens or Indonesian legal entities (Article 19).  Furthermore, at least 51% of the voting rights must be held by domestic parties (Indonesian citizens or legal entities) (Article 20).  The regulation also allows Bank Indonesia to set further policies regarding ownership composition, considering factors like materiality and national interest.

regulation of the minister of finance regulation no 17 pmk 010 2018 on the second amendment to minister of finance regulation number 6 pmk 010 2017 on the establishment of the goods classification system and imposition of import duty tariffs on imported goods 2018
Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia has not officially imposed custom duties on electronic transmissions such as software, multimedia, or other digital goods. However, the country has actively participated in discussions at the World Trade Organization (WTO) about ending the global moratorium on customs duties for electronic transmissions, which has been renewed regularly since 1998.

Regulation of the Ministry of Finance No. 17/PMK.010/2018 in Indonesia aligns with the WTO moratorium on customs duties for electronic transmissions, which prohibits imposing tariffs on cross-border digital goods and services. The regulation introduces Chapter 99 in the Indonesian Customs Tariff Book, which classifies intangible goods like software and other digital products under specific HS codes. However, these goods are explicitly subject to a 0% import duty rate, meaning no customs duties are imposed on electronic transmissions. This classification is more about administrative clarity and reporting rather than imposing duties.
Indonesia.timeframe:
Since March 2022;

Since February 2018, last amended in February 2022

Indonesia.impact:
Under the Regulation No. 23 of 2013, Indoesia Internet Domain Name Registry (PANDI) has overseen the registration of .id domain. 
Article 39 
1.State institutions and State-owned enterprises must use Indonesian Top-Level Domains.
2. Businesses and organizations domiciled in Indonesia with non-Indonesian Top-Level Domains must prioritize using Indonesian Top-Level Domains.
3. Foreign companies operating in Indonesia with electronic systems and non-Indonesian Top-Level Domains must also prioritize using Indonesian Top-Level Domains.
4. Users may manage subdomains as needed in compliance with laws and regulations.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Government of the Republic of Indonesia No.80 on Trading Through the Electronic System (E-commerce Law) 2019
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 80 on trading through the electronic system 2019
acts_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 80 on trading through the electronic system 2019
Indonesia.impact:
There is no explicit mandate in Indonesian law or regulations requiring the use of a .id domain name for foreign or domestic e-commerce businesses. However, the Government Regulation No.80/2019 encourages its use as part of creating a local digital presence.

Under the Regulation No.80/2019, e-commerce law states that subject to certain thresholds, foreign business actors that actively conduct e-commerce activities with consumers in Indonesia are deemed as physically present in Indonesia and conducting fixed business activities in Indonesia. These thresholds are related to (i) number of transactions, (ii) transaction value, (iii) number of shipped packages, and/or (iv) volume of traffic or number of users. A foreign business actor that meets any of the thresholds must appoint an Indonesian representative that can act on its behalf.

The regulation includes provisions related to the use of local domain names. Specifically, Article 21 of Government Regulation No. 80 of 2019 mandates the prioritization of using Indonesia’s top-level domain name (".id") for Electronic Systems in the form of websites. It also establishes sanctions for violations of this obligation, which may include: written warnings; inclusion in the priority monitoring list; inclusion in the blacklist; temporary blocking of domestic and/or foreign PPMSE services by the relevant authorized agency; and/or revocation of business licenses. 

Article 7 specifies that foreign electronic system operators that meet certain criteria (such as transaction volume or user base) are considered to have a physical presence in Indonesia. These operators are required to appoint a local representative who can act on their behalf within the country.The specific criteria for foreign PPMSEs, as referred to in Article 18 of Minister of Trade Regulation No. 31 of 2023, include: (a) having conducted transactions with at least 1,000 consumers within a one-year period; (b) having delivered at least 1,000 packages to consumers within a one-year period; and/or (c) having at least 1% of domestic internet users accessing their platform within a one-year period.

Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 5 on private electronic system operators 2020 minister of communication and informatics regulation no 10 on the amendment to minister of communication and informatics regulation no 5 of 2020 on private electronic system operators 2021

Indonesia.impact: According Minister of Communication and InformaticsRegulation No. 5 of 2020, e-commerce platforms and digital service providers are encouraged to use Indonesian top-level domain names (TLDs), such as .id, as part of their registration and compliance with local regulations. Article 2 indicates that businesses operating electronic systems in Indonesia must ensure they are registered and comply with local guidelines, which include the registration of a local domain name when operating locally.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Trade No.31 on Business Licensing, Advertising, Guidance and Supervision of Business Actors in Trading Through Electronic Systems 2023
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 31 on business licensing advertising guidance and supervision of business actors in trading through electronic systems 2023
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 31 on business licensing advertising guidance and supervision of business actors in trading through electronic systems 2023
Indonesia.impact:
Ministry of Trade Regulation No. 31 of 2023
Foreign e-commerce platforms must comply with certain operational requirements. For instance, under the Ministry of Trade Regulation No. 31 of 2023, foreign e-commerce operators that meet specific criteria, such as transacting with at least 1,000 consumers or delivering at least 1,000 packages annually, must establish a representative office in Indonesia. Additionally, if a foreign platform's traffic constitutes at least 1% of total local internet traffic, they are also required to set up a representative office.

Article 18
(1) Foreign e-commerce operators (PPMSE) that meet certain criteria must appoint a representative located within the jurisdiction of the Republic of Indonesia.
(2) The specific criteria for foreign PPMSE include:
- Having transacted with at least 1,000 consumers within a one-year period.
- Having delivered at least 1,000 packages to consumers within a one-year period.
- Having a traffic or user base that constitutes at least 1% of the total domestic internet users within a one-year period.

Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 71 on the provision of electronic system and transaction 2019 regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 5 on private electronic system operators 2020
acts_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 71 on the provision of electronic system and transaction 2019
regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 5 on private electronic system operators 2020
Indonesia.impact:
The Minister of Communication and Informatics Regulation No. 5 of 2020, as amended by the The Minister of Communication and Informatics Regulation No. 10 of 2021 on Private Electronic System Operators mandates foreign Private Electronic System Operators (ESOs) to register their businesses to the relevant ministry through the online single submission system. ESOs should also appoint liaison officers, who have to be domiciled in Indonesia. The duty of the liaison officer is to facilitate any access request by government authorities and takedown request. According to the regulation, ESOs are persons, business entities, or communities that operate an electronic system. ESOs include electronic system operators that are supervised by ministers or institutions in accordance with laws and regulations, and electronic system operators that have an online portal, site, or application through the internet. The requirement was first enacted with the Government Regulation No. 71/2019 regarding the Provision of Electronic System and Transaction.

For foreign electronic system operators, Article 4 (MOCI 5/2020) outlines that they must register if they provide services in Indonesia, conduct business in Indonesia, or have systems used or offered in Indonesia. This implies a need for some form of local presence, but it does not specify the requirement for a physical office or other commercial presence beyond registration.

Indonesia.act:
Regulation of the Minister of Communication and Informatics of the Republic of Indonesia No.5 on Private Electronic System Operators 2020;

Minister of Communication and Informatics Regulation No. 10 on the Amendment to Minister of Communication and Informatics Regulation No. 5 of 2020 on Private Electronic System Operators 2021
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 5 on private electronic system operators 2020 minister of communication and informatics regulation no 10 on the amendment to minister of communication and informatics regulation no 5 of 2020 on private electronic system operators 2021
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of communication and informatics of the republic of indonesia no 5 on private electronic system operators 2020
minister of communication and informatics regulation no 10 on the amendment to minister of communication and informatics regulation no 5 of 2020 on private electronic system operators 2021
Indonesia.impact:
Digital service providers such as social media platforms, electronic commerce providers, and over-the-top (OTT) media services are required to register with the Ministry of Communication and Information under Article 2 of MOCI 5/2020. Additionally, these entities must establish a representative office in Indonesia.

For foreign electronic system operators, Article 4 (MOCI 5/2020) outlines that they must register if they provide services in Indonesia, conduct business in Indonesia, or have systems used or offered in Indonesia. This implies a need for some form of local presence, but it does not specify the requirement for a physical office or other commercial presence beyond registration.

While MOCI Regulation No. 5/2020 required foreign PSEs to register and implied the need for a local presence, Regulation No. 10/2021 did not explicitly mandate the establishment of a physical representative office in Indonesia. Instead, the focus is now on mandatory registration with the Ministry of Communication and Informatics (Kominfo) through the OSS RBA system and compliance with Indonesian laws, such as content moderation and taxation. There is no explicit requirement for a physical office or representative office in Indonesia under the updated regulations.

Note that Minister of Communication and Informatics Regulation No. 5/2020, last amended by the Indonesia Minister of Communication and Informatics Regulation No. 10/2021.

Indonesia.act: Law No.8 on Consumer Protection 1999
Indonesia.act_norm: law no 8 on consumer protection 1999
acts_norm: law no 8 on consumer protection 1999
Indonesia.impact:
Indonesia's Consumer Protection Law No. 8 is a comprehensive framework aimed at protecting consumer rights, ensuring transparency, and mandating the safety and quality of goods and services in electronic commerce. However, it does not raise specific e-commerce or distance sales issues. The Law forms the foundation of consumer protection in Indonesia.
- Article 4 enumerates consumer rights, including the right to safety, accurate information, and compensation for damages.
- Article 7 imposes obligations on businesses to provide clear, truthful information about goods/services and to guarantee their safety.
- Article 8 prohibits businesses from offering goods/services that fail to meet safety, quality, or labeling standards.

It is important to note that the Consumer Protection Law is the legal basis for the implementation of consumer protection in Indonesia, although it does not specifically regulate online consumer protection.

Indonesia.act_norm: law no 11 on information and electronic transactions 2008 law no 19 on amendments to law no 11 of 2008 on electronic information and transactions 2016 law no 1 on the second amendment to law no 11 of 2008 on electronic information and transactions 2024
acts_norm: law no 11 on information and electronic transactions 2008
law no 19 on amendments to law no 11 of 2008 on electronic information and transactions 2016
law no 1 on the second amendment to law no 11 of 2008 on electronic information and transactions 2024
Indonesia.impact:
Law (Law) No. 19 of 2016, Amendments to Law Number 11 of 2008 concerning Information and Electronic Transactions addresses digital and electronic transactions:
- Article 9 ensures consumer protection in electronic contracts, mandating transparency and clarity in terms and conditions.
- Article 18 governs the validity of electronic contracts, emphasizing fairness and requiring accessibility to contract information by consumers.

Moreover, the mechanism for regulating electronic contracts can also be found in Chapter X of Government Regulation No. 80 of 2019.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Government of the Republic of Indonesia No.80 on Trading Through the Electronic System (E-commerce Law) 2019
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 80 on trading through the electronic system 2019
acts_norm: regulation of the government of the republic of indonesia no 80 on trading through the electronic system 2019
Indonesia.impact:
Government Regulation No. 80 of 2019 explicitly stipulates that foreign Electronic System Operators in Trade (PPMSE) that meet certain criteria are deemed to have a physical presence in Indonesia and are therefore required to appoint a representative domiciled in Indonesia:
●	The obligation to provide consumer complaint services is stipulated in Article 27. 
●	The obligation to display clear and accurate product information is regulated in Article 13.
●	Article 53 states that an electronic contract must include information on the procedure for returning goods and/or services in case of any discrepancies between what was received and what was agreed upon. However, the mechanism that is explicitly detailed pertains to the refund process, as outlined in Article 71.
●	Article 32 relates to Electronic Advertising, the provisions concerning consumer rights in Electronic System-Based Trading (PPMSE) are found in Article 26. This article requires Business Actors to protect consumer rights in accordance with the prevailing laws and regulations on consumer protection;
●	One of the key consumer rights under the Consumer Protection Law (UUPK) is the right to receive compensation, reimbursement, and/or replacement if the goods and/or services received do not match the agreement or are not delivered as promised.

In addition, PPMSE conducting business activities in the context of electronic commerce (PMSE) are obligated to comply with business licensing requirements in accordance with the prevailing laws and regulations.

Furthermore, Minister of Trade Regulation No. 31 of 2023 serves as a regulatory framework for the governance of PMSE, aiming to establish a healthy digital trade ecosystem and ensure consumer protection, while taking into account the ever-evolving nature of PMSE business models.

It is important to note that Regulation 31/2023 does not stand alone, but rather refers to and aligns with other relevant laws and regulations, including those governing goods distribution, electronic system operators, and others.

Indonesia.act: Regulation of the Minister of Trade No.31 on Business Licensing, Advertising, Guidance and Supervision of Business Actors in Trading Through Electronic Systems 2023
Indonesia.act_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 31 on business licensing advertising guidance and supervision of business actors in trading through electronic systems 2023
acts_norm: regulation of the minister of trade no 31 on business licensing advertising guidance and supervision of business actors in trading through electronic systems 2023

The Regulation of the Minister of Trade No.50/2020 is no longer valid, replaced by Regulation of the Minister of Trade Number 31 of 2023 concerning Business Licensing, Advertising, Guidance and Supervision of Business Actors in Trading Through Electronic Systems. 

Advertising is regulated in Chapter IV Electronic Advertising (articles 23-31). The obligation to have a complaint service is regulated in Article 9.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Antidumping and Countervailing Measures No. 65/NA 2019
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on antidumping and countervailing measures no 65 na 2019
acts_norm: law on antidumping and countervailing measures no 65 na 2019
Lao PDR.impact: Lao PDR currently does not engage in any form of antidumping, countervailing duties, and safeguard practices on ICT products. However, on 14 June 2019, Lao PDR  adopted the Law on Antidumping and Countervailing measures which would allow the country to raise tariffs or restrict imports to counteract dumping or subsidies (active 15 days after publication). On 15 November 2019, Lao PDR notified the Law to the WTO.

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on public procurement no 30 na 2017 instruction of implementation of law on public procurement no 0477 mof 2019 the ministry of finance regulations implementing rules and regulations on government procurement of goods construction repairs and services no 01639 mof 1998
acts_norm: law on public procurement no 30 na 2017
instruction of implementation of law on public procurement no 0477 mof 2019
the ministry of finance regulations implementing rules and regulations on government procurement of goods construction repairs and services no 01639 mof 1998
Lao PDR.impact:
Lao PDR do not specify any explicit exclusions for foreign entities in the procurement of ICT goods and digital services. Instead, Lao PDR Law on Public Procurement No.30/NA 2017 framework emphasizes fair and open bidding (Article 19), with international cooperation (Article 7) encouraged and participation allowed for both local and foreign entities (Articles 4 and 5). The methods described include open bidding and limited bidding that do not restrict foreign competition, unless national security concerns apply (Article 6).

Article 3 of the Ministry of Finance Regulations Implementing Rules and Regulations on Government Procurement of Goods, Construction, Repairs, and Services (No. 01639/MOF) clarifies that the implementing rules and regulations do not apply to contracts for goods or services declared secret or requiring special security measures to protect state interests. Article 1 of the regulation also emphasizes transparency, regularity, and uniformity in public procurement, guaranteeing equal access for suppliers and contractors.

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Public Procurement No.30/NA 2017;

Instruction of Implementation of Law on Public Procurement No.0477/MOF 2019
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on public procurement no 30 na 2017 instruction of implementation of law on public procurement no 0477 mof 2019
acts_norm: law on public procurement no 30 na 2017
instruction of implementation of law on public procurement no 0477 mof 2019
Lao PDR.impact:
The Public Procurement Law stipulates that public procurement contracts shall consist of the principle contents, such as names of the contracting parties, purpose, price, scope, rights and obligations, perform customs, etc. (See Article 49). The scope can include software. However, no requirement to surrender patents, source codes or trade secrets, or use specific encryption in order to win tenders is found.

There is no explicit requirement for bidders to surrender patents, source codes, or trade secrets as a condition for participating in tenders. However, the Public Procurement Law (Article 15) mentions that "The information required by this Article shall be limited to the subject of the contract; the procuring entity shall protect the technical or trade secrets of the bidders disclosed during this procedure". This indicates a clear stance on maintaining the confidentiality of sensitive bidder information, without requiring disclosure beyond what is necessary for the evaluation. There is no specific mention of encryption requirements in any of the tender-related documents.

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Public Procurement No.30/NA 2017;

Instruction of Implementation of Law on Public Procurement No.0477/MOF 2019
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on public procurement no 30 na 2017 instruction of implementation of law on public procurement no 0477 mof 2019
acts_norm: law on public procurement no 30 na 2017
instruction of implementation of law on public procurement no 0477 mof 2019
Lao PDR.impact:
The Public Procurement Law explicitly states that contractors, suppliers or consultants meeting all conditions shall be given domestic preference and considered to be awarded the contracts. Conditions for domestic preference are set out in separate regulations (Article 45 domestic preference). The domestic preference shall apply for international competitive bidding only. 

According to the Instruction of Implementation of Law on Public Procurement No.0477/MOF (Clause 14):
1. For works and maintenance: If a local bidder's bid price is not exceed 7.5% of an international bidder's bid price, the local bidder will be considered as the winning bidder for works. 
2. Supply of goods: Preference is given to goods manufactured in Lao PDR to promote domestically manufactured goods. Bidding documents shall clearly indicate any preference to be granted to domestically manufactured goods indicating production-base (origin) and the information required to establish the eligibility of a bid for such preference. The nationality of the manufacturer or supplier is not a condition for such eligibility.

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010 notification of the list of business activities for foreign investors no 1327 moic erm 2015 decree on approval the list of controlled and concession businesses 2019
acts_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010
notification of the list of business activities for foreign investors no 1327 moic erm 2015
decree on approval the list of controlled and concession businesses 2019
Lao PDR.impact:
Under the Investment Promotion Act, both domestic and foreign investors have the right to invest in all investment sectors and areas which are not prohibited by the law of the Lao PDR (Articles 67.1 and 3). The Act introduces a controlled business list, including businesses that are sensitive to national security, public order, national fine tradition, and socio-environmental impact. There is no foreign investment ownership restrictions in sectors relevant to the digital trade. 

Moreover, Law on Investment Promotion Article 4 (Government policies on investment promotion), the Government promotes investment in all sectors, business operations and in areas throughout the country except for the areas and business operations which are detrimental to national security, natural environment, at present and in long-term, public health and fine national cultures. Article 40 (Term of Investment for general investment), investment in general investments has no term limit except for those activities of which term limits have been specified by regulations of relevant sector authority.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Investment Promotion Act No.14/NA 2010
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010
acts_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010
Lao PDR.impact: There is no requirement mandated that the foreign investors shall engage in joint venture. Article 28 (Joint venture between domestic and foreign investors) of the Act provides that A joint venture between domestic and foreign investors is a joint investment between domestic and foreign investors who conduct business operations, share ownership and establish a new legal entity under the laws of Lao PDR. Foreign investor(s) in the joint venture shall contribute at least ten percent (10%) of the total capital.

Lao PDR.act: Enterprise Law No. 46/NA 2013
Lao PDR.act_norm: enterprise law no 46 na 2013
acts_norm: enterprise law no 46 na 2013
Lao PDR.impact: The Enterprise Law Articles 34 (Manager) and 121-137 under Section D (Director and board of directors of a limited company) does not restrict the number of foreigners that can sit on the board of directors of a company or managers established in the Lao PDR (See Healy Consultants and DFDL articles in notes).

Lao PDR.act: Law on Investment Promotion Act No.14/NA 2010
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010
acts_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Investment Promotion introduces a national security screening. Article 4 states that foreigners may invest in any sector or business except those that the government deems to be detrimental to national security, health, or national traditions, or to harm the natural environment at present and in long-term, public health and fine national cultures. Article 40 (Term of Investment for general investment), Investment in general investments has no term limit except for those activities of which term limits have been specified by regulations of relevant sector authority.

Moreover, Article 29 (Amended) (Business Cooperation by Contract), business cooperation by contract is a joint business arrangement between domestic and foreign legal entities including public and private parties through a business cooperation contract. Domestic entity entering into a business cooperation contract shall inform the investment one-stop service office for approval and supervision according to relevant regulations and shall subject to notarization by the relevant notary public of Lao PDR where the contract is performed. Thus a restriction if engaging in Contract based joint venture.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Business Competition No. 60/NA 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on business competition no 60 na 2015
acts_norm: law on business competition no 60 na 2015
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Business Competition Article 39 establishes a general mergers and acquisitions (and joint-ventures) review regime. Specially, for those combination that could result in holding a market share above the threshold defined by the Competition Commission, restricting market access or technological development and negatively impacting consumers or socio-economic development require approval from the Competition Commission​.

The exact threshold for market share that would trigger the need for Competition Commission approval is not explicitly defined. Instead, it is left to the discretion of the Competition Commission to periodically determine and set this threshold.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Investment Promotion Act No.14/NA 2010
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010
acts_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010
Lao PDR.impact:
According to the Law on Investment Promotion Act Article 41 (Amended) (Concession investments),  concession investment is an investment for which an investor is authorized by the Government in accordance with relevant laws and regulations to develop and operate a business notably concession of land, development of special economic zones, zones for industrial processing for export, mining, electric energy development, aviation and telecommunication. Therefore, foreign investors cannot own land but can only lease it, which is a national treatment. Additionally, ISIC 6110, 6120 and 6130 are related to telecomminucation services. These sectors have to receive an investment license prior, thus a screening requirement for this sector.

Article 2 and Article 3 of the Decree describe the screening mechanisms for investments in Lao PDR. Investments in sectors listed as controlled, which impact national security, social stability, and the environment, require screening and approval by relevant agencies, facilitated by a one-stop service unit that collaborates with the committee for promotion and management of investment. For concession investments, such as land concessions and the development of special economic zones, the approval process is extended, with screening taking up to 65 working days. These mechanisms aim to ensure that investments align with national priorities, excluding those that threaten stability, security, or the environment. While the government promotes investment across various sectors, businesses that could negatively impact national stability or violate other regulations are excluded from this promotion.

The Telecommunications Law does not explicitly ban or impose direct foreign equity limits for telecommunications businesses (nor in the FDI as discussed in the FDI Pillar). Article 4 of the Telecommunications Law encourages domestic and foreign individuals or entities to invest in the telecommunications sector, indicating that foreign investments are allowed under the licensing system guidelines. Foreign entities must adhere to investment and enterprise operations laws, including the requirement to apply for a telecommunications business license.

Lao PDR.act: Decree on Approval the List of Controlled and Concession Businesses No.30/PM 2019
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on approval the list of controlled and concession businesses no 30 pm 2019
acts_norm: decree on approval the list of controlled and concession businesses no 30 pm 2019
Lao PDR.impact: Decree on Controlled Business List and Concession Activity No. 03/PM (2019): Certain online activities (such as VPN) that could impact national security, social order, or the environment are categorized under the controlled business list. These businesses require special approval from relevant ministries and the central or local investment committee before operating (Article 2). For businesses in this category, the investment approval process involves submitting a proposal to the central or local One-Stop Service Unit, which forwards the request for review and approval by the committee for promotion and management of investments (Article 3).

Lao PDR.act: Enterprise Law No.46/NA 2013
Lao PDR.act_norm: enterprise law no 46 na 2013
acts_norm: enterprise law no 46 na 2013
Lao PDR.impact: There is no regulation that mandates commercial presence in Lao PDR for cross-border provision of services.

Lao PDR.act: Decision on Enterprise Registration No.0023/MOIC.DERM 2019
Lao PDR.act_norm: decision on enterprise registration no 0023 moic derm 2019
acts_norm: decision on enterprise registration no 0023 moic derm 2019
Lao PDR.impact:
There are no explicit commercial presence requirements specified in the provided files for offering cross-border services in sectors such as ICT, e-commerce, or software development.

However, as a general principle, according to the Enterprise Registration (Article 5) and the Law on Investment Promotion, businesses operating in Lao PDR, including foreign entities, are typically required to have a registered business entity within the country, even for sectors that are not on the controlled business list.

Lao PDR.act: Decree on Satellite Communications No. 471/GOL 2019
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on satellite communications no 471 gol 2019
acts_norm: decree on satellite communications no 471 gol 2019
Lao PDR.impact:
Article 42 of the Decree on Satellite Communications No. 471/GOL (2019), the conditions for operating a satellite communication business include:

	1.	Having an enterprise registration certificate.
	2.	Maintaining a physical office.
	3.	Having a stable financial position.
	4.	Employing personnel with appropriate knowledge and abilities for the specific type of business.
	5.	Having a business plan.
	6.	Providing after-sales services to customers.
	7.	Obtaining the right to provide satellite communications services for foreign satellites.

These conditions create a local commercial requirement, as they necessitate the establishment of a registered business entity with a local office, employing qualified personnel, and meeting specific operational conditions. This essentially means that foreign or domestic businesses must maintain a physical and commercial presence in Lao PDR, thus supporting the local economy through investment, infrastructure, and employment.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Intellectual Property Right No. 50/NA 2023
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
acts_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
Lao PDR.impact:
Since January 2024, the Law on Intellectual Property Right No.50/NA replaces the Law on Intellectual Property No. 38/NA, dated 15 November 2017 (Article 141 Implementation). 

Article 6 (The Scope of Application of the Law) provides that this law is applicable to domestic and foreign individuals, legal entities and organizations who are implementing and involving in intellectual property activities in the Lao PDR.

Moreover, Article 27 (revised) (Filing of Applications) states that domestic or foreign individuals, legal entities or organizations may apply for registration of their industrial property with the Minsitry of Industry and Commerce or with an international intellectual property registration organization to which the Lao PDR is a party. Any individual, legal entity or organization residing in a foreign country wishing to apply for industrial property registration shall have a business premise or an authorized representative in the Lao PDR. The applicant without a business premise or residence in the Lao PDR shall appoint an authorized representative in the Lao PDR to carry out transactions related to intellectual property in the Lao PDR.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Intellectual Property Right No. 50/NA 2023
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
acts_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
Lao PDR.impact:
Since January 2024, the Law on Intellectual Property Right No.50/NA replaces the Law on Intellectual Property No. 38/NA, dated 15 November 2017 (Article 141 Implementation). 

Under Chapter 4 Rights and obligations of Industrial Property Rights Owner, Article 47(5) (Rights of the Industrial Property Right Owner), and Article 56 (Term of Protection of Patents) grant the Patent owners the right to file lawsuits for patent infringement, seek damages, and prevent unauthorized use of their patents. They may take legal action to protect their rights and request compensation for violations.

Article 84 (revised) (Provisional Protection) grants Provisional Protection during the period between the publication of the application for the grant of a breeder’s right and the grant of that right. These measures aim to prevent further harm while grant rights are ongoing. Additionally, Article 137 (revised) (Inspection of Intellectual Property at Border Checkpoints) stipulates that In order to intercept intellectual property violations, customs officers assigned to border checkpoints have the rights by ex-officio to inspect exported and imported goods, seize and impound goods which violate trademark, copyright and related right of the law.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Intellectual Property Right No. 50/NA 2023
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
acts_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
Lao PDR.impact:
Since January 2024, the Law on Intellectual Property Right No.50/NA replaces the Law on Intellectual Property No. 38/NA, dated 15 November 2017 (Article 141 Implementation). 

There is no restriction on patent enforcement. The law protect a patent owner for both a product or a process to prevent other from exploit the right and against infringement (Article 56 revised regarding term of protection of patents).

Article 64 (Authorization to Exploit without Permission of Patent or Petty Patent Owner): In cases of national emergency, public health crises, or critical public interest (e.g., defense or food security), the government can authorize the exploitation of a patent without the owner's permission. This applies across sectors and circumstances, allowing wide-reaching use of patented inventions to serve public needs. Such a measure could affect various industries, especially during crises like epidemics, where multiple patents related to pharmaceuticals might be impacted. Under this article, it is important to note that compensation is possible and is fair ("right holder shall have the right to propose terms for compensation, how it shall be determined, and terms for payment thereof").

Lao PDR.act: Law on Intellectual Property Right No. 50/NA 2023
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
acts_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Intellectual Property Right No. 50/NA 2023;

Penal Code No. 26/NA 2017
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023 penal code no 26 na 2017
acts_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
penal code no 26 na 2017
Lao PDR.impact:
Under the Act, Article 123 (revised Copyright Piracy), 'copyright piracy' refers to a copy of a work of authorship or object of related rights where a copy is made without the consent of the right holder or person duly authorized by the right holder in the country of production, shall be a violation of this law. 

Chapter 4 of the Intellectual Property Right Act introduces rights of the copyright owners. Article 102 prescribes that no individual, legal entity or organization other than the author shall undertake any of the acts described in Article 100 (moral rights), such as the rights to make a collection of such works, reproduce such work, or tranlsation of such works.

Moreover, the Penal Code regulates the criminal charges for infringers and the punishments and fines relating to IP infringement (fines and punishment are mainly related to cybercrime offense such as pornographic content dissemination or financial identity theft). 

The following apply broadly to all forms of intellectual property rights, including not only patents but also copyrights and potentially other types of IP rights. Articles 47(5) and 56 grant all IP rights holders, including patent and copyright owners, the ability to file lawsuits for infringement, seek damages, and prevent unauthorized use of their rights. Legal action can be pursued to protect their IP and request compensation for any violations. Articles 44 and 45 allow administrative actions to terminate or invalidate any IP rights, including patents and copyrights, in cases where the rights are not properly maintained or do not meet required standards, and also allow third parties to request the cancellation of improperly granted IP rights. Article 84 provides provisional protection for all IP rights, including copyrights, during the application process, preventing harm before the rights are fully granted. Additionally, Article 144 allows individuals or entities to file complaints for provisional measures to prevent IP infringement, prevent goods from entering commerce, and preserve evidence of alleged infringements, which includes measures for copyright protection.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Intellectual Property Right No. 50/NA 2023
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
acts_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Intellectual Property Rights Article 20 (Trade Secrets) provides that a trade secret is a secret information which cannot be disclosed regarding to formula, production process or any information which has commercial value in the sense
that it is not known among or readily accessible to persons within the circles that normally deal with the kind of information in question.

Article 61 (Rights of Proprietor of Trade Secret) states that the proprietor has the right to prevent the unauthorized disclosure, acquisition, or use of the trade secret by others without consent, provided such actions are contrary to honest commercial practices. However, exceptions apply, such as when the information is obtained through reverse engineering, laboratory testing, or other lawful means without a confidentiality obligation. The proprietor also has the right to take legal action and claim compensation for damages resulting from infringements, as well as to prevent individuals, legal entities, or organizations from misappropriating the trade secret. Additionally, the proprietor may disclose, withdraw, utilize, or transfer the trade secret to others, setting conditions for maintaining confidentiality. Finally, the proprietor can enforce confidentiality obligations on individuals lawfully in control of the trade secret, with such obligations continuing beyond the termination of employment or contractual relationships, as long as the information remains confidential.

Note that the exceptions are the legitimate means by which third parties may lawfully obtain or use trade secret information without it being considered a violation of trade secret rights. These exceptions are not about mandatory disclosure of trade secrets.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Intellectual Property Right No. 50/NA 2023
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
acts_norm: law on intellectual property right no 50 na 2023
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Intellectual Property Rights Article 20 (Trade Secrets) provides that a trade secret is a secret information which cannot be disclosed regarding to formula, production process or any information which has commercial value in the sense
that it is not known among or readily accessible to persons within the circles that normally deal with the kind of information in question.

Article 61 (Rights of Proprietor of Trade Secret) states that the proprietor has the right to prevent the unauthorized disclosure, acquisition, or use of the trade secret by others without consent, provided such actions are contrary to honest commercial practices. However, exceptions apply, such as when the information is obtained through reverse engineering, laboratory testing, or other lawful means without a confidentiality obligation. The proprietor also has the right to take legal action and claim compensation for damages resulting from infringements, as well as to prevent individuals, legal entities, or organizations from misappropriating the trade secret. Additionally, the proprietor may disclose, withdraw, utilize, or transfer the trade secret to others, setting conditions for maintaining confidentiality. Finally, the proprietor can enforce confidentiality obligations on individuals lawfully in control of the trade secret, with such obligations continuing beyond the termination of employment or contractual relationships, as long as the information remains confidential.

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Telecommunications No.09/NA 2011;

Decision on the Management of Telecommunications and ICT No.3583/MoTC 2022
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011 decision on the management of telecommunications and ict no 3583 motc 2022
acts_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
decision on the management of telecommunications and ict no 3583 motc 2022
Lao PDR.impact:
Service providers shall collectively share the telecommunication infrastructure without discrimination in terms of conditions, quality and services based on mutual benefit to avoid overlapping investment and promote efficient use of resources. This principle encourages fair access to infrastructure among providers​ (Article 20 Utilization of Infrastructure). The telecommunication infrastructure means buildings and structures, including equipment and transmission line to support the telecommunication network services, such as: duct, pole, mast, tower, antenna, optic fiber, copper wire, mixture wire, transformers (Article 15 Telecommunication Infrastructure). 

In the Decision on the Management of Telecommunications and ICT No. 3583 (2022), it emphasizes that telecommunications providers are required to share essential infrastructure, such as radio towers and core network elements, to ensure efficient use of resources and to avoid unnecessary duplication. This decision further clarifies obligations related to sharing infrastructure for various telecommunications networks, including satellite and terrestrial networks​.

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010 law on investment promotion act 2010 no 14 na amendment in 2016 decree on approval the list of controlled and concession businesses 2019
acts_norm: law on investment promotion act no 14 na 2010
law on investment promotion act 2010 no 14 na amendment in 2016
decree on approval the list of controlled and concession businesses 2019
Lao PDR.impact:
Foreigners are permitted to invest in telecommunication business.

According to the Telecommunications Law, any domestic and foreign individual, legal entity or organization wishing to establish a telecommunication enterprise shall apply for establishment and registration to the concerned organization as defined in the Law on Investment Promotion, the Law on Enterprise, and other relevant laws of Lao PDR (Article 24 Application for the Establishment of a Telecommunication Enterprise). Under the Investment Promotion Act, both domestic and foreign investors have the right to invest in all investment sectors, including telecommunications, and areas which are not prohibited by the law of the Lao PDR (Articles 67.1 and 3). 

Still, telecommunications business (wire telecommunications and wireless telecommunications related activities, and satellite telecommunications related activities) is subject to the controlled business/concession list, and therefore requires approval from relevant agencies.

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011 lao telecom tplus digital co ltd unitel etl
acts_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
lao telecom
tplus digital co ltd
unitel
etl
Lao PDR.impact:
There are four authorized enterprises (LTC, ETL, UNITEL, and TPLUS) providing fixed and mobile telecommunications services in Lao PDR, and the fifth (Sky Telecom) holds a mobile license but currently only offers internet access. All of them have some share of government ownership.

- In 2023, it is reported that Lao Telecom (LaoTel) is a dominant telecom player in the country. Specifically, LaoTel is a joint venture between the government of the Lao People’s Democratic Republic (51%) and Shenington Investments (49%). 
- TPLUS Digital Sole Co., Ltd 100% is owned by the Telecommunication Public Company (LaoTel)
- UNITEL is a joint venture between Viettel Global and Lao Asia Telecom (fully owned by the Lao Government)
- Enterprise of Telecommunications Lao (ETL) is a company owned by Hong Kong Jiafu Holding Co., Ltd. and the Lao Government in accordance with 51%: 49% 

Additionally, in the amended Lao Telecommunications Law, the government has a significant role in controlling and managing telecommunications companies. For instance, Article 44 (Telecommunication Authority) centralizes the management of telecommunications under the state, giving authority to the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications and local bodies to actively manage the sector nationwide. Additionally, Article 45 (Control of Telecommunications) specifies that the government can temporarily control telecommunication networks and services during national security incidents. It also has the authority to inspect and seize telecommunication equipment.
Lao PDR.timeframe:
Since December 2011;

Last reviewed in September 2024

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Telecommunications No.09/NA 2011;

Decision on Competition of Telecommunication No.019/MPT 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011 decision on competition of telecommunication no 019 mpt 2015
acts_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
decision on competition of telecommunication no 019 mpt 2015
Lao PDR.impact:
Under the Law on Telecommunications No.09/NA, there is no specific mention of functional or accounting separation mandates for operators. However, Article 27.5 (Obligations of the Telecommunication Service Providers) of the Law on Telecommunications outlines the financial and accounting obligations of service providers, indicating they must comply with financial-accounting standards and reporting rules. 

According to the Decision, functional separation is mandated for operators with significant market power, however accounting separation is not required. The Decision indicates the three types of anti-competitive conduct: collusion of telecom service providers, market domination, and monopoly (Article 11). Telecommunication service provider shall summarize, conclude, and report its statistical data, volume, prices of services, in monthly, quarterly, annually period, to Department of Telecommunications in timely and regularly manner (Article 22 Reporting).

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Telecommunications No.09/NA 2011;

Decision on Telecommunication Business Operating License No.221/MPT 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011 decision on telecommunication business operating license no 221 mpt 2015
acts_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
decision on telecommunication business operating license no 221 mpt 2015
Lao PDR.impact:
There is no explicit mention of stringent licensing requirements such as discrimination against foreign providers, minimum capital requirements, or mandatory performance requirements. The licensing provisions, such as in Article 24, outline the general process for the establishment and operation of telecommunication enterprises by both domestic and foreign individuals or entities (apply uniformly to all telecom operators foreign and domestic).

The Decision on Telecommunication Business Operating License No. 221/MPT, particularly in Article 16, specify several key requirements that telecommunication operators must meet, including adequate financial resources, qualified staff, necessary infrastructure, and the full payment of fees. These criteria form a structured licensing framework.

However, while these conditions establish a thorough vetting process, they do not necessarily constitute a strict licensing scheme that specifically targets or discriminates against foreign providers. The requirement for "adequate financial resources" could be interpreted as a form of a minimum capital requirement, but the decision does not explicitly set discriminatory conditions that would disadvantage foreign applicants. Instead, both domestic and foreign providers must meet the same conditions regarding financial resources, technical capacity, and infrastructure.

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Telecommunications No.09/NA 2011;

Prime Minister Decree on Establishing the Framework for the Creation of a Telecommunication Regulatory Authority 2017
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011 prime minister decree on establishing the framework for the creation of a telecommunication regulatory authority 2017
acts_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
prime minister decree on establishing the framework for the creation of a telecommunication regulatory authority 2017
Lao PDR.impact:
According to the WTO Trade Policy Review 2019, Lao Telecommunication Regulatory Authority (LTRA) is established in 2017. The LTRA is fully independent from interference by operators not receiving any benefit from them so as to ensure a level playing field in terms of competition, numbering assignment, consumer safeguard and licensing. The Ministry of Post and Telecommunications is responsible for policy formulation and implementation in this area, and prior to the establishment of LTRA, it ensured regulation in the sector.

Article 19 (Network interconnection) explicitly mandates interconnection for all telecom service providers. It ensures that service providers have the right to interconnect with others, and they must do so on a non-discriminatory basis, following technical standards set by the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications (MPT).

Article 44 (Telecommunication Authority) establishes the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications as the telecom regulatory authority, tasked with regulating and managing the telecom sector independently from operators. However, the regulatory function of the MPT was then replaced by the Prime Minister Decree on Establishing the Framework for the Creation of an independent Telecommunication Regulatory Authority (March 2017).

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017 law on electronic transactions no 20 na 2010
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
law on electronic transactions no 20 na 2010
Lao PDR.impact:
Lao PDR does not implement ban to transfer and requirement to process data locally. The definition of data processing is provided and to process data, the data controller must obtain a data subject's consent. 

The law does not enforce a strict local processing mandate but rather sets forth a series of conditions that must be met before data can be processed or transferred, particularly abroad. It focuses on security measures, such as encryption and consent, rather than requiring that all data processing must remain within the country.

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017 law on electronic transactions no 20 na 2010
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
law on electronic transactions no 20 na 2010
Lao PDR.impact: There is no local storage requirement implemented.

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017 law on electronic transactions no 20 na 2010 decision on approval to operate internet data center 2012
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
law on electronic transactions no 20 na 2010
decision on approval to operate internet data center 2012
Lao PDR.impact:
There is no infrastructure requirement implemented. The operation of data center has been regulated instead. The Decision on Approval to Operate Internet Data Center requires that all internet data centers have a licence to operate.

The Law on Electronic Data Protection and the Law on Electronic Transactions in Lao PDR do not currently enforce an explicit infrastructure data localization requirement across all sectors. However, there are key provisions that emphasize ensuring secure systems and adequate data safeguards, especially for sensitive and critical data.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Data Protection No.25/NA 2017
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Electronic Data Protection specifies that the delivery or transfer of data must be performed as follows:
- with the consent of the data subject and guarantee that the transferee can protect such data;
- with the encryption of important information, such as financial, accounting, and investment data and with the electronic certificate issued by the Ministry of Posts and Telecommunication (Article 25);
- without forging the source of data sent or transferred;
- that the transfer must be in accordance with the agreement of the transferee and transferor; and
- that the transfer must be stopped upon refusal by transferee.

 The scope of the Law on Electronic Data Protection (2017) primarily covers the protection of personal data and other sensitive data. Specifically, Article 2 (Definitions) and Article 4 (Scope of Application) provide insights into the applicability:
- Article 2 defines personal data as any information relating to an individual, which can be used directly or indirectly to identify that person.
-Article 4 clarifies that the law applies to personal data and the management, processing, and storage of such data by both public and private sector entities within Lao PDR. This includes ensuring appropriate levels of security and confidentiality for handling personal data. The law, therefore, explicitly focuses on the protection of personal data and outlines measures for safeguarding the privacy of individuals, without extending to all categories of non-personal or generic data.
- Article 10: Defines sensitive data as information that cannot be accessed, used, or disclosed without the consent of the Information Owner. This includes information on financials, health, personal data, etc.
-Article 11: Outlines the responsibilities of the Data Administrator, including ensuring that sensitive data is adequately protected.
-Article 15: Requires periodic risk assessments, backup systems, and other measures to safeguard sensitive data. Specifies requirements for protecting sensitive data, including ensuring secure storage and conducting annual risk assessments.
- Article 16: Addresses encryption requirements for transferring sensitive data, specifically mentioning that important documents (e.g., financial, banking, and investment data) must be encrypted during cross-border transfers.

The transfer of private data outside of Lao PDR is subject to the express consent of data subject and compliance with law (Article 17 Sending or Transferring of Electronic Data).

Lao PDR.act: Decree on the Financial Consumer Protection No. 225/GOL 2020
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on the financial consumer protection no 225 gol 2020
acts_norm: decree on the financial consumer protection no 225 gol 2020
Lao PDR.impact:
There is no specific mention in the documents regarding conditional flow regimes for all sectors. However, there are provisions and requirements for specific types of data, mainly sensitive data and financial data, that must be handled with extra security measures. Sensitive data (e.g., financial, medical, customer information) must be handled with explicit consent from the data owner and must be encrypted when being transferred, especially across borders. Additionally, the Decree on Consumer Protection Concerning Financial Services, which ensures that financial institutions must protect customer data and may not transfer sensitive financial information without proper safeguards.
- Article 21: Ensures that financial institutions take responsibility for collecting and handling customer information with transparency, including obtaining consent.
- Article 30: Discusses provisions to protect sensitive financial information and customer data, ensuring financial institutions implement proper safeguards for transferring data.

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017 guidelines on the implementation of the law on electronic data protection no 2126 moptc
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
guidelines on the implementation of the law on electronic data protection no 2126 moptc
Lao PDR.impact:
Lao PDR does not have comprehensive data protection law. The data protection in Lao PDR mainly refers to the protection of data in electronic format. The protection can be found in specific regulations, such as the Law on Electronic Data Protection No.25/NA, Law on Prevention and Combating Cybercrime No. 61/NA, and Penal Code No. 26/NA. 

Specifically, under the Electronic Data Protection No. 25/NA, the electronic data refers to general data (data of individual, legal entities or organizations) and specific data (official/personal data that not allow individual, legal entities or organizations to access, use or disclose without permission from the owner or relevant organizations) (Articles 8-10). Additionally, in August 2018, to clarify the requirements under the Law on Electronic Data Protection, the Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications issued the Guidelines on the Implementation of the Law on Electronic Data Protection No. 2126/MoPTC. The E-Data Protection Guidelines provide greater clarity on the classification of electronic data, the compliance obligations of data managers, and rules associated with regulated data protection activities. These Guidelines cover: (i) data collection; (ii) electronic data inspection; (iii) saving/storing of electronic data; (iv) maintaining electronic data; (v) using and disseminating electronic data; (vi) transmission and transfer of electronic data; (vii) access to electronic data; (viii) amending and updating electronic data; and (ix) deletion of electronic data.

Lao PDR has no comprehensive data protection framework that applies universally across all sectors. Instead, the country has adopted a more sector-specific (sectoral) approach to data protection. The Law on Electronic Data Protection (2017) does provide guidelines for the handling and processing of sensitive data, such as personal, financial, and medical information, but these rules are often integrated into other sectoral regulations. Lao PDR is still in the process of developing a more robust data protection regime, with ongoing efforts to enhance the legal and institutional framework for data security​ (ESCAP-2022-PB-Readiness)​ (Implementation Guide). However, the country has not yet enacted a comprehensive, cross-sectoral data protection law similar to the GDPR or California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), which would apply universally across all sectors.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No.61/NA 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
acts_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
Lao PDR.impact:
Lao PDR does have a dedicated cybersecurity legal framework, though it is somewhat sector-specific rather than being a comprehensive, horizontal framework that applies across all sectors. The Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime (2015) provides a foundation for cybersecurity in Lao PDR. This law establishes principles, regulations, and measures for managing and monitoring cybersecurity efforts, including the protection of computer systems, data, and information​. The law focuses on cybercrime, detailing offenses like unauthorized access, data forgery, and system interference.

Article 6 (Scope of the law enforcement), applies to person, legal entity, and organizations both domestic and foreign living and resarching and operating computer system and computer's data and inforamtion in Lao PDR. Also Article 3 (Definitions), the term 'personal data and information' refers to any data and information. 

Although the law provides a dedicated legal framework for cybersecurity, it primarily focuses on criminal aspects of cyber threats rather than encompassing a broad, horizontal cybersecurity strategy that applies to all sectors, including critical infrastructure, public sector entities, and private industries. Additionally, sectoral regulations, like those for telecommunications, further address cybersecurity concerns in specific areas.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Transactions No.20/NA 2010
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic transactions no 20 na 2010
acts_norm: law on electronic transactions no 20 na 2010
Lao PDR.impact: The language used in the Law on Electronic Transactions (2012), specifically in Article 16, states that individuals, legal entities, or organizations "may store" documents electronically, which implies that electronic storage is an option rather than a mandatory requirement. This means that the law provides the flexibility to retain documents electronically, but it does not impose a strict, mandatory minimum retention period across all sectors.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Data Protection No.25/NA 2017
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
Lao PDR.impact: There are no explicit minimum data retention periods mentioned in the Law on Electronic Data Protection (2017). However, the law requires that data controllers (organizations responsible for managing data) ensure the security and integrity of data they collect and store. This responsibility implies that data controllers must maintain data as long as necessary to fulfill their obligations under national laws, but no specific period is outlined.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No.61/NA 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
acts_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
Lao PDR.impact: Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No. 61/NA (2015) specifies that service providers must retain computer traffic data for a minimum period. According to Article 40 (Prohibitions for Service Providers) of the law, service providers are prohibited from deleting computer traffic data for a minimum of 90 days if they have a connection system, and 365 days if they do not. Implying that service providers are expected to monitor users' data to some extent and provide it to authorities when requested. This ensures that the data is available for legal or investigative purposes, particularly in cybercrime investigations. The law allows authorities to monitor, inspect, and take necessary actions regarding online content that violates national security or public order​.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Data Protection No.25/NA 2017
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
Lao PDR.impact: There is a general obligation for a data processor or controller to maintain staff for data security administration under the Law. The Law does not explicitly state the requirements of a data protection officer ('DPO') for administrators. Articles 29 and 30 states that data controller has the right and obligation to establish an internal department and officer to supervise the protection of the data. Article 46 also mandates that the content of inspection of electronic data protection should include the responsibilities, behavior, and working methodologies of Electronic Data Protection officers. However, there is no clarification to what that roles and its responsibilities entail.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Data Protection No.25/NA 2017
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Electronic Data Protection (2017) includes provisions that allow government access to personal data under specific conditions, primarily for reasons related to national security, public order, or in the case of legal proceedings. Electronic Data Administration Authorities (Article 3) refers to individual, legal entities, or organizations that responsible for administrating the electronic data which mainly are Ministries, Data Center through internet, telecom services providers, and banks. According to the law, personal data may be accessed or disclosed by government authorities if it aligns with the needs of public safety or legal requirements. For instance, Article 14 of the law emphasizes that the protection of personal data must be balanced with the need to maintain national security and stability. 

The authority has the right to intercept the electronic data that effect on the stability of the nation, peace and orderliness of the society (Article 29 Rights of the Data Administration Authority, Clause 1). However, the authority require to request permission from data owner. It is prohibited to access, use, collect, disclose, or disseminate electronic data without permission (Articles 30 Obligation of data administration authority and 33 Prohibition for the Data Administration Authority).

Lao PDR.act: Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No.61/NA 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
acts_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
Lao PDR.impact: The Cybercrime Law does mention that government authorities can access and retain certain types of data for investigations under specific conditions, particularly in the context of cybercrime. Article 40 of the Cybercrime Law prohibits service providers from deleting certain data, like computer traffic data, within a specific retention period unless instructed by relevant authorities. Article 45 allows for emergency investigation measures where authorities may access computer data to prevent imminent threats or cybercrimes. Therefore, while there are cases where government authorities may gain access to data, it is generally part of an established legal process rather than unrestricted access without judicial oversight.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Transactions (Amended Version) No.31/NA
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic transactions no 31 na
acts_norm: law on electronic transactions no 31 na
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Electronic Transactions (Amended Version) No. 31/NA 2023 replaces the Law on Electronic Transactions No. 20/NA, where the law provides certain protections under a "safe harbor" mechanism for intermediaries, such as those involved in the transmission and storage of electronic data messages and records. Law on Electronic Transactions Article 44 (Intermediary liability) states the liability of intermediaries: An intermediary must: 1. Follow telecom ministry rules; 2. Bear civil/criminal liability if it knowingly handles damaging data; 3. Honor all contractual/legal duties.
 Article 45 (Intermediary Non-Liability) states that intermediaries are not held liable to: 1. Monitoring user data; 2. User-originated content it merely handles; 3. Content it has no actual knowledge is illegal; 4. Background data it has no actual knowledge of. However, according to Article 44, once the intermediary has actual knowledge that data incur liability, it must: 1. Immediately delete/cease services for that data and alert the originator; 2. Report to the telecom regulator or police, naming the client if known; 3. Accept any additional liabilities under applicable laws. 

However, there are no provisions that specifically regulate the copyright infringement.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Transactions (Amended Version) No.31/NA
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic transactions no 31 na
acts_norm: law on electronic transactions no 31 na
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Electronic Transactions (Amended Version) No. 31/NA 2023 replaces the Law on Electronic Transactions No. 20/NA, where the law provides certain protections under a "safe harbor" mechanism for intermediaries, such as those involved in the transmission and storage of electronic data messages and records. Law on Electronic Transactions Article 44 (Intermediary liability) states the liability of intermediaries: An intermediary must: 1. Follow telecom ministry rules; 2. Bear civil/criminal liability if it knowingly handles damaging data; 3. Honor all contractual/legal duties.
 Article 45 (Intermediary Non-Liability) states that intermediaries are not held liable to: 1. Monitoring user data; 2. User-originated content it merely handles; 3. Content it has no actual knowledge is illegal; 4. Background data it has no actual knowledge of. However, according to Article 44, once the intermediary has actual knowledge that data incur liability, it must: 1. Immediately delete/cease services for that data and alert the originator; 2. Report to the telecom regulator or police, naming the client if known; 3. Accept any additional liabilities under applicable laws.

Lao PDR.act: Decree on Internet Information Management No.327/Gov 2014
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on internet information management no 327 gov 2014
acts_norm: decree on internet information management no 327 gov 2014
Lao PDR.impact: The Decree No. 327/Gov Article 8 (Creating a Personal Account on Social Media) requires individuals to register on social media sites with their full names and current address correctly, making it difficult for people in Lao PDR to share news articles or other information anonymously.

Lao PDR.act: Notification on Registration of Telephone and SIM Card Numbers in Lao PDR No.1056/CO.TMO 2020
Lao PDR.act_norm: notification on registration of telephone and sim card numbers in lao pdr no 1056 co tmo 2020
acts_norm: notification on registration of telephone and sim card numbers in lao pdr no 1056 co tmo 2020
Lao PDR.impact: Notification on Registration of Telephone and SIM Card Numbers in Lao PDR, No. 1056/CO.TMO, Dated 17 June 2020 prescribes instructions for SIM Card registration.

Lao PDR.act: Decree on the Management and Use of Internet, and Domain Name of the Lao PDR No.164/Gov 2012
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on the management and use of internet and domain name of the lao pdr no 164 gov 2012
acts_norm: decree on the management and use of internet and domain name of the lao pdr no 164 gov 2012
Lao PDR.impact:
The Decree No. 164/Gov requires internet service providers (ISPs) and website managers/ website owners to monitor the information disseminated through their services, i.e., content not allowed for dissemination on the Internet (Article 10 such as propaganda, pornography, national secrets, false information, etc.). Specifically, the Decree defines the ISP as entity which provides Internet service to consumers so that they can use the Internet; and the Website manager as the person who has the right to enter the system legally in order to create, edit, improve, and delete the system and the content of the website (Article 3).  

Article 9 states the rights and functions of ISP. The ISP is authorized to inspect the activities of its own internet service user; and warning, stopping, imposing  discipline and measures (Article 16). The ISP has duties to manage and train the users to comply with the relevant policies, decrees and regulations (Article 17.1).

Additionally,  website managers or website owners have the responsibility of checking content and information thoroughly before allowing others to disseminate it through their website (Article 7 Regulations for Disseminating Information on the Internet). Article 14 states that the website manager shall be responsible to check the content and information before disseminating on the website (Article 14.2).

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Data Protection No.25/NA 2017
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
Lao PDR.impact: The Law on Electronic Data Protection does not explicitly require the monitoring of users’ activities or the removal/blocking of content. Neither does it require Active Monitoring of Users’ Activities Without a Legal Obligation to Remove or Block Content.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No.61/NA 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
acts_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
Lao PDR.impact: Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No. 61/NA (2015) specifies that service providers must retain computer traffic data for a minimum period. According to Article 40 (Prohibitions for Service Providers) of the law, service providers are prohibited from deleting computer traffic data for a minimum of 90 days if they have a connection system, and 365 days if they do not. Implying that service providers are expected to monitor users' data to some extent and provide it to authorities when requested. This ensures that the data is available for legal or investigative purposes, particularly in cybercrime investigations. The law allows authorities to monitor, inspect, and take necessary actions regarding online content that violates national security or public order​.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Electronic Data Protection No.25/NA 2017
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
acts_norm: law on electronic data protection no 25 na 2017
Lao PDR.impact:
There was no record found on the blocked or filtered website and other communication services in Lao PDR. The Law on Electronic Data Protection defines the Electronic Data Administration Authorities under Article 3 of this law, which outlines the responsibilities of individuals, legal entities, or organizations (such as Ministries, Data Centers, telecom service providers, and banks) that are tasked with administering electronic data.

Article 29 grants these authorities specific rights, including the right to suspend the service of a data owner who violates agreements or whose behavior causes damage to the authority or disrupts societal peace and orderliness. This indicates that the data administration authorities have regulatory power over service suspensions in cases of non-compliance or harmful actions.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Telecommunications No.09/NA 2011
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
acts_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
Lao PDR.impact: Law on Telecommunications No. 09/NA grants the government the authority to block telecommunications services, including the internet, during times of national security concerns. Specifically, Article 45 mentions the government’s right to temporarily control telecommunication networks and seize telecommunication equipment if they are involved in public unrest or national security threats​.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No. 61/NA 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
acts_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
Lao PDR.impact:
Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No. 61/NA: This law allows authorities to block or remove content related to cybercrime, such as content that undermines national security, public order, or social stability. The Ministry of Post and Telecommunications can issue emergency notices or block access to specific harmful content​. While it does not explicitly mandate filtering, it does provide the framework for service providers and authorities to manage and mitigate the spread of harmful content, including disinformation or illegal content.

•Article 13: Causing Damages via Online Social Media: This article allows authorities to act against content that threatens national security, public order, or social stability by ordering the removal or blocking of content that falls under these categories.
•Article 22: Emergency Notification: This article allows authorities to take immediate actions, including issuing emergency notifications for incidents affecting national security or the dignity of any person, which may involve blocking access to specific content.
•Article 20: Warning Notice: The Ministry of Post and Telecommunications has the authority to issue notices about risks, fake websites, malicious software, and other hazards.
