Service providers must then implement conditions to limit or disallow access to specific user categories, which can include blocking harmful content.

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Consumer Protection No. 02/NA 2010; 

Decision on Protection of Consumers Using Telecommunications and Internet Services No. 1061/MPT 2020
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on consumer protection no 02 na 2010 decision on protection of consumers using telecommunications and internet services no 1061 mpt 2020
acts_norm: law on consumer protection no 02 na 2010
decision on protection of consumers using telecommunications and internet services no 1061 mpt 2020
Lao PDR.impact:
The Law on Consumer Protection Article 15 (Permission for Advertisement) mandates that individuals, legal entities, and organizations intending to advertise goods and services must seek approval from the committee for consumer protection in accordance with laws and regulations. For the contents of the advertisement on goods and services shall be in accordance with the regulations as stipulated by the relevant sectors.

Moreover, online marketing campaigns in Lao PDR must adhere to strict guidelines to ensure transparency and truthfulness, with authorities having the power to regulate and enforce these rules. Any violation of these rules can result in fines or other penalties:
- Content of Advertisement: Advertisements must provide accurate and truthful information about the type, characteristics, and quality of goods or services. Misleading or exaggerated claims are prohibited (Article 14). The content should also avoid defaming or discrediting competitors (Article 14)​.
- Modifications to False Advertisements: If an advertisement is deemed untrue and harmful to consumers, the authorities can require the advertiser to stop and correct the advertisement before continuing it (Article 16)​.
- Prohibited Practices for Suppliers: Suppliers are prohibited from advertising or providing false information about the quality of goods and services, which would mislead consumers (Article 58)​.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime No.61/NA 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
acts_norm: law on prevention and combating cyber crime no 61 na 2015
Lao PDR.impact:
In 2020, Lao PDR has issued notices to online media to register with the government or be prohibited from publishing information and news on social media platforms like Facebook. It is reported that the registration enables the government to enforce the 2015 Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime, which categorizes the followings as offenses causing damage via online social media: 
1. Posting data and information containing aspects of persuading people to resist the government or separate national solidarity; 
2. Posting data and information destroying national security, peace, the order in society, national culture and fine tradition of the nation; 
3. Posting data and information containing blaspheming and using impolite words through the computer system. 

The Law on Prevention and Combating Cyber Crime ensure that online content providers operate in a way that aligns with national security and public safety standards, and violations can lead to penalties or legal action:
- Service Provider Obligations: Service providers who manage communication and data through computer systems must comply with the regulations outlined in this law. This includes ensuring the integrity and security of the computer data and systems they manage (Article 8)​.
- Online content platforms, including social media, are required to adhere to security protocols that protect users from unauthorized access, data breaches, and illegal content. Providers must also facilitate law enforcement by preserving traffic data for a specified period (90 days to one year) and providing this data to authorities when required (Article 40)​.
- Cyber Crime Prevention Measures  require that platforms ensure their systems are not used for unlawful activities, including the distribution of false information, promotion of illegal goods, or violation of national security. They must establish mechanisms to monitor and report suspicious activities (Articles 8, 19, and 24)​.
- Registration and Supervision: Providers of computer systems, including VPNs and cloud services, are required to register with relevant authorities, such as the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications. They are also subject to inspection and monitoring to ensure compliance with cybersecurity regulations (Articles 48 and 53)​.

Lao PDR.act: Enterprise Law No. 46/NA 2013
Lao PDR.act_norm: enterprise law no 46 na 2013
acts_norm: enterprise law no 46 na 2013
Lao PDR.impact: Enterprise Law No. 46/NA (2013): All businesses, including online content providers, must register their enterprises with the Department of Enterprise Registration or relevant local authorities. Once registered, an Enterprise Registration Certificate is issued, which is valid for the entire period of the business’s operation (Article 5 and Article 2). After registration, businesses that are subject to technical review or specific regulations must also obtain a business operating license from the relevant sectoral authority (Article 16).

Lao PDR.act:
Decree on Import and Export of Goods No.114/GoL 2011;

Notification on Goods Subject to Automatic and Non-automatic Import or Export Licensing 2012
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on import and export of goods no 114 gol 2011 notification on goods subject to automatic and non automatic import or export licensing 2012
acts_norm: decree on import and export of goods no 114 gol 2011
notification on goods subject to automatic and non automatic import or export licensing 2012
Lao PDR.impact:
Under the Decree, the Import and Export Administration Authorities have the rights to prohibit the import or export of goods (Article 7). The import ban has been applied on certain products and under different laws, such as chemicals of high-level danger used in industries, bullets and weapons, opium, destructive fishing tools, pornographic media/materials, and banknotes and coin-making machines. 

However, there is no record of the import ban on ICT-related products. ICT products are not included in the list of prohibited items or non-automatic import licensing products.

Lao PDR.act: Notification on Temporary Suspension of all Satellite Receiver Importation. No.55/CO 2018
Lao PDR.act_norm: notification on temporary suspension of all satellite receiver importation no 55 co 2018
acts_norm: notification on temporary suspension of all satellite receiver importation no 55 co 2018
Lao PDR.impact: The Notification on Temporary Suspension of all Satellite Receiver Importation. No. 55/CO, dated 01 February 2018 -  prohibit importing foreign satellite receivers without prior approval. The current validity period for the suspension extends until December 31, 9999, indicating no planned expiration date at present.

Lao PDR.act:
Decision on the Registration of Importers and Exporters of Goods No.0752 2023; 

Decision on Goods Subject to Licensing Prior to Import or Export No.0333/MOIC 2022
Lao PDR.act_norm: decision on the registration of importers and exporters of goods no 0752 2023 decision on goods subject to licensing prior to import or export no 0333 moic 2022
acts_norm: decision on the registration of importers and exporters of goods no 0752 2023
decision on goods subject to licensing prior to import or export no 0333 moic 2022
Lao PDR.coverage: Goods having a direct impact on people’s lives
Lao PDR.impact:
Decision on the Registration of Importers and Exporters of Goods No.0752 requires importers and exporters to register their activities and the related goods with the Ministry of Industry and Commerce (MOIC). This decision regulates the registration of importers and exporters of goods that the government regulates and that have a direct impact on people’s lives. It is possible that the goods regulated by the new decision could be the same as those defined in the 2022 list, under the Decision on the List of Goods subject to Applying for an Import or Export Permit No. 0333/IC—such as drugs, medical products, land vehicles, petrol, and hazardous chemicals—but further confirmation and clarification will be necessary to determine this.

Before importing and exporting of goods, importers and exporters must register the goods with the Import and Export Department (“IED”) of the MOIC. The IED will issue a certificate within three working days after receiving all required documents, which will be valid for one year and may be renewed. The decision has entered into effect on 10 July 2023.

Lao PDR.act: Notification on the Import, Distribution, and Use of Telecommunication Equipment (Walkie-Talkie) No. 1229/MPT.PSO 2013
Lao PDR.act_norm: notification on the import distribution and use of telecommunication equipment no 1229 mpt pso 2013
acts_norm: notification on the import distribution and use of telecommunication equipment no 1229 mpt pso 2013
Lao PDR.impact: The Notification on the Import, Distribution, and Use of Telecommunication Equipment (Walkie-Talkie), No. 1229/MPT.PSO, dated 13 August 2013 explicitly bans individuals, entities, or organizations from importing or using Walkie-Talkies without a permit or a license. Section I.3: "Do not allow import by individual, entities or organizations and use of telecommunication equipment (Walkie-Talkie) without a permit or a license"​. Multiple Goods Ban: Specific HS codes for Walkie-Talkies are 852560, 852691 and 852712.

Lao PDR.act: Decree on Satellite Communications No. 471/GOL 2019
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on satellite communications no 471 gol 2019
acts_norm: decree on satellite communications no 471 gol 2019
Lao PDR.impact: The Decree on Satellite Communications, No. 471/GOL, dated 27 December 2019, specifies prohibitions related to satellite communication equipment. Article 52.2: Prohibits the "manufacture, import, and export of satellite communication equipment without permission"​. Multiple Goods Ban: Specific HS codes for Satellite Communication Equipment are 852560 and 852691.

Lao PDR.act: Decision on Import and Distribution of ICT Equipment No.3201/MPT 2016
Lao PDR.act_norm: decision on import and distribution of ict equipment no 3201 mpt 2016
acts_norm: decision on import and distribution of ict equipment no 3201 mpt 2016
Lao PDR.impact: There is a general ban without certification on ICT equipment: Decision on Import and Distribution of ICT Equipment No. 3201/MPT (2016). ICT devices such as computers, smartphones, network equipment, and other data transmission devices must be certified and approved before they can be imported or distributed. This can be considered a ban on multiple ICT goods if they do not meet the technical and safety requirements or if they are imported without the necessary permits.

Lao PDR.act:
Decision on the Management of Telecommunications and ICT No. 3583/MoTC 2022;

Items requiring certification of the Technical Barriers to Trade – TBT before import and export, No. 0782/MOIC 2024
Lao PDR.act_norm: decision on the management of telecommunications and ict no 3583 motc 2022 items requiring certification of the technical barriers to trade tbt before import and export no 0782 moic 2024
acts_norm: decision on the management of telecommunications and ict no 3583 motc 2022
items requiring certification of the technical barriers to trade tbt before import and export no 0782 moic 2024
Lao PDR.impact:
Decision No. 3583/MoTC on the Management of Telecommunications and ICT (13 October 2022) and Technical Barriers to Trade (TBT) Decision No. 0782/MOIC. A wide range of ICT goods—such as radio communication devices, core networks, and transmission networks—must meet specific technical standards (e.g., RF, EMC, EMF) and obtain certification before import​.
Relevant HS Codes: 
- 852691: Radio navigation aid apparatus
- 851762: Machines for the reception, conversion, and transmission of data
- 854370: Articles specifically designed for connection to telegraphic or telephonic networks.

Lao PDR.act: Law on Telecommunications No.09/NA 2011
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
acts_norm: law on telecommunications no 09 na 2011
Lao PDR.impact: Article 18 (Importation of Telecommunication Equipment) manages the importation of hi-technology telecommunication equipment. The regulation states that the import of some types of telecommunication equipment should be subject a further control by the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications. The imported telephone sets and computers should also have a menu and be able to use content in Lao language.

Lao PDR.act: Decision on the Radio Frequency Inspection, No. 2119/MPT 2020
Lao PDR.act_norm: decision on the radio frequency inspection no 2119 mpt 2020
acts_norm: decision on the radio frequency inspection no 2119 mpt 2020
Lao PDR.impact:
Decision on the Radio Frequency Inspection, No. 2119/MPT (2020) includes Non-Technical NTMs and Other Import Restrictions on ICT Goods:
1. Import Licensing and Certification Requirements:
	•Article 6 and Article 7: ICT equipment that uses radio frequencies must be inspected and certified before importation. This includes:
	•Obtaining a standards certificate for radio frequency devices.
	•Inspecting the device code number and measuring the technical data value of the equipment, such as electric field strength and specific energy absorption rate (SAR).
	•These measures add administrative burdens and potential delays to the import process, effectively acting as non-technical NTMs on ICT goods that use radio frequencies 
2. Prohibition on Unauthorized Equipment:
	•Article 8 (General Prohibitions): It is prohibited to import, install, or use radio frequency equipment that does not comply with local regulations. This effectively restricts the import of certain ICT devices unless they have been approved and certified by the relevant Lao authorities 
3. Seizure of Non-Compliant Equipment:
	•Article 11 (Rights and Duties of Radio Frequency Inspectors): Inspectors are authorized to seize radio frequency equipment that lacks proper licenses or certification. This includes the ability to:
	•Stop broadcasting by unauthorized stations.
	•Temporarily halt the distribution or use of radio frequency equipment if it is found to be non-compliant.
	•This adds a level of risk for importers, as non-compliant goods can be seized without prior warning 
4. Monitoring and Compliance Costs:
	• ICT importers must comply with ongoing inspection and monitoring requirements, such as ensuring that their equipment is regularly checked and that they maintain up-to-date licenses and certifications.
	• The requirements for inspections of radio frequency safety standards (e.g., electric field strength, transmission voltage) also impose additional compliance costs on businesses importing and distributing ICT goods in Lao PDR

Lao PDR.act: Decree on the Cancellation of Local Content Requirements set out in Previous Legislations 2012
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on the cancellation of local content requirements set out in previous legislations 2012
acts_norm: decree on the cancellation of local content requirements set out in previous legislations 2012

Lao PDR.act:
Decree on Import and Export of Goods No.114/GoL 2011;

Notification on Goods Subject to Automatic and Non-automatic Import or Export Licensing 2012
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on import and export of goods no 114 gol 2011 notification on goods subject to automatic and non automatic import or export licensing 2012
acts_norm: decree on import and export of goods no 114 gol 2011
notification on goods subject to automatic and non automatic import or export licensing 2012
Lao PDR.impact: Under the Decree, the Import and Export Administration Authorities have the rights to prohibit the import or export of goods (Article 7). However, there is no record of the import ban on ICT-related products. ICT products are not included in the list of prohibited items or non-automatic import licensing products.

Lao PDR.act: Decree on Satellite Communications No. 471/GOL 2019
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on satellite communications no 471 gol 2019
acts_norm: decree on satellite communications no 471 gol 2019
Lao PDR.impact: Decree on Satellite Communications No. 471/GOL (2019) explicitly prohibits the export of satellite communication equipment without prior permission from the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications. Article 51: "Individuals, legal entities or organizations, both domestic and foreign, intending to export all types of satellite communications equipment must obtain permission from the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications"​.

Lao PDR.act: Decision on Goods Subject to Licensing Prior to Import or Export No.0333/MOIC 2022
Lao PDR.act_norm: decision on goods subject to licensing prior to import or export no 0333 moic 2022
acts_norm: decision on goods subject to licensing prior to import or export no 0333 moic 2022
Lao PDR.impact:
Under Decision on Goods Subject to Licensing Prior to Import or Export No. 0333/MOIC (2022), certain goods are required to obtain export licenses. While ICT goods are not explicitly listed, any goods requiring export licensing due to national security or public safety concerns may fall under this rule​.

Article 3 of the Decision on Goods Subject to Licensing Prior to Import or Export No. 0333/MOIC (2022) specifies that certain goods require export licenses. HS code 8517 for Satellite Phones and VSAT are explicitly mentioned in the list of goods. It also includes provisions for any products that may be deemed necessary for licensing due to concerns over national security, public safety, or other public interest considerations. This means that ICT goods are subjected to licensing requirements as determined to fall under these categories

Lao PDR.act: Decree on Import and Export of Goods No.114/GoL 2011
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on import and export of goods no 114 gol 2011
acts_norm: decree on import and export of goods no 114 gol 2011
Lao PDR.impact:
Decree No. 114/GoL on Import and Export of Goods (2011): The general framework for export controls applies to any goods subject to national security, public safety, or environmental concerns. Export licensing is required for goods in this category, though specific ICT products are not directly highlighted​.

Article 6: The article establishes that goods which have implications for national security, public safety, or environmental concerns must obtain an export license. Although specific ICT products are not explicitly mentioned, any goods that fall under the categories of national security or public safety could require an export license as stipulated by this decree.

Lao PDR.act:
Standard Law (Amended) No.49/NA 2014;

Decree on the Implementation of the Law on Standardization No.234 2012
Lao PDR.act_norm: standard law no 49 na 2014 decree on the implementation of the law on standardization no 234 2012
acts_norm: standard law no 49 na 2014
decree on the implementation of the law on standardization no 234 2012
Lao PDR.impact:
Lao Standards is the standards that is adopted by the National Standard Council and promulgated by the Ministry of Science and Technology. Lao Standards, the abbreviation in Lao is “mor lor” and in English is “Lao Standard or “LS” (Article 16 Lao Standards). 

The development of Lao Standards shall comply with the following principles to ensure the conformity with the foreign, regional and international standards (Article 17 Principles for Developing Lao Standards). The use of technical terms for the establishment of standards shall ensure consistent with the terminologies used by the International Standard Organization (ISO) (Article 15 Use of Technical Terms). 

Moreover, individual, legal entity, or organization and the National Standard Council wishing to develop Lao Standard shall submit a proposal to the Ministry of Science and Technology (Article 20 Procedures for Developing Lao Standards). Lao PDR became a signatory to the ASEAN Consultative Committee on Standards and Quality (ACCSQ), ISO, and the International Electrotechnical Commission Asia-Pacific Regional Centre (IEC-APRC). The ISO/IEC Guides 21-1 and 21-2:2005 are adopted.

The Standard Law (Amended) No. 49 encourages international cooperation and explicitly states that the Lao government promotes the involvement of both the domestic and foreign private sectors in standardization activities​. Article 4 (Policy on Standardization) specifically encourages foreign entities to participate in standardization activities, including development, implementation, and management of standards​. This includes participation in the development and implementation of standards, as well as management and inspection, suggesting that there is no formal restriction on foreigners or foreign entities from being involved in the process. 

Additionally, the Decree on the Implementation of the Law on Standardization No. 234 (2012): Article 3 (Participation between Public and Private Sectors): This article encourages the private sector, including foreign entities, to participate in the development of standards and conformity assessment procedures​.

Lao PDR has specific technical standards for ICT goods, impacting several product categories under relevant HS codes. These standards, which may differ from international benchmarks, favor products that can easily align with local regulatory requirements. According to Article 8 of the Decision on the Inspection and Certification of ICT Equipment No. 2118/MPT (2018), all imported ICT equipment, including products under HS Code 8517 (telecommunication equipment, e.g., mobile phones, routers) and HS Code 8525 (transmission apparatus), must comply with local standards related to RF, EMC, EMF, SAR, connection, and electrical safety. Article 12 further specifies these technical requirements.

Lao PDR.act_norm: standard law no 49 na 2014 decree on the implementation of the law on standardization no 234 2012 asean sectoral mra for electrical and electronic equipment 2002
acts_norm: standard law no 49 na 2014
decree on the implementation of the law on standardization no 234 2012
asean sectoral mra for electrical and electronic equipment 2002
Lao PDR.coverage: Horizontal, including electrical appliances and accessories
Lao PDR.impact:
There is no explicit provision banning SDoC (Suppliers' Declaration of Conformity) entirely, but Lao PDR regulations emphasize third-party certification for certain products. Specifically, Article 50 (Voluntary Conformity Assessments) of the Standard Law (Amended) outlines that conformity assessment for technical regulations (e.g., products affecting safety) should be conducted by government conformity assessment bodies or accredited third-party entities. This suggests that SDoC might not be sufficient for some regulated product categories​.

According to Article 12 and Article 54, Lao PDR conforms to international standards for third-party certification and accepts conformity assessments from recognized bodies in countries with Mutual Recognition Agreements (MRAs). This implies that while SDoC is not universally permitted, third-party certifications from certain accredited bodies abroad are accepted.

Article 49 (Self-Conformity Assessment) of the Standard Law permits self-conformity assessment (SDoC) by suppliers or manufacturers, indicating that foreign businesses could use SDoC if the products are not subject to compulsory third-party conformity​. However, for high-risk products such as those involving safety concerns (e.g., EMC/EMI), third-party certification would likely be required. Thus, for some product safety related ICT  (e.g., radio transmissions, EMC/EMI): SDoC is generally not allowed, and third-party certification is preferred, especially if covered under an MRA​.

Specifically, ASEAN Framework Agreement on Mutual Recognition Arrangements provides the principles for developing sectoral Mutual Recognition Arrangements (MRAs) among ASEAN member states, for instance the ASEAN Sectoral MRA for Electrical and Electronic Equipment (ASEAN EE MRA) 2002. A member state shall accept or recognize the test reports and certifications issued by the testing laboratories and certification bodies of other parties (ASEAN EE MRA Article 3). 

In addition, Article 10 of the Decision on the Management of Telecommunications and ICT No. 3583/MoTC (2022) mandates that equipment under HS Code 8517 and HS Code 8526 (radar apparatus, radio communication equipment) be tested locally to ensure compliance with domestic standards. Article 22 requires the use of certification labels to indicate compliance. These regulations create a framework that can serve as an indirect local content requirement by imposing barriers on non-compliant products, including higher compliance costs and delayed market entry for products that don't meet local standards.

Lao PDR.act:
Standard Law (Amended) No.49/NA 2014;

Decree on the Implementation of the Law on Standardization No.234 2012
Lao PDR.act_norm: standard law no 49 na 2014 decree on the implementation of the law on standardization no 234 2012
acts_norm: standard law no 49 na 2014
decree on the implementation of the law on standardization no 234 2012
Lao PDR.impact:
Article 8 (Types of Lao Standards) of the Standard Law (Amended) lists categories of products subject to Lao standards, including products, goods, services, and environmental standards​. ICT products that involve radio transmissions or electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) could fall under these categories if they relate to safety or environmental concerns. Article 45 (Standard and Technical Regulation Conformity) mentions that products linked to safety, public health, and environmental protection must comply with standards and technical regulations​. This could include ICT products, especially those with radio transmission and EMC/EMI considerations.  Article 25 (Contents of Technical Regulations) of the Standard Law indicates that products falling under technical regulations would require testing and certification, and ICT products likely require compliance with performance and safety standards​). 

Article 46 (Principles of Conformity Assessment) stipulates that Lao PDR allows for self-assessments of conformity, including testing and inspection (for more information about the scope, see Article 5). Testing centers and laboratories shall be accredited or recognized by both domestic and foreign concerned accredited body (Article 68 Testing Centers, Laboratories). Specifically, Article 65 (Accreditation) of the Standard Law (Amended) mandates that testing centers and laboratories must be accredited and recognized by both domestic and foreign accredited bodies. The preparation, adoption and application of conformity assessment procedures shall be applied at least equally between the imported products and domestic products and between products originating in any other country (Article 53.1). 

Moreover, National Accreditation Bureau (LNAB) is a body that is officially recognized the capacity of individuals, legal entities or organizations in performing testing activities and certification of quality. The LNAB provides accreditation services for the accreditation of laboratories, certification and inspection bodies in accordance with the appropriate international ISO/IEC standards and guidance documents issued by the International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation (ILAC) and the International Accreditation Forum (IAF) (for more information, see Article 67  Rights and Duties of the National Accreditation Bureau). Also, testing centers [and] laboratories shall be accredited or recognized by both domestic and foreign concerned accredited body (Article 68 Testing Centers, Laboratories). 

There is no blanket exemption from testing requirements. All products within the scope of technical regulations or standards in Lao PDR must undergo some form of conformity assessment. Lao PDR has clear measures in place for product testing, with acceptance of third-party results for certain products via MRAs. However, products under national technical regulations must comply with testing requirements through accredited bodies.

Lao PDR.impact:
Lao PDR has no policy on encryption standard.

Based on the general principles and obligations under the international agreements to which Lao PDR is a party, including its commitment to WTO and ASEAN guidelines, the country is bound to harmonize its standards, including technical regulations such as those related to encryption, with international norms.

Article 29 of the Standard Law (Amended) mentions that any technical regulations, including those potentially related to encryption, must align with international agreements and treaties to which Lao PDR is a party, such as WTO’s Agreement on Technical Barriers to Trade and ASEAN guidelines​. Article 7 of the Decree on the Implementation of the Standard Law highlights the Lao government’s commitment to international cooperation, ensuring that standards and technical regulations (including encryption) are consistent with international agreements​.

Lao PDR.act:
Decree on E-Commerce No.296/GoL 2021;

Decision on Retail and Wholesale Business No.1005/MOIC.ITD 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on e commerce no 296 gol 2021 decision on retail and wholesale business no 1005 moic itd 2015
acts_norm: decree on e commerce no 296 gol 2021
decision on retail and wholesale business no 1005 moic itd 2015
Lao PDR.impact:
In 2021, foreign investment in e-commerce in Lao PDR is subject to a limit of 90% of the entity’s shares, with a minimum registered capital requirement of LAK 10 billion (approximately USD 1 million). This decree does not replace existing laws and regulations governing specific goods or services, which may have additional requirements, such as licensing, notification, or minimum investment.

Foreign participation in retail and wholesale services is governed by the Decision on Wholesale and Retail Business, No. 1005/MOIC.DTD, dated 22 May 2015. Article 13 of this decision outlines the relevant foreign equity limits:
• A foreign shareholder holding up to 50% of the shares must invest at least LAK 4 billion (approximately USD 423,350).
• For businesses with registered capital between LAK 10 billion (approximately USD 1.05 million) and less than LAK 20 billion (approximately USD 2.1 million), foreign equity participation is capped at 70%.
• For businesses with more than LAK 20 billion in registered capital, foreign investors may hold 100% equity.
These foreign equity limitations apply broadly to the retail and wholesale sectors and can indirectly encompass e-commerce platforms under broader business classifications.

Lao PDR.act: Decree on E-Commerce No.296/GoL 2021
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on e commerce no 296 gol 2021
acts_norm: decree on e commerce no 296 gol 2021
Lao PDR.impact: There is no limit on amount of products that can be purchases online and restriction to delivery of products bought online implemented.

Lao PDR.act: Decree on E-Commerce No.296/GoL 2021
Lao PDR.act_norm: decree on e commerce no 296 gol 2021
acts_norm: decree on e commerce no 296 gol 2021
Lao PDR.impact:
The Decree on E-Commerce No. 296/GoL requires the existing and new e-commerce businesses that trade through their platforms or electronic marketplaces to notify and submit documents to the Ministry of Industry and Commerce to receive an acknowledgment certificate. Traders using e-commerce platforms are required to provide information on goods and services, including their business operator’s information such as name, address, contact information, company registration certificate, and business operation license.

Moreover, electronic marketplace is required to be an incorporated entity and must secure an e-commerce business license from the Ministry. As for technical requirements, electronic marketplaces must obtain a Ministry of Technology and Communications certificate confirming their “technical conformity.”  

The licensing scheme for e-commerce providers, both B2B and B2C, is primarily regulated under the Decree on Electronic Commerce, No. 296/GoL, dated 12 April 2021, which outlines the rules and requirements for business entities that engage in e-commerce activities.

- Registration Requirement: Any entity conducting e-commerce activities (both B2B and B2C) must register with the Ministry of Industry and Commerce. This includes both domestic and foreign entities operating within Lao PDR.
- Types of Activities:
B2B (Business-to-Business): These platforms facilitate transactions between businesses, such as wholesale or service exchanges.
B2C (Business-to-Consumer): These platforms allow businesses to sell directly to consumers through online means.
- Licensing Documentation: To obtain a license, applicants must submit several documents, including:
A completed application form.
A business feasibility study.
Proof of business registration with the Ministry of Industry and Commerce.
Evidence of compliance with standards set by the Ministry of Science and Technology, where applicable​.
- Ongoing Compliance: Licensed e-commerce platforms must regularly report on their business activities and ensure that they comply with the terms of their license. This includes submitting annual reports, maintaining financial transparency, and ensuring the security of customer data​.
This framework ensures that e-commerce operations in Lao PDR, whether B2B or B2C, are regulated to protect consumers, ensure fair competition, and promote compliance with national trade and digital laws.

acts_norm: decision on electronic signatures no 1101 mpt 2020

Lao PDR.impact:
There is a requirement for e-commerce platforms in Lao PDR to use a local bank account. Specifically, according to the Decision on Electronic Signatures, No. 1101/MPT, dated 29 May 2020, legal entities applying for an electronic signature certification must submit a certificate of a bank statement from a bank in Lao PDR as part of their application. 

Lao PDR Law on Payment System, Article 9 mandates for a Local Bank Account: Online payment services must utilize local bank accounts for processing payments, including both receipt and disbursement. This ensures compliance with local regulations regarding electronic payments. Article 6 imposes Compliance with Domestic Regulations: Service providers, including those enabling cross-border payments, are required to comply with the regulatory framework set by the Lao PDR authorities. This includes the use of electronic transaction systems approved by relevant ministries, such as the Ministry of Post and Telecommunications and the Bank of the Lao PDR.

Lao PDR Law on Electronic Transactions allows payments over the internet and other forms of electronic transactions, but it emphasizes that financial transactions must align with national security, financial stability, and anti-money laundering standards. Payments should be conducted through legally recognized systems, often implying the need for a local bank as the intermediary.

Lao PDR.act: Notification on VAT obligations from electronic commerce and digital platform services in Lao PDR, No. 0541/MOF 2022
Lao PDR.act_norm: notification on vat obligations from electronic commerce and digital platform services in lao pdr no 0541 mof 2022
acts_norm: notification on vat obligations from electronic commerce and digital platform services in lao pdr no 0541 mof 2022
Lao PDR.impact:
The Notification on VAT obligations from electronic commerce and digital platform services in Lao PDR, it is stated that foreign entities providing digital services in Lao PDR must register and fulfill VAT obligations through a local bank account. Specifically, the provider must report transactions and fulfill tax payments through the government’s online system, which requires the use of a local bank account for transactions​. Thus, this implies that setting up a local bank account is essential for both operational and regulatory purposes in Lao PDR. 

Article 2 of the notification on VAT obligations for electronic commerce and digital platform services in Lao PDR explicitly mentions the affected digital services. The services include platforms such as:
	•	Streaming Services: Netflix, Apple TV+, Disney+
	•	Digital Content and Applications: YouTube, TikTok, JOOX, Humble, Zoom, CODASHOP
	•	Social Media Advertising: Facebook, Google
	•	Booking Services: Agoda, Booking.com, Airbnb
	•	E-commerce Platforms: Shopee, Lazada

Lao PDR.act_norm: decree of the president of the lao people s democratic republic on the promulgation regarding to the law on payment system 2017 law on payment system no 45 na 2023 order on the controlling foreign currency no 10 pm 2023
acts_norm: decree of the president of the lao people s democratic republic on the promulgation regarding to the law on payment system 2017

Lao PDR.impact:
Article 6 (Domain of enforcement). This Law enforces to domestic and foreign individual, entity and organization that engaging in the Lao PDR’s payment system activities in domestic and abroad. 

Article 19 (Electronic Money) states that electronic money is a payment instrument with a money value which stored in the magnetic band, subscriber identity module or program for which the user spend cash or deposit to buy the value of electronic money from payment services provider in order to pay for goods, service and other debt. The unit of electronic money shall be in Kip only.

There is no specific mention of mandated currency requirements for international payments in the context of online payments. However, there are general references to the use of the national currency, Lao Kip, for transactions within the country, and foreign currency regulations apply broadly to cross-border transactions. 

To ensure compliance with these regulations, businesses conducting international payments typically need to adhere to the general foreign exchange laws and regulations. For example, under the Order on the Controlling Foreign Currency, No. 10/PM (2023), Article 2 states that businesses involved in cross-border transactions must use official foreign exchange channels, and payments must follow specific exchange guidelines to ensure the stability of the currency. This article mandates the use of official foreign exchange channels for transactions and provides detailed requirements to ensure compliance with the national foreign exchange policies.

Lao PDR.act:
Decision on Electronic Signatures No. 1101/MPT 2020; 

Law on Electronic Transaction (Amended) No. 31/NA 2022
Lao PDR.act_norm: decision on electronic signatures no 1101 mpt 2020 law on electronic transaction no 31 na 2022
acts_norm: decision on electronic signatures no 1101 mpt 2020
law on electronic transaction no 31 na 2022
Lao PDR.impact:
There are no specific national standards for payment security in Lao PDR that deviate significantly from recognized international standards. In fact, many of the security requirements in the Decision on Electronic Signatures, No. 1101/MPT (2020), align closely with international standards, such as the Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) 140-2, which is widely recognized internationally for cryptographic security.

Article 43 specifies that key pair generation for electronic signatures must follow FIPS 140-2 Level 3 standards.
Article 44 outlines cryptographic module controls and also mandates that key management practices follow FIPS 140-2 Level 3 standards, which is an international standard for secure cryptographic operations​.
Thus, while Lao PDR has its own set of regulations, these largely follow global standards for cryptographic security in digital transactions.

Lao PDR seem to align with international standards with respect to electronic transactions and digital signatures, as described in the Law on Electronic Transactions. The specific regulations for secure digital signatures (Article 26) are in compliance with the standards defined by the Ministry of Science and Technology. Additionally, the law allows for the recognition of foreign digital signature certificates if they meet equivalent levels of genuineness and reliability, consistent with international standards (Article 29).

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Payment System (Amendment) No. 45/NA 2023
;
Law on Anti-Money Laundering and
Counter-Financing of Terrorism No. 012/PO 2015
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on payment system no 45 na 2023 law on anti money laundering and counter financing of terrorism no 012 po 2015
acts_norm: law on payment system no 45 na 2023
law on anti money laundering and
counter financing of terrorism no 012 po 2015
Lao PDR.impact:
There are licensing requirements in place for electronic payment and signature providers in Lao PDR, which include some restrictive conditions:

Law on Payment System (Amendment) No. 45/NA 2023:
1- The requirements to be licensed to operate a payment business are outlined in:
* Article 7: This article specifies that applicants must demonstrate financial capability, have proper infrastructure in place, and comply with technical standards set by the Bank of the Lao PDR.
* Article 8: The applicant must meet specific financial and management requirements, including a minimum capital requirement and a clear organizational structure. Besides financial capacity, it also includes technical expertise, and compliance with legal and regulatory requirements specific to payment systems.
2- Factors for Granting a License and Timeframe: Article 9 specifies the factors considered when granting a license, such as the financial soundness of the applicant, the suitability of its shareholders, and the quality of its management. The decision on licensing must be made within 60 days of receiving a complete application, as outlined in Article 10.
3- The restrictions on the use of the license, including assignment, transfer, or letting someone else use it, are mentioned in Article 15. It prohibits license holders from assigning or transferring the license without prior approval from the licensing authority.

Law on Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Financing of Terrorism (AML-CFT):
- Article 19 requires financial institutions to implement AML/CFT programs, which include developing policies and ongoing training, auditing internal procedures, and evaluating AML efforts. These requirements must be adhered to by payment service providers for licensing and compliance.
- Article 20 outlines the need for risk assessment and risk-based management for financial service providers, including payment businesses, to mitigate risks associated with money laundering and terrorism financing.

Lao PDR.act: Decision on Electronic Signatures No. 1101/MPT 2020
Lao PDR.act_norm: decision on electronic signatures no 1101 mpt 2020
acts_norm: decision on electronic signatures no 1101 mpt 2020
Lao PDR.impact:
There are licensing requirements in place for signature providers in Lao PDR, which include some restrictive conditions. 

Article 11 (Minimum Registered Capital) of the Decision on Electronic Signatures, No. 1101/MPT (2020), specifies that entities applying for licenses to provide electronic signature services must have a minimum registered capital of 10 billion Kip. 
Moreover, Article 11(6) (Lao Technical Expertise) requires that service providers employ Lao technical personnel to manage the electronic signature certification system and database.

Lao PDR.act_norm: law on payment system no 45 na 2023 law on anti money laundering and counter financing of terrorism no 012 po 2015 decree on entrust and responsibilities in implementing the activities of anti money laundering and counter financing of terrorism no 127 gov 2020 decision on the reporting of wire transfers in exceeding the specified limit no 963 bol 2015

Lao PDR.impact:
There is no explicit ceiling on the maximum amount that can be paid through electronic payment methods. The regulations concerning online and electronic payments seem to focus more on taxation, compliance, and security requirements, rather than setting strict monetary caps for transactions.

Article 30 of the Law on AML-CFT, which states: The Bank of the Lao PDR is responsible for setting thresholds for cash transactions and wire transfers, which must be reported to AMLIO (Anti-Money Laundering Intelligence Office) if they exceed a certain threshold. However, the actual limit values are determined by the Bank of the Lao PDR and are not directly specified (Similarly mentioned in Article 14(13) of the Decree 127/Gov 2020).

However, Decision No:963/BOL - Article 2 specify reporting requirement of Wire transfers in exceeding the specified limit of wire transfers equaling 8,000,000 kip or more per instance, as specified by the AMLIO periodically. Furthermore, Article 11 (Exceptions) Reporting on Wire transfers in exceeding the specified limit is not needed for payment of goods or services through credit cards, debit cards, and others.

acts_norm: law on payment system no 45 na 2023
decision on currency exchange service no 1026 bol 2022
law on electronic transaction no 31 na 2022
Lao PDR.impact:
The "Law on Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Financing of Terrorism" discusses financial institutions and designated non-financial businesses and professions (DNFBPs), such as money transfer service companies and banks, that are subject to regulations aimed at preventing money laundering and the financing of terrorism​. However, it does not explicitly state that specific intermediaries must be used for online payments. Similarly, the Decision on Currency Exchange Service, No. 1026/BOL does not contain provisions that require the use of specific intermediaries for online transactions​.

Article 33 in the Law on Electronic Transactions defines an intermediary as any individual or legal entity that provides services to others for sending, receiving, or storing data messages, hosting temporarily, providing access to communication systems, or handling data messages and electronic documents. This article outlines that intermediaries play a significant role in facilitating electronic transactions. However, the law does not appear to mandate specific intermediaries for online payments. Instead, it establishes the roles and liabilities of intermediaries, such as compliance with regulations and procedures from relevant authorities like the Ministry of Posts and Telecommunications, and outlines their responsibilities, including liability in certain cases of civil or criminal wrongdoing​.

Lao PDR.act:
Law on Electronic Transaction (Amended) No. 31/NA 2022;

Decision on Electronic Signatures No. 1101/MPT 2020
Lao PDR.act_norm: law on electronic transaction no 31 na 2022 decision on electronic signatures no 1101 mpt 2020
acts_norm: law on electronic transaction no 31 na 2022
decision on electronic signatures no 1101 mpt 2020
Lao PDR.impact:
There is no requirements mandating the use of specific intermediaries for online payments. Restrictions on Secure Digital Signature Providers: Article 36 of the Law on Electronic Transactions prohibits digital signature providers from engaging in fraudulent activities or misusing personal information. They are also restricted from creating, using, or publishing digital signatures for any unlawful purposes, and from using information in ways that harm national stability, security, or social order.

Article 36 details the following prohibitions: Providing false information in registration applications. Using personal data for purposes other than stated. Engaging in unauthorized secure digital certification activities. Misusing signature certification services. Facilitating transactions that cause national security risks​.

Intermediary Liabilities: Article 38 of the Law on Electronic Transactions defines restrictions for intermediaries (e.g., payment processors, hosting services). Intermediaries are prohibited from: Providing online transaction services without proper authorization. Facilitating unlawful activities that could endanger national security or social order. Monitoring the contents of electronic messages for which they are not responsible, unless required by law.

Article 35 emphasizes the intermediary’s liability if they knowingly process transactions that violate legal or security protocols​. Prohibitions for Users of Digital Signature Services:

Article 37 of the Law on Electronic Transactions restricts users of digital signature services from: Using digital signatures for fraudulent purposes. Providing false information in digital signature applications. Continuing to use invalid digital signatures.
Engaging in unlawful activities through digital signatures that affect national stability. These restrictions aim to prevent users from exploiting digital signatures for malicious activities​

Lao PDR.act_norm: the law of customs no 81 na 2020 decree on the implementation of the customs law no 362 pmo 2007
acts_norm: the law of customs no 81 na 2020
decree on the implementation of the customs law no 362 pmo 2007
Lao PDR.impact: Lao PDR did not have a de minimis rule until 2020. An Amendment of Law on Customs 2020 introduces a de minimis threshold for goods exempt from customs duties is set at 1,500,000 LAK (around 150 USD) (amended Article 94 Exemption of Customs Duties). This threshold applies to non-commercial imported goods, such as personal effects. For example, goods worth no more than this amount are subject to customs duty exemption.

Lao PDR.act_norm: the law of customs no 81 na 2020 decree on the implementation of the customs law no 362 pmo 2007
acts_norm: the law of customs no 81 na 2020
decree on the implementation of the customs law no 362 pmo 2007
Lao PDR.impact:
There are no explicit legal mechanisms or regulations directly related to the imposition of customs duties on electronic transmissions in Lao PDR. Article 9 of the Law of Customs (Amendment), customs duties apply to all categories of physical goods or non-commercial goods imported into or exported out of Lao PDR, as defined by the Tariff Nomenclature of Lao PDR. This article confirms that customs duties are limited to physical goods. Furthermore, the law does not explicitly address the imposition of customs duties on electronic transmissions or digital services, indicating the absence of a legal framework for customs duties on these types of transactions.

Article 1 of the Decree on the Implementation of the Customs Law No. 362/PM (2007), customs duties apply to the import, export, and transit of goods within Lao PDR. The decree ensures the enforcement of customs regulations, particularly for the importation and exportation of physical goods, which align with Lao PDR’s obligations under international treaties and agreements. However, no explicit legal framework is mentioned regarding the imposition of customs duties on electronic transmissions, reaffirming that customs duties are limited to physical goods.

Article 14 (Importation of Office and Household Equipment): This article allows the temporary importation of office equipment, such as computers, copy machines, and other related items, under the temporary importation regime. These items are authorized to be imported if they have a long use life and can be re-exported in the same condition and quantity. The equipment is subject to customs duties and other obligations unless re-exported as required under the temporary importation rules.
	•	Office equipment covered includes:
	•	Computers
	•	Copy machines
	•	Typing machines
	•	Fax machines
	•	Cabinets, among others
This provision primarily applies to the temporary importation of office equipment for projects or administrative use and outlines the regulations for re-exportation and customs compliance.

The country aligns with the global practice, particularly the WTO moratorium on customs duties for digital products and services transmitted electronically, which has been in place since 1998. The 1998 declaration also included a so-called moratorium stating that “members will continue their current practice of not imposing customs duties on electronic transmission.

Lao PDR.act:
Decree on the Management and Use of Internet, and Domain Name of the Lao PDR No.164/Gov;

Lao PDR.impact:
The Decree No. 164/Gov Article 9 states that the National Internet Center (NIC) is the body that authorizes individual, legal entity and organization both within the country and abroad to be a sole service provider related to Domain Name in the Lao PDR. Individual, legal entities and organizations who wish to provide the service and use the internet under the Domain Name “.la” shall be registered with the National Internet Center. Individual and legal entity existed within the country and in abroad engaging in Internet related business can register and use the system of Domain name ".la" (Article 10). 

There are no specific references or articles requiring a physical presence or a local representative to register a local domain name for conducting electronic retail in Lao PDR. Additionally, there are no explicit restrictions related to domain name registration for electronic commerce. The laws primarily regulate electronic transactions, digital signatures, and service providers but do not impose strict requirements on the registration of domain names for retail businesses that involve the need for local physical presence or representative.

Lao PDR Law on Telecommunications No. 09/NA (2011), Article 12 does not mention specific conditions like physical presence, local representative, or other limitations. It only mentions that the Ministry oversees the assignment and management of domain names, without specifying additional criteria or restrictions for registrants. Article 13 does not mention specific conditions like physical presence, local representative, or other limitations. It only states that individuals, legal entities, or organizations must register their domain names with the telecommunication customer services or the office of Posts and Telecommunications.

Lao PDR.impact: The Law on Consumer Protection No. 02/NA, dated 30 June 2010, consumer protection laws in Lao PDR apply to all goods and services, including those sold online. Article 1 (Objectives) and Article 2 (Consumer Protection) outline the general objectives and scope of consumer protection, including the protection of consumers' rights, safety, and legitimate interests from impacts resulting from the consumption of goods and services​. Article 7 (Scope of Application) specifies that the law applies to individuals, legal entities, and organizations operating within the country, including those conducting online commerce, and thus consumer protection is applicable to e-commerce. This suggests that consumer protection laws are applicable to online commerce in the country​.

Mongolia.impact:
Mongolia currently does not have any legal acts implementing the WTO Agreement on Subsidies and Countervailing Measures and the Anti-Dumping Agreement. The Government of Mongolia approved the regulations for implementing the WTO Safeguards Agreement through Resolution No. 24 on January 27, 2021.

Moreover, as per Article 10.2 of the Constitution of Mongolia, an international treaty of Mongolia is applied in the same manner as domestic legislation once the law of ratification or accession of Mongolia comes into force. Mongolia is a signatory of the WTO Agreement and WTO Anti-Dumping Agreement. 

Mongolia has not imposed anti-dumping and countervailing duties, or safeguard measures against ICT-related goods imported from other countries.

Revised Law on the Procurement of Goods, Works, and Services by State and Local Property Assets;

Resolution of the Government of Mongolia No. 90 on the Update and Approval of the List

resolution of the government of mongolia no 90 on the update and approval of the list
Mongolia.impact:
According to Article 8.1 of the Revised Law on the Procurement of Goods, Works, and Services by State and Local Property Assets, The tender documents shall specify that goods that meet the quality and standard requirements that can be provided by domestic production will be purchased from domestic manufacturers. Moreover, Article 8.2, the list of names and types of goods specified in Article 8.1 of this Law shall be jointly developed by the central state administrative body in charge of economy, development, heavy and light industry, and approved by the Government.

The list of products reserved to local companies is found in Annex to Government Resolution No. 90. This includes all types of electrical connection tools and equipment, including collectors, connectors, switches, converters, meters, transformers, distributors, isolators, and control panels.

Moreover, according to Article 8.3 of the Revised Law on the Procurement of Goods, Works, and Services by State and Local Property Assets, the procuring entity should not prohibit foreign parties from participating in tenders with a budget of more than MNT 30 billion for work, MNT 1 billion for goods, and MNT 300 million for services. Purchases that exceed these values require an international open tender to be announced mandatorily.

Mongolia.act:
Law on the Procurement of Goods, Works, and Services by State and Local Property Assets;

Law on Organization Secret
Mongolia.act_norm: law on the procurement of goods works and services by state and local property assets law on organization secret
acts_norm: law on the procurement of goods works and services by state and local property assets
law on organization secret
Mongolia.impact: Mongolia does not demand companies to surrender source code, encryption, or other trade secrets, like patents, to win public procurement bids. It also doesn't require the use of specific encryption standards for bidding success.

Mongolia.act: Law on the Procurement of Goods, Works, and Services by State and Local Property Assets
Mongolia.act_norm: law on the procurement of goods works and services by state and local property assets
acts_norm: law on the procurement of goods works and services by state and local property assets
Mongolia.impact:
Under Article 8 of the Law on the Procurement of Goods, Works, and Services by State and Local Property Assets, preference is given to domestic goods meeting quality standards. Article 8.1 prioritizes goods from domestic production, with a list developed by the central state administrative organization and approved by the Government (Article 8.2). Additional preferences include goods from recycled materials, those using local resources, innovative products, and those produced by businesses employing disabled workers (Article 8.4). 

Moreover, Article 8.5 gives preference to participants using domestic goods in their work. Discounts are provided on tender prices: 10% for domestic goods, or 15% if from small or medium-sized enterprises (Article 8.6). For work tenders, reductions are given for local operations and Mongolian-owned entities (Article 8.7). However, general contracts do not receive these preferences (Article 8.8), and tender documents must specify the required information for priority calculations (Article 8.9).

Note that the term "general contract method" means purchasing goods and services from the online store by entering into a general contract with one or more participants that sets the maximum unit price and other conditions for the goods and services that the customer frequently purchases or regularly needs for a certain period of time (Article 4.1.8).

Mongolia.act_norm: constitution of mongolia law on investment mineral law
acts_norm: constitution of mongolia
law on investment
mineral law
Mongolia.impact: Article 6.2 of the Constitution unequivocally affirms the public ownership of land, its resources, forests, water resources, and wildlife. This fundamental principle is firmly enshrined in the Mineral Law, which dictates that the state will assert ownership of up to 50% of the assets invested in strategic deposits with reserves explored using state budget funds and up to 34% of the assets invested in strategic deposits with reserves explored without state budget funds. Furthermore, it confidently asserts that the percentage of state ownership can be precisely determined based on the investment made in the deposit. There are no limits for foreign equity in digital trade-related sectors.

Mongolia.act: Law on Investment
Mongolia.act_norm: law on investment
acts_norm: law on investment
Mongolia.impact: According to Mongolia's Investment Law, foreign investors are not required to have a Mongolian joint venture partner, enabling businesses to be wholly foreign-owned and operated.

Mongolia.act: Law on Investment
Mongolia.act_norm: law on investment
acts_norm: law on investment
Mongolia.impact: No nationality or residency requirements exist for the board of directors or managers in any business sector, including the digital trade.

Mongolia.act: Law on Investment
Mongolia.act_norm: law on investment
acts_norm: law on investment
Mongolia.impact: Foreign state-owned legal entities that own 33% or more of the total issued shares of Mongolian legal entities in mining, banking and finance, press, information, and communication must obtain operating permission under Article 21 of the Investment Law. The application for authorization should be directly submitted to the state central administrative body responsible for investment affairs in Mongolia, along with the necessary documents. The central state administrative body must resolve the request within 45 days, as specified in Articles 5.2 and 5.3 of the Law on Permits.

Mongolia.act_norm: law on investment the company law law on state registation of legal entities
acts_norm: law on investment
the company law
law on state registation of legal entities
Mongolia.impact: Article 4 of the Investment Law states that foreign and domestic investors shall conduct activities in Mongolia upon registration to state registration by the Company Law, Law on State Registration of Legal Entities, and other relevant laws and regulations. Moreover, legal entities must apply for authorization from the state through a representative office and authorized representative in Mongolia. Article 5.1.1 of the Investment Law also permits foreign companies to establish their representative office or a subsidiary in Mongolia.

Mongolia.act: Law of Mongolia on Patent
Mongolia.act_norm: law of mongolia on patent
acts_norm: law of mongolia on patent
Mongolia.impact: Article 15 of the Patent Law focuses on filing of patent applications for inventions and utility. According to Article 15.2, an intellectual property agent may represent a patent application. According to Article 15.3 of this law, a foreign citizen, a stateless person, or a foreign legal entity who does not permanently reside in Mongolia or does not engage in activities shall be represented as an authorized intellectual property agent when applying for a patent for a new product or industrial design.

Mongolia.act: Law of Mongolia on Patent
Mongolia.act_norm: law of mongolia on patent
acts_norm: law of mongolia on patent
Mongolia.impact:
The Patent Law of Mongolia addresses civil and administrative procedures, remedies, and provisional measures. Chapter 7 (Articles 46 to 52) focuses on the protection of patent rights and patent owners' rights. According to Articles 46.2.3 and 46.2.4, patent owners have the right to protect their inventions, utility models, and product designs from unauthorized use and to file lawsuits in court to enforce their rights. Article 55 (Liability for Violators) states that any person or legal entity violating this law shall be subject to the provisions of the Criminal Code or held liable as prescribed by law.

Additionally, provisional measures are addressed under Article 53. Chapter 8 outlines the role of the intellectual property authority and the dispute resolution council. Specifically, state inspectors have the power to stop violations (Article 53.1.3), seize infringing items (Article 53.1.3), prohibit sales (Article 53.1.4), and destroy infringing products (Article 53.1.4). These actions serve as provisional measures to prevent further harm to the rightsholder while a legal determination is pending.

Mongolia.act: Law on Infringement
Mongolia.act_norm: law on infringement
acts_norm: law on infringement
Mongolia.impact: The Law on Infringement entered into force in 2017. The State Intellectual Property Inspectors of the Intellectual Property Office of Mongolia (IPOM) Enforcement Department are responsible for enforcing IPR-related laws under the prosecutor's supervision in accordance with the Law on Infringement and its procedure. Since 2013, the Enforcement Department has conducted 1,414 cases and determined 1,230 IPR infringements.

Mongolia.act: Law of Mongolia on Copyrights
Mongolia.act_norm: law of mongolia on copyrights
acts_norm: law of mongolia on copyrights
Mongolia.impact: The Law on Copyright provides a clear fair use regime. Article 38 of the Law provides circumstances where the works were used without contradicting the normal exploitation of works and without affecting the rights and legal interests of the right holders shall not be deemed as copyright infringement. Aricles 39 to 46 further describe the partial exploitation conditions of work for activities such as news, academic, research and teaching purpose.
Mongolia.timeframe: Since January 2006, last amended in May 2021

Mongolia.act: Law of Mongolia on Copyrights
Mongolia.act_norm: law of mongolia on copyrights
acts_norm: law of mongolia on copyrights
Mongolia.impact:
The Copyright Law Chapter 7 (Articles 17 to 25) prescribes copyright protection to particular works, including computer program (Article 22) and database (Article 23). Regarding infringement and liability, Article 56 concerns infringement of copyright and related rights. The infringement also covers the placement, making internet (digital) linking or development of works in the social network without authorization of right holder (Article 56.1.5). Moreover, Article 57 addresses the consequences of copyright infringement. Individuals who violate copyright law face penalties as outlined in the Criminal Code or the Law on Infringement. Compensation for damages resulting from the infringement of exclusive rights is governed by the Civil Code. Additionally, devices and technology used for unauthorized reproduction of copyrighted works will be confiscated and become state property.

However, the exploitation of work or objects without an authorization legitimate software products remain rare in Mongolia, with the General Authority for Intellectual Property and State Registration (GAIPSR) estimating in early 2017 that at least 85 percent of the domestic market uses pirated software. GAIPSR enforces the law where it can but the scale of the problem dwarfs its capacity to deal with it. Second, pirated optical media are also readily available and subject to spotty anti-piracy enforcement. The growth of online downloads of pirated digital media by individuals, local Mongolian TV stations using pirated videos, radio broadcasters playing pirated music, and cellular service providers offering pirated ringtones has eclipsed local production and imports of fake CDs, videos, and DVDs. GAIPSR acknowledges that most local public and privately held TV stations, some 184 at latest count, regularly broadcast pirated materials; however, GAIPSR hesitates to move on these broadcasters, most of which are connected to major government or political figures. GAIPSR rarely initiates action on its own without prompts from rights holders.
Mongolia.timeframe: Since January 2006, last amended in May 2021

Mongolia.act:
The Law of Mongolia on Information Transparency and Right to Information;

Law on Organizational Secrets

Mongolia.act: Law on Organizational Secrets
Mongolia.act_norm: law on organizational secrets
acts_norm: law on organizational secrets
Mongolia.impact: Article 3.2 of the Organizational Secrets Law, enacted in 1995, unequivocally states that any information, technological solutions, projects, research, and analysis documents about the specific official activities of an organization or designated as confidential to safeguard its market and competitive advantages are eligible to be classified as organizational secrets. The organization possesses the exclusive authority to determine what qualifies as a secret under this law. Furthermore, according to Article 9.2, individuals or legal entities found violating this law will be subject to accountability under the Criminal Law or the Law on Violations.

Mongolia.act: Law of Mongolia on Communications
Mongolia.act_norm: law of mongolia on communications
acts_norm: law of mongolia on communications
Mongolia.impact:
According to Article 6.1.14 of the Telecommunications Law enacted in 2001, the Central State Administrative Organization for Telecommunications is mandated to approve the regulation of shared Internet connections. Despite this, the regulation is still pending approval. Mongolia consists of public service networks, domestic networks, and special-use networks. Telecommunications operators are exclusively authorized to utilize shared networks for public service networks.

Moreover, it is becoming more common for active infrastructure sharing for 5G implementation. Some of them (e.g., Vodafone) have implemented 5G Open RAN to work with a broader range of suppliers and drive innovation.
Mongolia.timeframe:
Since October 2001, last amended in December 2019;

Last reviewed in April 2024

Mongolia.impact:
2.5. The service provider shall not refuse, without justification, to lease or share their own network, infrastructure, premises, or equipment with other service providers, except for those defined as resources.
2.5.1. The service provider shall make their network and infrastructure available in accordance with Clause 23.1 of the Communication Law of Mongolia. 
2.5.2 In cases where the service provider holds a dominant position in the market, they shall fully ensure the opportunity for joint use of their own network and infrastructure with other service providers.

Mongolia.act_norm: information and communication network llc mongolian post jsc mongolian telecommunications corporation jsc mongolian telecommunications corporation jsc
acts_norm: information and communication network llc
mongolian post jsc

mongolian telecommunications corporation jsc
Mongolia.impact:
The leading companies in the telecommunications sector in Mongolia include the privately owned Mobicom, Unitel, Univision, Skytel, and Gmobile companies, as well as the state-owned and state-dominated companies such as Mongolian Post, Mongolian Telecom, and Information and Communication Network. 

In terms of total sales in 2022, Mobicom Corporation ranked 19th, Unitel LLC ranked 25th, Univision LLC ranked 50th, Skytel LLC ranked 82nd, Jimobile LLC ranked 95th, Mongolian Post LLC ranked 99th, Data Communications Network LLC ranked 94th, and Mongolian Telecommunications Corporation ranked 100th among all business entities in Mongolia.  

KDDI Corporation from Japan owns 63.9% of Mobicom Corporation's shares, STGM from Japan owns 34.9%, and Mongolia's Newcom Group owns 1.15%. On the other hand, MCS Holding of Mongolia owns 100% of the shares of Unitel Group and Univision LLC, which provides triple internet services. Altai Holding and Shunkhlai Holding jointly own Skytel LLC mobile communications company shares, while GEM International Mongolia owns Gmobile shares. 

The government owns 100% of the shares of Information and Communication Network LLC, which is responsible for the integrated fiber optic cable network. Mongolian Post JSC, which provides postal services, is 66% owned by the State Government, 30.94% by the Ard financial group, and 3.06% by other small shareholders. The government also owns 94.7% of the shares of Mongolian Telecommunications Corporation JSC, with 5.3% owned by foreign and domestic citizens and legal entities.)

Mongolia.act: Law of Mongolia on Communications
Mongolia.act_norm: law of mongolia on communications
acts_norm: law of mongolia on communications
Mongolia.impact: Mongolia does not implement functional and/or accounting separation requirements. Under Article 17.2 of the Law of Mongolia on Communication, the primary telecommunication network is defined as the network providing public services, including intercountry and intercity transmission and intercity connection facilities. Additionally, Article 17.4 of this law states that all government and private enterprises can establish and own the primary network.
Mongolia.timeframe: Since October 2001, last amended in July 2019

Mongolia.act: Law on Permit
Mongolia.act_norm: law on permit
acts_norm: law on permit
Mongolia.impact:
Following Article 8.1.9 of the Permit Law enacted by the Parliament in 2022, the Central State Administrative Organization for Telecommunications or Communication Regulatory Commission is mandated to issue specialized licenses for activities in 15 specific areas of information and communication. This provision unequivocally affirms that no legal obstacles should prevent a sole entity from participating in various functional information and communication activities and establishing and operating its network. Specifically, the authorized person has no right to refuse to accept the application for a permit without reason, and shall notify the applicant of the acceptance of the application and record it in the register (Article 5.2.1). The authorized person shall verify the application without 10 working days for special permit and within 5 days for simple approval. 

Moreover, there is no restriction on licensing renewal. The license holder shall notify the authority 45 days before the expireation of the permit.
Mongolia.timeframe: Since June, 2022, last amended in July 2023

Mongolia.act: Law of Mongolia on Communications
Mongolia.act_norm: law of mongolia on communications
acts_norm: law of mongolia on communications
Mongolia.impact: The Communications Regulatory Commission (CRC) ensures fair competition for businesses and citizens in the telecommunications market. The CRC has the authority to issue special licenses as required by law and to make decisions based on professional judgment. It was established in 2002 with financial support from the World Bank and consulting services from Analysis LLC of the United States and McCarthy Tetrault LLC of Canada. According to the law, the chair and members of the CRC are appointed by the Prime Minister, which may lead to the Commission being influenced by the government's decisions.
Mongolia.timeframe: Since October 2001, last amended in December 2019

Mongolia.act: Law on Personal Data Protection
Mongolia.act_norm: law on personal data protection
acts_norm: law on personal data protection
Mongolia.impact: In general, there is no ban or local processing requirements in Mongolia.

Mongolia.act: Law on Personal Data Protection
Mongolia.act_norm: law on personal data protection
acts_norm: law on personal data protection
Mongolia.impact:
There is no explicit data local storage requirement.

The Ministry shall establish the requirements for data security, evaluation guidance, and storage technology requirements for data collection, processing, and use (Article 20.2 of the Law on Personal Data Protection). However, so far no approved regulation that provides detailed procedures in this regard is observed.

Moreover, following Article 18.6 of the Law on Transparency of Public Information, enacted by the Parliament in 2021, it is mandatory for the data controller's system to fully adhere to the government's communication and information technology guidelines and security standards. There is no requirement for the location of the information processing server.
