Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 126 fz on communication
acts_norm: federal law no 126 fz on communication
Russian Federation.impact: Article 46, part 5.1 requires Russian telecommunications operators, particularly those whose networks handle high-speed data traffic, to install special technical systems designed to block or filter internet content deemed illegal or threatening by the state. These systems are meant to operate automatically under centralized government control. If such systems are in place, the operators themselves are not obligated to manually restrict access to prohibited information. Furthermore, if any network disruptions occur as a result of these systems, the operators are not held legally responsible or subject to penalties for licensing violations.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since July 2003, last amended in December 2024

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No.149-FZ "On information, information technologies and the protection of information"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 149 fz on information information technologies and the protection of information
acts_norm: federal law no 149 fz on information information technologies and the protection of information
Russian Federation.impact:
Article 10.1. Responsibilities of the organizer of information dissemination on the Internet
Part 4.2 establishes a censorship complicnace regime. It states that if a government authority asks the service to block a user from sending illegal content, the service must do so within 24 hours. “Illegal content” here refers to anything banned under Russian law, like extremism, drugs, or politically sensitive material.

Furthermore, Article 15.1 of Russia's Federal Law No. 149-FZ establishes a legal framework for blocking websites that contain information prohibited from dissemination within the Russian Federation. It introduces the creation of a Unified Register of domain names, web pages, and IP addresses that can be used to identify such content. The register is maintained by Roskomnadzor, Russia's media and communications regulator. Information subject to blocking includes content related to child pornography, drug production and distribution, methods of suicide, illegal gambling, unauthorized online sales of alcohol or pharmaceuticals, and more. Once a site is added to the register, hosting providers must notify site owners and require removal of the content. If the site owners fail to comply, hosting providers and Internet service providers are obligated to restrict access. The article also provides a procedure for appealing inclusion in the register and for removing a site once the violations have been corrected.

Article 15.8 of the Law stipulates that VPN providers, along with other owners of information and telecommunication networks or resources that enable access to restricted content, are prohibited from allowing users in the Russian Federation to use their services to reach websites or networks that have been officially blocked. The law requires that such providers connect to a federal state information system maintained by Roskomnadzor which contains a registry of banned websites and resources. Once connected, VPN providers are obligated to block access to all listed content. If they do not comply, Roskomnadzor is authorized to identify the provider, issue warnings, and instruct internet service providers to block access to the non-compliant VPNs within 24 hours.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since July 2006, last amended in July 2024

Russian Federation.act:
Federal Law No. 2124-I
"On Mass Media"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 2124 i on mass media
acts_norm: federal law no 2124 i
on mass media
Russian Federation.impact:
Article 56.2 (Suspension of the Activities of a Mass Media Outlet, Invalidation of Its Registration, or Termination of Its Broadcasting License Based on a Request from the Prosecutor General of the Russian Federation or Their Deputies) outlines the legal grounds and procedures for suspending the operations of a mass media outlet in Russia, invalidating its registration, or terminating its broadcasting license. This can occur if the Prosecutor General or their deputies determine that the outlet is distributing false or harmful information under the guise of factual reporting. Such content includes material deemed to threaten public safety, discredit the Russian Armed Forces, encourage illegal public gatherings, or promote extremist activity. The initial suspension can last up to three months and, in the case of repeated violations, up to six months. If the outlet stops spreading the offending content, it may be allowed to resume operations early. If violations persist, the Prosecutor General can request the full revocation of the outlet’s registration or license. The federal media oversight authority is responsible for enforcing these measures and updating official records accordingly.

Furthermore, according to the Open Observatory of Network Interference (a global community measuring internet censorship around the world), as part of their analysis of OONI data collected from Russia between 1st September 2023 to 1st September 2024, OONI confirmed the blocking of 279 domains that are categorized as “News Media (NEWS)” based on the Citizen Lab test list category codes. These domains pertain to a wide range of different types of news media websites, including both foreign (such as BBC, Deutsche Welle, and Voice of America) and independent Russian news media sites (such as Meduza). They also include several Ukrainian news media sites, the Kavkaz Center (a Chechen internet news agency which is also blocked in Kazakhstan), and investigative journalist site Bellingcat – all of which have been blocked in Russia for years.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since December 1991, last amended in November 2024

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No. 236-FZ “On the Activities of Foreign Entities in the Information and Telecommunications Network “Internet” in the Russian Federation”
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 236 fz on the activities of foreign entities in the information and telecommunications network internet in the russian federation
acts_norm: federal law no 236 fz on the activities of foreign entities in the information and telecommunications network internet in the russian federation
Russian Federation.coverage: Foreign entity operating on the Internet
Russian Federation.impact:
According to Article 5 of Federal Law No. 236-FZ, a foreign entity carrying out activities on the Internet within the territory of the Russian Federation is required to:
[...]
3) establish a branch or open a representative office or incorporate a Russian legal entity and ensure the operation of such branch, representative office, or Russian legal entity within the territory of the Russian Federation in accordance with the requirements set out in Article 7 of this Federal Law.

According to Article 9 of Federal Law No. 236-FZ, in the event that a foreign entity carrying out activities on the Internet within the territory fails to comply, one or more of the following enforcement measures may be applied against such foreign entity:
[...]
7) partial restriction of access to the foreign entity’s information resource;
8) complete restriction of access to the foreign entity’s information resource.

Article 7. Requirements for a Branch or Representative Office of a Foreign Entity Operating on the Internet in the Russian Federation, or a Russian Legal Entity Established by a Foreign Entity Operating on the Internet in the Russian Federation

A branch or representative office of a foreign entity operating on the Internet in the Russian Federation, or a Russian legal entity established by such a foreign entity, must ensure the following:

1. Handling Russian citizens' and organizations' requests, complying with court decisions, and fulfilling orders or requirements from Russian government authorities that apply to the foreign entity operating on the Internet in Russia.

2. Representing the foreign entity’s interests in Russian courts.

3. Taking measures within Russia to restrict access to or remove information that is distributed in violation of Russian laws on information, information technologies, and data protection, if such an obligation is required by Russian law.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since July 2021, last amended in June 2024

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No. 38-FZ "On Advertising"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 38 fz on advertising
acts_norm: federal law no 38 fz on advertising
Russian Federation.impact:
Article 7 of the Law on Advertisement defines the list of goods and services that may not be advertised in any platform including on internet in Russia. Goods whose production is prohibited under Russian legislation, including certain weapons, drugs, explosive materials, human body parts or tissue, goods subject to state registration in the absence of such registration (e.g., unregistered medical devices), goods that require licensing or other permits in the absence of such permits (e.g., insurance services provided by an unlicensed insurer), tobacco and tobacco products and medical services for abortion. The advertisement of alcoholic products is specifically prohibited on internet. Furthermore, advertisement of certain goods and services, such as gambling and betting services is limited to certain Russian websites only. 

Moreover, under Artilce 7, the advertisement of digital currency, as well as goods (works, services) for the purpose of organizing the circulation and/or circulation of digital currency is also not allowed.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since March 2006, last amended in March 2025

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No. 347-FZ on Amendment to the  "On Online Advertising"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 347 fz on amendment to the on online advertising
acts_norm: federal law no 347 fz on amendment to the on online advertising
Russian Federation.impact:
The Amendment to the Federal Law On Advertising No. 38-FZ, entered into force in September 2022, requires that all online advertising disseminated within the Russian Federation be properly marked in accordance with Article 18.1 of the law. Marking entails assigning a unique advertising identifier, or token, to each advertisement, labeling the material with the word “advertising,” and disclosing information about the advertiser (i.e., the manufacturer or seller of the advertised product or service). 

This information must be submitted to the Unified Register of Online Advertising (UROA), a centralized database operated by Roskomnadzor. The process involves two key actors: the Advertising Data Operator (ADO), responsible for collecting and transmitting advertising data to the UROA, and the Operator of the Advertising System (OAS), which refers to the platform distributing the advertisement.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since September 2022

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No. 479-FZ on Amendment to the  "On Online Advertising"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 479 fz on amendment to the on online advertising
acts_norm: federal law no 479 fz on amendment to the on online advertising
Russian Federation.impact:
The amendments to the Federal Law On Advertising No. 38-FZ, entered into force in March 2025, introduce new obligations and broaden the scope of regulation for entities involved in distributing advertisements online, particularly with regard to social advertising. The amendment extends to include owners of advertisement placement services and aggregators of information on goods and services.

According to the new Article 10, Part 3.1 (Social Advertising on the Internet), online platforms: advertising distributors, ad placement service owners, and owners of aggregators of goods and services, shall allocate at least 5% of their annual advertising volume or user reach to unpaid social advertising. The amendments under Part 3.10 and 3.15 requires these entities to track and report the number of  users accessing their platforms daily and the volume of advertising distributed. 

Moreover, under Article 10, Part 3.13-2,  owners of aggregators of information on goods and services are required to install government-approved software designed to track the number of users accessing their platforms. This requirement applies only to platforms that fall under the scope of Part 3.13, which was amended to increase the threshold from 100,000 to 200,000 daily users. The software will be designated by the federal executive body responsible for mass media, communications, and information technologies.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since March 2025

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No. 72-FZ "On Amendments to Article 12 of the Federal Law 'On Countering Extremist Activities' and the Federal Law 'On Advertising'"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 72 fz on amendments to article 12 of the federal law on countering extremist activities and the federal law on advertising
acts_norm: federal law no 72 fz on amendments to article 12 of the federal law on countering extremist activities and the federal law on advertising
Russian Federation.impact:
According to the Amendments, the following content will be added to Article 12 of the Federal Law No. 114-FZ "On Countering Extremist Activities":

"If a court issues a final and binding decision on the liquidation or prohibition of the activities of a public or religious association or any other organization on the grounds provided by this Federal Law or Federal Law No. 35-FZ of March 6, 2006 'On Countering Terrorism', the dissemination of advertising on the information resources of such association or organization in information and telecommunication networks, including the Internet, is prohibited in accordance with the legislation of the Russian Federation on advertising."

Furthermore, Article 5 of the Federal Law No. 38-FZ "On Advertising" shall be supplemented with Part 10.7 as follows:

"10.7. The dissemination of advertising on the information resources of a foreign or international organization whose activities have been recognized as undesirable in the territory of the Russian Federation, in accordance with Federal Law No. 272-FZ of December 28, 2012 'On Measures of Influence on Persons Involved in Violations of Fundamental Human Rights and Freedoms, and the Rights and Freedoms of Citizens of the Russian Federation', or on the resources of a public or religious association or other organization for which a final and binding court decision has been issued on liquidation or prohibition of activities on the grounds established by Federal Law No. 114-FZ of July 25, 2002 'On Countering Extremist Activities' or Federal Law No. 35-FZ of March 6, 2006 'On Countering Terrorism', as well as on other information resources access to which is restricted in accordance with the legislation of the Russian Federation on information, information technologies, and information protection, is prohibited."

These amendments will enter into force on September 1, 2025.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since April 2025, entry into force in September 2025

Russian Federation.act: Decision of the Board of the Eurasian Economic Commission No. 30 “On non-tariff regulation measures”
Russian Federation.act_norm: decision of the board of the eurasian economic commission no 30 on non tariff regulation measures
acts_norm: decision of the board of the eurasian economic commission no 30 on non tariff regulation measures
Russian Federation.impact:
The list of goods subject to import and export prohibitions or restrictions in the Eurasian Economic Union, including Russia is determined by the Decision of the Board of the Eurasian Economic Commission No. 30 dated April 21, 2015 No. 30 “On non-tariff regulation measures”.

The Decision has been amended a few times since then. Appendix 1 of the decision is the "List of Goods Prohibited for Import into and/or Export from the Customs Territory of the Eurasian Economic Union". According to the list, no prohibition exists on the import of digital goods, except for the prohibition of importation of the audiovisual materials and other information media containing information against the public morals, public order and security. Such information includes content promoting extremism or terrorism, pornographic materials, political and economic interests of the state, including national security, public health and morals of the citizens, among others.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since April 2015, last amended in February 2025

Russian Federation.act: Decision of the Board of the Eurasian Economic Commission No. 30 “On non-tariff regulation measures”
Russian Federation.act_norm: decision of the board of the eurasian economic commission no 30 on non tariff regulation measures
acts_norm: decision of the board of the eurasian economic commission no 30 on non tariff regulation measures
Russian Federation.impact: According to Decision No. 30 of the Board of the Eurasian Economic Commission dated April 21, 2015, titled “On Non-Tariff Regulation Measures”, a permitting procedure was established for the import of certain goods into the customs territory of the Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU) (see Appendix No. 2 to the Decision). This licensing procedure applies to the importation of specific digital goods into the EAEU, including Russia. These digital goods include: (i) civil radio-electronic equipment and/or high-frequency devices, including those built into or forming part of other goods; (ii) special technical equipment intended for the covert acquisition of information; and (iii) encryption (cryptographic) devices (2.16, 2.17, 2.19 respectively).
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since April 2015, last amended in February 2025

Russian Federation.impact: In 2016, the Center of Competence for Import Substitution in the Field of Information and Communication Technologies (ЦКИТ – Центр компетенций по импортозамещению в сфере ИКТ) was established by directive of the President of the Russian Federation. Its mission is to support the implementation of the Russian government's policy on import substitution in the ICT sector. ЦКИТ operates independently of the decisions of businesses and public authorities, although it cooperates with government bodies, the business community, professional associations, and scientific and educational institutions.

Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 2300 i on protection of consumer rights
acts_norm: federal law no 2300 i on protection of consumer rights
Russian Federation.impact:
According to Article 4.1, ehen selling certain types of technically complex goods with pre-installed computer software, the consumer must be able to use those goods with software originating from the Russian Federation or other member states of the Eurasian Economic Union. The list of such goods, the procedure for compiling and maintaining the list of required pre-installed software, and the rules for such pre-installation, including operational requirements, are defined by the Government of the Russian Federation.

Starting July 1, 2021, any pre-installed software enabling internet access must also ensure, by default and without additional settings, the ability to use a search engine originating from Russia or other EAEU member states. The Government also defines the requirements for this search engine and the procedure for selecting it.When selling certain types of technically complex goods with pre-installed computer software, the consumer must be able to use those goods with software originating from the Russian Federation or other member states of the Eurasian Economic Union. The list of such goods, the procedure for compiling and maintaining the list of required pre-installed software, and the rules for such pre-installation, including operational requirements, are defined by the Government of the Russian Federation.

Starting July 1, 2021, any pre-installed software enabling internet access must also ensure, by default and without additional settings, the ability to use a search engine originating from Russia or other EAEU member states. Russian Federation.act: The Resolution of the Government of the Russian Federation No. 311 "On Measures to Implement the Decree of the President of the Russian Federation"
Russian Federation.act_norm: the resolution of the government of the russian federation no 311 on measures to implement the decree of the president of the russian federation
acts_norm: the resolution of the government of the russian federation no 311 on measures to implement the decree of the president of the russian federation
Russian Federation.impact:
This decree issued by the Government of the Russian Federation introduces export bans and restrictions on a wide range of goods as part of measures to implement a presidential decree responding to international sanctions. It establishes that until December 31, 2025, the export of certain goods listed in Appendix No. 1 of the decree is completely prohibited. In addition to this outright ban, the export of other categories of goods—such as those listed in Appendices No. 2 and No. 3, including specific types of electrical equipment—is only allowed upon the decision of the Prime Minister or designated Deputy Prime Ministers, based on recommendations from the Ministry of Industry and Trade and, where applicable, in coordination with the Ministry of Energy and the Federal Customs Service.

The decree outlines a number of exceptions to the ban. Some examples are: it does not apply to goods confirmed to originate from Russia (supported by a certificate of origin), nor to goods exported to countries within the Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU). Other exceptions include goods in international transit, goods supporting Russian military operations abroad, leased vehicles being returned to foreign lessors from designated "unfriendly" countries, and exports undertaken by Rosatom (the Russian state nuclear corporation) and its affiliates. Special provisions are also made for goods manufactured in special economic zones or free warehouses under specific customs procedures.

According to the decree, the "List of Certain Types of Goods Subject to Temporary Export Ban" includes over 200 products, including technological, telecommunication and medical equipment, vehicles, agricultural machinery, electric equipment, as well as railway cars and locomotives, containers, turbines, metal and stone cutting machines, video displays, projectors, consoles and switchboards.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since March 2022, last amended in December 2024

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No. 162-FZ "On Standardization"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 162 fz on standardization
acts_norm: federal law no 162 fz on standardization
Russian Federation.impact:
For the purpose of conformity assessment, the Eurasian Economic Union member States, including Russia, can apply international and regional standards, and in the absence thereof, national standards. Application of these standards on a voluntary basis ensures the compliance with requirements of technical regulations of the EAEU. The Eurasian Economic Commission (EEC) adopts a list of such standards. For adoption of list of standards vis-a-vis a specific technical regulation and amendments to the list public consultations have to take place. Interested domestic and foreign juridical and natural persons (including those from non-members of the EAEU) can submit their comments and proposals on the draft technical regulations to the EEC. The period for comments is at least 60 days from the publication of the notification on the official website of the EEC.  

According to the Federal Law N 162-FZ on Standardization, participation in technical committees for standardization created by Rosstandard in order to organize the work on standardization takes place on a voluntary basis and on the basis of equal representation of the parties. Participants include representatives of federal executive bodies, state enterprises, scientific organizations, including those operating in the field of standardization, professional unions, public associations, small and medium-sized businesses, legal entities and (or) individuals who are manufacturers, performers, consumers of products (works, services). As observers, the technical committees for standardization include representatives of international and regional standardization organizations, as well as national standardization bodies of foreign states. The observer takes part in the meeting of the technical committee for standardization without the right to vote.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since June 2016, last amended in December 2020

Russian Federation.act:
Federal Law No. 126-FZ "On Communications";

Decree of the Government of the Russian Federation No. 113 "On approval of the list of communication facilities subject to mandatory certification and invalidation of certain acts"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 126 fz on communications decree of the government of the russian federation no 113 on approval of the list of communication facilities subject to mandatory certification and invalidation of certain acts
acts_norm: federal law no 126 fz on communications
decree of the government of the russian federation no 113 on approval of the list of communication facilities subject to mandatory certification and invalidation of certain acts
Russian Federation.impact:
Article 41 of the Law on Communications establishes a separate procedure for licensing and certification in the sphere of telecommunications. Communication devices are subject to a procedure of mandatory conformity acknowledgement by way of either compulsory certification or compulsory declaration of conformity from Federal Communications Agency (FCA) if it is to be supplied in connection with a common carrier network in Russia. 

A declaration of conformity is a document in which the applicant confirms that the product it has manufactured complies with the applicable conformity requirements. For a declaration of conformity related to telecommunications devices to be valid, it must be registered with the Federal Communications Agency (FCA). The applicant must submit the declaration for registration, accompanied by relevant evidence of the device’s compliance, obtained through accredited testing laboratories. In contrast, the authority responsible for certification is the Certification Agency. A manufacturer or supplier must submit an application to the Certification Agency, which then conducts the certification testing.

Notably, the Supplier’s Declaration of Conformity (SDoC) and voluntary third-party certification are not recognized as valid procedures in this context. The list of accredited certification bodies and testing laboratories (centers) is published on the official website of the Rossvyaz information portal of the Ministry of Communications and Mass Media of the Russian Federation.

The complete list of communication products subject to mandatory certification is approved by the Decree of the Government of the Russian Federation No. 113.
Russian Federation.timeframe:
Since July 2003, last amended in December 2024;

Since February 2022

Russian Federation.act: Eurasian Economic Union Treaty
Russian Federation.act_norm: eurasian economic union treaty
acts_norm: eurasian economic union treaty
Russian Federation.impact:
Section X (Technical Regulation) of the Eurasian Economic Union Treaty establishes the uniform technical standards, including the unity of the rules and methods of research (tests) and measurements during the mandatory conformity assessment procedures (Article 51(6)). Supplier’s Declaration of Conformity (SDoC) and related conformity assessment results including testing reports from one EAEU Member State must be recognized and accepted by all other Member States. 

Article 53(2) (Circulation of Products and Technical Regulations of the EAEU) of the Treaty stipulates that products that are subject to the enforced technical regulation of the EAEU can be put into the circulation at the territory of the EAEU, provided that it has passed the necessary conformity assessment procedures established under the technical regulation of the EAEU. Member States shall ensure circulation of products conforming to the requirements of the technical regulation of the EAEU on its territory without application of additional with regard to the technical regulation of the EAEU requirements to such products and without additional conformity assessment procedures.

Annex 9 to the Treaty "Protocol on Technical Regulation within the Eurasian Economic EAEU" prescribes that Conformity assessment of objects of technical regulating established in the technical regulation of the EAEU shall be made in form of registration (state registration), testing, confirmation of compliance, expertise and (or) in any other form. Mandatory conformity assessment is carried out in the form of declaration of conformity and certification. (Clause 5). Specifically, “Certificate of compliance with technical regulations of the EAEU” means the document by which the authority for certification certifies compliance of the products released for circulation with requirements of technical regulation of the EAEU (technical regulations of the EAEU).
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since Janury 2015

Russian Federation.act: Decision of the Board of the Eurasian Economic Commission No. 30 “On non-tariff regulation measures”
Russian Federation.act_norm: decision of the board of the eurasian economic commission no 30 on non tariff regulation measures
acts_norm: decision of the board of the eurasian economic commission no 30 on non tariff regulation measures
Russian Federation.impact:
According to Annex No. 9, equipment and devices containing encryption must be registered with the Federal Security Service (FSS). The manufacturer or seller is required to obtain an FSS notification before importing or exporting such equipment.

This notification acknowledges that the product contains cryptographic or encryption elements. It is a mandatory requirement for importing equipment with encryption capabilities into the territory of the Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU), or exporting it from the EAEU.

FSS notification is required for a wide range of civil, industrial, scientific, and general-purpose devices that contain encryption components. This includes many high-tech products such as mobile phones, tablets, laptops, wireless keyboards and mice, servers, RFID tags, cryptocurrency mining equipment, and others.

However, if a device has already been included in the EAEU’s unified register, no separate notification is required.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since April 2015, last amended in February 2025

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No. 161-FZ “On the National Payment System”
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 161 fz on the national payment system
acts_norm: federal law no 161 fz on the national payment system
Russian Federation.impact:
Article 9.1 outlines the requirements for accepting and processing electronic payment instruments in the Russian Federation.

The article states that foreign payment service providers can only operate in Russia if they meet specific conditions, including concluding a formal agreement with a Russian money transfer operator. This agreement must detail responsibilities for dispute resolution, settlement timelines, client identification procedures, and information security in accordance with Central Bank of Russia standards.

The article further states that electronic payment instruments issued by foreign providers can only be accepted in Russia under strict conditions, including if the foreign provider is included in a register maintained by the Central Bank of Russia.

This effectively means that if a foreign payment system is not registered with the Bank of Russia and does not have a subsidiary or agreement in place with a local licensed operator, Russian financial institutions are prohibited from processing transactions involving that system.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since June 2011, last amended in February 2025

Russian Federation.act:
Decision of the Eurasian Economic Commission No. 107 on the Categories of Goods for Personal Use and Methods of their Import, including Value, Weight, and Quantity Import Standards (О категориях товаров для личного пользования и способах их ввоза, включая стандарты по стоимости, весу и количеству импортируемых товаров);

Decision of the Eurasian Economic Commission No. 87 on the Value and Weight Limits for Goods Imported via International Mail for Personal Use (О пределах стоимости и веса товаров, ввозимых для личного пользования посредством международной почты)
Russian Federation.act_norm: decision of the eurasian economic commission no 107 on the categories of goods for personal use and methods of their import including value weight and quantity import standards decision of the eurasian economic commission no 87 on the value and weight limits for goods imported via international mail for personal use
acts_norm: decision of the eurasian economic commission no 107 on the categories of goods for personal use and methods of their import including value weight and quantity import standards
decision of the eurasian economic commission no 87 on the value and weight limits for goods imported via international mail for personal use
Russian Federation.impact:
The de minimis thresholds for duty-free importation of personal use goods into Eurasian Economic Union (EAEU) member states have evolved over time and currently depend on the mode of transport and import date. The foundational framework was established by Decision No. 107 of 2017 Appendix 1, which first set the categories of goods for personal use and initial value, weight, and quantity standards. 

On October 22, 2024, the EEC Council Decision No. 87 dated October 18, 2024, introduced changes to the value, weight, and/or quantity norms within which goods for personal use are imported into the customs territory of the EAEU without payment of customs duties and taxes, as established by EEC Council Decision No. 107 dated December 20, 2017. The new version clarifies the value, weight, and/or quantity norms for the importation of goods for personal use sent by international postal shipments. These are as follows:
- For goods — the value does not exceed the equivalent of 200 euros, and the gross weight does not exceed 31 kg;
- For tobacco; tobacco products; vapes — 200 cigarettes, or 50 cigars (cigarillos), or 200 heated tobacco products (“sticks”), or 250 grams of tobacco, or assorted products with a total weight of no more than 250 grams.

Therefore, as of 2025, the EAEU maintains a de minimis threshold of €200 (approximately USD 230) and 31 kg for goods imported for personal use via international mail.
Russian Federation.timeframe:
Since December 2017;

Since November 2024

Russian Federation.act: Decision No. 107 of the Council of the Eurasian Economic Commission "On certain issues related to goods for personal use"
Russian Federation.act_norm: decision no 107 of the council of the eurasian economic commission on certain issues related to goods for personal use
acts_norm: decision no 107 of the council of the eurasian economic commission on certain issues related to goods for personal use
Russian Federation.impact:
For Russia, the de minimis threshold for importing goods for personal use is set at a total value not exceeding €10,000 (approximately USD 10,800).

However, this higher threshold of €10,000 applies exclusively to goods carried as airline luggage; it does not apply to internet purchases or goods delivered by post or courier. If the de minimis threshold for mail shipments is exceeded, a duty of 15% of the excess value is charged, but not less than €2 per kilogram.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Last reviewed in April 2025

Russian Federation.act: Rules for Domain Name Registration in the .RU and .РФ Domains
Russian Federation.act_norm: rules for domain name registration in the ru and domains
acts_norm: rules for domain name registration in the ru and domains
Russian Federation.impact: It is not necessary to have a domain name with a Russian extension to sell goods to Russian customers. Russia-specific domain names are .ru and .pф. Rules for registration and use of .ru and .pф domain names are established by the non-commercial organisation Coordination Centre for TLD RU. Domain name registration is performed by registration agents accredited by the Coordination Centre. Foreign persons and non-residents can equally apply for and own a domain name in .ru and .pф domains. There is a list of ‘reserved domains’ attributed by the Coordination Centre under special rules such as domain names for government like gov.ru. Domain names may be transferred from one company or individual to another, subject to payment of a fee to a registration agent.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since December 2022

Russian Federation.act: Federal Law No. 236-FZ “On the Activities of Foreign Persons on the Internet Information and Telecommunication Network in the Territory of the Russian Federation”
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 236 fz on the activities of foreign persons on the internet information and telecommunication network in the territory of the russian federation
acts_norm: federal law no 236 fz on the activities of foreign persons on the internet information and telecommunication network in the territory of the russian federation
Russian Federation.impact:
The Law applies to owners of any information resources on the Internet, including entertainment sites, online stores, web search engines, hosting providers, etc.

According to Article 5 of the Law, foreign person who is the owner of an information source (internet sites) with daily audience of more than 500,000 users in the territory of Russia, has the following obligations:

1. To develop an electronic feedback form for Russian citizens and organisations.
2. To create a personal account (hereinafter - PA) on the Roskomnadzor website and use it for interaction with government agencies. Legally significant messages will be exchanged through the PA. Also, the PA shall be used to submit an application for inclusion in the to be developed list of foreign persons operating through the Internet in the territory of the RF (hereinafter — Register of Foreign Persons).
3. To set up, by 1 January 2022, a branch, representative office or legal entity (hereinafter - Representation) in the territory of the RF and ensure its operations.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since July 2021, last amended in June 2024

Russian Federation.act:
Federal Law No. 2300-1 
"On Consumer Rights Protection"
Russian Federation.act_norm: federal law no 2300 1 on consumer rights protection
acts_norm: federal law no 2300 1
on consumer rights protection
Russian Federation.impact:
Consumer rights in Russia are protected under the Consumer Rights Protection Law No. 2300-1-FZ, as well as relevant administrative, civil, and criminal procedure legislation. 

Even if foreign law governs the relationship—either through party choice or conflict-of-law rules—Russian consumer protection laws will still apply if the online business targets consumers in Russia.

As a result, Russian courts will assert jurisdiction over disputes involving electronic contracts whenever a foreign entity offers goods or services to Russian consumers online. This applies even if the foreign business has no legal presence in Russia or operates through a website registered in a foreign domain.

For instance, Russian courts may assume jurisdiction if the defendant conducts business in Russia, if contractual performance is carried out in Russia, if property damage or a related event occurs in Russia, or in other circumstances demonstrating a close connection to the country.
Russian Federation.timeframe: Since February 1992, last amended in December 2024

Anti-Dumping and Countervailing of Foreign Products Act B.E.2542 (พระราชบัญญัติการตอบโต้การทุ่มตลาดและการอุดหนุนซึ่งสินค้าจากต่างประเทศ พ.ศ. 2542);

Safeguard Measures on Increased Imports Act B.E.2550 (พระราชบัญญัติมาตรการปกป้องจากการนำเข้าสินค้าที่เพิ่มขึ้น พ.ศ. 2550)

safeguard measures on increased imports act b e 2550
Thailand.impact:
The Anti-Dumping and Countervailing Act 1999, the Anti-Dumping and Countervailing Act (Amendment) 2019, and the Safeguard Measure on Increased Imports Act 2007 prescribe specific laws concerning anti-dumping, anti-subsidy, anti-circumvention, safeguard measures, etc.

Thailand has imposed trade defense on products such as steel or steel alloys, Biaxially oriented polypropylene (BOPP) film, and citric acid from certain economies. However, no trade defence measure is applied on the ICT products.

Thailand.act: Public Procurement and Supplies Administration Act B.E.2560 (พระราชบัญญัติ การจัดซื้อจัดจ้างและการบริหารพัสดุภาครัฐ พ.ศ. 2560)
Thailand.act_norm: public procurement and supplies administration act b e 2560
acts_norm: public procurement and supplies administration act b e 2560
Thailand.impact: There is no legislative measure which fully excludes foreign firms from public procurement under the Public Procurement Act in sectors relevant to digital trade. Procurement and supplies administration by a State agency must generate optimal benefits to the State agency and must conform with the following principles: the value for money principle, the transparency principle, the efficiency and effectiveness principle, and the accountability principle. Specifically, for the transparency principle, procurement and supplies administration must be carried out openly, offer opportunities for fair competition, be conducted with equal treatment to all business operators, require an appropriate and sufficient time for tendering proposals and proceed with clear evidence and with disclosure of information on procurement and supplies administration at all stages (Section 8).

Thailand.act: Protection of Layout Designs of Integrated Circuits Act B.E. 2543
Thailand.act_norm: protection of layout designs of integrated circuits act b e 2543
acts_norm: protection of layout designs of integrated circuits act b e 2543
Thailand.impact: There is no requirement under the Protection of Layout Designs of Integrated Circuits Act to disclose source code, software, or the design logic behind an integrated circuit in order to participate in public procurement. The Act specifically protects layout designs, also referred to as topographies, which are the physical, three-dimensional arrangements of components and interconnections within an integrated circuit. According to Section 6, only those layout designs that are original and not commonly known in the industry are eligible for protection. This protection applies strictly to the physical configuration, and does not extend to the functional logic or any underlying software.

Thailand.act_norm: public procurement and supplies administration act b e 2560 regulation of the ministry of finance regarding public procurement and supplies administration b e 2560 ministerial regulation prescribing criteria procedures and conditions for registration as consultants b e 2560
acts_norm: public procurement and supplies administration act b e 2560
regulation of the ministry of finance regarding public procurement and supplies administration b e 2560
ministerial regulation prescribing criteria procedures and conditions for registration as consultants b e 2560
Thailand.impact:
Section 73 of the Public Procurement and Supplies Administration Act B.E.2560 (2017) imposes requirements for foreign consultants in public procurement. Foreign consultants are able to participate in tendering for consultancy services. They must register with the Consultants Information Centre of the Ministry of Finance unless there is written certification from the Centre that there is no consultant offering services for such work or it is the consultancy work of a State agency in a foreign country. 

The Ministerial Regulation Prescribing Criteria, Procedures, and Conditions for Registration as Consultants B.E.2560 states that both independent and juristic consultants must have Thai nationality (Clause 4). Additionally, the Regulation of the Ministry of Finance on Public Procurement and Supplies Administration B.E.2560 provides that foreign consultant can only participate in the public procurement when it is necessary and a State agency shall indicate reasons in the report of procurement. In the procurement of such foreign consultant, there shall be Thai personnel joining the team in order to convey knowledge and technologies to the Thai personnel, except that the field of service or procurement does not have Thai personnel (Clause 101, Chapter 3).

Thailand.act:
Ministerial Regulation Prescribing Supplies and Procurement Methods for Supplies that the State Must Promote or Support B.E.2563 (กฎกระทรวงกำหนดพัสดุและวิธีกำรจัดซื้อจัดจ้างพัสดุที่รัฐต้องการส่งเสริมหรือสนับสนุน พ.ศ.2563);

Budget Bureau website - Thai's Innovation List
Thailand.act_norm: ministerial regulation prescribing supplies and procurement methods for supplies that the state must promote or support b e 2563 budget bureau website thai s innovation list
acts_norm: ministerial regulation prescribing supplies and procurement methods for supplies that the state must promote or support b e 2563
budget bureau website thai s innovation list
Thailand.impact:
The Innovation Products is introduced under the Ministerial Regulation 2020 (Chapter 4). “Innovation Promotion Goods” means goods or services that are listed in the Thai Innovation Registry of the National Budget Office, but does not include medicines or pharmaceuticals that are not classified under Group 5 which have been listed as Thai innovations (Clause 11). Innovation promotion goods are goods that the state is required to promote or support (Clause 12). 

State agencies shall procure innovation promotion goods in accordance with Clause 12, using funds from the procurement budget amounting to not less than 70 percent of the budget allocated for the procurement of goods listed in the Thai Innovation Registry, as follows (Clause 3):
(1) If the innovation promotion goods to be procured have only one seller or service provider, the state agency shall procure them by direct negotiation with that seller or service provider.
(2) If the innovation promotion goods to be procured have two or more sellers or service providers, the state agency shall procure them by selection, notifying all sellers or service providers to participate in submitting quotations.

State agencies shall procure innovation promotion goods in accordance with Clause 12, utilizing no less than thirty percent of the total procurement budget allocated for the acquisition of goods listed in the Thai Innovation Registry, pursuant to the provisions of Clause 13.

In 2015, the Cabinet Resolutions proposed to grant privilege to the products listed as Thai Innovative Product. Since 2016. The Budget Bureau has introduced the monthly report of 'Thai Innovation List' to develop domestic industrial capacity in several innovation-centered economic sectors. The Innovation List grants special government procurement privilege only to the products from authorized Thai majority-owned companies. The list covers the product from various sectors: pharmaceuticals, agriculture, transportations, construction, electronics, etc. In particular, the list of electronic sector covers Electrical, Electronics, and Telecommunication. 

Additionally, the Thai government has established a strategy to support emerging innovations from local suppliers. Thus, it has been designated as a national agenda.
Thailand.timeframe:
Since January 2020, last amended in September 2023;

Last reviewed in Febraruy 2024

Thailand.act:
Ministerial Regulation Prescribing Supplies and Procurement Methods for Supplies that the State Must Promote or Support (No.2) B.E.2563 (กฎกระทรวงกำหนดพัสดุและวิธีกำรจัดซื้อจัดจ้างพัสดุที่รัฐต้องการส่งเสริมหรือสนับสนุน ฉบับที่ 2 พ.ศ.2563);

Federation of Thai Industries (FTI) website - Made in Thailand
Thailand.act_norm: ministerial regulation prescribing supplies and procurement methods for supplies that the state must promote or support b e 2563 federation of thai industries website made in thailand
acts_norm: ministerial regulation prescribing supplies and procurement methods for supplies that the state must promote or support b e 2563
federation of thai industries website made in thailand
Thailand.coverage: Products manufactured in Thailand including electronics and digital goods (certified 'Made in Thailand' by the Federation of Thai Industries)
Thailand.impact:
The Made in Thailand List is introduced under the Ministerial Regulation (No.2) 2020. Chapter 7/1 prioritizes domestically produced supplies. Clause 27/1 stipulates that “domestically promoted supplies” refers to supplies with a certificate and the “Made in Thailand” (MIT) marking issued by the Federation of Thai Industries (FTI).

Clause 27/3 prescribes the requirements for procurement of supplies, construction procurement, non-construction procurement, and reporting obligations:
(1) Procurement of supplies: State agencies must procure domestically promoted supplies as per the Public Procurement Act. Exceptions apply if domestic supplies are insufficient, limited suppliers exist, or foreign supplies are necessary and approval from a higher authority is required.
(2) Construction Procurement: No less than 60% of materials used in construction must be domestically produced. Steel must constitute no less than 90% of domestically promoted materials. If unmet, other domestic materials should be used and if the requirement cannot be fulfilled, approval from a higher authority is needed.
(3) Non-Construction Procurement: No less than 60% of materials or equipment must be domestically produced. If a foreign bidder offers a lower price, but a Thai bidder's price is within 3%, the Thai bidder must be selected.
(4) Reporting Obligation: State agencies must establish procedures for contractors to report the value or quantity of domestic materials used.
Thailand.timeframe:
Since December 2020;

Last reviewed in February 2024

Thailand.act:
Ministerial Regulation Prescribing Supplies and Procurement Methods for Supplies that the State Must Promote or Support (No.4) B.E.2566 (กฎกระทรวงกำหนดพัสดุและวิธีกำรจัดซื้อจัดจ้ำงพัสดุที่รัฐต้องการส่งเสริมหรือสนับสนุน ฉบับที่ 4 พ.ศ.2566);

Digital Economy Promotion Agency (DEPA) website - Thailand Digital Catalog
Thailand.act_norm: ministerial regulation prescribing supplies and procurement methods for supplies that the state must promote or support b e 2566 digital economy promotion agency website thailand digital catalog
acts_norm: ministerial regulation prescribing supplies and procurement methods for supplies that the state must promote or support b e 2566
digital economy promotion agency website thailand digital catalog
Thailand.impact:
Digital Promotion Goods is introduced under the Ministerial Regulation (No.4) 2023. Digital Promotion Goods registered in the Digital Service Account of the Digital Economy Promotion Agency refer to goods that are promoted on the agency’s official website. Specifically, the digtial promotion goods prescribed under Clause 27/7 are computer programs, software, hardware, smart devices, digital services, and digital content (digital content), including programs or sets of instructions used to command a computer to operate or devices that enable users to access lower-level systems which form parts of the operational devices, as well as the provision of cloud-based software services (software as a service) without having to install software on a computer. It also includes hardware and devices with embedded systems or microcontroller systems having processing units that use chips or microprocessors specially designed and certified under the digital standard as specified by the Digital Economy Promotion Agency (DEPA).

Clause 27/9 provides the methods for the procurement and contracting of Digital Promotion Goods pursuant shall be as follows:
(1) If the digital promotion goods to be procured have only one seller or service provider, the state agency shall procure by direct negotiation with that seller or service provider.
(2) If the digital promotion goods to be procured have two or more sellers or service providers, the state agency shall procure by selection.
If the state agency does not wish to procure by either method (1) or (2), it may use the method of public invitation.

Additionally, the DEPA website provides detailed information regarding the digital promotion goods by introducing them as the 'Thailand Digital Catalog'. To qualify for registration, businesses must have specified qualifications, such as haveing a registered office in Thailand, presenting VAT registraton certificate, and acquiring certifications for software, SaaS, smart hardware, or digital devices from recognized certification bodies, ensuring compliance with DEPA’s prescribed standards.
Thailand.timeframe:
Since September 2023;

Last reviewed in February 2025

Thailand.act: Foreign Business Act  B.E.2542 (พระราชบัญญัติการประกอบธุรกิจของคนต่างด้าว พ.ศ. 2542)
Thailand.act_norm: foreign business act b e 2542
acts_norm: foreign business act b e 2542
Thailand.impact:
The Foreign Business Act (FBA) B.E.2542 regulates foreign investments in Thailand. Section 4 of the Act defines "foreigner" as companies with at least one-half of capital or shares held by foreigners as well as a limited partnership or a registered ordinary partnership with foreigners as the managing partner or the manager. Section 8 prescribes the List of businesses that are not allowed foreigners to operate: 
(1) List 1: Businesses stricto sensu not permissible to foreigners by special reason. 
(2) List 2: Businesses related to national safety or security or having impacts on arts, culture, traditions, customs and folklore handicrafts or natural resources and the environment, unless upon obtaining permission from the Minister with the approval of the Council of Ministers.
(3) List 3: Businesses in respect of which Thai nationals are not ready to compete with foreigners, unless upon obtaining permission from the Director-General with the approval of the Commission.

Sectors relevant to digital trade do not fall under Annex Lists 1 and 2. However, Annex List 3 Business no.21 refers to 'Other services, except those prescribed in the Ministerial Regulations', broadly covers all businesses not exempt under the Ministerial Regulations, including the digital sectors. 

Despite these restrictions, foreigners can operate businessees under List 3 after receiving permission of the Government of the Kingdom of Thailand i.e. Board of Investment or the Industrial Estates Authority of Thailand, such foreigners shall notify the Director-General of Department of Business Development in order to obtain a certificate (Sections 11, 12). Additionally, the foreigns will be exempted if they comply with the provisions of the treaty which may in return include the entitlement of Thai nationals and Thai enterprises to operate the businesses in the country of the foreigners, i.e. Treaty of Amity Between U.S.–Thailand (Section 10). Therefore, due to these exemptions, the maximum foreign equity cap in digital trade sectors is 100%.
Thailand.timeframe: Since November 1999, last amended in August 2019

Thailand.act: Broadcasting and Television Business Act B.E.2551 (พระราชบัญญัติการประกอบกิจการกระจายเสียงและกิจการโทรทัศน์ พ.ศ. 2551)
Thailand.act_norm: broadcasting and television business act b e 2551
acts_norm: broadcasting and television business act b e 2551
Thailand.impact:
Foreign ownership in sound broadcasting business or television business services is capped at 25%. Any person operates these services shall obtain a license from the National Broadcasting Commission Commission (Section 7). 

Section 13 states that license applicants for business services must be juristic persons under Thai law. 
(1) Regional/Local Business Services: At least one-third of equity shareholders or investors must reside in the region or locality. Applicants must demonstrate financial stability, an accounting audit system, and other qualifications as specified by the Commission.
(2) Other Business Types: Applicants must be state enterprises or companies under Thai law with primary objectives related to sound broadcasting or television.

Section 15 states that license applicants must meet these criteria:
(1) Thai nationals must invest or hold at least 75% of the total capital.
(2) Thai nationals must control at least 75% of voting rights.
For companies, at least 75% of directors and all binding-authority directors must be Thai nationals. For partnerships, the managing partner or manager must be Thai. The Commission may prohibit foreign takeovers, defining a Thai national as one not classified as a foreigner under foreign business laws. Voting rights are calculated per share held by Thai nationals.

(Section 4 defines that “sound broadcasting business” means the sound broadcasting business to assign frequency spectrum and to regulate radio broadcasting,
television and telecommunications businesses; and “television business” means the television business and to regulate radio broadcasting, television and telecommunications businesses).

Thailand.act: Foreign Business Act  B.E.2542 (พระราชบัญญัติการประกอบธุรกิจของคนต่างด้าว พ.ศ. 2542)

Thailand.act_norm: foreign business act b e 2542 public companies limited act b e 2535
acts_norm: foreign business act b e 2542
public companies limited act b e 2535
Thailand.impact:
While Thailand does not impose a general nationality requirement for directors, residency requirements are in place for both directors and managers of foreign businesses. Specifically, managers must either reside in Thailand or be permitted to temporarily enter the Kingdom under immigration law.

However, for businesses operating under List 2 of the Foreign Business Act (FBA), Section 15 requires that at least two-fifths of the company’s directors be Thai nationals, thereby imposing a nationality requirement in this context. Section 16.2 of the FBA further stipulates that a foreigner applying for a business license must reside in Thailand or be permitted temporary entry. In addition, Section 18 authorizes the Minister to issue Ministerial Regulations prescribing conditions such as the number of foreign directors who must have domicile or residence in Thailand, or the required duration for maintaining minimum capital in the country.

Moreover, the Public Companies Limited Act provides that a board of directors of a public company limited must consist of at least five directors to conduct the business of the company, where shares are publically available, and not less than half of whom shall reside within the country (Section 67). For other kinds of juristic person applying for foreign business license, the board of directors shall have residency or being permitted to temporarily enter into Thailand under the immigration law.

Thailand.act: Broadcasting and Television Business Act B.E.2551 (พระราชบัญญัติการประกอบกิจการกระจายเสียงและกิจการโทรทัศน์ พ.ศ. 2551)
Thailand.act_norm: broadcasting and television business act b e 2551
acts_norm: broadcasting and television business act b e 2551
Thailand.impact:
Broadcasting service providers are required to be juristic persons established under Thai law, as specified in Section 13 of the Broadcasting and Television Business Act B.E. 2551. Section 15 further outlines the qualifications for license applicants regarding foreign shares and participation in broadcasting business.

For example, the applicant is required to have Thai-nationality shareholders holding not less than three quarters of the total capital, Thai-nationality voters controlling not less than 3/4 of the total voting rights, and Thai nationality directors comprising not less than three quarters of the total directors of the juristic person.

In the case of a regional license, not less than one third of the shareholders or investors must have their domicile in the relevant region or locality.

The application for other business types under this Act shall be a State enterprise or a company established under Thai law, which has the main objective of operating a broadcasting business or television business in Thai territory.

Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunication commission notificationregarding criteria and procedures to license foreign satellite to provide domestic services b e 2563 notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding criteria and procedures for licensing and the right to orbit satellites b e 2563
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunication commission notificationregarding criteria and procedures to license foreign satellite to provide domestic services b e 2563
notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding criteria and procedures for licensing and the right to orbit satellites b e 2563
Thailand.impact:
The Notification regarding License Foreign Satellite to Provide Domestic Services and the Notification regarding Criteria and Procedures for Licensing and the Right to Orbit Satellites B.E.2563 are issued under the Organization to Assign Frequency Waves and to Regulate the Radio Broadcasting, Radio Television and Telecommunications Services Act B.E.2553. 

The Notification regarding License Foreign Satellite to Provide Domestic Services aims to accommodate the growing demand for foreign satellite transponders by satellite communication providers in Thailand and to create opportunities for Thai operators to provide services through these transponders. Clause 5.1 provides that the applicant who wishes to operate satellite must be: (1) a limited company or public limited company established as a juristic person under Thai law with an establishment in Thailand, and also (2) a representative of a foreign satellite operator who meets the qualifications prescribe state policy. 

Moreover, the Notification regarding Criteria and Procedures for Licensing and the Right to Orbit Satellites allows Thai operators to use foreign satellite channels for services, in line with the government’s policy on permitting foreign satellites to operate in the country. Clause 7 provides that for commercial service, applicants must be a limited or public company established under Thai law, have a business presence in Thailand, and not qualify as a foreign entity under the Foreign Business Act. For government missions or specific non-commercial use: Applicants must be a government agency, state enterprise, or a Thai-incorporated legal entity that is not classified as a foreign entity (Clause 8).

Thailand.act_norm: royal decree on supervision of service businesses relating to digital identification and authentication systems that are subject to licensing b e 2565 thai civil and commercial code public companies limited act b e 2535
acts_norm: royal decree on supervision of service businesses relating to digital identification and authentication systems that are subject to licensing b e 2565
thai civil and commercial code
public companies limited act b e 2535
Thailand.impact:
Under this Royal Decree, services related to digital identification and authentication systems are required to obtain a license before commencing operations, as outlined in Section 7. Exemptions from the licensing requirement are specified in Section 8. Furthermore, Section 9 stipulates that service providers must be juristic persons, such as a limited company, a public limited company, or another form of juristic person as defined by the Notification of the Commission.
Section 7. The provision of a service relating to digital identification and authentication that possess any of the following characteristics shall be a service that is subject to licensing:
(1) an identity proofing service;
(2) an authenticator lifecycle management service
(3) an authentication service;
(4) a service for exchanging data for digital identification and authentication that is a network or system which connects and exchanges data relating to digital identification and authentication, excluding a service of an intermediary.


Section 8. The provision of any of the following services relating to digital identification and authentication systems shall be exempt from obtaining a license:
(1) an identification and authentication service operated by a service provider issuing a certificate for supporting electronic signatures, in order to support the use of electronic signatures under the law on electronic transactions;
(2) an identification and authentication service used by a person for his or her internal businesses without providing the service to
external parties;
(3) a service relating to digital identification and authentication systems in businesses prescribed by the Notification of the
Commission.

Section 9. A service business relating to digital identification and authentication systems that is subject to licensing under section 7 may only be operated by a juristic person which is a limited company, a public limited company or other juristic person as specified by the Notification of the Commission.

The requirements under the Royal Decree may not align fully with the concept of commercial presence requirements. However, under Section 1098 of the Civil and Commercial Code (CCC), a private limited company must have its registered office located within Thailand. Similarly, under Section 18(5) of the Public Limited Companies Act, a public limited company must have its head office located within Thailand.

Thailand.act: Ministerial Regulation No.21 Issued under the Patent Act B.E.2552 (กฎกระทรวง ฉบับที่ 21 ว่าด้วยหลักเกณฑ์การขอรับสิทธิบัตร พ.ศ. 2542)
Thailand.act_norm: ministerial regulation no 21 issued under the patent act b e 2552
acts_norm: ministerial regulation no 21 issued under the patent act b e 2552
Thailand.impact: To file patents, the Ministerial Regulation states that if the patent applicant does not reside in Thailand, the applicant shall authorize an agent or patent attorney registered with the Director-General of the Department of Intellectual Property to act on his behalf (Clause 13). The Power of Attorney (POA) shall be attached with the revenue stamp of 30 Thai Baht (around 1 USD) for each patent agent/patent attorney/application. The POA document if not in a foreign language is required to translate to the Thai language. The translation should be certified by the translator and agent to be accurate translation (Clause 15).

Thailand.act: Patent Act B.E.2522 (พระราชบัญญัติสิทธิบัตร พ.ศ. 2522)
Thailand.act_norm: patent act b e 2522
acts_norm: patent act b e 2522
Thailand.impact: Civil and administrative procedures and remedies, and provisional measures are in place under Chapter VI (Offenses) Sections 81 to 88. For instance, violations under this Act shall be subject to imprisonment or a fine or both. For instance Section 85 provides that any person who commits any act which violates the patentee right (under Section 36 or Section 63) without the permission of the patentee shall be punished with imprisonment not exceeding two years or a fine not exceeding four hundred thousand baht or both.
Thailand.timeframe: Since March 1979, last amended in March 1999

Thailand.act: Patent Act B.E.2522 (พระราชบัญญัติสิทธิบัตร พ.ศ. 2522)
Thailand.act_norm: patent act b e 2522
acts_norm: patent act b e 2522
Thailand.impact:
Part V governs licenses of right, compulsory licenses, and government use, aligning with the TRIPS Agreement (Trade-Related Aspects of Intellectual Property Rights) exceptions under Articles 31 and 31bis. Section 48 provides that remuneration for compulsory licenses goes to the patentee unless a licensee under Section 38 holds exclusive licensing rights, in which case the licensee is compensated. Section 49 requires applicants to define remuneration, usage conditions, and restrictions on patentee and licensee rights.

Section 50 empowers the Director-General to issue compulsory licenses, ensuring fair remuneration, limited scope and duration, and prioritization of domestic supply. Section 51 allows government agencies to use patents for public services, defense, or emergencies like food and drug shortages, bypassing standard licensing procedures while adhering to fair compensation rules. Section 52 grants the Prime Minister, with Cabinet approval, authority to use patents during war or emergencies, with fair remuneration and appeal rights for patentees. These provisions ensure compliance with TRIPS by balancing public interest with patent-holder rights.
Thailand.timeframe: Since March 1979, last amended in March 1999

Copyright Act B.E.2537
(พระราชบัญญัติ ลิขสิทธิ์ พ.ศ. 253);

Copyright Act (No.5) B.E.2565
(พระราชบัญญัติลิขสิทธิ์ (ฉบับที่ 5) พ.ศ. 2565)
Thailand.act_norm: copyright act b e 2537 copyright act b e 2565 2565
acts_norm: copyright act b e 2537
copyright act b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
The scope of protection has covered copyright online. Copyright infringement is subjected to penalty either fine and imprisonment, or both (Sections 69-77). The amended Copyright Act (No.5) B.E.2565 includes measures for the suspension of copyright infirngements on the Internet by adopting the Notice and Takedown process in place of court proceedings. An ISP can immediately remove infringing contents from the online platforms upon receiving notification from the copyright owner without any court procedures.

Moreover, civil and administrative procedures and remedies and provisional measures are in place under Chapter 8 (Penalties). Any person who commits a copyright infringement shall be liable to a fine (Sections 69 and 70). Any person who, having committed an offense and having been sanctioned under this Act, subsequently commits an offense under this Act within five years after being discharged from the penalty shall be liable to twice the penalty prescribed for the offense (Section 73). Additionally, all articles made or imported into the Kingdom which constitute an infringement of copyright or performers’ rights under this Act and which are still in the ownership of the offender under Section 69 or 70 shall pass to the owner of copyright or performers’ rights and the objects used for committing the offense shall all be confiscated (Section 75).

Thailand.act:
Computer-Related Crime Act
B.E.2550 (พระราชบัญญัติ ว่าด้วยการกระทำความผิดเกี่ยวกับคอมพิวเตอร์ พ.ศ.2550)
Thailand.act_norm: computer related crime act b e 2550
acts_norm: computer related crime act
Thailand.impact:
According to section 20 (3) of Computer-Related Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007), a competent official, with the approval of the Minister, may file a petition with the court having jurisdiction requesting for an order to block or delete computer data that constitutes a criminal offence under intellectual property law.

After receiving a request from an intellectual property right holder to suspend dissemination or delete infringing computer data, the Intellectual Property Rights Enforcement Office—authorized under an order of the Department of Intellectual Property (DIP)—forwards the request to the Ministry of Digital Economy and Society (DE) to request a court order. In 2024, as a result of this mechanism, 34 court orders were issued, and access to 231 URLs and 3 websites were blocked or disabled.
Thailand.timeframe: Since June 2007, last amended in January 2017

Thailand.act:
Trade Secret Act B.E.2545 (พระราชบัญญัติ
ความลับทางการค้า พ.ศ.2545)
Thailand.act_norm: trade secret act b e 2545 2545
acts_norm: trade secret act b e 2545
Thailand.impact:
Under the Trade Secrets Act, the disclosure or use of trade secrets without being given consent by the owner is an infringement (Section 6). Under Section 7, any of the following acts against trade secrets shall not be considered an infringement: (2) Disclosure or use of trade secrets by state agency which is responsible for their maintenance in the following circumstances:
(a) When it is necessary for the protection of public health or safety; or,
(b) When it is necessary for the benefit of other public interests with no commercial purpose. In such case, the state agency which is responsible for the maintenance of trade secrets, or other state agency or person concerned who has access to the trade secrets has taken reasonable steps for the protection of the trade secrets from being used in unfair trading activities.

The provision aligns with WTO TRIPS to some extent, particularly with Article 39 on the protection of undisclosed information (trade secrets). Under TRIPS Article 39(2) requires members to protect trade secrets against unauthorized disclosure, acquisition, or use in a manner contrary to honest commercial practices. Moreover, Article 39(3) allows governments to disclose undisclosed test or other data when necessary to protect the public. It also requires efforts to prevent unfair commercial use.
references:
https://www.ipthailand.go.th/th/dip-law-2/item/พระราชบัญญัติความลับทางการค้า-ฉบับที่-๒-พ-ศ-๒๕๕๘-copy.html;

https://www.ipthailand.go.th/th/dip-law-2/item/trade-secrets-act-b-e-2545-amended-by-trade-secret-act-no-2-b-e-2558.html

Thailand.act:
Trade Secret Act B.E.2545 (พระราชบัญญัติ
ความลับทางการค้า พ.ศ.2545)
Thailand.act_norm: trade secret act b e 2545 2545
acts_norm: trade secret act b e 2545
Thailand.impact:
Trade Secrets Act provides that any “trade information” that meets the following 3 requirements is protected as a trade secret (Section 3). The three conditions are:  
- It is confidential, i.e. the trade information not being publicly known to or accessible by persons who are not related to the trade information.
- It has a commercial value derived from its secrecy.
- Its secrecy is protected by its owner / controller has taken appropriate and sufficient protection measures to maintain its secrecy.

If there is a dispute in relation to the trade secret because a person commits an infringement against its secrecy, the trade secret owner can submit the dispute with the Trade Secret Board for mediation and settlement (Section 9). Alternatively, they can file a lawsuit in court against the infringer for an interim and permanent injunction orders and compensations for actual damages and punitive damages.  The lawsuit must be filed within 3 years from the date on which infringement act and the infringer are known, however the period shall not exceed 10 years from the date of infringement (Section 10).
