Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding telecommunications network access and interconnection b e 2556 notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding the regulatory practice to use invest build underground cable duct and infrastructure for providing telecommunications services b e 2560 notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding wireless infrastructure sharing b e 2562
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding telecommunications network access and interconnection b e 2556

notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding the regulatory practice to use invest build underground cable duct and infrastructure for providing telecommunications services b e 2560
notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding wireless infrastructure sharing b e 2562
Thailand.impact:
Passive sharing is mandated and is effective in both the mobile and fixed sectors. 

Passive sharing in the fixed sector is regulated under the following NBTC Notifications, which are (1) The Notification on Telecommunications Network Access and Interconnection B.E.2556 and (2) NBTC Notification on Regulatory Practice to Use, Invest, Build Underground Cable Duct and Infrastructure for Providing Telecommunications Services B.E.2560. These Notifications mandate telecommunications operators with their own networks, essential facilities, or infrastructure to share their infrastructure based on economic and technical feasibility. Regarding the interconnection of telecommunications network, the NBTC regulates network suppliers to make publicly available their reference offers for providing the infrastructure sharing to other network suppliers, on a cost-oriented rate basis.

Passive sharing in the mobile sector is regulated under the NBTC Notification on Wireless Infrastructure Sharing B.E.2562 applies to the sharing of
infrastructure related to wireless telecommunications network (e.g. masts, towers, physical sites for mobile service). Specifically, the licensee has a duty to allow other licensees to share the use of telecommunications infrastructure for wireless telecommunications networks in accordance with the criteria and procedures set out in this Notification. The types of telecommunications infrastructure for wireless networks that must be shared shall be as specified in the annex attached to this Notification. In sharing telecommunications infrastructure as stated in the first paragraph, the licensee shall treat all licensees requesting to use such infrastructure for wireless telecommunications networks fairly, reasonably, and without discrimination (Clause 5).

Thailand.act:
Foreign Business Act  B.E.2542 (พระราชบัญญัติการประกอบธุรกิจของคนต่างด้าว พ.ศ. 2542);

Telecommunications Business Act B.E.2544 (พระราชบัญญัติ การประกอบกิจการโทรคมนาคม พ.ศ. 2544)
Thailand.act_norm: foreign business act b e 2542 telecommunications business act b e 2544
acts_norm: foreign business act b e 2542
telecommunications business act b e 2544
Thailand.impact:
Foreign businesses in telecom sector are subject to the Foreign Business Act (FBA) B.E.2542 and Telecommunications Business Act B.E.2544. If there is a conflict between a provision of sectoral rules and general rules, the rules that impose a stricter standard will apply. The general rule FBA prescribes that foreign business must obtain a foreign business license from the Ministry of Commerce. Foreign equity cap is applied on the business sectors listed in the Annex Lists of the FBA, including telecommunications. 

Regarding the sectoral law, the Telecommunications Business Act, the foreign equity cap in telecommunications sector is varied depending on the type of telecom license (Section 7 and Section 8):
(1) Type 1 license (Telecommunications operators who provide telecommunications service without operating their telecommunications network): no foreign equity restrictions.
(2) Type 2 license (Telecommunications operators who provide services to a specific group of customers with or without operating their own telecommunications network): Foreign equity cap at 49% of the total shares.
(3) Type 3 (Telecommunications operators who provide their own telecommunications network, for the use of the general public): Foreign equity cap at 49% of the total shares.

Therefore, if there is a conflict between a provision of sectoral rules and general rules, the rules that impose a stricter standard will apply. Therefore, in this case,  foreigners are allowed to acquire shares in telecoms companies not exceeding 49% in 2 of those categories: 
(1) Type 1 licence: no ownership restrictions apply; thus, foreign operators with a Type 1 license are subject to the Telecommunications Business Act B.E.2544 and the Foreign Business Act B.E. 2542, and are also required to obtain a foreign business licence;
(2) Type 2 licence: the applicant for Type 2 license shall not be “a foreigner” under the Foreign Business Act B.E.2542; and
(3) Type 3 licence: the applicant for Type 3 license shall not be “a foreigner” under the Foreign Business Act B.E.2542.

“Foreigners” under the Foreign Business Act B.E.2542 refers to a juristic person having foreign shareholding at least 50% or more.
Thailand.timeframe:
Since November 1999, last amended in August 2019;

Since November 2001, last amended in July 2019

Thailand.act: Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission Regarding Requirements and Procedures to Establish an Accounting Separation Report B.E.2565 (ประกาศคณะกรรมการกิจการกระจายเสียง กิจการโทรทัศน์ และกิจการโทรคมนาคมแห่งชาติ เรื่องหลักเกณฑ์และวิธีการจัดทำรายงานบัญชีแยกประเภทในกิจการโทรคมนาคม พ.ศ. 2565)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding requirements and procedures to establish an accounting separation report b e 2565
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding requirements and procedures to establish an accounting separation report b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
There is no requirement for functional separation for operators with significant market power. However, accounting separation is mandated. Telecom service provider that is determinded as having a significant market power in the market or provider that is appointed by the NBTC shall submit the accounting separation within January of each year (Clause 5 thereof). 

(Remark: The previous accounting seperation Notification B.E.2555 has been repealed and replaced by the Notification B.E.2565 (Clause 2 thereof).  Accounting separation is repealed and replaced by the 2022 Notification (Clause 2 thereof). The amendment updates the procedure and aims to enhance transparency).

Thailand.act_norm: telecommunications business act b e 2544 notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding criteria and procedures for telecommunications business license b e 2563
acts_norm: telecommunications business act b e 2544
notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding criteria and procedures for telecommunications business license b e 2563
Thailand.impact:
Under the Telecommunications Business Act, any person who wishes to operate a telecommunications business in the country is required to obtain a license issued by the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission (NBTC) (Section 7). Determination of the telecom license applicant qualification shall be in accordance with the prescribed criteria in the Telecommunications Business Act (Section 8). 

Moreover, the NBTC Notification in 2020 provides specific requirements for obtaining a license, including the applicant's qualification, procedures to grant the license and conditions. A Type 1 and Type 2 telecommunications business license shall remain valid for the duration of the approved service period (Section 9.1).  A Type 3 telecommunications business license shall have a minimum validity period of no less than fifteen years and a maximum of not more than twenty-five years. The NBTC may consider revising the minimum and maximum duration framework for which the telecommunications business license is granted (Section 9.2). 
The criteria to obtain telecom business license covers foreign equity cap and commercial presence requirement. Specifically, telecoms service providers qualifications must include being a juristic person under Thai law, i.e., establish companies in Thailand, and has the main objective for operating telecommunications business (Clause 6.1).
Thailand.timeframe:
Since November 2001, last amended in July 2019;

Since March 2020

Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunication commission notificationregarding criteria and procedures to license foreign satellite to provide domestic services b e 2563 notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding criteria and procedures for licensing and the right to orbit satellites b e 2563
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunication commission notificationregarding criteria and procedures to license foreign satellite to provide domestic services b e 2563
notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding criteria and procedures for licensing and the right to orbit satellites b e 2563
Thailand.impact:
Previously, satellite businesses have been operated under concession contracts. In 2020, Thailand opened its satellite telecommunications services to foreign-owned satellites. Anyone who wants to provide satellite telecommunications services must apply to the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission (NBTC) for a telecommunication services license. Regarding the NBTC Notification, any persons who wish to orbit their satellites in the country must apply for a license and comply with the requirements. 

The Notification regarding License Foreign Satellite to Provide Domestic Services prescribes that the applicant who wishes to operate satellite must be: (1) a limited company or public limited company established as a juristic person under Thai law with an establishment in Thailand, and also (2) a representative of a foreign satellite operator who meets the qualifications prescribe state policy (Clause 5.1).

Moreover, the Notification regarding Criteria and Procedures for Licensing and the Right to Orbit Satellites  B.E.2563 prescribes that individuals seeking to secure Thailand’s or a government agency’s right to use satellite orbital slots must obtain authorization based on a first-come, first-served principle (Clause 4). The rights are categorized into preliminary rights and full rights. Authorization for planned orbital use or existing but unused satellite networks will follow criteria set by the NBTC (Clause 5). Those intending to use the rights for commercial services must obtain full rights and a Type 3 telecommunications business license. 
- For commercial services: Applicants must be a limited or public company established under Thai law, have a business presence in Thailand, and not qualify as a foreign entity under the Foreign Business Act (Clause 7)
- For government missions or specific non-commercial use: Applicants must be a government agency, state enterprise, or a Thai-incorporated legal entity that is not classified as a foreign entity (Clause 8).

Thailand.act: Organization to Assign Frequency Waves and to Regulate the Radio Broadcasting, Radio Television and Telecommunications Services Act B.E.2553 (พระราชบัญญัติองค์กรจัดสรรคลื่นความถี่และกำกับการประกอบกิจการวิทยุกระจายเสียงวิทยุโทรทัศน์ และกิจการโทรคมนาคม พ.ศ.2553)
Thailand.act_norm: organization to assign frequency waves and to regulate the radio broadcasting radio television and telecommunications services act b e 2553
acts_norm: organization to assign frequency waves and to regulate the radio broadcasting radio television and telecommunications services act b e 2553
Thailand.impact: The Act establishes Thailand independent telecommunications authority, the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission (the NBTC) (Chapter 1, Part 1). The NBTC shall not be a government official, employee of state-enterprises, or engage in any other independent occupation having a conflict of interest either directly or indirectly (Section 8). The NBTC has the power to supervise the telecommunications sector, for example, assign radio frequencies, prescribe criteria, and grant license (Section 27).
Thailand.timeframe: Since December 2010, last amended in October 2022

Thailand.act_norm: credit information business operation act b e 2545 bank of thailand notification regarding services related to debit cards issued and used domestically no sornorchor 12 2561 b e 2561
acts_norm: credit information business operation act b e 2545
bank of thailand notification regarding services related to debit cards issued and used domestically no sornorchor 12 2561 b e 2561
Thailand.coverage: Credit information, Information of debit card transaction
Thailand.impact:
The Credit Information Business Act 2002 specifically covers the collection and processing of credit information. Chapter 2 states that only a credit information company has the right to operate the credit information business (section 9). Section 12 states that No credit information company or information controller or information processor carrying on or operating the business in the Kingdom shall operate, control or process information outside the Kingdom. Specifically, the credit information refers to facts that consumer applying for credit, including qualifications of the customers and history of application for and approval of credit (Section 3). 

Section 3 defines “Credit Information” as facts or information relating to customers applying for Credit Facilities as follows,
(1) Facts that identify clients applying for credits: (a) In case of an individual, this subparagraph means client’s name, address, date of birth, marital status, occupation, official identification card number or government employee’s identification card or passport, and tax payer number (if any); (b) In case of a juristic person, this subparagraph means address, corporate registration
number, or tax payer number. (2) Record of credit application and approval and payment of credit transaction of client
who applies for credit including record of payments of goods or services by credit card.

Moreover, Payment Systems Act B.E. 2560 (Bank of Thailand Notification No. SorNorChor. 12/2561 Regarding Services related to Debit Cards Issued and Used Domestically) (Part 4.4) requires in-country processing for domestic electronic payment transactions for debit cards issued in Thailand. Such transactions include the following: receiving and sending information between an issuer and Payment Card Network Operator an acquirer and Payment Card Network Operator Payment Facilitator and an acquirer. Providing service as a transaction switching center Clearing service Settlement service.
Thailand.timeframe:
Since November 1999, last amended in July 2022;

Since April 2018

Thailand.act:
Notification of the Personal Data Protection Committee on Criteria for the Protection of Personal Data Sent or Transferred to a Foreign Country pursuant to Section 29 of the Personal Data Protection Act B.E. 2562 B.E. 2566
(ประกาศคณะกรรมการคุ้มครองข้อมูลส่วนบุคคล เรื่อง หลักเกณฑ์การให้ความคุ้มครองข้อมูลส่วนบุคคลที่ส่งหรือโอนไปยังต่างประเทศตามมาตรา 29 แห่งพระราชบัญญัติคุ้มครองข้อมูลส่วนบุคคล พ.ศ. 2562 พ.ศ. 2566)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the personal data protection committee on criteria for the protection of personal data sent or transferred to a foreign country pursuant to section 29 of the personal data protection act b e 2562 b e 2566
acts_norm: notification of the personal data protection committee on criteria for the protection of personal data sent or transferred to a foreign country pursuant to section 29 of the personal data protection act b e 2562 b e 2566
Thailand.impact:
Thailand's Personal Data Protection Committee (PDPC) published a subordinate regulation regarding cross-border transfer of personal data under the Personal Data Protection Act. The Notification concerns about the binding corporate rules (BCRs) and appropriate safeguards to transfer data in exemption of Section 28 of the PDPA. 

Part 1 of the Notification focuses on BCR. Personal data can be transferred to affiliated business or group undertaking if they have issued BCR which have been reviewed and certified by the PDPC (Clause 5) The BCR must be submitted for review and approval, and must meet the following criteria (Clause 7):
(1) A legally binding privacy policy for all parties involved (e.g., processors, senders, receivers, employees) that complies with data protection laws.
(2) A guarantee for data protection, data subject rights, and a proper complaint mechanism for cross-border transfers.
(3) Security measures that meet the minimum standards set by Thailand’s data protection laws.

Part 2 focuses on Approprite Safeguards provides that the sender or transferer must implement Appropriate Safeguards to protect data subjects' rights and ensure effective legal remedies (Clause 8). These safeguards must be legally binding and can take several forms:
(1) Standard Contractual Clauses (SCC): Contracts must include key provisions such as compliance with data protection laws, robust security measures, and specific obligations for data processors and controllers (e.g., following instructions, notifying breaches, and handling data upon contract termination). They must also guarantee effective legal remedies for data subjects, aligning with recognized models like the ASEAN or EU GDPR clauses.
(2) Certification: This involves meeting PDPC-established standards for cross-border transfers, ensuring the safeguards are legally enforceable.
(3) Code of Conduct: Used for transfers between government agencies, this must also provide binding and enforceable data protection measures.
In essence, Part 2 ensures that all methods of safeguarding data in cross-border transfers are legally robust, protect personal data, uphold data subject rights, and allow for effective complaint and remediation mechanisms.

Thailand.act: Personal Data Protection Act B.E.2562 (พระราชบัญญัติคุ้มครองข้อมูลส่วนบุคคล พ.ศ. 2562)
Thailand.act_norm: personal data protection act b e 2562
acts_norm: personal data protection act b e 2562
Thailand.impact:
The Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) of Thailand was published in the Government Gazette on 27 May 2019 and fully entry into force on 1 June 2022. Thai PDPA, a general rule on data protection, was drafted based on EU Regulation 2016/679 (GDPR) and governs how personal data is processed, collected, used, disclosed, and transferred. For example, Section 19 states that the Data Controller shall not collect, use, or disclose personal data unless the data subject has given explicit consent, which can be in the written statement or via electronic means. 

The PDPA will join a suite with other legislations related to the digital economy: cybersecurity, consumer protection, etc. Those who violate or fail to comply with the PDPA will incur penalties, including civil, criminal, and administrative liabilities (Chapter VII Penalties). However, the PDPA shall not apply to operations of data undertaken by a credit bureau company (Section 4.6). Therefore, the details of a particular issue are in the Credit Information Business Operation Act. 

Moreover, before PDPA, data protection is regulated under specific laws, including the National Health Act (personal health information), Credit Information Business Operation Act  (credit information), Telecommunications Business Act (personal information under the telecom sector), Commission of Computer-Related Offences Act (computer data, computer traffic data), and the Cyber Security Maintenance Act (computer data, computer system).

Thailand.act: Credit Information Business Operation Act B.E.2545 (พระราชบัญญัติการประกอบธุรกิจข้อมูลเครดิต พ.ศ. 2545)
Thailand.act_norm: credit information business operation act b e 2545
acts_norm: credit information business operation act b e 2545
Thailand.impact:
According to Section 3 of the Credit Information Business Operation Act 2002, 'information' means facts of credit information or credit mark, through any means and forms: document, diagram, sound recording, etc. The 'credit information' is defined as (1) Facts concerning a customer applying for credit: (a.) Natural person: name, address, date of birth, status, identification card number, passport number, and taxpayer identification number, (b.) juristic person: name, location, registration number or taxpayer-identification number; or (2) History of credit application, including the history of payment for goods or services by credit card.

Regarding rights and duties of a credit information company, Section 20 provides that a credit information company shall disclose or provide credit information to its member or the service user who wishes to use the information for credit analysis and issuance of the credit card. Prior consent must be obtained from the information subject every time before disclosing or providing the information. Without consent, the disclosure can be done only by court order,  the letter requested from the official, or the letter requested from the governmental agencies. The penalty, including fines or imprisonment, or both shall be applied to each committed offense.
Thailand.timeframe: Since November 1999, last amended in July 2022

Thailand.act_norm: telecommunications business act b e 2544 notification of the national telecommunications commission regarding telecommunications service users rights concerning personal information rights to privacy and freedom of communication b e 2566
acts_norm: telecommunications business act b e 2544
notification of the national telecommunications commission regarding telecommunications service users rights concerning personal information rights to privacy and freedom of communication b e 2566
Thailand.impact:
Section 50 Chapter 5: Rights of the Service User of the Telecommunications Business Act 2001 states that the Commission shall establish the measures for user protection concerning personal data, right to privacy, and freedom to communicate through telecommunications. The licensee shall have to comply with the prescribed measures. If there is a person who breaches the right of a service user, the licensee or the Commission shall take action to terminate such breach and inform the user promptly. 

Regarding Section 64, if a licensee has violated or not complied with the measures related to personal data protection, then the Secretary-General shall order the licensee to refrain from such action or carry out rectification and perform the action correctly within a specific period. In addition, Section 66 provides that if the licensee fails to perform under section 64 and the warning notice, then the licensee is subjected to the administrative procedure to pay not less than 20,000 Thai Baht (pprximately 600 USD) per day.

Under the Notification, a telecom licensee must collect users' personal data directly from the users only as necessary and in accordance with lawful purposes (Clause 6). The users must be informed before or at the time of collection, except in the following cases (Clause 10): 
(1) The user has given consent for the licensee to collect their personal data from other sources. In such cases, the licensee must inform the user of the collection from other sources without delay, but no later than 30 days from the date of collection, and must comply with the criteria set forth in the personal data protection law.
(2) The collection of users' personal data falls under an exemption from requiring consent. The licensee must provide users with a means to update their personal data to keep it current, ensuring a secure identity verification and authentication system.
Thailand.timeframe:
Since November 2001, last amended in July 2019;

Since September 2023

Thailand.act: Organization to Assign Frequency Waves and to Regulate the Radio Broadcasting, Radio Television and Telecommunications Services Act B.E.2553 (พระราชบัญญัติองค์กรจัดสรรคลื่นความถี่และกำกับการประกอบกิจการวิทยุกระจายเสียงวิทยุโทรทัศน์ และกิจการโทรคมนาคม พ.ศ.2553)
Thailand.act_norm: organization to assign frequency waves and to regulate the radio broadcasting radio television and telecommunications services act b e 2553
acts_norm: organization to assign frequency waves and to regulate the radio broadcasting radio television and telecommunications services act b e 2553
Thailand.impact: Section 32 of the Organization to Assign Frequency Waves and to Regulate the Radio Broadcasting, Radio Television, and Telecommunications Services Act 2010 stipulates that the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission (NBTC) shall authorize to prescribe the measures for protection of telecommunications service users rights including personal information, privacy rights, and freedom of communication. Wrongdoings by illegal interception, utilization, or disclosure of messages, information, or any other data communicated through telecommunications, the NBTC shall be deemed as the damage person Criminal Procedure Code. If a telecommunications services licensee is an offender of the wrongdoing activities or knows about the wrongdoings but neglects to respond within a reasonable time, the NBTC shall suspend or revoke the telecommunication services license.
Thailand.timeframe: Since December 2010, last amended in October 2022

Thailand.act: Cyber Security Maintenance Act B.E.2562 (พระราชบัญญัติ การรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์ พ.ศ.2562)
Thailand.act_norm: cyber security maintenance act b e 2562
acts_norm: cyber security maintenance act b e 2562
Thailand.impact:
The Cyber Security Maintenance Act is to efficiently protect cybersecurity and to establish approaches to protect and mitigate the risk of Cyber Threats which affect the national security and public order. The Act establishes the "National Cyber Security Committee" (NCSC) and the Coordinating with national and international agencies (CRC) (Section 5 and Section 12). The NCSC is primarily responsible for proposing and overseeing cybersecurity policies, setting standards, enhancing expertise, and coordinating efforts both nationally and internationally (Section 9). The CRC focuses more on monitoring and managing specific cybersecurity threats, particularly at a critical level. It regulates national cybersecurity agencies, sets operational standards, defines the duties of organizations, and analyzes cyber threats to recommend actions to the Committee (Section 13). Additionally, the Act also establishes an Office of the National Cybersecurity Committee as a Government Agency who is a juristic person and not a government sector entity or a state enterprise to responsible for administrative, academic, meeting, and secretarial tasks of the Committee and the CRC (Sections 20 and 22). 

Moreover, several aspects of cybersecurity have been addressed under the Act's Chapter 3 (Maintaining cybersecurity), consisting of policies and plans (Part 1), management (Part 2), critical information infrastructure (Part 3) and coping with cyber threats (Part 4). Those who commit offences under this Act shall be usjbect to fine and/or imprisonment as prescribed under Chapter 4 (Penalty provisions).
references: https://www.ncsa.or.th/กฏหมาย-ข้อบังคับ-และประก.html

Thailand.act:
Computer-Related Crime Act
B.E.2550 (พระราชบัญญัติ ว่าด้วยการกระทำความผิดเกี่ยวกับคอมพิวเตอร์ พ.ศ.2550)
Thailand.act_norm: computer related crime act b e 2550
acts_norm: computer related crime act
Thailand.impact:
The Commission of Computer-Related Offences Act B.E.2550 regulates various offences related to illegal access and actions involving computer systems and data, including penalties (fines and/or imprisonment). The following sections address various cybersecurity offences, such as data breaches and system disruptions:
Sections 5–8: Penalties for illegal access to computer systems, disclosure of access measures, unauthorized access to data, and eavesdropping on computer data. 
Sections 9–12: Penalties for damaging, altering, or disrupting third-party computer data or systems, especially in relation to national security or public safety.
Sections 13–14: Penalties for selling or disseminating tools for committing computer crimes and importing false or harmful data into computer systems. 
Section 15: Service providers who support or consent to these offenses face the same penalties.
Section 16: Penalties for importing or altering a third party’s image in a way that harms their reputation.
Section 17: Extraterritorial offenses involving Thai citizens or foreign offenders are punishable under Thai law if the victim is Thai or the offense occurs in Thailand.

Morever, Chapter 9 provides power and duties ot the competent officials, i.e., a person appointed by the Ministry of Digital Economy and Society.
Thailand.timeframe: Since June 2007, last amended in January 2017
references:
https://www.mdes.go.th/law/detail/3516-พระราชบัญญัติว่าด้วยการกระทำความผิดเกี่ยวกับคอมพิวเตอร์-พ-ศ--๒๕๕๐; 

https://www.mdes.go.th/law/detail/3618-COMPUTER-RELATED-CRIME-ACT-B-E--2550--2007-

Thailand.act: Telecommunications Business Act B.E.2544 (พระราชบัญญัติ การประกอบกิจการโทรคมนาคม พ.ศ. 2544)
Thailand.act_norm: telecommunications business act b e 2544
acts_norm: telecommunications business act b e 2544
Thailand.impact:
Under Chapter X Penalties, Section 74 Any person who commits an act of illegal interception, utilization or disclosure of message, information or any other data by means of telecommunications shall be liable to imprisonment for a term not exceeding two years or to a fine not exceeding four hundred thousand Baht or to both.

Section 77 The Commission shall have the power to settle all offences under this Act for which the penalty is the imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months. In this regard, the Commission may appoint a sub-committee to settle the offence on its behalf, and may prescribe the settlement criteria or any condition for the performance of duties of the sub- committee.

Thailand.act:
Notification of the National Cybersecurity Committee Regarding Standards and Guidelines for Promoting the Development of Cybersecurity Service Systems B.E. 2566
(ประกาศคณะกรรมการการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์แห่งชาติ (กมช.) เรื่องมาตรฐานและแนวทางส่งเสริมพัฒนาระบบการให้บริการเกี่ยวกับการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์ พ.ศ. 2566)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national cybersecurity committee regarding standards and guidelines for promoting the development of cybersecurity service systems b e 2566 2566
acts_norm: notification of the national cybersecurity committee regarding standards and guidelines for promoting the development of cybersecurity service systems b e 2566
Thailand.impact: This Notification is issued under the Cybersecurity Act to provide certification for the cybersecurity service providers. Cybersecurity service providers who are certified for quality in cybersecurity must meet the criteria outlined in this notification. Quality certification is not a form of authorization (Clause 4). There are three levels of certification: (i) initial certification, (ii) advanced certification, and (iii) high-level certification. The i and ii certification can be provided to a natural person or a juristic person, while the (iii) shall be provided only to a juristic person (Clause 5). The applicant shall submitted the required documents, such as work experience certificate and documents demonstrating expertise as prescribed under Clause 6. Once the certification is issued, each type has expiray date and can be renewed (Clause 11 and Clause 13).

Thailand.act:
Notification of the National Cyber Security Committee Regarding tandards for Defining the Security Category for Data or Information Systems B.E.2566
(ประกาศคณะกรรมการการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์แห่งชาติ (กมช.)เรื่องมาตรฐานการกำหนดคุณลักษณะความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์ให้แก่ข้อมูลหรือระบบสารสนเทศ พ.ศ. 2566);

Notification of the National Cyber Security Committee Regarding Minimum Standards for Data and Information Systems B.E.2566
(ประกาศคณะกรรมการการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์แห่งชาติ (กมช.) เรื่อง มาตรฐานขั้นต่ำของข้อมูลหรือระบบสารสนเทศ พ.ศ. 2566)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national cyber security committee regarding tandards for defining the security category for data or information systems b e 2566 2566 notification of the national cyber security committee regarding minimum standards for data and information systems b e 2566 2566
acts_norm: notification of the national cyber security committee regarding tandards for defining the security category for data or information systems b e 2566
notification of the national cyber security committee regarding minimum standards for data and information systems b e 2566
Thailand.impact:
The Notification of the National Cyber Security Committee Regarding Minimum Standards for Data and Information Systems B.E.2566 is issued under the Cybersecurity Act. This Notification prescribes a risk-based classification system for organizations' data or information systems, assessing three key security objectives: (i) confidentiality, (ii) integrity, and (iii) availability (Clause 4). Specifically, organization refers to state agency, public or private supervising organizations, and critical information infrastructure organizations. Each objective is evaluated across three risk levels (low, medium, and high) based on the potential impact on financial value, reputation, user base, operational capacity, and public order (Clauses 5 and 6). Organizations must assess all types of data handled and set the security category based on the highest risk level. The security category should be reviewed at least every three years, with results documented (Clauses 7 and 8).

The Notification of the National Cyber Security Committee Regarding Minimum Standards for Data and Information Systems B.E.2566 is also issued under the Cybersecurity Act. After the Committee has identified the risk-based classification system of information system, the Comittee shall publish the minimum standards for data and information system (Clause 4). These measures involve, for example,  conducting risk assessments, developing incident response plans, implementing access control and system hardining, and strengthening systems for low impact levels. For medium and high impact levels, additional steps are required such as cybersecurity audits, remote connection and removable storage for medium level, while vulnerability assessment and penetration testing, third party management, information sharing, and resilience and recovery are required for high level (Clause 4).

Thailand.act:
Notification of the National Cyber Security Committee Regarding Characteristics, Duties, and Responsibilities of the Computer Emergency Response Team for Organizations of Critical Information Infrastructure and Related Missions and Services B.E.2564
(ประกาศคณะกรรมการการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์แห่งชาติ (กมช.) เรื่องลักษณะหน้าที่และความรับผิดชอบของศูนย์ประสานการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยระบบคอมพิวเตอร์สำหรับหน่วยงานโครงสร้างพื้นฐานสำคัญทางสารสนเทศและภารกิจหรือให้บริการที่เกี่ยวข้อง พ.ศ.2564)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national cyber security committee regarding characteristics duties and responsibilities of the computer emergency response team for organizations of critical information infrastructure and related missions and services b e 2564 2564
acts_norm: notification of the national cyber security committee regarding characteristics duties and responsibilities of the computer emergency response team for organizations of critical information infrastructure and related missions and services b e 2564
Thailand.impact: This Notification is issued under the Cybersecurity Act to prescribe the roles and responsibilities of the Computer Emergency Response Team (CERT) for Organizations of Critical Information Infrastructure (CII) in Thailand. This regulation applies to government agencies, regulators, and public/ private organizations involved in critical information infrastructure (CII) in Thailand. It stipulates that relevant government agencies or regulators of Organizations of Critical Information Infrastructure in each sector must establish CERTs for CIIs, ensuring they meet prescribed characteristics, duties, and responsibilities (Clause 3). The CERTs shall coordinate with Thailand Computer Emergency Response (Attached enclosure, Clause 5).

Thailand.act:
Notification of the National Cyber Security Committee Regarding Establishment, Duties, and Powers of the Thailand Computer Emergency Response Team (ThaiCERT) B.E.2564
(ประกาศคณะกรรมการการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์แห่งชาติ (กมช.) เรื่อง การจัดตั้งหน้าที่และอำนาจของศูนย์ประสานการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยระบบคอมพิวเตอร์แห่งชาติ พ.ศ. 2564)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national cyber security committee regarding establishment duties and powers of the thailand computer emergency response team b e 2564 2564
acts_norm: notification of the national cyber security committee regarding establishment duties and powers of the thailand computer emergency response team b e 2564
Thailand.impact: This Notification is issued under the Cybersecurity Act to establish the Thailand Computer Emergency Response Team (ThaiCERT) as a unit of the National Cyber Security Agency to monitor cybersecurity risks, to track, analyze and process information related to cyber incidents, and to issue incident warnings. ThaiCERT duties and powers are available under Clause 5, including implement measures to prevent cybersecurity risks, and acting as a center of incident reporting when cyber incident occurs (For more information, see Clause 5).

Thailand.act:
Notification of the National Cyber Security Committee Regarding Characteristics and Measures for Prevention, Response, Assessment, Eradication, and Containment of Cyber Incidents at Each Level B.E. 2564 
(ประกาศคณะกรรมการการรักษาความมั่นคงปลอดภัยไซเบอร์แห่งชาติ (กมช.) เรื่อง ลักษณะภัยคุกคามทางไซเบอร์ มาตรการป้องกัน รับมือ ประเมิน ปราบปราม และระงับภัยคุกคามทางไซเบอร์แต่ละระดับ พ.ศ. 2564)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national cyber security committee regarding characteristics and measures for prevention response assessment eradication and containment of cyber incidents at each level b e 2564 2564
acts_norm: notification of the national cyber security committee regarding characteristics and measures for prevention response assessment eradication and containment of cyber incidents at each level b e 2564
Thailand.impact:
This Notification is issued under the Cybersecurity Act to identify the details of cyber incident characteristics (non-critical level, the critical level, and the crisis level) and measures for prevention, response, assessment, eradication, and containment of cyber incidents at each level (Clause 3). 

Under Enclosure 1 Regarding Characteristics and Assessment of Cyber Incidents at Each Level, the level of a cyber incident relies on four factors: impact on devices or operational systems, impacts on system data, system recovery ability, and impacts on customers or users. Characteristics and guideline for impact assessment are implemented.

Under Enclosure 2 Regarding Measures for Prevention, Response, Assessment, Eradication and Containment of Cyber Indicents at Each level, incident handling involves 4 key steps: prepare and protect, detect and analyze, contain/ eradicate, and recovery, and post-indicent activity. Additionally, timeframes for incident notification and report are specified. For instance, initial notification for all incidents via established channel (within the timeframe) shall be 30 minutes and the report submission to the Regulator and the National Cybersecurity Agency shall be within 2 or 4 hours, respectively.

Thailand.act:
Notification of the National Telecommunications Commission regarding Telecommunications Service Users' Rights Concerning Personal Information Rights to Privacy and Freedom of Communication B.E.2566
(ประกาศกสทช. เรื่องมาตรการคุ้มครองสิทธิของผู้ใช้บริการโทรคมนาคมเกี่ยวกับข้อมูลส่วนบุคคลสิทธิในความเป็นส่วนตัวและเสรีภาพในการสื่อสารถึงกันโดยทางโทรคมนาคม พ.ศ. 2566)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national telecommunications commission regarding telecommunications service users rights concerning personal information rights to privacy and freedom of communication b e 2566
acts_norm: notification of the national telecommunications commission regarding telecommunications service users rights concerning personal information rights to privacy and freedom of communication b e 2566
Thailand.impact:
The Notification regarding Telecommunications Service Users' Rights Concerning Personal Information Rights to Privacy and Freedom of Communication B.E.2566 (2023) repealed the Notification regarding Telecommunications Service Users' Rights Concerning Personal Information Rights to Privacy and Freedom of Communication B.E.2549 (2006) (Clause 2). 

Regarding the data retention requirement, Clause 11 provides that a licensee must retain users' personal data related to their service usage for at least 90 days throughout the period of service usage. However, if a user files a complaint regarding telecommunications service usage, requiring personal data to verify the accuracy of service usage, billing, or service quality assessment, the licensee must retain the data only as necessary for resolving the complaint. This retention period must not exceed two years from the date the complaint is filed.

In cases where telecommunications services are terminated, the licensee must retain the users' personal data for at least 90 days from the contract termination date. However, if necessary, or if there are outstanding payments, the licensee must retain the data but not beyond two years from the contract termination date, unless another law requires a different retention period.

Thailand.act:
Computer-Related Crime Act
B.E.2550 (พระราชบัญญัติ ว่าด้วยการกระทำความผิดเกี่ยวกับคอมพิวเตอร์ พ.ศ.2550)
Thailand.act_norm: computer related crime act b e 2550
acts_norm: computer related crime act
Thailand.coverage: Computer data, Computer traffic data/ Telecommunication and Broadcast carrier, Access Service Provider, Host Service Provider, Content Service provider, and Application Service Providers
Thailand.impact:
Section 26 of the Commission of Computer-Related Offences Act 2007 (so-called Computer Crimes Act 2007) defines 'Computer Traffic Data' as data in relation to the communication of computer system or the origin, time, duration, type of service, or else related to the computer system. The Act requires a service provider to retain computer traffic data for not less than 90 days from the date when the data was entered into the computer system. If necessary, the competent official may order any service provider to retain computer traffic data for a period exceeding 90 days but not exceeding 2 years as a matter of an individually exceptional case and on an ad hoc basis. The service provider shall maintain client data which is necessary for identifying the client since his or her first use of service and shall maintain such data for not less than 90 days as from the ending date of service. Those who fail to comply with this measure shall be liable to a fine not exceeding 500,000 Thai Baht (around 16,599 USD).

(**Remark: The Ministry of Information and Communications Technology (ICT) currently is the Ministry of Digital Economy and Society (MDES).)
Thailand.timeframe: Since June 2007, last amended in January 2017

Thailand.act: Notification of the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology Regarding Computer Traffic Data Retention Criterias of Service Providers B.E.2564
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the ministry of information and communications technology regarding computer traffic data retention criterias of service providers b e 2564
acts_norm: notification of the ministry of information and communications technology regarding computer traffic data retention criterias of service providers b e 2564
Thailand.impact:
The Notification of the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology B.E.2564 repealed the existing Notification B.E.2550 (Clause 3). Clause 5 provides that Certain service providers must retain computer traffic data in accordance with the specifications in Annex B, including telecom and broadcast carriers, access service providers, host service providers, internet cafe providers, online application store providers, social media service providers, content and application service providers, cloud computing service providers and digital service providers (Clause 6 and Clause 7). In general cases, service providers must retain computer traffic data for at least 90 days from the date it enters the system (Clause 12.1). If there is reason to suspect a violation of this Act or crimes related to national security, terrorism, transnational crime, or public order, officials may order service providers to retain specific user traffic data for up to six months per extension, but not exceeding two years in total (Clause 12.2). 

The service providers must securely store traffic data with integrity and access control, prevent unauthorized modifications, appoint a liaison for legal compliance, and ensure user identification, even for proxy or Wi-Fi services. Specifically, the service provider shall ensure that traffic data retention enables the identification and authentication of individual users. This includes logging services such as Proxy Servers, Network Address Translation (NAT), Proxy Cache, Cache Engine, or Free services (Clause 9).

Note: Annex A lists down types and examples of service providers, such as VOIP, mobile service providers, Internet data center, Game online, App store, Google play, Chatbot, Telegram, Facebook, Youtube, Linkedin, Instagram, Line, MSN Messenger, Whatsapp, Web board, Blog, Internet Banking, and E-commerce service providers. Service providers must retain traffic data from the effective date of this notification. However, Internet cafés must comply within 1 year, and digital service providers within 180 days of its entry into force (Clause 13).
references: https://www.mdes.go.th/law/detail/4727-ประกาศกระทรวงดิจิทัลเพื่อเศรษฐกิจและสังคม-เรื่อง-หลักเกณฑ์การเก็บรักษาข้อมูลจราจรทางคอมพิวเตอร์ของผู้ให้บริการ-พ-ศ--๒๕๖๔

acts_norm: credit information business operation act b e 2545
notification of the credit information protection committee regarding the duration of data processing b e 2557
Thailand.impact:
The Credit Information Business Operation Act 2002 stipulates the requirements for controlling and processing credit information. Section 13 states that the credit information company, information controller, or processor are not allowed to process the information that is older than that prescribed in the Notification of the Credit Information Protection Committee. Similarly, the processing period shall not exceed 3 years after receiving the credit information (Clause 3 of the Notification in 2014).

In addition, section 17(6) of the Credit Information Act requires that a credit information company or a person assigned to process information shall set up at least a system of recording and reporting the result of every access to the credit information. The information recording must keep for at least 2 years from the date of access to the information.

Thailand.act: Personal Data Protection Act B.E.2562 (พระราชบัญญัติคุ้มครองข้อมูลส่วนบุคคล พ.ศ. 2562)
Thailand.act_norm: personal data protection act b e 2562
acts_norm: personal data protection act b e 2562
Thailand.impact:
The appointment of a Data Protection Officer (DPO) is a mandatory condition under the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA). Section 41 of the Act specifies that the data controller and data processor shall designate a data protection officer in the following circumstances: the activities such as collection, use, or disclosure of the personal data. 

The duty of the DPO, including: advise the data controller and data processor,  investigate the performance of the data controller and data processor, coordinate and cooperate with the Office or the Personal Data Protection Committee (PDPC) when there are problems and keep confidentiality of the personal data (Section 42).

Thailand.act:
Computer-Related Crime Act
B.E.2550 (พระราชบัญญัติ ว่าด้วยการกระทำความผิดเกี่ยวกับคอมพิวเตอร์ พ.ศ.2550)
Thailand.act_norm: computer related crime act b e 2550
acts_norm: computer related crime act
Thailand.impact:
Section 18 of the Commission of Computer-Related Offences Act (so-called Computer Crimes Acts) provides that, when there is reasonable ground to believe that there is a violation of the Act, competent officials have the power to do:
(1) to address written inquiry or issue a summons on a person connected with the offender;
(2) to summon computer traffic data from providers of services;
(3) to order a service provider to hand over to the competent official data concerning users;
(4) to make a copy of computer data, or computer traffic data from the computer system
(5) to hand over such computer data from a person having in possession or custody
(6) to inspect and access a computer system, computer data, computer traffic data, or equipment used for retaining computer data 
(7) to decryption computer data 
(8) to seize or attach a computer system as necessary for identifying details of the offense

The person receiving a request from the competent official shall take action in response to the request without delay. Moreover, section 19 of the Act provides that the officials have the authority to access the computer data, computer traffic data from a computer system without filing a court petition. Only the power according to Section 18(4), (5), (6), (7), and (8) requires to apply motion to the court. 

The Act applies to Computer data, computer traffic data/ Telecommunication and Broadcast carrier, Access Service Provider, Host Service Provider, Content Service provider, and Application Service Providers. 

(*Remark: Sections 18 and 19 of the Commission of Computer-Related Offences Act 2007 is repealed and replaced by the information in Section 13 of Thailand's Computer-Related Crimes Act (No.2) 2017.)
Thailand.timeframe: Since June 2007, last amended in January 2017

Thailand.act: National Intelligence Act B.E.2562 (พระราชบัญญัติข่าวกรองแห่งชาติ พ.ศ.2562)
Thailand.act_norm: national intelligence act b e 2562
acts_norm: national intelligence act b e 2562
Thailand.impact: The National Intelligence Act B.E.2562 gives the power to the National Intelligence Agency to perform duties related to activities on intelligence operations, civil security safeguard and monitoring the situations which affect the national security (Section 5).  The National Intelligence Agency may request public agencies or any person to submit information or documents relevant to national security within a specified period (Section 6). In cases where it is necessary to obtain such information, the Agency is authorized to undertake any necessary actions by adopting electronic, scientific, telecommunication devices, or other technology tools to obtain such information. Regulations, means and requirements of the proceeding shall be in accordance with rules set by the Director with the approval of the Prime Minister. The proceedings under this section, if performing duties or acting in good faith, reasonably and for the benefit of the national security or the public safety, are considered legitimate.

Thailand.act:
Copyright Act B.E.2537
(พระราชบัญญัติ ลิขสิทธิ์ พ.ศ. 253);

Copyright Act (No.5) B.E.2565
(พระราชบัญญัติลิขสิทธิ์ (ฉบับที่ 5) พ.ศ. 2565)
Thailand.act_norm: copyright act b e 2537 copyright act b e 2565 2565
acts_norm: copyright act b e 2537
copyright act b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
Copyright “safe harbor” protection for intermediaries (service providers) is contained in the 2015 amendments to copyright laws (Copyright Act No.2 and No.3). The provisions exempt Internet intermediaries from liability in broad circumstances, provided that they did not control, initiate, or order the infringement. Section 32(3) provides that a copyright work of another person which does not conflict with normal exploitation of the copyright work by the owner fo copyright and does not unreasonably prejudice the legitimate rights of the copyright owner shall not be deemed an infringement. Specifically, comment, critisim or introduction of word with an acknowledgement of the ownership of copyright in such work shall not be considered as an infringement. 

Copyright Act (No.5) 2022 has added provisions to enhance protection to internet service providers and against online copyright infingement. For example, timely suspension of online copyright infringement is provided (Section 43).

Note: Section 32 (3) is one example of the copyright infringement exceptions provided in section 32, however, it is considered as a general exception and does not specifically address online or digital copyright infringement.

Thailand.act: Notification of the Ministry of Digital Economy and Society Regarding Process of the Notification, Blocking Dissemination, and Removal of Computer Data B.E.2565 (ประกาศกระทรวงดิจิทัลเพื่อเศรษฐกิจและสังคมเรื่องขั้นตอนการแจ้งเตือนการระงับการทำให้แพร่หลายของข้อมูลคอมพิวเตอร์และการนำข้อมูลคอมพิวเตอร์ออกจากระบบคอมพิวเตอร์ พ.ศ.2565)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the ministry of digital economy and society regarding process of the notification blocking dissemination and removal of computer data b e 2565
acts_norm: notification of the ministry of digital economy and society regarding process of the notification blocking dissemination and removal of computer data b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
The Notification of the Ministry of Digital Economy and Society (MDES) in 2017 introduces the 'Notice and Take Down' procedure to remove the offenses content and the intermediary's liability. However, the 2017 Notification has been amended since 2022, which no longer allows the content owner to submit an appeal complaint. The service providers shall remove the claimed content within 24 hours after received the Notification. 

This Notification also grants an authority to the official to submit the notices. Moreover, if the service providers do not remove certain content within the specified period or appeal, they shall be determined as internationally supports under the Act (Sections 14 or 15). Specifically, the Notification divided into offenses under Section 14 (1) that must be removed within 7 days, offenses under Section 14 (2) and (3) that must be removed within 24 hours, and offenses under Section 14 (4) that must be removed within 3 days, but If it is child pornography must be removed within 24 hours.

Thailand.act:
Computer-Related Crime Act
B.E.2550 (พระราชบัญญัติ ว่าด้วยการกระทำความผิดเกี่ยวกับคอมพิวเตอร์ พ.ศ.2550)
Thailand.act_norm: computer related crime act b e 2550
acts_norm: computer related crime act
Thailand.impact:
Section 26 of the Commission of Computer-Related Offences Act requires service providers to retain computer traffic data for no less than 90 days from the date that data enters the computer system. But in case of necessity the competent official may order. The service providers, including coffee shop owners in Thailand are required to retain a 'Log file' of their customers’ computer traffic data and their IP address for 90 days to 2 years depending on each case, unless they shall be subjected to fine  (Section 26). To connect to the coffee shop Free-WIFI, the customers require to fill in their identification, including their full name, ID card number, or passport as required. 

(*Remark: 'Computer Traffic Data' includes data in relation to the communication of computer system that indicates the origin, source, terminal, route, time, date, size, duration, type of service, or else relating to the communication (Section 3).)

Thailand.act: Notification of the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology Regarding Computer Traffic Data Retention Criterias of Service Providers B.E.2564
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the ministry of information and communications technology regarding computer traffic data retention criterias of service providers b e 2564
acts_norm: notification of the ministry of information and communications technology regarding computer traffic data retention criterias of service providers b e 2564
Thailand.impact:
The Notification of the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology B.E.2564 repealed the existing Notification B.E.2550 (Clause 3). Clause 5 provides that Certain service providers must retain computer traffic data in accordance with the specifications in Annex B, including telecom and broadcast carriers, access service providers, host service providers, internet cafe providers, online application store providers, social media service providers, content and application service providers, cloud computing service providers and digital service providers (Clause 6 and Clause 7). 

Clause 8 provides that service providers must implement a digital identity verification and authentication system for all users. This system must utilize technologies that comply with the conditions and minimum standards of reliability and electronic media used for authentication, as specified by the Electronic Transactions Development Agency (ETDA) under Chapter 3/1 on Digital Authentication Systems of the Electronic Transactions Act B.E. 2544 (2001) or other equivalent standards that are not lower than those stipulated in this notification or as prescribed by the Minister of Digital Economy and Society.

Note: Annex A lists down types and examples of service providers, such as VOIP, mobile service providers, Internet data center, Game online, App store, Google play, Chatbot, Telegram, Facebook, Youtube, Linkedin, Instagram, Line, MSN Messenger, Whatsapp, Web board, Blog, Internet Banking, and E-commerce service providers.

Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding registration and collection of mobile phone user information b e 2562 notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission on identity and information verification for mobile phone users b e 2567
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding registration and collection of mobile phone user information b e 2562
notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission on identity and information verification for mobile phone users b e 2567
Thailand.impact:
According to the Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission regarding Registration and Collection of Mobile Phone User Information B.E.2562, telecom service operators shall collect the information of the SIM card users. The required information, such as ID card, passport, and address, shall be provided to register mobile phone number (Clauses 4 and 9). The operator can refuse to offer a service to a person who do not  give their information (Clause 6). 

According to the Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission on Identity and Information Verification for Mobile Phone Users B.E. 2567, mobile operators are required to verify the identity of mobile phone users who have 6 or more mobile numbers registered in
their names (Clause 4).
1) users with 6 - 100 mobile numbers must be verified within 180 days from the date the Notification became effective (due 13 July 2024).
2) users with 101 or more mobile numbers must be verified within 30 days from the date the Notification became effective
(due 14 February 2024)
- Mobile operators must notify mobile phone users under the scope of the Notification regarding the deadlines, steps and verification channels. Mobile operators then must verify that the identity of the user and that of the registered user for such mobile number are the same (Clause 5).
- Mobile numbers that are not verified within their due dates will be suspended from all services except answering calls, 25 Findings/ Inquiries regarding the sub-pillars Response Further information Please specify any relevant information, such as the
name of the law, timeframe, and impacts of receiving SMS, mobile data for accessing the operator's app or webpage, and calls to emergency numbers (Clause 6).

Thailand.impact:
The Royal Decree on the Operation of Digital Platform Services Businesses that are Subject to Prior Notification supervises the operation of a digital platform service business. Specifically, Chapter II provides Rules for operation of digital platform service business and supervision. However, there are no monitoring requirements implemented in Thailand. 

Moreover, it is important to note that in the Royal Decree on the Operation of Digital Platform Services Businesses that are Subject to Prior Notification, the content monitoring requirement pertains to product safety monitoring, which may differ from content monitoring requirements under other laws.

Thailand.act_norm: constitution of the kingdom of thailand b e 2560 notification of the national council of peace and orderno 18 2557 b e 2557 notification of the national council of peace and order no 26 2557 regarding supervision and surveillance of the online social media b e 2557 ministry of digital economy and society website statistics on blocking illegal and counterfeit social media pages and urls 2024
acts_norm: constitution of the kingdom of thailand b e 2560
notification of the national council of peace and orderno 18 2557 b e 2557
notification of the national council of peace and order no 26 2557 regarding supervision and surveillance of the online social media b e 2557
ministry of digital economy and society website statistics on blocking illegal and counterfeit social media pages and urls 2024
Thailand.impact:
The Constitution of the Kingdom of Thailand B.E.2560 states that all notifications, orders, and performances of the National Council for Peace and Order (NCPO) shall be considered constitutional, lawful, and effective under this Constitution (Section 279). Thailand’s government was formally transferred from the military’s NCPO to the National Assembly in July 2019. The NCPO Notifications and Orders have been repealed, including 66 revoked in 2019 and 24 more in 2024. However, the government still retains several NCPO notifications and related laws that are reported to restrict freedom of expression, for example, the NCPO Notifications No. 18/2557 and No. 26/2557. 

Specifically, according to the NCPO Notification No.18/2557(2014) regarding Public Information Dissemination, all types of media services, including both public and private, must prohibit from broadcasting or disseminating the following information: (3) A criticism of the performance of the NCPO and related persons; (4) A confidential information of the state agency; (5) An information that creates misunderstanding and inciting conflicts in the Kingdom; and (7) Threatening to harm to any person that could create fear among the public.

The NCPO Notification No.26/2557 regarding Supervision and Surveillance of the Online Social Media provides that the Ministry of Digital Economy and Society (MDES) has the power to designate a competent officer to access/ investigate the computer traffic data and online content within the scope of inciting violence and to oppose the performance of NCPO (Clause 1). If the online content is under the scope of Clause 1, then the officer shall block/suspend certain information and access to the block/suspend the dissemination of the website, social media content, text, images, and audio clip, if certain content has the following nature, such as incite violence, against the law, or oppose the performance of NCPO (Clause 2).

The number of URLs blocked by court orders is not available. However, the MDES's Statistics on blocking illegal and counterfeit social media pages and URLs reported that from October 2023 to September 2024 has intensified efforts to combat online crime, particularly by blocking illegal social media pages and URLs. A total of 150,425 items were blocked—an 8.51-fold increase from the previous year. Aside from online gambling (62,213 URLs) and fraud/misinformation (51,088 URLs), 37,124 URLs were blocked. Websites have also been blocked for national security reasons.

Thailand.act:
Notification of Electronic Transactions Development Agency No.3/2567 Regarding Guidelines for Advertising on Digital Platforms B.E.2567
(ประกาศสำนักงานพัฒนาธุรกรรมทางอิเล็กทรอนิกส์ที่ธพด.32567เรื่องคู่มือการดูแลโฆษณาบนบริการแพลตฟอร์มดิจิทัล พ.ศ. 2567)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of electronic transactions development agency no 3 2567 regarding guidelines for advertising on digital platforms b e 2567
acts_norm: notification of electronic transactions development agency no 3 2567 regarding guidelines for advertising on digital platforms b e 2567
Thailand.impact:
This guideline, issued by the Electronic Transactions Development Agency (ETDA), aims to support digital platform service providers in implementing effective self-regulation mechanisms under Section 32 of the Royal Decree on Digital Platform Services B.E. 2565. The purpose of the Notification of Electronic Transactions Development Agency No.3/2567 regarding Guidelines for Advertising on Digital Platforms B.E.2567 is to serve as a guideline for digital platform service business operators and is not legally binding.

It provides best practices for overseeing advertisements to enhance credibility and accountability while addressing issues such as investment scams, impersonation, counterfeit goods, misinformation, and illegal activities. However, compliance with this guideline does not exempt businesses from legal responsibilities under other applicable laws, and it remains a non-legally binding reference that should be adapted as appropriate for each platform (Clause 1 Objectives). 

Digital platform operators shall have the following obligations:
- Verify advertisers (Clauses 5 and 6)
- Securely store data into machine-readable format, including watchlists for high-risk advertisers, blacklists for those with past violations, and whitelists for trusted advertisers. Data retention periods should be appropriately set, considering advertiser quality assessments (Clause 7).
- Review ads before publication (Clause 8)
- Establish Ads library (Clause 9)

Moreover, the digital platform operators shall monitor published ads using automated systems or personnel. They must provide reporting channels for flagged content, prioritize serious violations, and oversee advertiser accounts for compliance (Clauses 10-12).

Thailand.act:
Tobacco Product Control Act B.E. 2560 
(พระราชบัญญัติควบคุมผลิตภัณฑ์ยาสูบ พ.ศ. 2560)

Thailand.impact: According to the Tobacco Product Control Act, it is strictly prohibited for any individual or entity to engage in advertising or marketing communications for tobacco products (Section 30) and display the name or mark of manufacture or importer tobacco products, or any other symbol or thing which make the general public understand that the name or mark belongs to tobacco products in order advertise tobacco products in electronic media, computer network system (Section 31(1)).

(พระราชบัญญัติควบคุมเครื่องดื่มแอลกอฮอล์ พ.ศ. 2551)

acts_norm: alcoholic beverage control act b e 2551
Thailand.impact: Under the Alcoholic Beverage Control Act, advertising the name or trademark of an alcoholic beverage in a manner that highlights its properties or encourages others to consume is strictly forbidden. The only exception to this rule is for advertisements broadcast from outside the Kingdom (Section 32). These restrictions are applied to all forms of advertisement, including online.

Thailand.act:
Royal Decree on the Operation of Digital Platform Services Businesses that are Subject to Prior Notification B.E.2565 
(พระราชกฤษฎีกา การประกอบธุรกิจบริการแพลตฟอร์มดิจิทัลที่ต้องแจ้งให้ทราบ พ.ศ.2565)
Thailand.act_norm: royal decree on the operation of digital platform services businesses that are subject to prior notification b e 2565
acts_norm: royal decree on the operation of digital platform services businesses that are subject to prior notification b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
According to the Royal Decree, issued under the Electronic Transaction Act, digital service platforms are required to notify before operating their business (Sections 8 and 12). The royal decree defines "Digital Platform Service" as "the provision of intermediary services that connect business users, consumers, or users for the purpose of generating electronic transactions, with or without remuneration but does not include digital platform service, which is for the offering of goods or services of a single operator of a digital platform service or its affiliate acting as its representative, with or without remuneration." (Section 3).

Companies operating online platform services that have a gross income of more than THB 50,000,000 per year or have more than 5,000 monthly active users in Thailand must comply with the full-form notification requirement .Online platform service providers that do not meet the previous criteria, including e-service platforms containing web-boards hyperlinks/banners, are subject to the short-form notification. Both full-form and short-form notification information are required to be updated annually. 

The Royal Decree also applies to the offshore operator that meet the prescribed criteria (e.g.,using .th domain name, using the Thai language or currency on the platform) (Sections 9 and 10). Note that for full-form notification, platforms are required to notify the ETDA by 18 November 2023, and for short-form notification, platforms are required to notify the ETDA by 20 August 2024.
references:
https://www.mdes.go.th/law/detail/6356-พระราชกฤษฎีกาการประกอบธุรกิจบริการแพลตฟอร์มดิจิทัลที่ต้องแจ้งให้ทราบ-พ-ศ--๒๕๖๕;

https://www.etda.or.th/getattachment/Regulator/DigitalPlatform/law/Clean-Royal-Decree-on-DP-Corrected-1.pdf.aspx?lang=th-TH

Thailand.act_norm: export and import of goods act b e 2522 notification of the ministry of commerce on prescribing electronic waste to be goods subject to import prohibition measures b e 2563 2563 notification of the ministry of commerce regarding determination of counterfeit goods and pirated goods as goods prohibited from export import and bringing in transit b e 2565
acts_norm: export and import of goods act b e 2522
notification of the ministry of commerce on prescribing electronic waste to be goods subject to import prohibition measures b e
notification of the ministry of commerce regarding determination of counterfeit goods and pirated goods as goods prohibited from export import and bringing in transit b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
Thailand does not implement import ban on ICT products or services . Under the Export and Import of Goods Act B.E. 2522, the law stipulates that, where it is necessary or appropriate, the Minister of Commerce, with and by the approval of the Council of Ministers, shall have the power to issue a notification in the Government Gazette specifying any goods to be prohibited for export, import, or brought in transit (section 5(1) and section 5/1). Any person who imports or transits the prohibited goods shall be liable to a fine, imprisonment, or both (Section 20 and Section 20/1). Thailand has implemented an import ban on electronic waste under the Notification of the Ministry of Commerce on Prescribing Electronic Waste to be Goods Subject to Import Prohibition Measures, B.E. 2563 (2020), which came into force on 15 September 2020.

Furthermore, counterfeit and pirated goods are prohibited from being exported, imported, or brought in transit under the Notification of the Ministry of Commerce Re: Determination of Counterfeit Goods and Pirated Goods as Goods Prohibited from Export, Import and Bringing in Transit B.E. 2565 (2022)", which came into force on 29 July 2022.

(*Remark: Section 20/1 has added since the enactment of the Export and Import of Goods Act (No.2) 2015.)

Thailand.act_norm: export and import of goods act b e 2522 ministerial regulation on the application for and granting of a license to export and import goods into and out of the kingdom b e 2555
acts_norm: export and import of goods act b e 2522
ministerial regulation on the application for and granting of a license to export and import goods into and out of the kingdom b e 2555
Thailand.impact: Under the Export and Import of Goods Act B.E.2522, the law stipulates that the Minister of Commerce has the power to issue Notifications specifying any goods which require a license prior to the export or import (Section 5(2)). Section 7 provides that after the notification specifying any goods which requires a license for the export or import under section 5(2) has been issued, no one shall export or import such goods unless a license has been obtained from the Minister of Commerce or person entrusted by the Minister of Commerce. The Export and Import of Goods Act does not impose import restrictions on ICT products.

Since April 1979, last amended in September 2015;

Since 1980

Thailand.act_norm: radio communication act b e 2498 notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding the importation of radio communication equipment b e 2554
acts_norm: radio communication act b e 2498
notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding the importation of radio communication equipment b e 2554
Thailand.impact:
Regarding the Radio Communication Act, any persons who wish to import radio communication devices or any ancillary devices into the Kingdom are required to obtain a license (Section 6 amended). A license to import shall be valid for 180 days from the date of issuance (Section 9(4) amended). The telecommunications devices required to obtain a license include a Radio modem, Base station, Cellular repeater, Microwave link, Antenna, FM Transmitter, GPS Tracking, etc. The import license is valid for 180 days after issuance, and those who violate the law shall be liable to fine, imprisonment, or both (Section 23 amended). 

In addition, the Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission (NBTC) on Import of Radio Communication Equipment 2011 includes the different set of requirements to the purpose of importation that the importers must follow, including fees payment and the document of telecoms standard certification, and technical specification.

(*Remark: Section 4, 6, 9 and 23 of the Radio Communication Act 1955 are amended by Radio Communication Act (No.3) 1992.)
Thailand.timeframe:
Since January 1955, last amended in March 1992;

Since May 2011

factory act b e 2535
Thailand.impact:
The commitments of Thailand under the Agreement on 'Trade-Related Investment Measures' (TRIMs) include the elimination of local content requirement measures. The country implements TRIMs within the meaning of category 1(a) of the Illustrative List of Annex of the Agreement on TRIMs. These TRIMs are not general as the Ministers have authorities under respective legislations to determine product sectors upon which TRIMS are applicable. TRIMs could be initiated under Factory Act. The Minister of Industry empowers to  to prescribe the kind, quality, ratio of raw materials, sources of raw materials and/or factors or kind of energy to use or produce in the factory (Section 32 of the Factory Act 1992). 

Note that the Factory Act B.E.2535 was last amended by the Factory Act (No.3) B.E.2562.

Thailand.act_norm: national standardization act b e 2551 industrial products standards act b e 2511
acts_norm: national standardization act b e 2551
industrial products standards act b e 2511
Thailand.impact:
Standards and technical requirements regulations, as well as conformity assessment and accreditation are regulated under the National Standardization Act and Industrial Products Standards Act. The Thai Industrial Standards Institute (TISI) is established by the Ministry of Industry, the national standards body in Thailand. For industrial promotion, the foreign or international standard shall be referred to as any industrial standard. The Ministry may refer to certain foreign or international standards which their documents are available in the foreign language (Section 15 amended by the Industrial Product Standard Act (No.7) in 2015). Therefore, the country has implemented the TISI TIS standards on various products.

In addition, the country has adopted several international standards: WTO Technical Barriers to Trade (TBT), International Organization for Standardization (ISO), and International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC), as well as participated in international and regional standardization: WTO Technical Barriers to Trade (TBT) and the regional standard of ASEAN Consultative Committee for Standards and Quality (ACCSQ).

Since March 2008, last amended in October 2022;

Since December 1968, last amended in August 2019

Thailand.act:
Notification of the Conformity Assessment Standards
Development Committee Regarding the Procedures and
Format for Establishing the Thai Conformity Assessment
Standards B.E. 2565
(ประกาศคณะกรรมการกำหนดมาตรฐานด้านการตรวจสอบและรับรอง เรื่องกำหนดกระบวนการและรูปแบบในการจัดทำมาตรฐานการตรวจสอบและรับรองแห่งชาติ (มตช.) พ.ศ. 2565)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the conformity assessment standards development committee regarding the procedures and format for establishing the thai conformity assessment standards b e 2565 2565
acts_norm: notification of the conformity assessment standards
development committee regarding the procedures and
format for establishing the thai conformity assessment
standards b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
Pursuant to the National Standardization Act, the Notification of the Conformity Assessment Standards Development Committee Regarding the Procedures and Format for Establishing the Thai Conformity Assessment Standards B.E. 2565 repeals the Notification of the Conformity Assessment Standards Development Committee Regarding the Procedures and Format for Establishing the Thai Conformity Assessment Standards B.E. 2564. This Notification provides detailed procedures and format of the establishment of the Thai Conformity Assessment Standards (TCAS). The process for developing TCAS mainly involves the National Standards Body (NSB), the Standards Development Organization (SDO), and the Conformity Assessment Standards Development Committee.

The requirements for public participation in the standards development process are available under Clause 5 and 6 (Public Input in Drafting and Adopting
Standards via Sub-Committee) and Clause 7 (Standards Development via Workshop Agreements). These Clauses ensure public participation in drafting and adopting standards by allowing stakeholders to submit proposals and provide feedback on translations and relevance before approval. The public announcements in advance and a neutral chairperson to build consensus are also required, as well as the finalized drafts are submitted for approval and officially published.
