Thailand.impact:
The Organization to Assign Frequency Act provides that the telecom regulator, the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission (NTBC), is empowered to set desired standards and required technical specifications for sound broadcasting services, television services, telecommunication services, and radio communication services, for example, the Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission Regarding Standards for the Quality of Telecommunication Services B.E.2562 (Section 27 (10)). 

From the Telecommunication Standardization Framework, the NBTC develops mandatory and voluntary telecommunication standards. Thailand has implemented the technical standard from the recommendation for ITU-T Standardization recommendation in the telecommunication sector.
Thailand.timeframe:
Since December 2010, last amended in October 2022;

Since October 2019

Thailand.act: Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission Regarding Standards for the Quality of Telecommunication Services B.E. 2565
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding standards for the quality of telecommunication services b e 2565
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding standards for the quality of telecommunication services b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
The NBTC has issued a new announcement on telecommunications service quality standards, which continues to follow the recommendations of the International Telecommunication Union – Telecommunication Standardization Sector (ITU-T). The title of this new announcement is: Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission Regarding Standards for the Quality of Telecommunication Services B.E. 2565

Moreover, under the Telecommunication Standardization Framework, the NBTC develops both mandatory and voluntary telecommunication standards. Thailand has adopted technical standards based on the ITU-T Recommendation E.800 (Clause 4), which addresses quality of service (QoS) parameters in the telecommunications sector.

Thailand.act_norm: asean sectoral mra for electrical and electronic equipment 2002 asean harmonized electrical and electronic equipment regulatory regime 2005
acts_norm: asean sectoral mra for electrical and electronic equipment 2002
asean harmonized electrical and electronic equipment regulatory regime 2005
Thailand.impact: In general, the Thai Industrial Standards (TIS) is a technical requirement established by the Thai Industrial Standards Institute (TISI). The listed products, including electrical appliances and accessories, require apply for a compulsory certification. The ASEAN Framework Agreement on Mutual Recognition Arrangements provides the principles for developing sectoral Mutual Recognition Arrangements (MRAs) among ASEAN member states. A member state shall accept or recognize the test reports and certifications issued by the testing laboratories and certification bodies of other parties (ASEAN EE MRA Article 3).

Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding inspection and certification of the telecommunications devices and equipment b e 2556 notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding electromagnetic compatibility of the telecommunication and telecommunication equipment b e 2562
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding inspection and certification of the telecommunications devices and equipment b e 2556
notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding electromagnetic compatibility of the telecommunication and telecommunication equipment b e 2562
Thailand.impact:
The telecommunication and telecommunication equipment application are categorized into three groups: Class A (requires registration), Class B (requires certification), and Supplier's Declaration of Conformity (requires a test report or equipment certification). However, it is important to note that Class A and Class B telecommunication products are not subject to SDoC. 

Specifically, Chapter 4 focuses on suppliers declaration conformity. The suppliers are responsible to submit a written confirmation that telecommunications equipement and devices comply with technical standards or technical requirements must use the Supplier’s Declaration of Conformity (SDoC) as specified in Clause 10 and followed the format in Annex B (Clause 19). Operators must retain copies of the self-declaration form along with supporting documentation: product information, specifications or technical characteristics and test data verifying compliance with technical standards or technical requirements (Clause 19). Additionally, the NBTC has not entered into any Mutual Recognition Agreements (MRAs) with other countries at this time.

Additionally, in 2019, the NBTC Notification Regarding Electromagnetic Compatibility of the Telecommunication and Telecommunication Equipment requires that the telecommunication and telecommunication terminal equipment, including telephone set, facsimile equipment, key telephone system, modem, data terminal equipment, PABX, xDSL equipment, must follow the Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC) technical standard. Therefore, an applicant of such telecommunication equipment shall submit sDoC as the specifications laid down in the NBTC Notification issued in 2019.

Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission Regarding Inspection and Certification of the Telecommunications Devices and Equipment B.E.2556 (ประกาศคณะกรรมการกิจการกระจายเสียง กิจการโทรทัศน์และกิจการโทรคมนาคมแห่งชาติเรื่องการตรวจสอบและรับรองมาตรฐานเครื่องโทรคมนาคมเเละอุปกรณ์ พ.ศ.2556);

Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding inspection and certification of the telecommunications devices and equipment b e 2556 annex c supplier s declaration of conformity form
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding inspection and certification of the telecommunications devices and equipment b e 2556
annex c supplier s declaration of conformity form
Thailand.impact:
According to the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission (NBTC) Notification in 2013, suppliers are required to submit a Supplier’s Declaration of Conformity (SDoC) form to the NBTC office, confirming that the equipment complies with the applicable standards or technical requirements (Clause 3(2) and Clause 12). Registration is based on this declaration of conformity to demonstrate compliance with Thailand’s national standards. Standard test reports issued by a Competent Accreditation Body are generally accepted as the basis for such declarations.

The applicant to submit the SDoC must be a Thai person or a juristic person established under Thai law. If the juristic person is not established under Thai law or has a location in a foreign country, its representative or agent must be a Thai person or a Thai juristic person (Clause 2).
Thailand.timeframe:
Since September 2013;

Last reviewed in June 2025

Thailand.coverage: Telecommunication equipment Class A and Class B
Thailand.impact: Telecommunications and telecommunication equipment Class A and Class B must be tested to ensure that the products conform with the technical standard (Clause 6). Telecommunications and telecommunication equipment Class A and Class B must be tested to ensure that the products conform with the technical standard. For the inspection process, Class A and Class B equipment will be tested separately in a specific laboratory (Clause 3). The laboratory must be certified by the Accreditation Body (International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation: ILAC) or the listed Testing Laboratory, etc. After the testing, the office will grant the Report and Approval Certification/ Certificate of Conformity if the products have passed the inspection.

Thailand.act: Notification of the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission Regarding Inspection and Certification of the Telecommunications Devices and Equipment B.E.2556 (ประกาศคณะกรรมการกิจการกระจายเสียง กิจการโทรทัศน์และกิจการโทรคมนาคมแห่งชาติเรื่องการตรวจสอบและรับรองมาตรฐานเครื่องโทรคมนาคมเเละอุปกรณ์ พ.ศ.2556)
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding inspection and certification of the telecommunications devices and equipment b e 2556
acts_norm: notification of the national broadcasting and telecommunications commission regarding inspection and certification of the telecommunications devices and equipment b e 2556
Thailand.impact:
To apply for inspection and certification of standards or to submit a self-declaration (Clause 2), the applicant must be a Thai national or a juristic person established under Thai law. Foreign entities must appoint a Thai individual or juristic person as their representative. In addition, if the applicant is engaged in the manufacturing, importation, or trading of radiocommunication equipment, they must hold the relevant license under the radiocommunication law (Clause 3). Those involved in the production, importation, distribution, or possession for distribution of equipment used to receive or convert signals for subscription-based broadcasting or television services must also obtain the appropriate license under broadcasting and television laws (Clause 4).

To register or certify the radiocommunication equipment, an applicant shall submit the required document, including technical specification and test report of such equipment (Clause 6 and Clause 11, respectively).

Thailand.act: Foreign Business Act  B.E.2542 (พระราชบัญญัติการประกอบธุรกิจของคนต่างด้าว พ.ศ. 2542)
Thailand.act_norm: foreign business act b e 2542
acts_norm: foreign business act b e 2542
Thailand.impact: Foreign Business Act 1999 governs foreign investment in Thailand. Section 8(3) states that no foreigner may operate such businesses in respect of which Thai nationals are not yet ready to compete with foreigners, as prescribed in List Three, unless upon obtaining permission from the Director‐General with the approval of the Commission. E-commerce business is subjected to 'other service business' under List 3 (21). Foreigners can acquire 100% equity shares and operate such business upon obtaining permission from the Director‐General with the approval of the Commission (Sections 10, 11).
Thailand.timeframe: Since November 1999, last amended in August 2019

acts_norm: electronic transactions act b e 2544
Thailand.impact:
Thailand does not implement restrictions on e-commerce purchase. Products and services offered through online or e-commerce channel are subject to regulations by various government bodies and regulators, such as the Office of the Consumer Protection Board.  

Moreover, Thailand’s Electronic Transactions Act B.E. 2544 (2001) provides legal recognition for electronic data, signatures, and contracts, ensuring they have the same effect as paper-based documents. It aims to promote trust and confidence in digital transactions, including online purchases and deliveries, by ensuring that e-commerce activities are legally valid and enforceable. The ETA applies to both civil and commercial electronic transactions, excluding the transaction that relates to family or succession.
Thailand.timeframe: Since December 2001, last amended in May 2019

Thailand.act_norm: business registration act b e 2499 notification of the ministry of commerce regarding requirements for the business operators to register their businesses b e 2553 notification of the ministry of commerce re requiring business operators to register businesses and prescribing businesses not falling under the applicability of the business registration act b e 2499 b e 2567
acts_norm: business registration act b e 2499
notification of the ministry of commerce regarding requirements for the business operators to register their businesses b e 2553

notification of the ministry of commerce re requiring business operators to register businesses and prescribing businesses not falling under the applicability of the business registration act b e 2499 b e 2567
Thailand.impact:
The objective of electronic commercial registration is to improve reliability and confirm the existence of entrepreneurs. Under the Business Registration Act, the objective is to store business registration statistics. Section 10 provides that registration shall be made at the Business Registration Office in the area where the principal office of the operator of a business is located. If the principal office is located in a foreign country and the business is carried out in Thailand, registration shall be made at the Business Registration Office in the area where the principal branch office is located. The operator of a business shall file an application for registration of the business within 30 days from the date of commencement of such business (Section 11). The information to be submitted in the application, such as name, age, ethic origin, the amount of capital regularly used in the operation, and type of the businesses (for full details, please see Section 12). 

The Notification of the Ministry of Commerce Regarding Requirements for the Business Operators to Register their Businesses (No.11) B.E.2553 is issued under the Business Registration Act. Clause 5(4) provides that website that includes the transaction of goods and services must register the 'E-Commercial Registration' under the Commercial Registration Act 1956. Therefore, the electronic business operating in the internet system, service providers, web hosting, e-Marketplace or platform (for example, Lazada, Shopee, etc.), require to register to the Department of Business Development (DBD). In addition, the DBD registration is valid for 5 years. Ordinary persons and juristic persons engaged in E-commerce businesses without commercial registration are considered to be in violation and shall subject to a fine penalty as stipulated. Also, foreign companies require to submit additional documents for registration: 
(1) A copy of company registration; and
(2) An appointment of operation in Thailand: work permits and a foreign business license or document certificate (if any).

Additionally, the Notification of the Ministry of Commerce Regarding Requiring Business Operators to Register Businesses and Prescribing Businesses Not Falling Under the Applicability of the Business Registration Act, B.E. 2499, B.E. 2567 (2024), is issued under the Business Registration Act B.E. 2499 (1956). Clause 5(5) requires that business operators who are individuals or general partnerships engaged in online trading of goods or services — such as a clothing store operating through marketplace platforms like Lazada or Shopee — must register their business. Failure to register results in the imposition of regulatory fines under Section 19(1) of the Business Registration Act and the Act on the Imposition of Regulatory Fines, B.E. 2565 (2022). 

Moreover, Clause 6 provides that registration shall be made at the Business Registration Office in the area where the principal office of the operator of the business is located. The information to be submitted in the application includes: name, age, nationality, type of business, amount of capital regularly used in the operation of business, and addresses of the principal office (for full details, please see Section 12).

Thailand.impact: Thailand's Electronic Transactions Act applies to both civil and commercial electronic transactions, excluding the transaction which relates to family or succession. The service business concerning electronic transaction service require to notify, register, or obtain a license before commencing such business, where it is necessary for financial and commercial security; the credibility and recognition of electronic transactions systems; or for the prevention of damage to the public (Section 32 amended). Moreover, any person who wishes to operate the services prescribed in the Royal Decree must notify or submit an application for a license to the competent authority (Sections 33 and 34 amended).
Thailand.timeframe: Since December 2001, last amended in May 2019

Thailand.act:
Royal Decree on the Operation of Digital Platform Services Businesses that are Subject to Prior Notification B.E.2565 
(พระราชกฤษฎีกา การประกอบธุรกิจบริการแพลตฟอร์มดิจิทัลที่ต้องแจ้งให้ทราบ พ.ศ.2565)
Thailand.act_norm: royal decree on the operation of digital platform services businesses that are subject to prior notification b e 2565
acts_norm: royal decree on the operation of digital platform services businesses that are subject to prior notification b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
Under the Royal Decree on the Operation of Digital Platform Services Business, issued under the Electronic Transaction Act, digital service platforms are required to notify before operating their business (Sections 8 and 12). The royal decree defines "Digital Platform Service" as "the provision of intermediary services that connect business users, consumers, or users for the purpose of generating electronic transactions, with or without remuneration but does not include digital platform service, which is for the offering of goods or services of a single operator of a digital platform service or its affiliate acting as its representative, with or without remuneration." (Section 3). 

The Royal Decree also applies to the offshore operator that meet the prescribed criteria (e.g.,using .th domain name, using the Thai language or currency on the platform). Companies operating online platform services that have a gross income of more than THB 50,000,000 per year or have more than 5,000 monthly active users in Thailand must comply with the full-form notification requirement .Online platform service providers that do not meet the previous criteria, including e-service platforms containing web-boards hyperlinks/banners, are subject to the short-form notification. Both full-form and short-form notification information are required to be updated annually.
references:
https://www.mdes.go.th/law/detail/6356-พระราชกฤษฎีกาการประกอบธุรกิจบริการแพลตฟอร์มดิจิทัลที่ต้องแจ้งให้ทราบ-พ-ศ--๒๕๖๕;

Thailand.act:
Direct Sales and Direct Marketing Act, B.E.2545
(พระราชบัญญัติขายตรงและตลาดแบบตรง พ.ศ.2545);
Thailand.act_norm: direct sales and direct marketing act b e 2545
acts_norm: direct sales and direct marketing act b e 2545
Thailand.impact:
The Direct Sales and Direct Marketing Act under the supervision of the Office of the Consumer Protection Board requires the business involving direct sales/marketing to register to the Office of the Consumer Protection Board (OCPB). Since the enactment of the Direct Sales and Direct Marketing Act (No.3), the definition of 'direct marketing' is amended and will cover the e-commerce transaction. Any business operators who do not register as required shall be liable to a fine penalty, or imprisonment, or both. 

The requirements for Direct Marketing Registration:
1. Individuals or juristic persons both in Thai and abroad who wish to sell products by means of electronic commerce in Thailand.
2. The persons in item 1 whose income from selling products or services through electronic commerce exceeds 1.8 million Baht
per year.

Exemption from Direct Marketing Registration: in order to stimulate e-commerce by retail entrepreneurs, small and medium enterprises ("SME"), and community enterprises, a Ministerial Regulation on sales of goods or services through e-commerce which are not considered direct marketing was published in the Royal Gazette on 20 November 2018. As a result, the following types of sales and services through e-commerce are not considered direct marketing, and entities conducting these businesses no longer need to register with the OCPB as direct marketing business operators before commencing the business:
1. Sales of goods or provisions of services by an individual who is not registered as an entrepreneur performing direct marketing, and who derives income of no more than THB 1.8 million a year by selling goods or providing services through e-commerce.
2. Sales of goods or provisions of services by a SME that registered under the laws on SMEs.
3. Sales of goods or provisions of services by a community enterprise registered under the Community Enterprise
Incentives laws.
4. Sales of goods or provisions of services by a cooperative and group of farmers registered under the laws on cooperatives.
Thailand.timeframe: Since April 1999, last amended in May 2017

no sornorchor 3 2561 regarding regulations procedures and conditions on application for license to undertake
designated payment systems business b e 2561
Thailand.impact:
The Payment Systems Act B.E.2560 regulates electronic money services, acquiring services, payment facilitating services, electronic money transfer services, and services for the receipt of payment on behalf of sellers, service providers, or creditors. These services are subject to obtain E-Payment license from the Minister of Finance by recommendation of the Bank of Thailand (BOT) prior to the operation of such services. To be eligible to apply for an E-Payment License for Undersigned Payment Services, business providers must meet the minimum requirements specified below (Section 13, Section 14 of the Act and Clause 4.2 of the Bank of Thailand Notification):
(1) The applicant must be a limited company, a public limited company or financial institution, specialized financial institution or state enterprise.
(2) Company directors must meet the requirements of the BOT notification issued under the PSA and must not have prohibited characteristics, for example, being a bankrupt person; an incompetent person; and having been discharged from being a director or an executive of a public limited company due to lacking of appropriateness in respect of trustworthiness in managing business.  
(3) At least one director of a company must be a Thai national residing in Thailand.
(4) The paid-up capital requirements shall be in accordance with the prescribed threshold.
 For example, not less than 100 million thai baht for debit card, credit card or ATM card and for e-money.

Thailand.act:
Royal Decree on Customs Tariff B.E.2530
(พระราชกำหนด พิกัดอัตราศุลกากร พ.ศ.2530)
Thailand.act_norm: royal decree on customs tariff b e 2530
acts_norm: royal decree on customs tariff b e 2530
Thailand.impact: According to the Customs Tariff Decree B.E. 2530, Part 4 Category 12, imported goods with a Customs value (including freight and insurance charges) not exceeding 1,500 Thai Baht (approximately 46 USD as of June 2025) are exempt from taxes and duties collected by Customs.

Thailand.act: Custom Act B.E. 2560 (พระราชบัญญัติศุลกากร พ.ศ. 2560)
Thailand.act_norm: custom act b e 2560
acts_norm: custom act b e 2560
Thailand.impact:
There are no legal mechanisms or regulations currently in place to impose customs duties on electronic transmissions. Under the Customs Act, Section 13, it is specified that for the importation of goods into or exportation of goods out of the Kingdom, a duty shall be collected from the importer or exporter in accordance with this Act and the law on customs tariffs. The calculation of duties for goods imported into or exported out of the Kingdom shall be based on the nature of the goods, their customs value, the applicable customs tariff, and the duty rate at the time of importation completion (Sections 14 and 15).

Moreover, Thailand is a member of the World Trade Organization's (WTO) Joint Statement Initiative (JSI) on Electronic Commerce. The WTO JSI stabilized text Article 11.3 (Customs duties on electronic transmissions) provides that no Party shall impose customs duties on electronic transmissions between a person of one Party and a person of another Party.

Thailand currently adheres to the practice of not imposing customs duties on electronic transmissions, in accordance with the Ministerial Decision adopted at MC13. Furthermore, Thailand took part in the WTO Joint Statement Initiative (JSI) on Electronic Commerce and was actively engaged in the negotiations. Although the negotiation phase has concluded, Thailand's status with respect to the final agreement remains under consideration.

Thailand.act: .TH and .ไทย Domain Name Registration Policy B.E.2563
Thailand.act_norm: th and domain name registration policy b e 2563
acts_norm: th and domain name registration policy b e 2563
Thailand.impact:
Domain name .CO.TH is designed for business entities (Category 2). In this case, the business entities cover juristic persons registered with the Department of Business Development in Thailand, state enterprises, or any person who registered the Value Added Tax with the Revenue Department in Thailand. Importantly, this domain name holder can be foreigners who hold a registered trademark with the Department of Intellectual Property (DIP) in Thailand. However, the foreign juristic person or trademark owners must appoint a grantee of power of attorney (agent) to hold rights in a domain name on their behalf. 

(*Remark: Trademark Act applies to domain names on an analogical basis as the closest legislation. Thailand does not have a specific law on domain name registration.) All guidelines on domain names have been established in 2007: https://www.thnic.or.th/en/aboutus-en/).

This category is designed for all types of entities, including juristic person and a non-juristic person, with an official location in Thailand, or a natural person who is a Thai citizen or a non-Thai citizen who holds a valid work permit to work in Thailand.

Thailand.impact:
Domain name .NET.TH is designed for licensees of telecommunication business license (Category 5). This domain name category must conform with the Telecommunication Business Act. 

Under the Telecommunication Act, the domain name holders must obtain  a telecommunications license Type 1, Type 2, or Type 3 issued by the National Broadcasting and Telecommunications Commission (NBTC). Moreover, according to the amended Act in 2006, an applicant for Telecommunication Types 2 and 3 shall not be a foreigner under the Foreign Business Act. Therefore, the service providers Type 2 and 3 holding this domain name cannot be a foreign natural person or a juristic person not registered in Thailand or exceeding 49% of the total shares held by foreigners.

Thailand.impact: To operate business, foreigners must have a residence in Thailand to acquire the foreign business license from the ministry (Sections 8(3) and 11 FBA of the Foreign Business Act). The Ministerial Regulation stipulates the specific details to obtain the license. An appointed representative having a residence in the country suffices to apply for the license. Specifically, in the case where the power of attorney was executed in Thailand and the appointer does not have residence in Thailand, a copy or photocopy of the passport or certificate of temporary residence, or other evidence showing that at the time of the power of attorney such person had truly entered into Thailand, shall be submitted (Clause 4).

Thailand.act:
Royal Decree on the Operation of Digital Platform Services Businesses that are Subject to Prior Notification B.E.2565 
(พระราชกฤษฎีกา การประกอบธุรกิจบริการแพลตฟอร์มดิจิทัลที่ต้องแจ้งให้ทราบ พ.ศ.2565)
Thailand.act_norm: royal decree on the operation of digital platform services businesses that are subject to prior notification b e 2565
acts_norm: royal decree on the operation of digital platform services businesses that are subject to prior notification b e 2565
Thailand.impact:
Section 11 of the Royal Decree , issued under the Electronic Transaction Act, prescribes that the offshore digital platform providers shall appoint, in writing, a point of contact to perform duties to coordinate with the Agency in the Kingdom. The point of contact must be located within the Kingdom and must not carry out business operation under the law on foreign business operations. A contact person shall perform relevant duties, such as, filing annual reports or notifying any change to the terms and conditions. Notably, the Royal Decree solely establishes a point of contact for the purpose of notifying business operations and ensuring compliance with the regulations outlined in the Royal Decree. The point of contact mentioned in Section 11 does not require companies to have a representative office, local agent, legal representative, or a postbox within the economy to provide their services. Furthermore, the point of contact and the Digital Platform Services
Business Operator may be the same or separate entities. For example, a Digital Platform Services Business Operator (A) may designate a local entity in Thailand (B) to serve as the point of contact responsible for submitting documents and communicating with ETDA as the first-level liaison.

Moreover, the large-scale digital platform services shall be required to appoint a compliance officer along with implement a risk management system, conduct external audit, and comply with other obligations prescribed (Sections 19,20, 21). The large-scale digital platform services refer to digital platform services with revenue (before subtracting expenses) exceeding THB 300,000,000 per annum for each type of service or exceeding THB 1,000,000,000 per annum for all types of service, or with users in Thailand exceeding 10% of the country’s total population (Section 18). The compliance officer does not impose any local presence requirement.
references:
https://www.mdes.go.th/law/detail/6356-พระราชกฤษฎีกาการประกอบธุรกิจบริการแพลตฟอร์มดิจิทัลที่ต้องแจ้งให้ทราบ-พ-ศ--๒๕๖๕;

https://www.etda.or.th/getattachment/Regulator/DigitalPlatform/law/Clean-Royal-Decree-on-DP-Corrected-1.pdf.aspx?lang=th-TH

Thailand.act: Notification of the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology Regarding Computer Traffic Data Retention Criterias of Service Providers B.E.2564
Thailand.act_norm: notification of the ministry of information and communications technology regarding computer traffic data retention criterias of service providers b e 2564
acts_norm: notification of the ministry of information and communications technology regarding computer traffic data retention criterias of service providers b e 2564
Thailand.impact:
The Notification of the Ministry of Information and Communications Technology B.E.2564 repealed the existing Notification B.E.2550 (Clause 3). Clause 5 provides that Certain service providers must retain computer traffic data in accordance with the specifications in Annex B, including telecom and broadcast carriers, access service providers, host service providers, internet cafe providers, online application store providers, social media service providers, content and application service providers, cloud computing service providers and digital service providers (Clause 6 and Clause 7). 

The service providers must securely store traffic data and appoint a personnel responsible for coordinating and providing information to authorized officers under the Computer-Related Crime Act B.E. 2550 (2007) and its amendments to ensure prompt data delivery (Clause 9.3).

Note: Annex A lists down types and examples of service providers, such as VOIP, mobile service providers, Internet data center, Game online, App store, Google play, Chatbot, Telegram, Facebook, Youtube, Linkedin, Instagram, Line, MSN Messenger, Whatsapp, Web board, Blog, Internet Banking, and E-commerce service providers. Service providers must retain traffic data from the effective date of this notification. However, Internet cafés must comply within 1 year, and digital service providers within 180 days of its entry into force (Clause 13).
references: https://www.mdes.go.th/law/detail/4727-ประกาศกระทรวงดิจิทัลเพื่อเศรษฐกิจและสังคม-เรื่อง-หลักเกณฑ์การเก็บรักษาข้อมูลจราจรทางคอมพิวเตอร์ของผู้ให้บริการ-พ-ศ--๒๕๖๔

Thailand.act:
Consumer Protection Act B.E.2522
(พระราชบัญญัติคุ้มครองผู้บริโภค พ.ศ. 2522)
Thailand.act_norm: consumer protection act b e 2522
acts_norm: consumer protection act b e 2522
Thailand.impact:
The scope of the Consumer Protection Amendment Act or the (No.2) Act B.E.2541 (1998) provides protection for online consumers in an electronic transaction. A contract between the traders and the consumers must include necessary contract terms which not cause unreasonably disadvantage and unfair contract terms to the consumers (Section 35 bis). Additionally, advertising is considered as a part of the contract. Therefore, business operators must provide products or services to consumers exactly as offered through advertising. The conditions for advertising are in accordance with Section 22. Any businessman who fails to deliver the contract having the contract terms as required in Section 35 bis shall be liable to fine or imprisonment, or both (Section 57). 

Note that the Consumer Protection Act was amended by Consumer Protection Act (No.4) B.E.2562.
Thailand.timeframe: Since April 1979, last amended in May 2019
